> Students frequently forget to convert dB to linear scale before substituting into the Shannon equation. **A 30 dB SNR is 1000 (linear), NOT 30.** Also, the +1 in $\log_2(1 + S/N)$ is **mandatory** and must not be omitted. Dropping either leads to **−2 marks** immediately.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE Pattern]**  

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe a **general electronic communication system**. Identify and explain the function of each block.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between **Baseband and Passband transmission** with suitable diagrams. Why is passband transmission essential for wireless communication?  
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

A general electronic communication system consists of the following functional blocks (as shown in the diagram below):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Information Source"]:::src
    inEnc["Input Transducer"]:::enc
    txEnc["Transmitter Signal Processing"]:::enc
    ch["Channel Medium"]:::ch
    rxProc["Receiver Signal Processing"]:::enc
    outDec["Output Transducer"]:::enc
    sink["Destination User"]:::src
    noise["Noise Interference Distortion"]:::n

    src --> inEnc --> txEnc --> ch --> rxProc --> outDec --> sink
    noise -.->|corrupts| ch

    classDef src fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef enc fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#1f2937
    classDef ch fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef n fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,color:#111827
```

**Block Functions:**

1. **Information Source** — Originates the message (voice, text, video, sensor data). E.g., a human speaker, a temperature sensor, a video camera.

2. **Input Transducer** — Converts the physical message into an electrical signal. E.g., a **microphone** converts sound pressure to voltage, a **photodiode** converts light to current.

3. **Transmitter** — Performs signal conditioning: **amplification, modulation, coding, and filtering**. It prepares the signal for efficient propagation through the channel.

4. **Channel** — The physical medium (twisted pair, coaxial cable, optical fiber, free space, water). It introduces **attenuation, distortion, delay, and noise**.

5. **Noise Source** — External interference (thermal noise, electromagnetic interference, crosstalk) corrupting the signal in the channel.

6. **Receiver** — Performs the **inverse of the transmitter**: demodulation, decoding, equalization, and amplification. It extracts the original message from the corrupted received signal.

7. **Output Transducer** — Converts the recovered electrical signal back to the original physical form. E.g., a **loudspeaker**, **LED display**, or **monitor**.

8. **Destination** — The intended recipient of the message (human listener, data logger, computer).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Baseband Transmission:**
- The original information signal (e.g., audio bandwidth 20 Hz – 20 kHz, or digital pulses) is transmitted **directly** over the channel **without any frequency translation**.
- The signal occupies a frequency spectrum starting near DC (0 Hz) up to its maximum frequency $f_{\max}$.
- **Example:** Audio signal transmitted over a microphone cable; Ethernet 10BASE-T using Manchester encoding on twisted pair.
- **Limitation:** Cannot be used for wireless transmission because the required antenna length is $\lambda / 2 \approx c / (2 f_{\max})$, which for audio (~20 kHz) is **~7.5 km** — impractically large.

**Passband Transmission:**
- The information signal is **modulated onto a high-frequency carrier** $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ using AM, FM, PM, QAM, or OFDM.
- The spectrum is **shifted** from baseband to a band centered at $f_c$.
- **Example:** FM radio uses $f_c$ in 88–108 MHz, allowing $\lambda / 2 \approx 1.5$ m antenna — practical for vehicles.
- Enables **frequency division multiplexing (FDM)**, where many users share the medium on different carrier frequencies.

**Why Passband is Essential for Wireless Communication:**

1. **Practical Antenna Size:** Antenna length must be on the order of $\lambda / 2$ to $\lambda / 4$. For wireless carriers in MHz/GHz, this yields centimeter-scale antennas. For baseband audio (kHz), antenna size would be hundreds of meters — physically impossible.

2. **Spectrum Allocation:** Regulatory bodies (FCC, ITU) assign specific frequency bands. Passband modulation maps each user's signal into its allocated band, allowing **multiple non-interfering users** via FDM.

3. **Propagation Efficiency:** Lower frequencies (e.g., audio baseband) propagate as ground waves with severe attenuation. Higher carrier frequencies (e.g., UHF, SHF, EHF) enable efficient **line-of-sight, tropospheric, and ionospheric propagation** suitable for cellular, satellite, and radar.

4. **Multiplexing:** FDM and OFDM rely on placing multiple subcarriers in non-overlapping passbands, which is impossible in baseband.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:**  
> Confusing **baseband** with **digital**. A digital signal **can** be transmitted in passband (e.g., QAM, OFDM) and an analog signal **can** be transmitted in baseband (e.g., a microphone cable). The two classifications are **orthogonal axes**, not synonyms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before ESE):**

- **Simplex** = one-way, **Half-Duplex** = two-way alternating, **Full-Duplex** = two-way simultaneous.
- **Analog** signals are **continuous**; **Digital** signals are **discrete** bit streams.
- **Baseband** = direct, no carrier; **Passband** = modulated onto carrier for transmission.
- **Shannon-Hartley Theorem:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — capacity in bps.
- **SNR in dB:** convert to linear via $10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$ before substituting.
- **Wavelength formula:** $\lambda = c / f_c$ — governs antenna sizing.
- **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ — governs wireless link budgets.
- **Wired media:** twisted pair, coaxial, optical fiber. **Wireless media:** radio, microwave, mmWave, infrared, satellite.
- **Network topology:** Point-to-Point, Point-to-Multipoint, Broadcast.
- **Modern 5G NR** is full-duplex + digital + passband + wireless + point-to-multipoint.
- **Voice telephony bandwidth** is conventionally **4 kHz** (300 Hz to 3.4 kHz).
- **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s governs all wireless delay calculations.
- **Digital advantage:** noise immunity, encryption, regeneration, multiplexing.
- **Passband is essential for wireless** because of antenna size, spectrum allocation, and propagation efficiency.
- **FDD** uses two separate frequency bands; **TDD** uses one band split in time.
- Always draw a **block diagram** in 7-mark questions — it is worth **2 marks** on its own.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulation and Amplitude Modulation Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modulation and Amplitude Modulation Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Modulation?

**Modulation** is the process of varying one or more properties of a high-frequency periodic **carrier signal** (amplitude, frequency, or phase) with respect to a low-frequency **message (modulating) signal** that contains the information to be transmitted.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, modulation is the systematic encoding of information onto an electromagnetic carrier to enable efficient, reliable transmission over a communication channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Carrier Signal:** A high-frequency sinusoidal waveform (radio frequency) used as the vehicle to transport the information.
> **Message Signal:** A low-frequency signal (audio, video, data) containing the actual intelligence to be transmitted.

## 1.2 The Need for Modulation — A Real-World Analogy

Imagine you want to send a fragile glass vase from Kerala to Delhi via a fast-moving truck. The vase alone cannot survive the journey. So, you carefully **pack the vase inside a strong wooden box (carrier)**, strap it firmly, and then transport it. The box "carries" the vase safely.

In communications:
- The **vase** = low-frequency message signal (e.g., voice 20 Hz – 4 kHz)
- The **wooden box** = high-frequency carrier signal (e.g., 1 MHz)
- The **packing process** = **Modulation**
- The **truck/highway** = the wireless channel (free space)

### Why is modulation unavoidable?

1. **Antenna Size Reduction:** To radiate audio signals efficiently, the antenna length must be at least $\lambda/4$. For $f_m = 1 \text{ kHz}$, $\lambda = 3 \times 10^8 / 10^3 = 300 \text{ km}$, requiring a 75 km antenna — practically impossible. Modulation raises the frequency, drastically reducing antenna size.
2. **Avoiding Signal Interference:** Mixing audio signals directly would cause overlapping. Modulation shifts each signal to a different frequency band.
3. **Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Bandpass transmission techniques allow bandwidth-efficient trade-offs.
4. **Multiplexing Capability:** Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) is only possible after modulation.
5. **Power Efficiency and Range:** High-frequency carriers propagate over long distances with manageable power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The question "Why modulation is needed in communication systems?" is a guaranteed 3-mark question in the ESE. Always answer with: antenna size, multiplexing, SNR improvement, and interference reduction.

## 1.3 Types of Modulation

The modulating signal can alter one of three parameters of the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$:

| Parameter Varied | Modulation Type | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Amplitude $A_c$ | Amplitude Modulation (AM) | $A_c$ changes |
| Frequency $f_c$ | Frequency Modulation (FM) | $f_c$ changes |
| Phase $\phi$ | Phase Modulation (PM) | $\phi$ changes |

There are also hybrid and digital forms: DSB-SC, SSB, VSB, PAM, PWM, PPM, ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM — covered in later modules.

## 1.4 Amplitude Modulation (AM) — Formal Definition

**Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is the process in which the **amplitude $A_c$ of the carrier signal is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal**, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant.

The standard single-tone AM waveform is mathematically expressed as:

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where:
- $A_c$ = carrier amplitude
- $f_c$ = carrier frequency
- $f_m$ = message (modulating) frequency
- $m = A_m / A_c$ = **modulation index** (must satisfy $0 \le m \le 1$ for distortion-free AM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulation Index (m):** The ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal $A_m$ to the peak amplitude of the carrier $A_c$. It is dimensionless and represents the depth of modulation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of AM

Let the message signal be a single-tone sinusoid:
$$
m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)
$$

And the carrier be:
$$
c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

According to the AM definition, the instantaneous envelope of the modulated wave is $[A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)]$. Hence:

$$
s(t) = \left[ A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

Factoring out $A_c$:

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \frac{A_m}{A_c} \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

## 2.2 Frequency Spectrum of AM — Sideband Derivation

Expanding the product using the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$
s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c m}{2} \cos(2\pi (f_c - f_m) t) + \frac{A_c m}{2} \cos(2\pi (f_c + f_m) t)
$$

This expansion reveals **three frequency components**:

1. **Carrier component** at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$
2. **Lower Sideband (LSB)** at $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $\frac{A_c m}{2}$
3. **Upper Sideband (USB)** at $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $\frac{A_c m}{2}$

The **bandwidth of AM** is therefore:

$$
BW_{AM} = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Transmission Bandwidth for General Message:** If the message has maximum frequency $W$ (Hz), then $BW_{AM} = 2W$.

## 2.3 Power Analysis of AM Signal

The average power delivered to a load of resistance $R$ is calculated using the mean-square value of each component. The carrier power is:

$$
P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2 R}
$$

Assuming $R = 1 \, \Omega$ for simplicity (standard KTU convention):

$$
P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}
$$

Each sideband carries:

$$
P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{(A_c m / 2)^2}{2} = \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{8}
$$

The **total sideband power**:

$$
P_{SB} = P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{4}
$$

The **total transmitted power**:

$$
P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{4} = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{m^2}{2} \right)
$$

The **transmission efficiency** (fraction of total power carrying information):

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2} \times 100\%
$$

Maximum efficiency occurs at $m = 1$:

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{1}{2 + 1} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\%
$$

This is a major drawback of conventional AM — only **1/3 of the transmitted power** carries actual information.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 exam essentials. Memorize the table; expect direct numerical problems in Part B.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AM Signal | $s(t) = A_c [1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 2 | Modulation Index | $m = A_m / A_c$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le m \le 1$ |
| 3 | Percent Modulation | $m \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| 4 | Carrier Power | $P_c = A_c^2 / (2R)$ | Watts |
| 5 | Sideband Power (each) | $P_{SB,i} = A_c^2 m^2 / (8R)$ | Watts |
| 6 | Total Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = A_c^2 m^2 / (4R)$ | Watts |
| 7 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = P_c (1 + m^2/2)$ | Watts |
| 8 | Transmission Efficiency | $\eta = m^2 / (2 + m^2)$ | Unitless, max 33.33% |
| 9 | AM Bandwidth | $BW = 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 10 | Sideband Frequencies | $f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m$, $f_{USB} = f_c + f_m$ | Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In the table above, fractions are written using `/` to avoid breaking the markdown table. Do NOT use `\|` in your KTU answer sheet formulas.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AM Broadcast Radio (530 kHz – 1710 kHz):** Standard AM radio broadcasting worldwide still uses this scheme due to receiver simplicity and low cost.
- **Air Traffic Communication (108–137 MHz):** AM is preferred for aviation due to its robustness in envelope detection.
- **Two-way Radio (CB, Marine):** AM's compatibility with cheap diode detectors makes it ideal.
- **Heritage and Cost-Effective Systems:** Still used where simplicity beats spectral efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: AM Signal from First Principles

**Objective:** Derive the time-domain expression of an AM signal starting from the basic modulation definition.

**Step 1:** Write the message signal (a single-tone sinusoid):

$$
m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)
$$

**Step 2:** Write the high-frequency carrier signal:

$$
c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 3:** By the definition of AM, the envelope of $c(t)$ must follow $A_c + m(t)$. Thus, the modulated amplitude becomes:

$$
A(t) = A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $A(t)$ in place of $A_c$ in the carrier expression:

$$
s(t) = A(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) = \left[ A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $A_c$ to introduce the modulation index $m = A_m / A_c$:

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \frac{A_m}{A_c} \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $m$:

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**This is the standard KTU form of the AM signal.** The factor $[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]$ is the envelope, and $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is the carrier.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency Spectrum (Sideband Identification)

**Objective:** Show that the AM signal contains exactly three spectral components.

**Step 1:** Start with the standard form:

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 2:** Distribute $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ across the bracket:

$$
s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the product-to-sum identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2} [\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$ on the second term, with $A = 2\pi f_m t$ and $B = 2\pi f_c t$:

$$
\cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{1}{2} \cos(2\pi (f_c - f_m) t) + \frac{1}{2} \cos(2\pi (f_c + f_m) t)
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $A_c m$ and rewrite the full expression:

$$
s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c m}{2} \cos(2\pi (f_c - f_m) t) + \frac{A_c m}{2} \cos(2\pi (f_c + f_m) t)
$$

**Step 5:** Identify each term:

- **Term 1** → Carrier at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$
- **Term 2** → LSB at $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $A_c m / 2$
- **Term 3** → USB at $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $A_c m / 2$

**Step 6:** The transmission bandwidth is the difference between the highest and lowest frequency components:

$$
BW = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Power and Efficiency

**Objective:** Derive $P_t = P_c(1 + m^2/2)$ and $\eta = m^2/(2+m^2)$.

**Step 1:** Average power of a sinusoid $A \cos(2\pi f t)$ across resistance $R$ is $A^2 / (2R)$. For the carrier:

$$
P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}
$$

**Step 2:** For the LSB (amplitude $A_c m / 2$):

$$
P_{LSB} = \frac{1}{2R} \left( \frac{A_c m}{2} \right)^2 = \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{8R}
$$

**Step 3:** For the USB (amplitude $A_c m / 2$), by symmetry:

$$
P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{8R}
$$

**Step 4:** Total sideband power:

$$
P_{SB} = P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{8R} + \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{8R} = \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{4R}
$$

**Step 5:** Total transmitted power:

$$
P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} + \frac{A_c^2 m^2}{4R} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} \left[ 1 + \frac{m^2}{2} \right] = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{m^2}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Transmission efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{A_c^2 m^2 / (4R)}{A_c^2 (2 + m^2) / (4R)} = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2}
$$

**Step 7:** Maximum efficiency (at $m = 1$):

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{1^2}{2 + 1^2} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\%
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Modulation Index from Time-Domain Waveform

**Objective:** Derive the practical formula $m = (V_{\max} - V_{\min}) / (V_{\max} + V_{\min})$.

**Step 1:** From the envelope $A(t) = A_c[1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t)]$:

- **Maximum envelope** occurs at $\cos(2\pi f_m t) = +1$ → $A_{\max} = A_c(1 + m)$
- **Minimum envelope** occurs at $\cos(2\pi f_m t) = -1$ → $A_{\min} = A_c(1 - m)$

**Step 2:** Subtract to eliminate $A_c$:

$$
A_{\max} - A_{\min} = A_c (1 + m) - A_c (1 - m) = 2 A_c m
$$

**Step 3:** Add the two equations:

$$
A_{\max} + A_{\min} = A_c (1 + m) + A_c (1 - m) = 2 A_c
$$

**Step 4:** Divide Step 2 by Step 3:

$$
m = \frac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}} = \frac{V_{\max} - V_{\min}}{V_{\max} + V_{\min}}
$$

This is a frequently asked formula in KTU for graphical questions on AM waveforms.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — AM Modulation System

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the end-to-end functional flow of an AM communication system, from message generation to demodulated output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM[Message Signal m of t] --> nodeA[Amplifier and Level Shifter]
    nodeA --> nodeMul[Multiplier Product Modulator]
    nodeC[Oscillator Carrier A_c cos 2 pi f_c t] --> nodeMul
    nodeMul --> nodeAM[AM Signal s of t]
    nodeAM --> nodeCh[Channel Antenna and Free Space]
    nodeCh --> nodeRx[Receiver RF Stage]
    nodeRx --> nodeDet[Envelope Detector Diode plus RC]
    nodeDet --> nodeLPF[Low Pass Filter]
    nodeLPF --> nodeOut[Recovered Message m hat of t]

    style nodeM fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style nodeC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style nodeAM fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style nodeDet fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style nodeOut fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
```

**Explanation of the Flow:**

- **Message Signal $m(t)$:** Low-frequency information source (microphone, sensor).
- **Carrier Oscillator:** Generates the high-frequency sinusoid $c(t)$.
- **Multiplier (Product Modulator):** The core block where modulation mathematically occurs. In hardware, this is realized using a non-linear device (e.g., JFET, diode ring) followed by a bandpass filter.
- **AM Signal $s(t)$:** The transmitted waveform, a high-frequency carrier whose envelope follows $m(t)$.
- **Channel:** The wireless medium — antennas and free space.
- **Envelope Detector:** A simple diode + RC circuit that recovers the envelope.
- **Low Pass Filter:** Removes the carrier ripple, leaving the original message.

## 4.2 AM Waveform Envelope and Carrier Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG1[Time Domain Representation]
        nodeA1[Carrier cos 2 pi f_c t High Frequency] --- nodeA2[Envelope 1 + m cos 2 pi f_m t Low Frequency Variation]
        nodeA2 --- nodeA3[AM Signal s of t Carrier with Varying Envelope]
        nodeA3 --- nodeA4[Overmodulation m greater than 1 Causes Distortion Envelope Crossover]
    end

    subgraph SG2[Frequency Domain Spectrum]
        nodeB1[Impulse at f_c Carrier Amplitude A_c] --- nodeB2[Impulse at f_c minus f_m LSB Amplitude A_c m by 2]
        nodeB2 --- nodeB3[Impulse at f_c plus f_m USB Amplitude A_c m by 2]
        nodeB3 --- nodeB4[Total Bandwidth equals 2 f_m]
    end

    style nodeA4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style nodeB1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style nodeB2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style nodeB3 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style nodeB4 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
```

**Reading the Schematic:**

- **Time domain:** The AM waveform looks like a high-frequency carrier whose peaks trace the outline (envelope) of the message. When $m > 1$, the envelope crosses zero and the signal is said to be **overmodulated**, causing severe distortion.
- **Frequency domain:** Three discrete impulses appear — the carrier in the center, with two symmetric sidebands separated by $f_m$. The total spread is $2 f_m$.

## 4.3 Modulation Index Variation — Visual Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeLow[m equals 0.2 Undermodulated High Carrier Headroom] --> nodeMid[m equals 0.5 Standard AM Broadcast Typical]
    nodeMid --> nodeFull[m equals 1.0 Full Modulation Maximum Efficiency 33.33 percent]
    nodeFull --> nodeOver[m greater than 1.0 Overmodulation Envelope Distortion Illegal]

    style nodeLow fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style nodeMid fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style nodeFull fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style nodeOver fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overmodulation** ($m > 1$) is forbidden in commercial AM broadcasting because: (a) it creates envelope distortion, (b) it generates out-of-band spurious emissions, and (c) it interferes with adjacent channels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define modulation. List the types of modulation with the parameter they vary.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Modulation is the process of varying one or more parameters (amplitude, frequency, or phase) of a high-frequency carrier signal in accordance with the instantaneous value of the low-frequency message signal containing the information to be transmitted.

**Types of Modulation:**

| Type | Parameter Varied |
|---|---|
| Amplitude Modulation (AM) | Amplitude of carrier |
| Frequency Modulation (FM) | Frequency of carrier |
| Phase Modulation (PM) | Phase of carrier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** The examiner expects you to mention the three parameters. Writing a one-line definition is insufficient — always specify the high-frequency carrier and the low-frequency message signal.

---

### **Q2. Define modulation index. What is the significance of modulation index in AM?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The modulation index $m$ of an AM signal is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal $A_m$ to the peak amplitude of the carrier $A_c$:

$$
m = \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

**Significance:**

1. It determines the depth of modulation and the strength of the sidebands relative to the carrier.
2. It controls the bandwidth utilization and transmitted power distribution.
3. For distortion-free AM, $m$ must be in the range $0 \le m \le 1$.
4. It directly affects the transmission efficiency $\eta = m^2 / (2 + m^2)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU ESE pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks each and are split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You must answer either **Question A OR Question B** fully.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `OR` Choice 1**

#### **Q3 (a)** Derive the mathematical expression for an AM signal starting from the message and carrier signals. Hence, identify the sideband frequencies and determine the bandwidth of the AM signal. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the message and carrier**

Message signal: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$

Carrier signal: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

**Step 2: Apply the AM definition**

The amplitude of the carrier is varied in accordance with the message. So the modulated amplitude is $A_c + m(t) = A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$.

**Step 3: Form the AM signal**

$$
s(t) = \left[ A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4: Factor out $A_c$ and introduce $m$**

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \frac{A_m}{A_c} \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 5: Expand using product-to-sum identity**

$$
s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c m}{2} \cos\left[ 2\pi (f_c - f_m) t \right] + \frac{A_c m}{2} \cos\left[ 2\pi (f_c + f_m) t \right]
$$

**Step 6: Identify the three components**

- Carrier: frequency $f_c$, amplitude $A_c$
- LSB: frequency $f_c - f_m$, amplitude $A_c m / 2$
- USB: frequency $f_c + f_m$, amplitude $A_c m / 2$

**Step 7: Bandwidth**

$$
BW = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m
$$

---

#### **Q3 (b)** An AM broadcast transmitter radiates a carrier power of 10 kW when the modulation index is 0.6. Calculate:
(i) Total radiated power
(ii) Sideband power
(iii) Transmission efficiency
**(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P_c = 10 \text{ kW}$, $m = 0.6$.

**(i) Total radiated power:**

$$
P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{m^2}{2} \right) = 10 \left( 1 + \frac{0.36}{2} \right) = 10 \times 1.18 = 11.8 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Sideband power:**

$$
P_{SB} = P_t - P_c = 11.8 - 10 = 1.8 \text{ kW}
$$

Verification using direct formula:

$$
P_{SB} = \frac{P_c \cdot m^2}{2} = \frac{10 \times 0.36}{2} = 1.8 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(iii) Transmission efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{1.8}{11.8} = 0.1525 = 15.25\%
$$

Verification:

$$
\eta = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2} = \frac{0.36}{2.36} = 0.1525 = 15.25\% \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `OR` Choice 2**

#### **Q4 (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation of an AM signal. Define modulation index and explain what happens when $m > 1$. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (in text, for written exam):**

The AM signal is generated using a **product modulator** (multiplier) whose two inputs are:
- The message signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$
- The carrier signal $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

The output is the AM signal $s(t) = A_c[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

A **bandpass filter (BPF)** centered at $f_c$ follows the multiplier to suppress any harmonics introduced by the non-linear device. The filtered output is then amplified by a **power amplifier** and fed to the **antenna** for radiation.

**Modulation Index Definition:**

$$
m = \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

**What happens when $m > 1$ (Overmodulation):**

1. The envelope $A_c[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]$ becomes negative during part of the cycle, causing the carrier to undergo a **180° phase reversal**.
2. The demodulated output at the receiver suffers severe **envelope distortion**.
3. **Out-of-band harmonics** are generated, causing adjacent channel interference.
4. The FCC and other regulators strictly prohibit $m > 1$ in licensed AM broadcasting.
5. In some overmodulation cases, the efficiency technically exceeds 33.33%, but the distorted signal is unusable.

---

#### **Q4 (b)** The antenna current of an AM transmitter is 8 A when unmodulated and rises to 8.93 A when modulated. Find the modulation index. If the antenna current rises to 9.18 A on a different program, what is the new modulation index? **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Key relationship:** The radiated current is proportional to the square root of the radiated power. Total power:

$$
P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{m^2}{2} \right) \implies I_t^2 = I_c^2 \left( 1 + \frac{m^2}{2} \right)
$$

**Case 1:** $I_c = 8 \text{ A}$, $I_t = 8.93 \text{ A}$.

$$
8.93^2 = 8^2 \left( 1 + \frac{m_1^2}{2} \right)
$$

$$
79.7249 = 64 \left( 1 + \frac{m_1^2}{2} \right)
$$

$$
1 + \frac{m_1^2}{2} = \frac{79.7249}{64} = 1.2457
$$

$$
\frac{m_1^2}{2} = 0.2457 \implies m_1^2 = 0.4914 \implies m_1 = 0.701
$$

$$
\boxed{m_1 \approx 0.7}
$$

**Case 2:** $I_c = 8 \text{ A}$, $I_t = 9.18 \text{ A}$.

$$
9.18^2 = 8^2 \left( 1 + \frac{m_2^2}{2} \right)
$$

$$
84.2724 = 64 \left( 1 + \frac{m_2^2}{2} \right)
$$

$$
1 + \frac{m_2^2}{2} = 1.3168
$$

$$
\frac{m_2^2}{2} = 0.3168 \implies m_2^2 = 0.6335 \implies m_2 = 0.796
$$

$$
\boxed{m_2 \approx 0.796}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in AM Problems (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Forgetting to express $m^2/2$ correctly** — Many students write $P_t = P_c(1 + m)^2$ which is **wrong**. The correct form is $P_t = P_c(1 + m^2/2)$.
> 2. **Mixing $P_c$ and $P_t$** — In antenna-current problems, $I_c$ is the unmodulated current; $I_t$ is the modulated current. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Sign errors in sideband expressions** — The amplitude of each sideband is $A_c m / 2$, not $A_c m$. Forgetting the factor 2 in the denominator costs full marks.
> 4. **Bandwidth confusion** — For AM, $BW = 2 f_m$, not $f_m$. SSB has $f_m$, but conventional AM has $2 f_m$.
> 5. **Overmodulation tolerance** — Some students assume $m = 1.2$ is acceptable. In all KTU numericals, the answer must be in $0 \le m \le 1$ unless the question explicitly asks about overmodulation.
> 6. **Forgetting units** — Power must be in Watts (or kW with proper unit). Frequencies must carry Hz. The examiner deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Modulation** = varying carrier parameter (amplitude / frequency / phase) by the message.
- **Carrier frequency $f_c$** $\gg$ **message frequency $f_m$** — typically $f_c / f_m > 100$.
- **AM signal form:** $s(t) = A_c[1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **Modulation index:** $m = A_m / A_c$; valid range $0 \le m \le 1$ for distortion-free AM.
- **Spectrum:** Carrier at $f_c$ + LSB at $f_c - f_m$ + USB at $f_c + f_m$ — three discrete components.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW_{AM} = 2 f_m$ (for a general message: $BW = 2W$).
- **Carrier power:** $P_c = A_c^2 / (2R)$.
- **Total power:** $P_t = P_c (1 + m^2/2)$.
- **Sideband power:** $P_{SB} = P_c m^2 / 2$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = m^2 / (2 + m^2)$, **maximum 33.33%** at $m = 1$.
- **Overmodulation ($m > 1$):** Causes envelope distortion, phase reversal, and out-of-band radiation — illegal in AM broadcast.
- **Practical formula from waveform:** $m = (V_{\max} - V_{\min}) / (V_{\max} + V_{\min})$.
- **Antenna current relation:** $I_t = I_c \sqrt{1 + m^2/2}$ — frequently tested.
- **Major drawback of AM:** Wasteful power transmission (66.67% of power goes into the carrier, which carries no information).
- **Why AM still exists:** Simplicity of envelope detection → cheap receivers → still used in broadcast radio and aviation.
- **KTU keyword triggers to use in answers:** "envelope", "modulation index", "sideband", "bandwidth", "transmission efficiency".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expression for AM Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Expression for AM Wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is a modulation technique in which the **amplitude** of a high-frequency **carrier signal** is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of a low-frequency **message/modulating signal**, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant.

The standard time-domain expression for a single-tone amplitude modulated wave is:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where:
- **$A_c$** = Amplitude of the carrier signal (in Volts)
- **$A_m$** = Amplitude of the modulating (message) signal (in Volts)
- **$f_c$** = Frequency of the carrier signal (in Hz, typically high, e.g., 535 kHz – 1605 kHz for broadcast AM)
- **$f_m$** = Frequency of the modulating signal (in Hz, typically low, e.g., 20 Hz – 20 kHz for voice)
- **$m_a = A_m / A_c$** = Modulation index (must satisfy $0 \leq m_a \leq 1$ to prevent distortion)
- **$t$** = Time (in seconds)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Spotlight and the Speaker:**
> Imagine a person holding a strong **spotlight** (the carrier wave) sweeping steadily across a wall. The brightness of the spotlight represents the **amplitude** of the carrier. Now imagine the person is also speaking into a microphone: whenever the speaker talks **loudly**, the **brightness** of the spotlight increases; when the speaker is **silent**, the brightness returns to its normal resting level. The spotlight continues to sweep at the **same steady speed** (constant frequency), but its intensity rises and falls with the speaker's voice. This is exactly how Amplitude Modulation works — the carrier's **frequency stays fixed**, but its **amplitude follows the shape of the message signal**.

### 1.3 Underlying Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The two most important engineering metrics associated with an AM wave are:
> - **Modulation Index ($m_a$):** dimensionless, range $\mathbf{0 \le m_a \le 1}$
> - **Transmission Efficiency ($\eta$):** dimensionless, maximum theoretical value is **$\mathbf{33.33\%}$** when $m_a = 1$ (i.e., 100% modulation)

These quantities dictate the **fidelity**, **bandwidth**, and **power distribution** of the transmitted signal, and are heavily tested in KTU university examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of the AM System

The generation of an AM wave requires three fundamental signals:

1. **Carrier Signal — $c(t)$**
   - High-frequency sinusoid used as the "vehicle" for transmission.
   - Mathematical form: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$
   - Carries **no information** by itself.

2. **Modulating (Message) Signal — $m(t)$**
   - Low-frequency information-bearing signal (voice, music, data).
   - For a single-tone test signal: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$
   - For a general complex signal: $m(t)$ can be any band-limited waveform.

3. **Modulated Wave — $s(t)$**
   - The output of the modulation process.
   - Mathematical form: $s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + k_a m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$
   - For a single-tone case, $k_a m(t) = m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, where $k_a$ is the **amplitude sensitivity** of the modulator (V⁻¹).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the AM Expression

- The carrier alone, $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, has a **constant envelope** of $A_c$.
- To embed information, we **multiply** the carrier by a slowly varying envelope $\left[ 1 + k_a m(t) \right]$.
- The factor "$1$" inside the bracket ensures the envelope is **always positive** when $m_a \le 1$, preventing phase reversal and envelope distortion.
- The envelope, when traced, replicates the **exact shape** of $m(t)$ — this is why an AM receiver can recover information using a simple **envelope detector**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula tested under "Expression for AM Wave" in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus. Master these before attempting any numerical problem.

| **Sl. No.** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units / Range** |
|:-----------:|:--------------|:------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Carrier signal | $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 2 | Modulating signal | $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ | Volts |
| 3 | Modulation index | $m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le m_a \le 1$ |
| 4 | AM wave (time domain) | $s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 5 | AM wave (expanded) | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{m_a A_c}{2} \cos\left[2\pi (f_c - f_m) t\right] + \dfrac{m_a A_c}{2} \cos\left[2\pi (f_c + f_m) t\right]$ | Volts |
| 6 | Carrier component amplitude | $A_c$ | Volts |
| 7 | Sideband component amplitude | $\dfrac{m_a A_c}{2}$ | Volts |
| 8 | Lower Sideband (LSB) frequency | $f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m$ | Hz |
| 9 | Upper Sideband (USB) frequency | $f_{USB} = f_c + f_m$ | Hz |
| 10 | Transmission bandwidth | $BW = f_{USB} - f_{LSB} = 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 11 | Carrier power | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2 R}$ | Watts |
| 12 | Total sideband power | $P_{SB} = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2} A_c^{\,2}}{4 R}$ | Watts |
| 13 | Total transmitted power | $P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)$ | Watts |
| 14 | Transmission efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}}$ | Dimensionless, max **33.33 %** |
| 15 | Percentage modulation | $\%\, m_a = m_a \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 16 | Envelope (peak) | $A_{max} = A_c (1 + m_a)$ | Volts |
| 17 | Envelope (trough) | $A_{min} = A_c (1 - m_a)$ | Volts |
| 18 | Modulation index from envelope | $m_a = \dfrac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$ | Dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Broadcast Radio (MW/SW):** Standard AM broadcasting in the 540 kHz – 1600 kHz band uses the AM expression derived above, enabling long-distance ground-wave and sky-wave propagation.
> - **Aeronautical Communication:** Aircraft VHF voice links use AM due to simplicity of receiver hardware.
> - **Two-Way Radio (CB, Walkie-Talkies):** AM's resilience to fading and ease of demodulation make it ideal for low-cost mobile communication.
> - **Carrier Telegraphy (CW):** The conceptual basis of switching a carrier on/off (Morse keying) stems from the same AM mathematics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Carrier to Full AM Wave

**Given:**
$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{and} \quad m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 1 — Define the amplitude-modulated envelope.**
The amplitude of the carrier must vary linearly with the instantaneous value of $m(t)$. So we replace the constant $A_c$ with a time-varying amplitude:
$$A(t) = A_c + k_a \cdot m(t) = A_c + k_a A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2 — Define the modulation index.**
Let $m_a = k_a A_m$. By proper choice of modulator sensitivity, $m_a$ is purely a dimensionless ratio. The envelope becomes:
$$A(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the new envelope into the carrier.**
$$s(t) = A(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 4 — Distribute the product to separate the components.**
$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \, m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity.**
Using the standard identity $\cos A \cos B = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{m_a A_c}{2} \left\{ \cos\left[2\pi (f_c - f_m) t\right] + \cos\left[2\pi (f_c + f_m) t\right] \right\}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the spectral components.**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Carrier Component:** $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — at frequency $f_c$, amplitude $A_c$.
> - **Lower Sideband (LSB):** $\dfrac{m_a A_c}{2} \cos\left[2\pi (f_c - f_m) t\right]$ — at frequency $f_c - f_m$.
> - **Upper Sideband (USB):** $\dfrac{m_a A_c}{2} \cos\left[2\pi (f_c + f_m) t\right]$ — at frequency $f_c + f_m$.

This completes the **exhaustive derivation** of the AM wave expression.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Transmitted Power

**Given:** AM wave $s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ transmitted across a load resistance $R$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power.**
$$p(t) = \dfrac{s^2(t)}{R} = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{R} \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the squared envelope term.**
$$\left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]^2 = 1 + 2 m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) + m_a^{\,2} \cos^2(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 3 — Average over one carrier cycle.**
Over a period $T_c = 1/f_c$, the time-average gives $\langle \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \rangle = 1/2$ and the cross-terms involving $\cos(2\pi f_m t)$ vanish when averaged over many cycles (since $f_c \gg f_m$).

$$P_t = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2} A_c^{\,2}}{8R} \cdot 2 = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} \left( 1 + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Identify carrier and sideband powers.**
$$P_c = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} \quad \text{and} \quad P_{SB} = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2} A_c^{\,2}}{4R} = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} P_c$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.**
$$\boxed{P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Transmission Efficiency

**Step 1 — Definition.** Efficiency is the ratio of useful (sideband) power to total transmitted power.
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute expressions.**
$$\eta = \dfrac{\dfrac{m_a^{\,2} A_c^{\,2}}{4R}}{\dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} \left( 1 + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)} = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 \left( 1 + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**
$$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum efficiency case.**
For $m_a = 1$ (100% modulation):
$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 + 1} = \dfrac{1}{3} = 33.33\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 33.33% upper limit is the **single most-tested result** in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions on AM efficiency. Memorize it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AM Wave Generation and Spectral Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Modulating Signal m of t equals A sub m cos 2 pi f sub m t] -->|Input message| B[Multiplier Block]
    K[DC Source equals 1] -->|Bias term| B
    C[Carrier Signal c of t equals A sub c cos 2 pi f sub c t] -->|High frequency carrier| B
    B -->|Product output| D[AM Wave s of t]
    D --> E[Spectrum Analyzer Stage]
    E --> F1[Carrier Spike at f sub c with Amplitude A sub c]
    E --> F2[LSB Spike at f sub c minus f sub m with Amplitude m sub a A sub c over 2]
    E --> F3[USB Spike at f sub c plus f sub m with Amplitude m sub a A sub c over 2]
    D --> G[Transmitter Antenna Load R]
    G --> H[Total Transmitted Power P sub t equals P sub c times 1 plus m sub a squared over 2]
    H --> I[Sideband Power P sub SB equals m sub a squared P sub c over 2]
    H --> J[Carrier Power P sub c equals A sub c squared over 2R]
```

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain Spectrum of the AM Wave

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPECTRUM["Frequency Domain Spectrum of AM Wave"]
        direction LR
        SP1["f sub c minus f sub m : LSB Amplitude m sub a A sub c over 2"]
        SP2["f sub c : Carrier Amplitude A sub c"]
        SP3["f sub c plus f sub m : USB Amplitude m sub a A sub c over 2"]
        SP1 --- SP2 --- SP3
    end
    BWW["Total Bandwidth BW equals 2 f sub m"]
    SP1 -.-> BWW
    SP3 -.-> BWW
```

### 4.3 Envelope Behaviour for Different Modulation Indices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["m sub a less than 1 : Under-Modulation Envelope Never Crosses Zero"] --> OUT1["Distortion Free Recovery Possible"]
    M2["m sub a equals 1 : 100 percent Modulation Envelope Just Touches Zero"] --> OUT2["Maximum Efficiency 33.33 percent"]
    M3["m sub a greater than 1 : Over-Modulation Envelope Crosses Zero and Inverts"] --> OUT3["Severe Distortion - Information Loss"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The functional architecture shows how three signals combine at a multiplier to produce the AM wave, which is then decomposed into its three frequency components. The spectrum diagram highlights the **symmetric placement** of USB and LSB around the carrier, with the total occupied bandwidth being exactly **$2 f_m$**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Define modulation index for an AM wave. What is its range and significance?

**Model Answer:**
The modulation index $m_a$ of an AM wave is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the modulating signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier signal:
$$m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$$
It is a **dimensionless quantity** whose valid range is $0 \le m_a \le 1$. Its significance is twofold: (i) it controls the **depth of modulation** of the carrier envelope, and (ii) it determines the **transmission efficiency** and **sideband power** of the AM signal. When $m_a > 1$, over-modulation occurs, causing envelope distortion and irreversible information loss.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
What is the maximum transmission efficiency of an AM wave? Under what condition is it achieved?

**Model Answer:**
The maximum transmission efficiency of a standard AM wave is **33.33 % (or 1/3)**. It is achieved when the modulation index $m_a = 1$, i.e., under **100% modulation**. At this condition, all of the carrier power is fully utilized, and the sideband power equals half the carrier power. Beyond this point, $m_a > 1$ produces distortion, and efficiency is no longer physically meaningful in the standard formula.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Many students forget the factor of **"1 + "** inside the envelope bracket — this leads to the wrong conclusion that the envelope is sometimes negative. Always write the AM expression as $A_c[1 + m_a \cos(\cdot)]\cos(\cdot)$.
> - **Pitfall 2:** In power calculations, students often forget to apply the **½** factor from the sideband expansion. The sideband amplitude is $\dfrac{m_a A_c}{2}$, not $m_a A_c$.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Confusing **bandwidth** ($2 f_m$) with carrier frequency ($f_c$). The bandwidth is a function of the **modulating** frequency, not the carrier.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Writing modulation index in **percentage** in a derivation that requires its decimal form. Always convert: $m_a = (\% \text{ modulation})/100$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Derive the expression for an amplitude-modulated wave, starting from the carrier and modulating signals. Clearly identify the carrier, lower sideband, and upper sideband components in both time and frequency domains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an AM transmitter, the carrier amplitude is $A_c = 10$ V, modulating signal amplitude is $A_m = 6$ V, and the load resistance is $R = 50\ \Omega$. Calculate the modulation index, total transmitted power, carrier power, sideband power, and transmission efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation — see Section 3.1 above, Steps 1 through 6. Final answer:
$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{m_a A_c}{2} \cos\left[2\pi (f_c - f_m) t\right] + \dfrac{m_a A_c}{2} \cos\left[2\pi (f_c + f_m) t\right]$$
with LSB at $f_c - f_m$ and USB at $f_c + f_m$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Modulation index:
$$m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6$$

Step 2 — Carrier power:
$$P_c = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} = \dfrac{(10)^2}{2 \times 50} = \dfrac{100}{100} = 1\ \text{W}$$

Step 3 — Sideband power:
$$P_{SB} = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2} A_c^{\,2}}{4R} = \dfrac{(0.6)^2 \times 100}{4 \times 50} = \dfrac{0.36 \times 100}{200} = 0.18\ \text{W}$$

Step 4 — Total transmitted power:
$$P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right) = 1 \times \left( 1 + \dfrac{0.36}{2} \right) = 1 \times 1.18 = 1.18\ \text{W}$$

Step 5 — Transmission efficiency:
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \dfrac{0.18}{1.18} = 0.1525 = 15.25\%$$

**Verification using the closed-form efficiency formula:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}} = \dfrac{0.36}{2.36} = 0.1525 = 15.25\% \ \checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram and waveform sketches, explain the generation of a single-tone AM wave. State the conditions to avoid distortion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The output of an AM transmitter has a maximum envelope amplitude of $A_{max} = 15$ V and a minimum envelope amplitude of $A_{min} = 5$ V when measured across a $50\ \Omega$ load. Determine the carrier amplitude, modulating amplitude, modulation index (in percentage), total sideband power, and total transmitted power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Generation of single-tone AM wave:**

A single-tone AM wave is generated by applying the message signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ to a **non-linear device or balanced modulator**, followed by a **band-pass filter** centered at $f_c$. Mathematically:
$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Conditions to avoid distortion:**
1. The modulator must operate in its **linear region**.
2. The modulation index must satisfy $m_a \le 1$ so the envelope never crosses zero.
3. The carrier frequency must be much greater than the modulating frequency ($f_c \gg f_m$) to prevent sideband overlap.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Carrier amplitude from envelope extrema:
$$A_c = \dfrac{A_{max} + A_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{15 + 5}{2} = 10\ \text{V}$$

Step 2 — Modulating amplitude:
$$A_m = \dfrac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{15 - 5}{2} = 5\ \text{V}$$

Step 3 — Modulation index and percentage:
$$m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \%\, m_a = 50\%$$

Step 4 — Carrier power:
$$P_c = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} = \dfrac{100}{100} = 1\ \text{W}$$

Step 5 — Sideband power:
$$P_{SB} = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \cdot P_c = \dfrac{(0.5)^2}{2} \times 1 = 0.125\ \text{W}$$

Step 6 — Total transmitted power:
$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = 1 + 0.125 = 1.125\ \text{W}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Expression for AM Wave**

- The AM wave has the universal form: $\,s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$
- The factor **"1 +"** is critical — it keeps the envelope **non-negative** when $m_a \le 1$.
- The spectrum contains **three components**: Carrier (at $f_c$), LSB (at $f_c - f_m$), and USB (at $f_c + f_m$).
- **Sideband amplitude** = $\dfrac{m_a A_c}{2}$ — remember the factor of ½ from the product-to-sum identity.
- **Bandwidth** $= 2 f_m$ — depends only on the modulating frequency, not on the carrier.
- **Total power:** $P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}}$; maximum is **33.33%** at $m_a = 1$.
- **Over-modulation** ($m_a > 1$) causes **envelope crossover** and **distortion** — strictly forbidden in practice.
- **Modulation index from envelope:** $m_a = \dfrac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$.
- The carrier component carries **no information** — all information resides in the two sidebands.
- Use $k_a A_m = m_a$ to convert modulator sensitivity to modulation index in derivation problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulation Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modulation Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Modulation Index** (denoted by the Greek letter $\mu$ or $m_a$) is a dimensionless parameter in Amplitude Modulation (AM) that quantitatively describes the extent to which the amplitude of a carrier wave is varied in proportion to the amplitude of the modulating (information-bearing) signal. It is formally defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the modulating signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier signal.

$$m_a = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

where $A_m$ is the peak amplitude of the modulating signal and $A_c$ is the peak amplitude of the carrier signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Amplitude Modulation):** The modulation index is a foundational scalar quantity that governs the **depth of modulation**, determines the **spectral composition** of the transmitted AM signal, and directly influences the **transmission efficiency** and **power distribution** among the carrier and sidebands. It is the single most tested parameter in Module 1 university examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a stationary boat (the **carrier wave**) on a calm lake. Now, a friend starts rocking the boat up and down by pushing the dock (the **modulating signal**). The *Modulation Index* is essentially a measure of **how violently** your friend is shaking the boat relative to the boat's baseline size.

- If your friend barely taps the boat (small push) → the boat's envelope wiggles a little → **Low modulation index** (under-modulation).
- If your friend pushes exactly as hard as the boat's height → the boat's envelope just touches the zero water-line → **Modulation index = 1** (100% modulation, the ideal KTU target).
- If your friend pushes harder than the boat's height → the boat's envelope gets "crushed" and reverses phase → **Modulation index > 1** (over-modulation, causing severe signal distortion).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Boundaries for KTU Board Exams:**
> - $0 \le m_a \le 1$ → **Under-modulation to Perfect Modulation** (Distortion-free AM transmission).
> - $m_a > 1$ → **Over-modulation** (Envelope distortion, phase reversal, illegal bandwidth spread).
> - **Standard Reference Value:** For commercial AM broadcast (MW/SW radio), $m_a = 0.3$ to $0.5$ is mandated by regulatory bodies like the FCC to prevent adjacent channel interference and ensure linear amplification.

## 1.4 The Three Regimes of Modulation Index

| Regime | Range | Envelope Behavior | KTU Verdict |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Under-modulation** | $m_a < 1$ | Envelope never crosses zero, no distortion | Acceptable, but under-utilizes transmitter power |
| **Critical (100%) Modulation** | $m_a = 1$ | Envelope just touches zero at every trough | Ideal theoretical limit, maximum efficiency |
| **Over-modulation** | $m_a > 1$ | Envelope crosses zero, **phase reversal** occurs | **Strictly prohibited** — causes severe distortion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Framework of an AM Signal

An amplitude-modulated wave is constructed by multiplying a high-frequency carrier with a low-frequency message signal, plus the original carrier (to enable envelope detection at the receiver). The standard time-domain expression is:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \cdot m_a \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

This is conventionally compressed into a single product form:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

The bracketed term $[1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)]$ is the **envelope function**. The modulation index $m_a$ directly scales the depth of this envelope variation.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — The Carrier:** A pure sinusoid $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ with constant amplitude $A_c$. This carries no information.
- **Step 2 — The Modulating Signal:** $A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ where $f_m \ll f_c$ (information signal).
- **Step 3 — The Multiplication:** The carrier amplitude is made to vary linearly with the instantaneous value of the modulating signal. The instantaneous envelope amplitude becomes $A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$.
- **Step 4 — Normalization:** Dividing both the envelope peaks and troughs by $A_c$ yields a normalized envelope. The peak variation normalized is exactly $A_m / A_c = m_a$.
- **Step 5 — The Condition for Distortionless Detection:** For the envelope to remain non-negative at all instants, we require $A_m \le A_c$, which mathematically enforces $m_a \le 1$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Governing Equation | Boundary Condition | Engineering Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Modulation Index (AM) | $m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ | $0 \le m_a \le 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Percentage Modulation | $\% m_a = m_a \times 100$ | $0\%$ to $100\%$ | Percent |
| 3 | Peak Envelope Value | $A_{max} = A_c (1 + m_a)$ | Always positive | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Minimum Envelope Value | $A_{min} = A_c (1 - m_a)$ | $\ge 0$ for no distortion | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Modulation Index (from envelope) | $m_a = \dfrac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$ | Valid for any $m_a$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)$ | Depends on $P_c$ | Watts (W) |
| 7 | Carrier Power | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}$ | Fixed by transmitter | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Total Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2}$ | Zero when $m_a = 0$ | Watts (W) |
| 9 | Transmission Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}}$ | Maximum at $m_a = 1$ | Dimensionless (ratio) |
| 10 | Bandwidth of DSB-SC AM | $BW = 2 f_m$ | Independent of $m_a$ | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Equations 6, 8, and 9 reveal that the *information-carrying power* (sideband power) is **entirely** dependent on $m_a^{\,2}$. This is why the KTU examiner frequently tests why commercial AM transmitters operate at $m_a \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ — it is a trade-off between loudness (audibility) and distortion/interference.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern broadcast engineering, modulation index is a **directly measurable physical quantity**. Spectrum analyzers and modulation meters in AM broadcast transmitters display $m_a$ in real time. Regulatory bodies (FCC, ITU) mandate a maximum of $m_a = 1$ (100%) to prevent over-modulation which would cause:
- Adjacent channel splatter (out-of-band radiation).
- Envelope detector failure at the receiver.
- "Crackling" audio artifacts (harmonic distortion products like $2f_m$, $3f_m$).

In **single-sideband (SSB)** and **vestigial-sideband (VSB)** systems, the concept evolves into a **modulation percentage** computed differently, since the carrier is suppressed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modulation Index from Time-Domain AM Expression

We start with the standard AM waveform equation. We will explicitly derive $m_a$ from the peak and trough values of the envelope.

**Starting Expression:**

$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Envelope Function:**
The envelope $E(t)$ is the magnitude of the slowly varying term multiplying $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

$$E(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Find Maximum Envelope ($A_{max}$):**
The maximum value of $\cos(2\pi f_m t)$ is $+1$ (occurs at $t = 0, 1/f_m, 2/f_m, \ldots$).

$$A_{max} = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a (1) \right] = A_c (1 + m_a)$$

**Step 3 — Find Minimum Envelope ($A_{min}$):**
The minimum value of $\cos(2\pi f_m t)$ is $-1$ (occurs at $t = 1/(2f_m), 3/(2f_m), \ldots$).

$$A_{min} = A_c \left[ 1 + m_a (-1) \right] = A_c (1 - m_a)$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Two Equations Simultaneously for $m_a$:**
We now have a system of two linear equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{max} &= A_c (1 + m_a) \\
A_{min} &= A_c (1 - m_a)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the second from the first:**

$$A_{max} - A_{min} = A_c (1 + m_a) - A_c (1 - m_a) = 2 A_c m_a$$

$$\Rightarrow A_c m_a = \frac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Add the two equations:**

$$A_{max} + A_{min} = A_c (1 + m_a) + A_c (1 - m_a) = 2 A_c$$

$$\Rightarrow A_c = \frac{A_{max} + A_{min}}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Divide to isolate $m_a$ (the required formula):**

$$m_a = \frac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$$

This is the **oscilloscope-based measurement formula** for $m_a$, and it is a guaranteed 7-mark KTU question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Distribution and Transmission Efficiency

**Step 1 — Start with the expanded AM signal using the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:**

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c m_a}{2} \cos \left[ 2\pi (f_c - f_m) t \right] + \frac{A_c m_a}{2} \cos \left[ 2\pi (f_c + f_m) t \right]$$

**Step 2 — Identify the three spectral components:**
- Carrier term: $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ with amplitude $A_c$.
- Lower Sideband (LSB): amplitude $A_c m_a / 2$.
- Upper Sideband (USB): amplitude $A_c m_a / 2$.

**Step 3 — Compute the average power of each component across a $1\,\Omega$ load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{carrier} &= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} \\
P_{LSB} &= \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{A_c m_a}{2} \right)^2 = \frac{A_c^{\,2} m_a^{\,2}}{8} \\
P_{USB} &= \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{A_c m_a}{2} \right)^2 = \frac{A_c^{\,2} m_a^{\,2}}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Sum to obtain total transmitted power:**

$$P_t = P_c + P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} + \frac{A_c^{\,2} m_a^{\,2}}{8} + \frac{A_c^{\,2} m_a^{\,2}}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the sideband term:**

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} + \frac{A_c^{\,2} m_a^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} \left( 1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $P_c = A_c^{\,2} / 2$ to get the final form:**

$$\boxed{P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)}$$

**Step 7 — Derive transmission efficiency $\eta$ (ratio of sideband power to total power):**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{P_c \cdot m_a^{\,2} / 2}{P_c (1 + m_a^{\,2}/2)} = \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}}$$

**Step 8 — Maximum efficiency at $m_a = 1$:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1^2}{2 + 1^2} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.33\%$$

This is a **hallmark KTU result**: the maximum transmission efficiency of a conventional AM transmitter (with carrier) is only **33.33%**. This fact directly motivates the invention of **DSB-SC** and **SSB** systems in subsequent modules.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (For KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** An AM broadcast transmitter has a carrier amplitude of $200\,\text{V}$ (peak) into a $50\,\Omega$ antenna. The modulating signal amplitude is $80\,\text{V}$ (peak). Find: (i) modulation index, (ii) total radiated power, (iii) sideband power, (iv) transmission efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$m_a = \frac{80}{200} = 0.4$$

$$P_c = \frac{(200)^2}{2 \times 50} = 400\,\text{W}$$

$$P_t = 400 \left( 1 + \frac{(0.4)^2}{2} \right) = 400 \times 1.08 = 432\,\text{W}$$

$$P_{SB} = 432 - 400 = 32\,\text{W} \quad \text{(or equivalently } 400 \times 0.08\text{)}$$

$$\eta = \frac{32}{432} \times 100\% \approx 7.41\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AM Waveform and Envelope Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of an AM transmitter, illustrating how the modulation index $m_a$ is injected and controlled in the signal path.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Message Signal m(t) Amp Am"] --> nodeB["Variable Gain Amplifier"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Multiplier Stage"]
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeD["Crystal Oscillator Fc"] --> nodeE["Carrier Generator Amp Ac"]
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF["Adder Summer Stage"]
    nodeG["DC Bias Ac"] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH["Class C Power Amplifier"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Antenna Radiator"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Free Space Channel"]
    nodeK["Modulation Index Control Knob"] --> nodeB
    nodeK --> nodeF
    nodeL["Modulation Meter Display ma"] --> nodeB
```

## 4.2 Modulation State Machine — Under, Critical, and Over Modulation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UnderModulated
    UnderModulated : ma is less than 1
    UnderModulated : Envelope has no zero crossing
    UnderModulated : Low sideband power
    UnderModulated --> CriticalModulated : Increase Am until ma equals 1
    CriticalModulated : ma equals exactly 1
    CriticalModulated : Envelope just touches zero
    CriticalModulated : Efficiency 33.33 percent
    CriticalModulated --> OverModulated : Further increase Am
    OverModulated : ma is greater than 1
    OverModulated : Envelope crosses zero
    OverModulated : Phase reversal and distortion
    OverModulated : Illegal operation
    CriticalModulated --> UnderModulated : Decrease Am
```

## 4.3 Envelope Anatomy — A Visual Schematic of an AM Waveform

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1["Maximum Envelope Point A plus Am"] --> nodeP2["Carrier Peak Amp Ac"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Mean Envelope Line Amp Ac"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["Minimum Envelope Point A minus Am"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["Zero Reference Line"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP4
    nodeP1 -. "Peak Variation" .-> nodeP3
    nodeP3 -. "Peak Variation" .-> nodeP4
    nodeA["Modulation Index ma equals Am over Ac"] -. Annotation .-> nodeP3
    nodeB["Period of Message Tm equals 1 over Fm"] -. Annotation .-> nodeP1
    nodeC["Period of Carrier Tc equals 1 over Fc"] -. Annotation .-> nodeP2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The "Mean Envelope Line" represents the unmodulated carrier amplitude $A_c$. The vertical distance from this mean line to either $A_{max}$ or $A_{min}$ is the peak amplitude variation $A_m$. The ratio $A_m / A_c$ is by definition the modulation index $m_a$.

## 4.4 Spectral Composition Block Diagram (Frequency Domain View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeF1["Frequency Axis F"] --> nodeF2["Lower Sideband at Fc minus Fm"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF3["Carrier Spike at Fc"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF4["Upper Sideband at Fc plus Fm"]
    nodeF5["Carrier Amplitude Ac"] -. "Delta function height" .-> nodeF3
    nodeF6["Sideband Amplitude Ac ma over 2"] -. "Delta function height" .-> nodeF2
    nodeF6 -. "Delta function height" .-> nodeF4
    nodeF7["Bandwidth BW equals 2 Fm"] -. "Span annotation" .-> nodeF1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define modulation index for an AM signal. What is its significance in communication systems?**

**Model Answer:**
The modulation index $m_a$ of an AM signal is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the modulating signal ($A_m$) to the peak amplitude of the carrier signal ($A_c$), i.e., $m_a = A_m / A_c$. It is a **dimensionless quantity** that lies in the range $0 \le m_a \le 1$ for distortion-free transmission. Its significance includes:
1. It determines the **depth of modulation** and hence the loudness of the recovered audio.
2. It controls the **power distribution** between the carrier (wasted power) and sidebands (useful power).
3. It directly affects the **transmission efficiency** $\eta = m_a^{\,2} / (2 + m_a^{\,2})$.
4. It must be kept $\le 1$ to avoid **envelope distortion** and adjacent channel interference. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**An AM signal has a maximum envelope amplitude of $20\,\text{V}$ and a minimum envelope amplitude of $4\,\text{V}$. Calculate the modulation index.**

**Model Answer:**

$$m_a = \frac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}} = \frac{20 - 4}{20 + 4} = \frac{16}{24} = 0.667$$

**Percentage modulation** $= 66.7\%$. Since $m_a < 1$, the system is under-modulated and operates without envelope distortion. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the total transmitted power and transmission efficiency of an AM wave in terms of the carrier power $P_c$ and modulation index $m_a$. Explain why the maximum efficiency of a conventional AM transmitter is only $33.33\%$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AM transmitter radiates a carrier power of $10\,\text{kW}$. When sinusoidal modulation is applied, the modulation index is $0.8$. Calculate:
   (i) Total radiated power.
   (ii) Power in each sideband.
   (iii) Transmission efficiency.
   (iv) Peak amplitude of the carrier if the antenna resistance is $50\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The AM signal is:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c m_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{A_c m_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

The three components contribute powers (across load $R$):

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}, \quad P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^{\,2} m_a^{\,2}}{8R}$$

**Total Power:**

$$P_t = P_c + P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} \left( 1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right) = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{P_c m_a^{\,2}/2}{P_c(1 + m_a^{\,2}/2)} = \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}}$$

At $m_a = 1$:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{2+1} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\%$$

**Explanation:** The carrier component carries **no information**; it merely provides a reference for envelope detection. At $m_a = 1$, the sideband power equals $P_c / 2$, but the carrier still contributes $P_c$, meaning $\frac{2}{3}$ of the total radiated power is **wasted** as carrier. This fundamental limitation of conventional AM motivated the development of **DSB-SC** and **SSB** modulation schemes, where the carrier is suppressed and efficiency can theoretically approach 100%. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Given: $P_c = 10\,\text{kW}$, $m_a = 0.8$, $R = 50\,\Omega$.

**(i) Total radiated power:**

$$P_t = 10000 \left( 1 + \frac{(0.8)^2}{2} \right) = 10000 \times 1.32 = 13.2\,\text{kW}$$

**(ii) Power in each sideband:**

$$P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{P_c m_a^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{10000 \times 0.64}{4} = 1600\,\text{W} = 1.6\,\text{kW}$$

**(iii) Transmission efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{1.6 + 1.6}{13.2} \times 100\% = \frac{3.2}{13.2} \times 100\% \approx 24.24\%$$

**(iv) Peak carrier amplitude:**

$$A_c = \sqrt{2 P_c R} = \sqrt{2 \times 10000 \times 50} = \sqrt{1000000} = 1000\,\text{V}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted — INTERNAL CHOICE]

**(a)** With the help of a neat time-domain sketch, explain the phenomenon of **over-modulation** in an AM system. Discuss its effects on the demodulated signal at the receiver and outline the practical measures to prevent it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The antenna current of an AM transmitter is $10\,\text{A}$ when only the carrier is switched on. When modulation is applied, the antenna current rises to $11\,\text{A}$. Assuming a $50\,\Omega$ resistive antenna, find the modulation index and the percentage of power saved if the carrier were suppressed (DSB-SC transmission), keeping sideband power constant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Over-modulation** occurs when $m_a > 1$, i.e., $A_m > A_c$. In this regime, the envelope $[1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)]$ becomes **negative** for a portion of the modulating cycle. Physically, this means the carrier undergoes a **180° phase reversal** at every zero crossing of the negative envelope region.

**Time-Domain Sketch Description:**
- The envelope would dip below the zero axis and "fold" upward, creating a non-sinusoidal envelope shape with sharp V-shaped troughs.
- These troughs contain the modulating signal's information, but the phase reversal destroys the original waveform's shape.

**Effects on the Demodulated Signal:**
1. An **envelope detector** (diode + RC) cannot follow the phase reversal; it either outputs zero or an arbitrary DC offset during the reversal, producing a "clipped" audio waveform.
2. **Harmonic distortion** products at $2f_m$, $3f_m$, etc., are generated, spreading energy outside the allocated bandwidth (adjacent channel interference).
3. The recovered audio sounds **distorted and harsh**, often with "crackling" artifacts.

**Practical Measures to Prevent Over-Modulation:**
1. **Automatic Modulation Control (AMC)** circuits in broadcast transmitters that dynamically reduce gain when $m_a$ approaches 1.
2. **Speech Clipping and Filtering** at the studio to limit the peak amplitude of the modulating audio.
3. **Limiter Amplifiers** in the modulator chain.
4. Real-time **modulation monitoring** with feedback to the audio console operator.
5. Regulatory cap of $m_a = 1$ (or 125% for negative peak clipping per FCC standards in some regions). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Find Carrier Power (no modulation):**

$$P_c = I_c^{\,2} \times R = (10)^2 \times 50 = 5000\,\text{W} = 5\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Find Total Power with Modulation:**

$$P_t = I_t^{\,2} \times R = (11)^2 \times 50 = 6050\,\text{W} = 6.05\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Use the power relation to find $m_a$:**

$$P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)$$

$$6050 = 5000 \left( 1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \right)$$

$$1.21 = 1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} = 0.21$$

$$m_a^{\,2} = 0.42 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_a = \sqrt{0.42} \approx 0.648$$

**Step 4 — Sideband Power (must be conserved in DSB-SC):**

$$P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} = 5000 \times 0.21 = 1050\,\text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Power in DSB-SC transmission (carrier suppressed, only sidebands transmitted):**

$$P_{DSB-SC} = P_{SB} = 1050\,\text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Power saved:**

$$\Delta P = P_t - P_{DSB-SC} = 6050 - 1050 = 5000\,\text{W} = 5\,\text{kW}$$

**Percentage of power saved:**

$$\% \text{ Saved} = \frac{5000}{6050} \times 100\% \approx 82.64\%$$

**Conclusion:** Suppressing the carrier in a DSB-SC system saves **82.64%** of the transmitted power while delivering the exact same information-bearing sideband power to the receiver. This is a striking illustration of the inefficiency of conventional AM with full carrier. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Many students write $m_a^{\,2}$ in the efficiency formula instead of $m_a^{\,2}/2$. Remember: the LSB and USB each carry $P_c m_a^{\,2}/4$, so they sum to $P_c m_a^{\,2}/2$. Half-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 2. **Using $A_{max}/A_{min}$:** This is a *frequently-incorrect* formula circulating in coaching materials. The correct ratio is $(A_{max} - A_{min})/(A_{max} + A_{min})$. Loss of 2 marks if used in an ESE.
> 3. **Confusing RMS and Peak Amplitudes:** Antenna current in KTU problems is almost always **RMS**. Power $= I_{rms}^{\,2} R$, but amplitude $A_c = \sqrt{2} I_{rms} \sqrt{R}$.
> 4. **Over-modulation described as "just louder":** Many students incorrectly state that $m_a > 1$ simply increases volume. It *causes envelope distortion*, not louder audio. Up to 3-mark loss on the 7-mark over-modulation sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** $m_a = A_m / A_c$ — a **dimensionless** scalar ratio, no units.
- **Legal Range:** $0 \le m_a \le 1$ for distortion-free AM; $m_a = 1$ is the **100%** critical point.
- **Envelope Formula:** $m_a = (A_{max} - A_{min}) / (A_{max} + A_{min})$ — use this when given an oscilloscope trace.
- **Peak Envelope Power Relation:** $A_{max} = A_c(1 + m_a)$ and $A_{min} = A_c(1 - m_a)$. Both must remain non-negative.
- **Total Power:** $P_t = P_c \left( 1 + m_a^{\,2}/2 \right)$ — this is the single most important power formula in Module 1.
- **Sideband Power:** $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot m_a^{\,2} / 2$ — note that the sidebands together carry half the carrier power only when $m_a = 1$.
- **Maximum Efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = 1/3 = 33.33\%$ at $m_a = 1$. Conventional AM is fundamentally inefficient.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = 2 f_m$ for full AM (DSB with carrier). **Independent** of $m_a$ — the bandwidth does not change with modulation depth.
- **Over-modulation consequences:** Envelope crosses zero → phase reversal → envelope detector fails → harmonic distortion → out-of-band splatter. **Illegal operation.**
- **Spectral Components:** A conventional AM signal has **three** frequency components — carrier at $f_c$, LSB at $f_c - f_m$, USB at $f_c + f_m$.
- **KTU Hot Topics for 14-mark derivations:** (1) Time-domain AM expression, (2) Power distribution derivation, (3) Efficiency derivation, (4) Over-modulation explanation with remedies, (5) DSB-SC vs. conventional AM comparison.
- **Practical Rule of Thumb:** Commercial AM broadcasters operate at $m_a \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ to balance audio loudness, regulatory compliance, and minimal distortion.
- **Key Waveform Mnemonic:** "**One is One, Above is Done**" — $m_a = 1$ is the limit, anything above is forbidden.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AM Power and Sideband Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** AM Power and Sideband Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Amplitude Modulation (AM)

**Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is a modulation technique used in electronic communication, most commonly for transmitting information via a radio carrier wave. In AM, the **amplitude** (signal strength) of the carrier wave is varied in proportion to that of the message signal (baseband signal), while the **frequency** and **phase** of the carrier remain constant.

Mathematically, the standard AM signal is expressed as:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Where:
- $A_c$ = Amplitude of the carrier signal (Volts)
- $f_c$ = Frequency of the carrier (Hertz)
- $m(t) = k_a \cdot m_g(t)$ = Modulating (message) signal, normalized such that $\vert m(t) \vert \le 1$
- $k_a$ = Amplitude sensitivity of the modulator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The condition $\vert m(t) \vert \le 1$ is critical. If the message signal amplitude exceeds the carrier amplitude, **over-modulation** ($m > 1$) occurs, causing severe distortion called **"envelope clipping"** or **"carrier folding."** This is a frequently tested concept.

## 1.2 Definition of Sidebands
