When a carrier is amplitude-modulated by a single-tone (sinusoidal) message signal, the modulated output does not merely consist of a single frequency. Instead, the **non-linear multiplication process** (mixing) generates **two new frequency components**, mathematically appearing as a direct consequence of the trigonometric product-to-sum identity. These new frequencies are called **sidebands**:

- **Lower Sideband (LSB):** Located at $f_c - f_m$ (Hertz)
- **Upper Sideband (USB):** Located at $f_c + f_m$ (Hertz)

Where $f_m$ is the frequency of the modulating (message) signal.

For a general broadband message signal $m(t)$ containing a band of frequencies from $f_{min}$ to $f_{max}$, the AM spectrum consists of:
- The **carrier** at $f_c$
- A **Lower Sideband (LSB)** spanning from $f_c - f_{max}$ to $f_c - f_{min}$
- An **Upper Sideband (USB)** spanning from $f_c + f_{min}$ to $f_c + f_{max}$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Swing and Push" Model**
> Imagine a child on a swing moving back and forth at a steady rhythm (this is the **carrier wave** — a constant, rhythmic oscillation). Now, a parent standing behind gives small pushes at varying strengths (this is the **modulating signal** — the information). When the parent pushes gently, the swing's amplitude (back-and-forth height) increases slightly; when the parent pushes harder, the swing goes much higher. The rhythm (frequency) of the swing NEVER changes, only its **height (amplitude)** changes in step with the push strength.
>
> The "push" is the **message** (audio, voice, data). The swing's **envelope** (the imaginary curve tracing the swing's peaks) literally contains the information of the push strength. At the receiver, a simple **envelope detector** can recover the original push strength by tracking this envelope.

**Why sidebands exist — the "Echo" Analogy:** When you shout into a canyon, your voice (single frequency content) returns as echoes at slightly altered pitches. Similarly, the modulating signal "echoes" itself around the carrier frequency — once below ($f_c - f_m$) and once above ($f_c + f_m$). These echoes ARE the sidebands, and they contain the *real information*. The carrier itself carries NO information — it is just a "vehicle" for transmission.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Modulation Index ($\mu$ or $m_a$):** A dimensionless quantity in the range $[0, 1]$ for standard AM, given by $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$.
- **Carrier Power ($P_c$):** The average power dissipated by the unmodulated carrier, $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ Watts.
- **Total Transmitted Power ($P_t$):** Sum of carrier power and sideband powers, $P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$.
- **Sideband Power ($P_{SB}$):** Total power in both sidebands, $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}$.
- **Power Efficiency ($\eta$):** The fraction of total power actually carrying information, $\eta = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$.
- **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency:** **33.33%** (occurs at $\mu = 1$, i.e., 100% modulation).
- **Bandwidth ($BW$):** For a single-tone message, $BW = 2 f_m$; for a broadband message, $BW = 2 f_{max}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The AM process can be understood in the following structured logical flow:

### Step 1: The Baseband Message Signal
The information-bearing signal $m(t)$ is a low-frequency signal (audio range, typically 20 Hz to 20 kHz) with a peak amplitude $A_m$. It is bounded such that $\vert m(t) \vert \le A_m$.

### Step 2: DC Biasing (The "+1" Term)
The AM modulator adds a **DC offset** of 1 (normalized) to the message signal, ensuring the envelope $\left[ 1 + \frac{A_m}{A_c} \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]$ never goes negative. This is why standard AM is also called **Double Sideband with Carrier (DSB-C)** or **DSB-LC (Large Carrier)**.

### Step 3: Multiplication with Carrier
The DC-biased message is **multiplied** (not added) with a high-frequency carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ inside a non-linear device (typically a transistor configured as a modulator or a balanced mixer).

### Step 4: Trigonometric Expansion Generates Sidebands
Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]$, the multiplication produces two shifted copies of the message spectrum.

### Step 5: Power Distribution
The total transmitted power is split between:
- **The Carrier** (constant, contains NO information)
- **The Two Sidebands** (mirror images, BOTH contain identical information)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Wasteful Carrier" Insight:** For a 100% modulated signal, only **1/3 of the total power** is in the sidebands (which carry all the information). The remaining **2/3 is wasted in the carrier**, which carries no information. This is the primary motivation for **DSB-SC (Suppressed Carrier)** and **SSB (Single Sideband)** systems studied later in Module 1.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Purpose | Engineering Reason |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| DC biasing | Prevent envelope reversal | Allows simple envelope detection at receiver |
| Multiplication | Translate baseband to passband | Antennas need physically small dimensions ($\lambda/2$), requiring high-frequency transmission |
| Sideband generation | Spectral translation | Carries the baseband information via the carrier "vehicle" |
| Power calculation | Link budget analysis | Determines transmitter power amplifier ratings and battery life |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcast AM Radio (530 kHz – 1710 kHz):** Uses standard DSB-C (with carrier) for simple, low-cost receiver design.
- **Aviation Communication (118 MHz – 137 MHz, AM):** Still uses AM because multiple stations can be monitored simultaneously using envelope detectors.
- **Two-Way Radio (Walkie-Talkies, Marine):** AM chosen for robustness and simplicity of demodulation.
- **Citizen Band (CB) Radio:** Uses AM and SSB variants for hobby communications.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD:** The following table contains the complete formula set required for any AM Power/Sideband problem in KTU exams. Memorize this thoroughly.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | AM Signal (Single-Tone) | $s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | Standard DSB-C |
| 2 | AM Signal (Expanded) | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \dfrac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$ | Volts | Shows carrier + 2 sidebands |
| 3 | Modulation Index | $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Dimensionless | Range: $0 \le \mu \le 1$ for standard AM |
| 4 | Percentage Modulation | $\% \mu = \mu \times 100$ | Percent | KTU often asks for this |
| 5 | Carrier Power | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ | Watts | $R$ = load resistance (usually 50 $\Omega$ or 75 $\Omega$) |
| 6 | Total Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = \dfrac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R} = P_c \cdot \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}$ | Watts | Distributed equally between LSB and USB |
| 7 | Power in ONE Sideband | $P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \dfrac{P_c \mu^2}{4}$ | Watts | Each sideband carries duplicate information |
| 8 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$ | Watts | Sum of carrier + both sidebands |
| 9 | Power Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$ | Dimensionless (or %) | Max value = 33.33% at $\mu = 1$ |
| 10 | Bandwidth (Single-Tone) | $BW = 2 f_m$ | Hertz | Difference between USB and LSB |
| 11 | Bandwidth (Broadband) | $BW = 2 f_{max}$ | Hertz | $f_{max}$ = highest message frequency |
| 12 | Peak Envelope Power (PEP) | $P_{PEP} = \dfrac{(A_c (1 + \mu))^2}{2R}$ | Watts | Maximum instantaneous power |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation Constraint Reminder:** Notice that the table above uses `\vert m(t) \vert` style notation (or simple inequality $\le$) instead of vertical pipes $\vert \cdot \vert$ within cells. This is to preserve the markdown table rendering integrity — a critical KTU-prep technical note for digital notes.

## 2.5 Power Efficiency Discussion

The efficiency $\eta = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$ is a key topic in KTU exams. Let us understand its behavior:

- At $\mu = 0$ (no modulation): $\eta = 0$ (no information transmitted, makes sense).
- At $\mu = 0.5$ (50% modulation): $\eta = \dfrac{0.25}{2.25} = 0.111 = 11.11\%$.
- At $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation): $\eta = \dfrac{1}{3} = 33.33\%$ (maximum standard AM efficiency).
- For $\mu > 1$ (over-modulation): The formula breaks down because the envelope is clipped, causing distortion. Standard AM transmitters limit $\mu \le 1$.

**Engineering Insight:** Since max efficiency is only ~33%, this is why engineers developed:
- **DSB-SC** (eliminates carrier, so $\eta$ can approach 100% if all power is in sidebands)
- **SSB** (eliminates one redundant sideband, halving bandwidth and saving power)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expansion of the AM Signal into Carrier + Sidebands

This derivation proves mathematically why AM produces three distinct frequency components.

**Given:**

$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 1:** Distribute the carrier amplitude $A_c$ across both terms inside the bracket.

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

*Explanation:* This splits the AM wave into a pure carrier term (first) and a product term (second) that will generate the sidebands.

**Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:

$$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A - B) + \cos(A + B)\right]$$

Apply with $A = 2\pi f_c t$ and $B = 2\pi f_m t$:

$$\cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the equation.

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \mu \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the coefficients of the sideband terms.

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

*Final Result:* This is the **expanded AM signal**, clearly showing:
- A **carrier** at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$
- A **Lower Sideband (LSB)** at $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$
- An **Upper Sideband (USB)** at $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Both sidebands have IDENTICAL amplitudes $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$. This is why the sideband power calculation uses a factor of 2 (two sidebands).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power in the Carrier ($P_c$)

**Starting Point:** A sinusoidal signal $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ across a load resistance $R$.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous power is $p(t) = \dfrac{v^2(t)}{R}$.

$$p(t) = \frac{A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)}{R}$$

**Step 2:** The average power is the time-average of $p(t)$ over one period $T_c = \frac{1}{f_c}$.

$$P_c = \langle p(t) \rangle = \frac{1}{T_c} \int_0^{T_c} \frac{A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)}{R} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Use the identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$.

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_c} \int_0^{T_c} \frac{1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)}{2} \, dt$$

**Step 4:** The integral of the cosine term over a full period is zero (orthogonality of sinusoids).

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_c} \cdot \frac{T_c}{2} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}$$

*Result:*

$$\boxed{P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power in the Sidebands ($P_{SB}$)

**Step 1:** Each sideband is a sinusoid of amplitude $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$. Power of a single sideband:

$$P_{LSB} = \frac{(\mu A_c / 2)^2}{2R} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R}$$

**Step 2:** Similarly for the USB:

$$P_{USB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R}$$

**Step 3:** Total sideband power is the sum of both sidebands (since LSB and USB are at different frequencies, their powers add linearly — no cross-term).

$$P_{SB} = P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R}$$

**Step 4:** Express $P_{SB}$ in terms of $P_c$ by substituting $P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}$, which means $\frac{A_c^2}{R} = 2P_c$:

$$P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2}{4} \cdot \frac{A_c^2}{R} = \frac{\mu^2}{4} \cdot 2 P_c = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}$$

*Result:*

$$\boxed{P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R} = \frac{P_c \mu^2}{2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Transmitted Power ($P_t$)

**Step 1:** By the **superposition principle of power** (signals at different frequencies contribute powers additively):

$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the derived expressions:

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\frac{A_c^2}{2R}$ (which is $P_c$):

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}\left[ 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right]$$

*Result:*

$$\boxed{P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right)}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Power Efficiency ($\eta$)

**Step 1:** Definition of efficiency — useful power (sidebands, which carry information) divided by total transmitted power.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}$ and $P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$:

$$\eta = \frac{\mu^2 P_c / 2}{P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2} \right)}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $P_c$:

$$\eta = \frac{\mu^2 / 2}{1 + \mu^2 / 2}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by 2 to simplify:

$$\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$$

*Result:*

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example (For Exam Practice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical:** A 1000 kHz carrier is amplitude-modulated by a 5 kHz sinusoidal signal. The carrier amplitude is 100 V, and the modulation index is 0.8. The signal is fed to an antenna with a radiation resistance of 50 $\Omega$.
> 
> **Find:** (i) Carrier Power, (ii) Total Sideband Power, (iii) Total Power, (iv) Power Efficiency, (v) Bandwidth.

**Given:** $f_c = 1000$ kHz, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $A_c = 100$ V, $\mu = 0.8$, $R = 50$ $\Omega$.

**Solution:**

(i) Carrier Power:

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} = \frac{(100)^2}{2 \times 50} = \frac{10000}{100} = 100 \text{ W}$$

(ii) Total Sideband Power:

$$P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{2} = 100 \times \frac{(0.8)^2}{2} = 100 \times 0.32 = 32 \text{ W}$$

(iii) Total Power:

$$P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right) = 100 \times (1 + 0.32) = 100 \times 1.32 = 132 \text{ W}$$

(iv) Power Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} = \frac{0.64}{2.64} = 0.2424 = 24.24\%$$

(v) Bandwidth:

$$BW = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 \text{ kHz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

**Sideband Frequencies (bonus):** LSB at $f_c - f_m = 995$ kHz, USB at $f_c + f_m = 1005$ kHz.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AM Transmitter Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMsg[Message Signal m of t] --> nodeSum
    nodeDC[DC Bias of 1] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum[Adder Summing Junction] --> nodeMult
    nodeCarrier[Carrier Ac cos 2 pi fc t] --> nodeMult
    nodeMult[Multiplication Stage Modulator] --> nodePA
    nodePA[Power Amplifier] --> nodeAntenna
    nodeAntenna[Transmit Antenna 50 ohms]
```

*Description:* This diagram shows the canonical AM generation flow. The message signal $m(t)$ is summed with a DC bias of 1 to prevent envelope reversal. The result is multiplied with the high-frequency carrier in a non-linear modulator. The output is amplified and radiated.

## 4.2 AM Signal Spectral Structure (Frequency Domain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSpec[AM Frequency Spectrum for Single Tone Modulation] --> nodeAxis
    nodeAxis[Frequency Axis f] --> nodeLSB
    nodeAxis --> nodeC
    nodeAxis --> nodeUSB
    nodeLSB[LSB at fc minus fm] --> labelA[Amplitude = mu Ac divided by 2]
    nodeC[Carrier at fc] --> labelB[Amplitude = Ac]
    nodeUSB[USB at fc plus fm] --> labelC[Amplitude = mu Ac divided by 2]
    labelA --> obs[Observation: USB and LSB are mirror images about the carrier]
    labelB --> obs
    labelC --> obs
```

*Description:* This block-level diagram illustrates the spectral structure of an AM wave. The carrier is the tallest spike at $f_c$. The two sidebands are smaller spikes of equal height, symmetrically placed at $f_c - f_m$ and $f_c + f_m$.

## 4.3 Power Distribution Pie Chart (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodePt[Total Transmitted Power Pt] --> nodePc
    nodePt --> nodePsb
    nodePc[Carrier Power Pc] --> labelD[Constant: NO information carried]
    nodePsb[Sideband Power Psb] --> labelE[Information-bearing: split equally between LSB and USB]
    labelE --> nodeLSB2
    labelE --> nodeUSB2
    nodeLSB2[LSB Power = Pc times mu squared divided by 4]
    nodeUSB2[USB Power = Pc times mu squared divided by 4]
```

*Description:* This flow visualizes how total power is partitioned. At $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation), the carrier holds 2/3 of the total power (wasted), while both sidebands together hold only 1/3 (useful).

## 4.4 Modulation States Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeMu[Modulation Index mu] --> nodeUnder
    nodeMu --> nodeHundred
    nodeMu --> nodeOver
    nodeUnder[Under Modulation: mu less than 1] --> noteUnder[Envelope follows message smoothly: safe operation]
    nodeHundred[Hundred Percent: mu equals 1] --> noteHundred[Maximum efficiency 33.33 percent: ideal AM]
    nodeOver[Over Modulation: mu greater than 1] --> noteOver[Envelope distortion and carrier folding: REJECTED in standard AM]
```

*Description:* This decision-flow style block diagram shows the three operating regimes of an AM modulator and their practical implications. KTU exams often include a numerical on determining $\mu$ from a given AM waveform diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (Conceptual)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define modulation index for an AM signal. What happens when the modulation index exceeds unity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **modulation index ($\mu$)** of an AM signal is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal ($A_m$) to the peak amplitude of the carrier signal ($A_c$):

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates the extent to which the carrier amplitude is varied by the message signal.

When the modulation index exceeds unity ($\mu > 1$), the condition $\vert m(t) \vert \le 1$ is violated, leading to **over-modulation**. The envelope of the AM wave becomes **clipped** (the trough of the envelope reverses phase), causing severe **harmonic distortion** and **carrier folding**. The recovered message at the receiver contains spurious harmonics, making it unintelligible. Hence, standard AM transmitters are designed with $\mu \le 1$.

*(1 mark — definition with formula; 1 mark — explanation of over-modulation; 1 mark — consequence and remedy.)*

---

### **Question A2 (Definition)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define sidebands in AM. Why are both upper and lower sidebands considered redundant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In amplitude modulation, when a carrier $f_c$ is modulated by a single-tone message $f_m$, two new frequency components are generated at $f_c - f_m$ (Lower Sideband, LSB) and $f_c + f_m$ (Upper Sideband, USB) due to the multiplication process. Collectively, these are called **sidebands**. For a broadband message, the sidebands form continuous bands of frequencies flanking the carrier.

Both LSB and USB are **mirror images** of each other (symmetric about $f_c$) and contain **identical information** about the message signal. The amplitude spectrum of the baseband message is faithfully reproduced in both sidebands. Hence, transmitting both sidebands is **redundant** — either one alone (as in SSB) is sufficient to reconstruct the original message. This redundancy is what makes SSB (Single Sideband) modulation an efficient alternative, halving the bandwidth and improving power efficiency.

*(1 mark — define LSB and USB; 1 mark — state mirror-image property; 1 mark — explain redundancy and link to SSB.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the generation of an AM signal. Derive the expression for the total power and power efficiency of an AM wave. A 750 kHz carrier is modulated by a 5 kHz audio signal. The carrier amplitude is 100 V and modulation index is 0.6. The signal is transmitted across a 75 $\Omega$ antenna. Calculate (i) the carrier power, (ii) total sideband power, (iii) total transmitted power, and (iv) the power efficiency.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Generation of AM Signal (7 marks):**

The AM signal is generated using a **block diagram** consisting of:

1. **Message Signal Source:** Produces the low-frequency message $m(t)$ with peak amplitude $A_m$.
2. **DC Bias Source:** Provides a constant DC offset of magnitude $A_c$ (or normalized to 1) to ensure the modulating envelope never goes negative.
3. **Adder/Summing Junction:** Adds the DC bias to the message signal, producing $A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$.
4. **Carrier Oscillator:** Generates the high-frequency sinusoidal carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ using a crystal oscillator.
5. **Multiplier/Modulator Stage:** A non-linear device (e.g., a transistor in the non-linear region or an IC multiplier) multiplies the DC-biased message with the carrier.
6. **Power Amplifier (PA):** Boosts the AM signal to a level suitable for transmission.
7. **Antenna:** Radiates the AM signal into free space.

The mathematical expression at the multiplier output is:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Expanding using the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$, we obtain the three frequency components: carrier at $f_c$, LSB at $f_c - f_m$, and USB at $f_c + f_m$.

**(b) Derivation of Total Power and Efficiency (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Carrier Power* — The carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ across resistance $R$ delivers:

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}$$

*Step 2: Sideband Power* — Each sideband has amplitude $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$:

$$P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}$$

*Step 3: Total Power* — Since the carrier and sidebands are at different frequencies, powers add:

$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}\left[ 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right] = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$$

*Step 4: Power Efficiency:*

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c / 2}{P_c (1 + \mu^2 / 2)} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$$

*Numerical Computation:* Given $A_c = 100$ V, $\mu = 0.6$, $R = 75$ $\Omega$.

(i) Carrier Power:

$$P_c = \frac{(100)^2}{2 \times 75} = \frac{10000}{150} = 66.67 \text{ W}$$

(ii) Total Sideband Power:

$$P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{2} = 66.67 \times \frac{0.36}{2} = 66.67 \times 0.18 = 12 \text{ W}$$

(iii) Total Transmitted Power:

$$P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right) = 66.67 \times 1.18 = 78.67 \text{ W}$$

(iv) Power Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{12}{78.67} = 0.1525 = 15.25\%$$

Alternatively: $\eta = \dfrac{0.36}{2.36} = 15.25\%$. ✓

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the frequency spectrum of an AM signal. Derive expressions for the power in the carrier, sidebands, and total power of an AM wave. A transmitter supplies 10 kW to a 50 $\Omega$ antenna when unmodulated. Determine the total power radiated when the carrier is modulated to a depth of (i) 50% and (ii) 100%. Also find the efficiency in each case.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AM Frequency Spectrum (7 marks):**

The **frequency spectrum** of an AM signal is a plot of amplitude (or power) versus frequency. For a single-tone message of frequency $f_m$ modulating a carrier of frequency $f_c$, the spectrum consists of three discrete spectral lines:

- A **carrier component** at $f = f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$.
- A **lower sideband (LSB)** at $f = f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$.
- An **upper sideband (USB)** at $f = f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$.

The **bandwidth** of the AM signal is the difference between the highest and lowest frequency components:

$$BW = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m$$

For a broadband message signal containing frequencies from $f_{min}$ to $f_{max}$, the spectrum becomes continuous. The carrier appears as a tall delta function at $f_c$, and the sidebands form two mirror-image bands of width $f_{max} - f_{min}$ on either side of the carrier. The total bandwidth is $2 f_{max}$.

**Observation:** The total bandwidth is **twice** the highest message frequency, and the sidebands are mirror images of the message spectrum. The carrier amplitude is independent of the message — it is a **fixed-amplitude spike**.

**(b) Power Derivations and Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

*Derivation:* (Recapitulated)

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}, \quad P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}, \quad P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$$

*Numerical:* Given: Unmodulated carrier power $P_c = 10$ kW, $R = 50$ $\Omega$.

**Case (i): 50% Modulation ($\mu = 0.5$):**

$$P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{2} = 10 \times \frac{0.25}{2} = 1.25 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right) = 10 \times \left( 1 + 0.125 \right) = 10 \times 1.125 = 11.25 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{1.25}{11.25} = 0.1111 = 11.11\%$$

**Case (ii): 100% Modulation ($\mu = 1.0$):**

$$P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{2} = 10 \times \frac{1}{2} = 5 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right) = 10 \times \left( 1 + 0.5 \right) = 10 \times 1.5 = 15 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta = \frac{5}{15} = 0.3333 = 33.33\%$$

**Summary Table:**

| Modulation Depth | $\mu$ | $P_{SB}$ (kW) | $P_t$ (kW) | $\eta$ (%) |
|------------------|-------|----------------|------------|------------|
| 50% | 0.5 | 1.25 | 11.25 | 11.11 |
| 100% | 1.0 | 5.00 | 15.00 | 33.33 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Students frequently forget to convert kW to W or vice versa. Always write units explicitly in the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing $P_t$ with $P_{SB}$:** A common error is computing total power using the sideband formula. Remember: $P_t = P_c + P_{SB}$, not just $P_{SB}$.
> 3. **Squaring Errors:** The term $\mu^2/2$ is sometimes miscalculated as $\mu^2 \cdot 2$ or $\mu/2$. Be precise with the algebra.
> 4. **Over-modulation:** If the problem gives $\mu > 1$, do NOT blindly apply the standard AM formula. Mention over-modulation and that the efficiency formula is no longer valid in the standard sense (envelope distortion invalidates the simple trigonometric expansion).
> 5. **No internal consistency check:** Always verify $P_t > P_c$. If your answer gives $P_t < P_c$, an error has been made.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Read This 5 Minutes Before Entering the Hall!**

- [ ] **AM Signal Equation:** $s(t) = A_c [1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — write this FIRST on the answer sheet for any AM problem.
- [ ] **Three Frequency Components:** Carrier ($f_c$), LSB ($f_c - f_m$), USB ($f_c + f_m$). All three have the same phase reference if the message is a pure cosine.
- [ ] **Sideband Amplitude:** $\dfrac{\mu A_c}{2}$ — half the product of modulation index and carrier amplitude.
- [ ] **Modulation Index:** $\mu = A_m / A_c$ — keep it $\le 1$ for distortion-free standard AM.
- [ ] **Carrier Power Formula:** $P_c = A_c^2 / (2R)$ — remember to use PEAK amplitude, not peak-to-peak.
- [ ] **Sideband Power Formula:** $P_{SB} = \mu^2 P_c / 2$ — depends on $\mu^2$, so doubling $\mu$ quadruples the sideband power.
- [ ] **Total Power Formula:** $P_t = P_c (1 + \mu^2 / 2)$ — never forget the "1+" in the bracket.
- [ ] **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \mu^2 / (2 + \mu^2)$ — maximum is **33.33%** at $\mu = 1$.
- [ ] **Bandwidth Formula:** $BW = 2 f_m$ (single tone) or $BW = 2 f_{max}$ (broadband).
- [ ] **Redundancy in Sidebands:** LSB and USB are mirror images — only one is needed (motivates SSB).
- [ ] **Power Distribution at 100% Modulation:** Carrier = 2/3, Sidebands = 1/3 of total power.
- [ ] **Over-modulation Penalty:** $\mu > 1$ causes envelope reversal, harmonic distortion, and unintelligible reception.
- [ ] **Trigonometric Identity to Memorize:** $\cos A \cos B = \dfrac{1}{2} [\cos(A - B) + \cos(A + B)]$ — this is the HEART of AM analysis.
- [ ] **Envelop Detector Hint:** A simple diode + RC circuit at the receiver recovers the message — no synchronous demodulation needed for DSB-C.
- [ ] **Common KTU Problem Pattern:** Given $A_c$, $A_m$ (or $\mu$), and $R$, compute $P_c$, $P_{SB}$, $P_t$, and $\eta$ — practice at least 5 such problems.
- [ ] **Frequency-spectrum drawing:** Always label amplitudes, frequencies, and bandwidth. A neat spectrum diagram fetches full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — AM Power and Sideband Modulation**
> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEBMT751 — Communication Techniques, Module 1. All formulas, derivations, and numerical patterns are model-tested for ESE (End Semester Evaluation) and internal assessments.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amplitude Modulators – Low-Level and High-Level AM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amplitude Modulators – Low-Level and High-Level AM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are AM Modulators?

An **Amplitude Modulation (AM) Modulator** is an electronic circuit that performs the frequency translation of a low-frequency baseband (message) signal onto a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier wave, such that the **instantaneous amplitude of the carrier varies in proportion to the message signal**, while the carrier frequency and phase remain unaltered.

In the KTU 2024 scheme classification (PEBMT751 – Module 1), AM modulators are broadly categorized into two distinct architectures based on the **stage at which modulation is performed** and the **DC power level present at the modulator output**:

| Category | Point of Modulation | Power Level |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-Level AM Modulator** | Performed **before** power amplification | Output power typically **below 1 W** |
| **High-Level AM Modulator** | Performed **at** the final RF power amplifier stage | Output power typically **above 1 W** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to (i) derive the AM waveform using the **square-law modulator** and **switching modulator**, (ii) explain the operation of the **collector modulator** (high-level), and (iii) clearly differentiate low-level vs high-level transmitters with block diagrams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **inflating a balloon with air (the carrier)** at a steady rhythm using a pump. Now, someone gently **squeezes the balloon's nozzle** according to a music signal (the message) — the balloon's size (amplitude) expands and contracts in sync with the music, but the rhythm of pumping (frequency) does not change.

- **Low-Level AM:** You first shape the balloon's vibrations at low pressure (low power), and then use a **booster pump** (linear RF power amplifier) to project it loudly. The drawback: the booster must be a *perfect, linear* amplifier, which is expensive and inefficient.
- **High-Level AM:** You inflate the balloon directly with a **powerful industrial pump** (Class-C amplifier), and modulate the **air supply pressure** of the pump itself. This is highly efficient (≈ 80–90%), but the modulating control requires significant audio power.

## 1.3 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The general AM signal is mathematically defined as:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[1 + \mu \cdot m(t)\right] \cos(\omega_c t)$$

where:
- $A_c$ = Carrier amplitude (**volts**)
- $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ = Carrier angular frequency (**rad/s**)
- $m(t)$ = Normalized message signal, with $\vert m(t) \vert \leq 1$
- $\mu$ = **Modulation index** (dimensionless), $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$ to avoid distortion

> [!NOTE]
> The standard AM signal contains **three spectral components**: the carrier at $f_c$, the Lower Sideband (LSB) at $f_c - f_m$, and the Upper Sideband (USB) at $f_c + f_m$. The total bandwidth required is $BW = 2 f_m$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Low-Level AM Modulators — Operational Analysis

In **low-level modulation**, the AM waveform is generated using **non-linear devices** (diodes, transistors) operating at small signal levels. The resulting low-power AM signal is then fed to a chain of **linear RF power amplifiers** (Class A or Class B) before being radiated. The three principal low-level modulator topologies are:

### (a) Square-Law Modulator
- Uses a **non-linear device** (e.g., diode or FET) whose transfer characteristic is approximated by a **second-order polynomial**: $v_o = a v_i + b v_i^2$.
- The **square term** mathematically generates **sum and difference frequencies** $(f_c \pm f_m)$ through the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$.
- A **band-pass filter (BPF)** centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2 f_m$ extracts the desired AM signal and rejects the harmonics at $2f_c$, $2f_m$, and DC.

### (b) Switching Modulator
- Uses a **diode or transistor switch** driven by a large-amplitude square wave at the carrier frequency $f_c$.
- Mathematically equivalent to multiplying the input sum signal by a square wave, which is expanded in a **Fourier series**:

$$s_{sq}(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5...}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n} \cos(n \omega_c t)$$

- A BPF again selects the $f_c \pm f_m$ components.
- **Advantage:** More efficient than square-law because the switching action provides higher gain.

### (c) Balanced Modulator (DSB-SC Generation)
- A **symmetric** arrangement (lattice modulator or Gilbert cell) that **suppresses the carrier component**, yielding only the two sidebands:

$$v_{DSB-SC}(t) = 2 b A_m A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \cos(\omega_m t)$$

- Adding the carrier back (using a summer) recovers a standard AM signal.
- **Application:** Forms the basis of **single-sideband (SSB) transmitters** when followed by a sideband filter.

## 2.2 High-Level AM Modulators — Operational Analysis

In **high-level modulation**, the AM waveform is generated at the **final RF power amplifier stage** by varying the DC supply voltage of a **Class-C amplifier** in proportion to the message signal.

### Collector Modulator (Plate Modulator equivalent)
- The RF carrier is amplified by a **Class-C amplifier** (highly efficient, ≈ 80–90%, but inherently non-linear — only usable as an *amplifier*, not a modulator).
- The modulating audio signal is applied via an **audio transformer** in series with the collector DC supply $V_{CC}$.
- The instantaneous collector supply becomes: $V_{CC}(t) = V_{CC} + A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$.
- This directly modulates the **amplitude of the output RF pulses**, generating AM at high power.

> [!NOTE]
> Why not modulate at low level? Because Class-C amplifiers are **non-linear** and cannot faithfully amplify a pre-modulated AM envelope without distortion. Thus, modulation *must* occur **at** the final Class-C stage for efficiency.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu m(t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$ | Standard AM waveform |
| 2 | $\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$ (collector modulator) | Modulation index for high-level modulator |
| 3 | $\mu = \frac{4 A_m}{A_c}$ (square-law modulator) | Modulation index at square-law output |
| 4 | $\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$ (switching modulator) | Modulation index at switching output |
| 5 | Carrier Power: $P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2 R_L}$ | Unmodulated carrier power |
| 6 | Total Power: $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | AM total power with modulation |
| 7 | Sideband Power: $P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2}{2} P_c$ | Power in both sidebands combined |
| 8 | Bandwidth: $BW = 2 f_m$ | Required transmission bandwidth |
| 9 | $\eta_{Class-C} \approx 80\%$ to $90\%$ | DC-to-RF efficiency of high-level stage |
| 10 | $\eta_{Class-A} \leq 50\%$ | Maximum efficiency of linear low-level amplifier |
| 11 | $V_{CC}(t) = V_{CC} + A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ | Instantaneous collector supply in collector modulator |
| 12 | $v_o = a v_i + b v_i^2$ | Square-law device transfer characteristic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Low-level modulators are preferred in **portable, low-power transceivers** (walkie-talkies, telemetry) where linearity matters more than efficiency. High-level modulators are used in **broadcast AM transmitters** (MW/SW radio stations, 5 kW – 50 kW) where DC-to-RF conversion efficiency directly determines electricity bills and thermal management cost.

## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Low-Level vs High-Level Choice

| Parameter | Low-Level Modulator | High-Level Modulator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Modulation Stage | Before RF power amplifier | At the final RF amplifier |
| Amplifier Used | Class-A or Class-B (linear) | Class-C (non-linear, efficient) |
| Overall Efficiency | **Low** (≤ 50 %) | **High** (≈ 80–90 %) |
| Linearity | **Excellent** | Requires careful design to avoid envelope distortion |
| Output Power | < 1 W (typical) | Watts to kilowatts |
| Cost of Subsequent Stages | **High** (linear amplifier is bulky & expensive) | **Low** (Class-C is compact & cheap) |
| Carrier Suppression | Easy (balanced topology) | Difficult (modulation at high power) |
| Typical Application | SSB/DSB transmitters, instrumentation | AM broadcast transmitters |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Square-Law Modulator (Low-Level)

### Step 1 — Apply Sum Signal to Non-Linear Device
The modulating signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ and carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ are summed and fed to a non-linear device (diode/FET).

$$v_i(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$$

### Step 2 — Non-Linear Transfer Characteristic
The output of the square-law device follows a second-order polynomial:

$$v_o(t) = a \, v_i(t) + b \, v_i(t)^2$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Input
Substituting $v_i(t)$ into the polynomial:

$$v_o(t) = a \left[A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)\right] + b \left[A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)\right]^2$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Squared Term
Let us isolate and expand $\left[A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)\right]^2$:

$$\left[A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)\right]^2 = A_m^2 \cos^2(\omega_m t) + A_c^2 \cos^2(\omega_c t) + 2 A_m A_c \cos(\omega_m t) \cos(\omega_c t)$$

### Step 5 — Apply Trigonometric Identities
We apply the standard identities:
- $\cos^2 x = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \cos(2x)\right]$
- $2 \cos A \cos B = \cos(A - B) + \cos(A + B)$

After expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)\right]^2 &= \frac{A_m^2}{2}\left[1 + \cos(2 \omega_m t)\right] \\
&\quad + \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left[1 + \cos(2 \omega_c t)\right] \\
&\quad + A_m A_c \left[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Complete Output Expression
Multiplying by $b$ and adding the linear term $a \cdot v_i(t)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_o(t) &= \underbrace{\frac{b A_m^2}{2} + \frac{b A_c^2}{2}}_{\text{DC term}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{a A_m \cos(\omega_m t)}_{\text{Message freq}} + \underbrace{\frac{b A_m^2}{2}\cos(2\omega_m t)}_{\text{2nd harmonic of message}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{a A_c \cos(\omega_c t)}_{\text{Carrier}} + \underbrace{\frac{b A_c^2}{2}\cos(2\omega_c t)}_{\text{2nd harmonic of carrier}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{b A_m A_c \cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t)}_{\text{LSB}} + \underbrace{b A_m A_c \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)}_{\text{USB}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Apply the Band-Pass Filter (BPF)
A BPF centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2 f_m$ removes all components **except** the carrier and the two sidebands:

$$v_{AM}(t) = \underbrace{\frac{b A_c^2}{2}\cos(\omega_c t)}_{\text{Carrier}} + \underbrace{b A_m A_c \cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t)}_{\text{LSB}} + \underbrace{b A_m A_c \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)}_{\text{USB}}$$

### Step 8 — Convert to Standard AM Form Using Identity
Apply the identity $\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B) = 2 \cos A \cos B$ to combine the two sidebands:

$$b A_m A_c \left[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)\right] = 2 b A_m A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \cos(\omega_m t)$$

Therefore:

$$v_{AM}(t) = \frac{b A_c^2}{2}\cos(\omega_c t) + 2 b A_m A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \cos(\omega_m t)$$

Factor out $\frac{b A_c^2}{2}\cos(\omega_c t)$:

$$\boxed{\,v_{AM}(t) = \frac{b A_c^2}{2} \cos(\omega_c t) \left[1 + \frac{4 A_m}{A_c} \cos(\omega_m t)\right]\,}$$

### Step 9 — Identify the Modulation Index
By direct comparison with the standard AM expression $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$:

$$\boxed{\,\mu = \frac{4 A_m}{A_c}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Unlike the high-level collector modulator where $\mu = A_m / V_{CC}$, the square-law modulator produces an output modulation index that is **four times** the input amplitude ratio. The factor of 4 arises from the squaring action — the squaring introduces a 2× from the identity and another 2× from combining the two sidebands.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Switching Modulator (Low-Level)

### Step 1 — Multiplication by a Carrier-Frequency Square Wave
The sum signal is multiplied by an ideal square wave at carrier frequency:

$$s_{sq}(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \left[\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(3\omega_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(5\omega_c t) - \cdots\right]$$

### Step 2 — Compute Output

$$\begin{aligned}
v_o(t) &= \left[A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)\right] \cdot s_{sq}(t) \\
&= \frac{4 A_m}{\pi}\cos(\omega_m t)\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{4 A_m}{3\pi}\cos(\omega_m t)\cos(3\omega_c t) + \cdots \\
&\quad + \frac{4 A_c}{\pi}\cos^2(\omega_c t) - \frac{4 A_c}{3\pi}\cos(\omega_c t)\cos(3\omega_c t) + \cdots
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Apply BPF
The BPF retains only the $f_c \pm f_m$ components. Using $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$ on the first term and $\cos^2(\omega_c t) = \frac{1}{2}[1 + \cos(2\omega_c t)]$ on the second term, only $\frac{4 A_c}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{2 A_c}{\pi}$ survives from the second term at $f_c$ after harmonic filtering. Combining:

$$v_{AM}(t) = \frac{2 A_c}{\pi}\cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{4 A_m}{\pi}\cos(\omega_c t)\cos(\omega_m t)$$

### Step 4 — Final Form

$$\boxed{\,v_{AM}(t) = \frac{2 A_c}{\pi}\cos(\omega_c t)\left[1 + \frac{2 A_m}{A_c}\cos(\omega_m t)\right]\,}$$

And the modulation index becomes:

$$\boxed{\,\mu = \frac{2 A_m}{A_c}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 2 (instead of 4 for square-law) occurs because the switching action involves *multiplication* by a square wave, not *squaring* of a sum. The result is still AM but with **higher conversion gain**, making the switching modulator more efficient than the square-law modulator.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Collector Modulator Power Efficiency (High-Level)

### Step 1 — DC Power Delivered by the Modulated Supply
The collector supply voltage is $V_{CC}(t) = V_{CC} + A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$. For Class-C operation, the average collector current is a pulsed waveform whose fundamental component amplitude is proportional to $V_{CC}(t)$. The DC collector current is:

$$I_{CC}(t) = I_0 \left[1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)\right]$$

The average DC power delivered is:

$$P_{DC} = \overline{V_{CC}(t) \cdot I_{CC}(t)} = V_{CC} I_0 \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — RF Output Power
The RF output voltage amplitude is $V_c(t) = V_c[1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)]$ across load $R_L$. The total RF output power is:

$$P_{RF} = \frac{V_c^2}{2 R_L}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Collector Efficiency

$$\eta_C = \frac{P_{RF}}{P_{DC}} \approx \frac{V_c^2 / 2 R_L}{V_{CC} I_0}$$

For a Class-C amplifier, this approaches **80–90 %** regardless of modulation, because the collector current pulse conduction angle is kept small.

### Step 4 — Audio (Modulation) Power Required
The audio amplifier must supply the **additional power** that the modulating signal injects into the collector supply:

$$\boxed{\,P_{audio} = \frac{\mu^2}{2} P_c\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Design Note:** The audio modulator (usually a Class-B push-pull stage) must be sized to deliver at least $\mu^2 P_c / 2$ watts. For 100 % modulation ($\mu = 1$) and a 10 kW carrier, the audio stage must deliver **5 kW** of clean audio power — this is why high-level broadcast transmitters require massive audio transformers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Square-Law | Switching | Balanced (DSB-SC) | Collector |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Power Level | Low | Low | Low | **High** |
| Carrier Suppressed? | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| Modulation Index Formula | $\mu = 4 A_m / A_c$ | $\mu = 2 A_m / A_c$ | N/A (DSB-SC) | $\mu = A_m / V_{CC}$ |
| Conversion Gain | Low | Medium | Medium | **High** |
| Efficiency | Low | Low | Low | **High (≈ 85 %)** |
| Linearity | Poor (small-signal) | Fair | Good | Good (Class-C trick) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Low-Level AM Transmitter

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the signal flow in a **low-level AM transmitter**. The AM signal is generated first and then amplified by linear power stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Message Signal m(t)"] --> nodeSum[("Adder Σ")]
    nodeB["Carrier Oscillator f_c"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeMod["Low-Level AM Modulator (Square-Law / Switching / Balanced)"]
    nodeMod --> nodeBPF["Band-Pass Filter (BPF) at f_c"]
    nodeBPF --> nodeIPA["Intermediate Power Amplifier (Class-A)"]
    nodeIPA --> nodePA["Final Linear RF Power Amplifier (Class-B Push-Pull)"]
    nodePA --> nodeAnt["Antenna (Radiated Output)"]
    nodeMod -. Modulation .-> nodeMod
    style nodeA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style nodeB fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style nodeMod fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style nodePA fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **sum** of message and carrier enters the non-linear/low-level modulator.
- After BPF filtering, a **low-power AM** signal is obtained.
- The chain of **linear amplifiers** (Class-A → Class-B) boosts the AM signal to the desired transmit power.
- **Limitation:** All amplifiers must be strictly linear; efficiency is low.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — High-Level AM Transmitter

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a **high-level AM transmitter** where modulation occurs at the final Class-C amplifier via collector modulation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM["Message Signal m(t) Audio"] --> nodeAud["Audio Amplifier (Class-B Push-Pull)"]
    nodeAud --> nodeXform["Modulation Transformer"]
    nodeXform --> nodeVCC["Variable DC Supply V_CC + v_m t"]
    nodeC["Crystal Oscillator f_c"] --> nodeBuff["Buffer Amplifier"]
    nodeBuff --> nodeDriver["Class-C RF Driver"]
    nodeDriver --> nodeClassC["Final Class-C RF Power Amplifier"]
    nodeVCC --> nodeClassC
    nodeClassC --> nodeTank["Tank Circuit / Output Network"]
    nodeTank --> nodeAnt["Antenna"]
    style nodeM fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style nodeC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    nodeClassC fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    nodeAnt fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style nodeVCC fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2
```

**Interpretation:**
- The carrier is generated and amplified through a chain of Class-C stages.
- The modulating signal modulates the **collector supply voltage** of the **final** Class-C stage.
- Class-C operation is **non-linear**, but it only needs to amplify an *unmodulated* carrier — the envelope shape comes from $V_{CC}(t)$.
- High DC-to-RF efficiency (≈ 85 %) is achieved.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Square-Law Modulator Internal Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn["Input: v_i = A_m cos ω_m t + A_c cos ω_c t"] --> nodeNL["Non-Linear Device: v_o = a v_i + b v_i²"]
    nodeNL --> nodeGen["Generated Components: DC, ω_m, 2ω_m, ω_c, 2ω_c, ω_c ± ω_m"]
    nodeGen --> nodeBPF["BPF: Center f_c, Bandwidth 2 f_m"]
    nodeBPF --> nodeOut["Output: AM Signal s t = A_c 1 + μ cos ω_m t cos ω_c t"]
    style nodeIn fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style nodeNL fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style nodeBPF fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style nodeOut fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Collector Modulator Envelope Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeVcc["V_CC Constant DC Supply"] --> nodeSum[("Collector Node")]
    nodeVm["v_m t = A_m cos ω_m t"] --> nodeXform["Modulation Transformer"]
    nodeXform --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeVtotal["V_CC t = V_CC + A_m cos ω_m t"]
    nodeVtotal --> nodeClassC["Class-C RF Amplifier"]
    nodeClassC --> nodePulse["Output RF Pulses: Amplitude proportional to V_CC t"]
    nodePulse --> nodeTank["Resonant Tank Circuit"]
    nodeTank --> nodeEnvelope["Recovered AM Envelope: A_c 1 + μ cos ω_m t cos ω_c t"]
    style nodeVcc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style nodeVm fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style nodeClassC fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style nodeEnvelope fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Compare low-level and high-level AM modulation systems on the basis of (i) point of modulation, (ii) amplifier class used, (iii) overall efficiency, and (iv) typical application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Low-Level Modulator | High-Level Modulator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Point of modulation | Before RF power amplifier | At the final RF power amplifier |
| (ii) Amplifier class | Class-A or Class-B (linear) | Class-C (non-linear, efficient) |
| (iii) Overall efficiency | Low (≤ 50 %) | High (≈ 80–90 %) |
| (iv) Typical application | SSB, telemetry, instrumentation | AM broadcast (MW/SW) transmitters |

In **low-level modulation**, the AM signal is generated at low power and amplified linearly. In **high-level modulation**, modulation is performed at the collector of the final Class-C amplifier, giving very high efficiency — preferred for high-power broadcast transmitters. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**Define modulation index. Write the expression for the total power of an AM signal in terms of carrier power and modulation index.**

**Model Answer:**

The **modulation index** $\mu$ is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier. It indicates the extent to which the carrier is modulated:

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

For a distortion-free AM signal, $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$.

**Total Power of an AM signal:**

$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

where $P_c$ is the unmodulated carrier power and $P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2}{2} P_c$ is the combined power in both sidebands. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A [14 Marks]**

#### (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
**Derive the expression for the AM signal generated by a square-law modulator. Assume a single-tone message signal. Also, obtain an expression for the modulation index at the modulator output.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Let the modulating signal be $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ and the carrier be $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$.

**Step 2:** Their sum is applied to the input of the non-linear device:

$$v_i(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$$

**Step 3:** The square-law device has the characteristic:

$$v_o(t) = a \, v_i(t) + b \, v_i(t)^2$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and expanding $v_i^2(t)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_o(t) &= a\left[A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)\right] \\
&\quad + b\left[A_m^2 \cos^2(\omega_m t) + A_c^2 \cos^2(\omega_c t) + 2 A_m A_c \cos(\omega_m t)\cos(\omega_c t)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Applying identities $\cos^2 x = \frac{1}{2}[1 + \cos 2x]$ and $2 \cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_o(t) &= \frac{b A_m^2}{2} + \frac{b A_c^2}{2} \\
&\quad + a A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + \frac{b A_m^2}{2}\cos(2 \omega_m t) \\
&\quad + a A_c \cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{b A_c^2}{2}\cos(2 \omega_c t) \\
&\quad + b A_m A_c \cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + b A_m A_c \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** The BPF centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2 f_m$ retains only the carrier and sideband terms:

$$v_{AM}(t) = \frac{b A_c^2}{2}\cos(\omega_c t) + b A_m A_c \left[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)\right]$$

**Step 7:** Applying $\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B) = 2 \cos A \cos B$:

$$v_{AM}(t) = \frac{b A_c^2}{2}\cos(\omega_c t) + 2 b A_m A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \cos(\omega_m t)$$

**Step 8:** Factoring:

$$\boxed{\,v_{AM}(t) = \frac{b A_c^2}{2}\cos(\omega_c t)\left[1 + \frac{4 A_m}{A_c}\cos(\omega_m t)\right]\,}$$

**Step 9:** Comparing with $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$:

$$\boxed{\,\mu = \frac{4 A_m}{A_c}\,} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

---

#### (b) [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a high-level collector modulator for AM generation. Derive the expression for the modulation index in terms of the modulating signal amplitude and DC supply voltage.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** In a high-level AM transmitter, the AM signal is generated at the collector of the final RF power amplifier. The RF amplifier operates in **Class-C mode** for high efficiency.

**Step 2 — Carrier Path:** The carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ is generated by a crystal oscillator and amplified by a chain of Class-C stages up to the **final RF power amplifier**.

**Step 3 — Modulating Signal Path:** The audio message $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ is amplified by an audio amplifier (Class-B push-pull) and applied to the **secondary of the modulation transformer**, whose primary is in series with the DC collector supply $V_{CC}$.

**Step 4 — Combined Collector Supply:** The instantaneous collector supply voltage is:

$$V_{CC}(t) = V_{CC} + A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$$

**Step 5 — Effect on Output:** The amplitude of the Class-C output pulses is directly proportional to $V_{CC}(t)$. After the resonant tank circuit restores the sinusoidal envelope:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[1 + \frac{A_m}{V_{CC}} \cos(\omega_m t)\right] \cos(\omega_c t)$$

**Step 6 — Modulation Index:**

$$\boxed{\,\mu = \frac{A_m}{V_{CC}}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Advantages:**
- Very high efficiency (≈ 85 %) since Class-C amplifier is used.
- Audio power required = $\mu^2 P_c / 2$.
- Simple, rugged, and economical for high-power AM broadcast transmitters. **(7 marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B [14 Marks] — INTERNAL CHOICE**

#### (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Explain the working of a balanced modulator using diodes. Show that the output contains only the sidebands (DSB-SC). How is a conventional AM signal recovered from it?** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Topology:** A diode balanced modulator consists of **four diodes** arranged in a ring (or lattice) configuration, with two transformers: an **input (carrier) transformer** and an **output transformer**.

**Step 2 — Operation:** The carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ is applied to the center tap of the input transformer's secondary and is large enough to drive the diodes alternately into ON/OFF states — behaving as a switch at the carrier rate. The message signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ is applied between the input transformer center tap and the output transformer center tap.

**Step 3 — Switching Analysis:** During the positive half-cycle of the carrier, diodes D1 and D2 conduct (D3 and D4 OFF); during the negative half-cycle, D3 and D4 conduct (D1 and D2 OFF). The result is equivalent to multiplying $m(t)$ by the square-wave carrier $s_{sq}(t)$.

**Step 4 — Output Expression:** The output contains only odd harmonics of the carrier multiplied by $m(t)$:

$$v_o(t) = \frac{4 A_m}{\pi}\cos(\omega_m t)\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{4 A_m}{3\pi}\cos(\omega_m t)\cos(3\omega_c t) + \cdots$$

**Step 5 — Filtering:** A BPF centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2 f_m$ retains only the fundamental term:

$$\boxed{\,v_{DSB-SC}(t) = \frac{4 A_m}{\pi}\cos(\omega_c t)\cos(\omega_m t) = \frac{2 A_m}{\pi}\left[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)\right]\,}$$

**Step 6 — Carrier Suppression:** The carrier component is **completely cancelled** due to the balanced symmetry — any residual carrier is due to diode mismatches and is typically **< 60 dB below** the sideband level.

**Step 7 — AM Recovery:** To obtain a standard AM signal, the carrier is added back using a summer:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{4 A_m}{\pi}\cos(\omega_c t)\cos(\omega_m t) = A_c\left[1 + \frac{4 A_m}{\pi A_c}\cos(\omega_m t)\right]\cos(\omega_c t)$$

with $\mu = \frac{4 A_m}{\pi A_c}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]
**Draw and explain the block diagrams of (i) low-level AM transmitter and (ii) high-level AM transmitter. Compare their efficiencies.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Low-Level AM Transmitter Block Diagram:**

```
Message m(t) ─┐
              ├─► SUM ─► Low-Level Modulator ─► BPF ─► Class-A IPA ─► Class-B PA ─► Antenna
Carrier c(t) ─┘
```

The AM signal is generated at low power using square-law/switching/balanced modulators. Subsequent amplifiers are **linear** (Class-A or Class-B) to preserve the envelope shape.

**(ii) High-Level AM Transmitter Block Diagram:**

```
Message m(t) ─► Audio Amp ─► Modulation Transformer ──┐
                                                        ├─► V_CC(t) modulates Class-C RF PA ─► Antenna
Crystal Oscillator ─► Class-C Driver ──────────────────┘
```

Modulation occurs at the **collector** of the final Class-C amplifier. The modulating signal varies the DC collector supply.

**Efficiency Comparison:**

| Stage | Low-Level | High-Level |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Maximum theoretical efficiency | 50 % (Class-A) / 78.5 % (Class-B) | **≈ 90 % (Class-C)** |
| Practical overall efficiency | 25–35 % | **70–85 %** |
| Heat dissipation | High | Low |

For a 10 kW AM broadcast transmitter:
- **Low-level design:** Needs a 30 kW DC supply; wastes 20 kW as heat.
- **High-level design:** Needs only a 12 kW DC supply; wastes only 2 kW as heat.

**Conclusion:** High-level modulation is universally preferred for **high-power AM broadcast** because of vastly superior efficiency, even though it requires a heavy audio transformer and modulation inductor. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the BPF** — Students often derive the full $v_o(t)$ but fail to mention that a **band-pass filter centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2 f_m$** removes the DC, $2f_m$, $2f_c$ terms. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Incorrect modulation index formula** — Using $\mu = A_m / A_c$ for the square-law modulator is **wrong**; the correct formula is $\mu = 4 A_m / A_c$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Missing Class-C efficiency justification** — Just stating "Class-C is efficient" without quantifying (80–90 %) loses marks. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing DSB-SC with AM** — A balanced modulator gives **DSB-SC** (no carrier), not AM. Adding the carrier back is an *extra* step. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Forgetting the role of the modulation transformer** — In collector modulation, the audio transformer is what couples the modulating signal into the collector DC rail. Drawing a direct audio feed without the transformer is structurally incorrect. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Missing units** in formula tables. **Loss: 0.5 mark per instance.**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AM Modulators** are classified by **power level**: **Low-Level** (modulation before amplification) vs **High-Level** (modulation at the final amplifier).
- **Low-Level Modulators:** **Square-law**, **Switching**, and **Balanced** modulators — all rely on **non-linear devices** and **band-pass filtering** at $f_c$.
- **Square-law modulator** uses $v_o = a v_i + b v_i^2$; output modulation index $\mu = 4 A_m / A_c$.
- **Switching modulator** multiplies input sum by a square wave at $f_c$; output $\mu = 2 A_m / A_c$.
- **Balanced modulator** suppresses the carrier; output is **DSB-SC**.
- **High-Level Modulator (Collector Modulator):** Class-C RF amplifier with modulating signal in series with $V_{CC}$; output $\mu = A_m / V_{CC}$.
- **Total AM Power:** $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = 2 f_m$ for conventional AM.
- **Efficiency order:** Square-law < Switching < Balanced < **Collector (highest)**.
- **For 100 % modulation ($\mu = 1$):** Sideband power is 50 % of carrier power — meaning **only 33 % of total power** carries information; the carrier (which carries no information) consumes **67 %** of total power — the well-known **inefficiency of conventional AM**.
- **Audio power required for collector modulation at $\mu = 1$** is $\frac{1}{2} P_c$, which dictates the size of the audio transformer.
- **Use Class-A/B** for low-level; **use Class-C** for high-level (modulate after the amplifier, *via* the supply rail).
- **DC component and harmonics** at $2f_m$, $2f_c$ must be removed by the BPF — this is a common exam pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amplitude Demodulators – Diode Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Amplitude Demodulators – Diode Detectors # Amplitude Demodulators – Diode Detectors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Amplitude Demodulator** is a communication receiver sub-system that extracts the original baseband (modulating) signal from a received Amplitude Modulated (AM) wave by recovering its time-varying envelope. The most widely used non-coherent AM demodulator is the **Diode Detector** (also called an **Envelope Detector** or **Peak Detector**), which consists of a non-linear element (diode) followed by a low-pass filter (RC network) that tracks the positive peaks of the incoming AM signal. Mathematically, if the input AM wave is expressed as: $$s(t) = A_c \left[1 + m(t)\right] \cos(\omega_c t)$$ the diode detector delivers an output proportional to the envelope: $$v_o(t) \approx A_c \left[1 + m(t)\right]$$ which, after DC removal (capacitive coupling), reproduces the original message $m(t)$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The diode detector belongs to the class of **non-coherent (envelope) detectors** — it does NOT require a locally generated carrier synchronized in phase or frequency with the transmitter. This is the principal reason it is preferred for commercial AM broadcast receivers operating at carrier frequencies between **540 kHz and 1640 kHz**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing on a beach watching ocean waves roll in. The **height of each wave crest** is constantly changing — small waves, tall waves, moderate waves. The pattern of how these heights rise and fall represents your "message" (the information). The carrier wave is like the constant rhythmic arrival of the waves themselves, and the message is encoded in **how tall each wave gets**. Now, suppose you want to "decode" the height pattern. You could simply place a **float** (a buoyant marker) on the water that rides the crests and slowly settles back down — never quite reaching the troughs. As long as the float descends slowly between waves but quickly jumps to the next crest, the vertical position of the float traces out the **envelope** of the wave heights — which is exactly the message you wanted to recover. - The **diode** acts like a one-way valve — it only allows current to flow on the upward (positive) half-cycles, charging a capacitor to the peak. - The **capacitor + resistor** behave like the float — they store charge on each peak and slowly discharge between peaks. - The result: the capacitor voltage traces the **outline (envelope)** of the AM wave, which is the original modulating signal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constants / Standards (Bold for Emphasis):** > - Standard AM Broadcast Band: **540 kHz – 1640 kHz** (Medium Wave) > - Standard Diode Used: **1N34A / OA90** (Germanium, forward drop ≈ **0.3 V**) > - Typical Detector RC Time Constant: **τ = RC ≈ 0.1 µs – 100 µs** (must satisfy **1/ω_c ≪ RC ≪ 1/ω_m**) > - Minimum Modulation Index for distortion-free detection: **m ≤ 1** (otherwise envelope distortion) ### Why a Diode Detector? — Engineering Motivation | Property | Diode Detector | Other Detectors | |----------|---------------|-----------------| | Carrier sync required? | **No (non-coherent)** | Yes (synchronous detector) | | Circuit complexity | **Very simple** | Complex (PLL, multipliers) | | Cost | **Very low** | High | | Suitable for m(t) positive? | **Yes (with DC bias)** | Yes | | Frequency range | Up to **GHz** (microwave) | Limited | > [!TIP] > **Real-world Use Case:** Every commercial AM radio receiver, from the cheapest pocket radio to high-end communications receivers, uses a diode detector (often a single germanium diode + RC network) as the first stage of demodulation. The circuit you study in this topic is *literally* inside the radio on your table. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology The canonical **diode envelope detector** consists of three functional blocks: 1. **Diode D** — Non-linear device providing half-wave rectification. 2. **Resistor R** — Provides a discharge path for the capacitor. 3. **Capacitor C** — Stores charge at the peak voltage of each carrier cycle. The input AM signal $s(t)$ is applied across the series combination of the diode and the parallel RC network. The output $v_o(t)$ is taken across the capacitor. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step ### Step 1: Input Application The incoming AM signal is applied to the series combination of diode D and parallel RC. $$s(t) = A_c \left[1 + m(t)\right] \cos(\omega_c t)$$ The envelope on the positive side is: $$E_+(t) = A_c \left[1 + m(t)\right]$$ ### Step 2: Diode Conduction (Charging Phase) During the positive half-cycle of the carrier, when $s(t) > v_o(t)$, the diode is **forward-biased and conducts**. The diode acts like a closed switch, and the capacitor charges very rapidly (through the small diode forward resistance $r_d$) to the peak of the input voltage. $$v_o(t) \approx A_c \left[1 + m(t)\right] \quad \text{at each positive peak}$$ ### Step 3: Diode Cut-off (Discharging Phase) Once $s(t)$ falls below the capacitor voltage $v_o(t)$, the diode becomes **reverse-biased and stops conducting** (acts like an open switch). The capacitor then discharges exponentially through the load resistor R. $$v_o(t) = V_{\text{peak}} \cdot e^{-t/(RC)} \quad \text{while diode is off}$$ ### Step 4: Tracking the Envelope The capacitor voltage slowly decays (controlled by RC) until the next positive carrier peak arrives, at which point the diode conducts again and the capacitor is recharged to the new peak. The result is a "staircase-like" output that **approximates the positive envelope** of the AM signal. > [!NOTE] > **Why it must be a "slow" discharge:** If the capacitor discharged too fast, the output would follow the rapid oscillations of the carrier instead of the slow envelope. If it discharged too slowly, the output would miss the falling envelope, causing **diagonal clipping** distortion. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below consolidates **all formulas** the KTU board examiner expects you to reproduce in the 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the conditions on RC — they are tested every year. | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description / Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | AM Input Signal | $s(t) = A_c\left[1+m(t)\right]\cos(\omega_c t)$ | Standard AM-DSB-TC waveform | | 2 | Envelope of Positive Side | $E_+(t) = A_c\left[1+m(t)\right]$ | What the detector must reproduce | | 3 | Detector Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | Controls tracking speed | | 4 | Carrier Period | $T_c = 1/f_c = 2\pi/\omega_c$ | Must be ≪ RC | | 5 | Tracking Condition (Upper Bound) | $RC \le \dfrac{\sqrt{1-m^2_{\max}}}{m_{\max}\,\omega_m}$ | Prevents diagonal clipping | | 6 | Tracking Condition (Lower Bound) | $RC \gg T_c = \dfrac{1}{f_c}$ | Ensures ripple filtering | | 7 | Diagonal Clipping Condition | $RC > \dfrac{\sqrt{1-m^2}}{m\,\omega_m}$ | Diagonal clipping begins | | 8 | Negative Peak Clipping (AC Coupled) | $V_{R,\min} = \dfrac{A_c\,m_{\max}}{1+m_{\max}}$ | Caused by capacitive coupling | | 9 | Voltage Transfer Constant | $v_o = \eta \cdot A_c\left[1+m(t)\right]$ | $\eta \approx 0.9$ for Ge diode | | 10 | Diode Efficiency | $\eta \approx 1 - \dfrac{V_D}{A_c}$ | $V_D$ = diode forward drop | | 11 | Discharge Slope (Volts/sec) | $\dfrac{dv_o}{dt}\big\vert_{\text{discharge}} = -\dfrac{v_o}{RC}$ | Exponential decay rate | | 12 | Maximum Envelope Slope | $\left\vert\dfrac{dE_+}{dt}\right\vert_{\max} = A_c\,m_{\max}\,\omega_m$ | Required to be < discharge slope | | 13 | Ripple Frequency in Output | $f_r = 2f_c$ (full-wave) or $f_c$ (half-wave) | Residual carrier ripple | | 14 | Ripple Voltage (peak-to-peak) | $V_r \approx \dfrac{A_c(1+m)}{f_c RC}$ | Small if RC large | > **Notation Convention Used in KTU 2024 Scheme:** > - $A_c$ — Carrier amplitude (volts) > - $m$ — Modulation index (unitless, $0 \le m \le 1$) > - $m(t)$ — Normalized message (unitless) > - $\omega_c$ — Carrier angular frequency (rad/s) > - $\omega_m$ — Highest message angular frequency (rad/s) > - $V_D$ — Diode forward voltage drop (~0.3 V for Ge, ~0.7 V for Si) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **AM Broadcast Receivers:** Standard envelope detection in MW/SW radios. - **RF Power Measurement:** Used in RF voltmeters as a peak detector. - **Direction-of-Arrival Systems:** Envelope detection of pulsed signals. - **Radar Receivers:** Envelope of the IF amplifier output is detected. - **Instrumentation:** Precision peak-detect-and-hold circuits for laboratory equipment. - **Low-Cost Wireless Links:** ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying) demodulation for short-range digital comms. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For an AM broadcast receiver with $f_c = 1 \text{ MHz}$ and audio bandwidth $f_m = 5 \text{ kHz}$, choosing $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \text{ μF}$ gives $\tau = 100 \text{ μs}$. Check: $T_c = 1 \text{ μs} \ll 100 \text{ μs} \ll 1/(2\pi f_m) = 32 \text{ μs}$. Note the violation — this is the classic engineering trade-off and is precisely why a **2-stage RC filter** is used in practice (small RC right after the diode, larger RC at the next stage). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Diagonal Clipping Condition This is the **most important derivation** in this module and is a guaranteed question in KTU ESE. ### Given - AM signal: $s(t) = A_c\left[1 + m(t)\right]\cos(\omega_c t)$ - Modulation: $m(t) = m_{\max}\cos(\omega_m t)$ - Envelope detector with time constant $\tau = RC$ ### Goal Determine the condition on $RC$ so that the capacitor voltage **always tracks the envelope** without diagonal clipping. ### Step 1: Express the Envelope The instantaneous envelope (positive side) is: $$E_+(t) = A_c\left[1 + m_{\max}\cos(\omega_m t)\right]$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Maximum Negative Slope of the Envelope The maximum rate at which the envelope falls is when $\cos(\omega_m t) = -1$ and $\sin(\omega_m t) = 0$, evaluated at the point of steepest descent. Differentiating: $$\frac{dE_+}{dt} = -A_c\, m_{\max}\,\omega_m \sin(\omega_m t)$$ The maximum negative value of this slope is: $$\left(\frac{dE_+}{dt}\right)_{\min} = -A_c\, m_{\max}\,\omega_m$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Maximum Discharge Slope of the Capacitor When the diode is off, the capacitor discharges through R. The exponential discharge is governed by: $$v_c(t) = V_{\text{peak}} \cdot e^{-(t-t_0)/RC}$$ The discharge slope at the moment the diode turns off ($t = t_0$) is: $$\left(\frac{dv_c}{dt}\right)_{t_0} = -\frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{RC}$$ Substituting $V_{\text{peak}} = A_c\left[1 + m_{\max}\right]$ (worst case at peak envelope): $$\left(\frac{dv_c}{dt}\right)_{\text{discharge}} = -\frac{A_c\left[1+m_{\max}\right]}{RC}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the Tracking Condition For the capacitor voltage to remain **just below or equal to** the envelope at all times, the discharge slope must be **no steeper** than the envelope's maximum negative slope. This guarantees that the capacitor voltage is always "ready" to be topped up by the next carrier peak: $$\left\vert \left(\frac{dv_c}{dt}\right)_{\text{discharge}} \right\vert \ge \left\vert \left(\frac{dE_+}{dt}\right)_{\min} \right\vert$$ Substituting: $$\frac{A_c\left[1+m_{\max}\right]}{RC} \ge A_c\, m_{\max}\,\omega_m$$ ### Step 5: Solve for RC Rearranging: $$RC \le \frac{1+m_{\max}}{m_{\max}\,\omega_m}$$ ### Step 6: Refine for Practical Use Note that the worst-case envelope is actually at the **midpoint** between peak and trough, not at the peak itself. A more precise (and KTU-preferred) expression is obtained by evaluating the envelope at the steepest portion of the cosine. The result is: $$RC \le \frac{\sqrt{1-m_{\max}^2}}{m_{\max}\,\omega_m}$$ This is the **classical KTU formula** for the maximum allowable time constant to prevent diagonal clipping. Note that as $m_{\max} \to 1$, this becomes $RC \le 0$, meaning **distortion is inevitable at 100% modulation** with this simple detector. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the √(1−m²) term?** It arises from evaluating the envelope slope not at the very peak (where slope = 0) but at the point of maximum slope. Setting $\frac{d^2E_+}{dt^2} = 0$ identifies the inflection point, which yields the cosine of the modulation angle equal to $m_{\max}$, and then $\sqrt{1-m_{\max}^2}$ emerges naturally. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Negative Peak Clipping Voltage When the envelope detector is followed by an **AC-coupled** stage (capacitive coupling to the next amplifier), the DC component is blocked. This causes the operating point to shift. ### Setup - Output of detector: $v_o(t) = A_c\left[1+m(t)\right]$ - DC component: $A_c$ - AC component (after blocking): $v_o'(t) = A_c\,m(t)$ If the message $m(t)$ has a maximum value of $m_{\max}$, the minimum value of the AC output is $-A_c\,m_{\max}$. The DC bias available across the coupling capacitor is $A_c$. For the diode (which can only conduct in the forward direction) to remain forward-biased, the AC swing on either side of the bias cannot exceed the bias itself. The **maximum negative swing** is limited by: $$V_{R,\min} = A_c - A_c\,m_{\max} = A_c\left[1 - m_{\max}\right]$$ But the *peak negative signal current* into the next stage is $A_c\,m_{\max}$. For no clipping, we need: $$A_c\left[1 - m_{\max}\right] \ge A_c\,m_{\max}$$ Solving: $$1 - m_{\max} \ge m_{\max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{\max} \le 0.5$$ This means **without any DC restoration circuit, the modulation index must be ≤ 0.5** to avoid negative peak clipping when AC coupled. In the general case, the threshold at which clipping begins is given by: $$V_{R,\min} = \frac{A_c\,m_{\max}}{1+m_{\max}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This formula is obtained by equating the charge supplied by the diode during each carrier cycle to the charge drained by the resistor in the next stage. The exact derivation involves equating average diode current to average resistor current and is covered in the textbook analysis. ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style) **Problem:** An AM broadcast receiver uses a diode envelope detector with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \text{ μF}$. The carrier frequency is $f_c = 1 \text{ MHz}$ and the highest modulating frequency is $f_m = 5 \text{ kHz}$. The modulation index is $m = 0.7$. Determine: 1. Whether diagonal clipping occurs. 2. The discharge slope at peak envelope. 3. The maximum allowable RC to avoid diagonal clipping. ### Solution **Step 1: Calculate the actual time constant** $$\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3)(0.01 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 100 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 2: Compare with carrier period** $$T_c = \frac{1}{f_c} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 1 \text{ μs}$$ Since $T_c = 1 \text{ μs} \ll \tau = 100 \text{ μs}$, the capacitor does not discharge significantly between carrier cycles. ✓ (Ripple filtering satisfied) **Step 3: Compute maximum allowable RC** $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sqrt{1-m^2}}{m \cdot \omega_m} = \frac{\sqrt{1-0.49}}{0.7 \times 2\pi \times 5000}$$ $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sqrt{0.51}}{7000\pi} = \frac{0.7141}{21991.1} = 32.47 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 4: Compare** Actual $\tau = 100 \text{ μs} > \tau_{\max} = 32.47 \text{ μs}$ > [!WARNING] > **Diagonal clipping WILL occur.** The capacitor cannot discharge fast enough to follow the rapidly falling envelope. To prevent distortion, the designer must reduce either R or C. For example, choosing $C = 3000 \text{ pF}$ gives $\tau = 30 \text{ μs} < 32.47 \text{ μs}$ — clipping eliminated. **Step 5: Discharge slope at peak envelope** $$\left(\frac{dv_c}{dt}\right)_{\text{discharge}} = -\frac{A_c(1+m)}{RC} = -\frac{A_c \times 1.7}{100\,\mu s} = -17{,}000\,A_c \text{ V/s}$$ **Step 6: Maximum envelope negative slope** $$\left(\frac{dE_+}{dt}\right)_{\min} = -A_c\,m\,\omega_m = -A_c \times 0.7 \times 2\pi \times 5000 = -21{,}991\,A_c \text{ V/s}$$ Since $|17{,}000| < |21{,}991|$, the capacitor falls **slower** than the envelope → **diagonal clipping confirmed**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Schematic — Diode Envelope Detector ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT [AM Signal Input] AM["s(t) = Ac[1+m(t)]cos(wct)"] end subgraph DETECTOR [Envelope Detector Circuit] D["Diode D
Forward-biased
on positive peaks"] RC["Parallel RC Network
R to GND, C to GND
tau = RC"] D -- "conduction path" --> RC end subgraph OUTPUT [Demodulated Output] VOUT["vo(t) approx Ac[1+m(t)]
envelope of AM wave"] DCBLOCK["AC Coupling Cap
(optional stage)"] VOUT --> DCBLOCK MSG["m(t)
recovered message"] DCBLOCK --> MSG end AM --> D RC --> VOUT ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Flow of Detection ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Stage 1: AM Wave
Received RF Signal
Ac1+mtcoswct"] B["Stage 2: Diode
Half-Wave Rectifier
Non-linear element"] C["Stage 3: RC Low-Pass Filter
Time Constant tau = RC
1/wc less than tau less than 1/wm"] D["Stage 4: AC Coupling
DC Blocking Capacitor
Removes DC bias Ac"] E["Stage 5: Recovered
Message Signal m(t)"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E ``` ## 4.3 Operational Waveforms — Charging and Discharging Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CYCLE [One Carrier Cycle Operation] P1["Positive Half-Cycle
Diode ON
Capacitor CHARGES
to peak voltage"] P2["Negative Half-Cycle
Diode OFF
Capacitor DISCHARGES
