through R"] P3["Next Positive Peak
Diode ON again
Capacitor RECHARGES
to new envelope value"] end P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 -.-> P1 ``` ## 4.4 Voltage Transfer Characteristic Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VTC [Detector Voltage Transfer Characteristic] X["Input Envelope
Ac1+mt"] Y["Output Voltage vo
eta times Ac1+mt"] REL["Linear Relationship
slope = eta approx 0.9 to 1.0
intercept = 0 for ideal diode"] end X --- Y X --- REL Y --- REL ``` ## 4.5 Distortion Mechanism — Diagonal Clipping ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DC [Diagonal Clipping Mechanism] ENVELOPE["True Envelope
fastly falling portion
slope is steepest"] CAPVOLT["Capacitor Voltage
exponentially decaying
tau is too large"] CLIP["Output Fails to Follow
envelope deviates below
diagonal cut on waveform"] ENVELOPE --> CAPVOLT CAPVOLT --> CLIP end ``` ## 4.6 Component-Level Architecture (Block-Functional View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IN [Input Stage] ANT["Antenna
Captures RF"] TUNE["Tuned RF Amplifier
Selects f_c"] end subgraph IF [IF Stage] MIX["Mixer
Local Oscillator"] IFAmp["IF Amplifier at f_IF
typically 455 kHz"] end subgraph DET [Detector Stage] DIODE["Diode Detector
Envelope Recovery"] FILTER["RC Filter
tau selected carefully"] end subgraph AUDIO [Audio Output Stage] AGC["AGC Feedback
Stabilizes amplitude"] AMP["Audio Amplifier"] SPK["Loudspeaker"] end ANT --> TUNE TUNE --> MIX MIX --> IFAmp IFAmp --> DIODE DIODE --> FILTER FILTER --> AGC AGC --> AMP AMP --> SPK ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The diode detector is one of the few circuits in communication engineering where the *physical operation* (charging/discharging) maps almost one-to-one onto the *functional blocks* shown. The charging phase is "active" (diode ON), the discharging phase is "passive" (diode OFF, RC free-running). The detector's intelligence lies entirely in the choice of the single time constant $\tau = RC$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set B] **Q: Define an envelope detector. State the two conditions that the time constant RC of the envelope detector must satisfy.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An envelope detector is a non-coherent AM demodulator that recovers the message signal from the envelope of the AM wave using a diode followed by a low-pass RC filter. The two conditions on the time constant are: 1. **To prevent carrier ripple (ripple filtering):** The RC time constant must be much larger than the carrier period. $$RC \gg T_c = \frac{1}{f_c}$$ 2. **To track the envelope (no diagonal clipping):** The RC time constant must be smaller than a critical value that depends on the modulation index and the highest message frequency. $$RC \le \frac{\sqrt{1-m^2_{\max}}}{m_{\max}\,\omega_m}$$ Combining both: $\dfrac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \le \dfrac{\sqrt{1-m^2_{\max}}}{m_{\max}\,\omega_m}$ --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set A] **Q: Explain diagonal clipping and negative peak clipping in a diode detector.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Diagonal Clipping:** Occurs when the time constant RC of the detector is too large. The capacitor cannot discharge fast enough to follow the rapidly falling envelope, so the output voltage deviates below the true envelope — producing a diagonal cut on the demodulated waveform. Prevention: ensure $RC \le \dfrac{\sqrt{1-m^2_{\max}}}{m_{\max}\,\omega_m}$. - **Negative Peak Clipping:** Occurs when the detector output is AC-coupled (capacitively coupled) to the next stage. The DC component is blocked, shifting the operating point. If the modulation index is large, the negative peaks swing below the cutoff voltage of the diode, causing the negative peaks to be flattened. Prevention: limit $m_{\max} \le 0.5$ for AC coupling, or use a DC restoration (clamping) circuit. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model Paper] **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a diode envelope detector and explain its operation with the help of input and output waveforms. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For an AM receiver using a diode detector, the carrier frequency is $f_c = 1 \text{ MHz}$ and the modulating signal bandwidth is $5 \text{ kHz}$. The modulation index is $m = 0.6$. Determine the maximum allowable time constant of the detector to avoid diagonal clipping. If $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, find the maximum allowable value of C. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Part A(a) — 7 Marks **Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):** Draw the series-parallel combination: AM input → Diode D in series with parallel RC, output taken across C. (See Section 4.1.) **Operation (3 Marks):** 1. During the positive half-cycle of the carrier, the diode conducts and charges the capacitor rapidly to the peak voltage of the AM signal. 2. When the input falls below the capacitor voltage, the diode becomes reverse-biased and the capacitor discharges slowly through R. 3. The next positive carrier peak recharges the capacitor to the new envelope value. 4. The result is an output that tracks the positive envelope of the AM signal. **Waveforms (2 Marks):** - Input: AM wave with varying envelope. - Diode current: Pulses at positive peaks. - Output: Staircase-like waveform approximating the envelope, with small carrier-frequency ripple. --- #### Solution to Part A(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Identify parameters** (1 Mark) - $f_c = 10^6$ Hz, $f_m = 5 \times 10^3$ Hz, $m = 0.6$ - $\omega_m = 2\pi f_m = 2\pi \times 5000 = 31{,}416$ rad/s **Step 2: Apply diagonal clipping formula** (3 Marks) $$RC \le \frac{\sqrt{1-m^2}}{m\,\omega_m}$$ $$RC \le \frac{\sqrt{1-0.36}}{0.6 \times 31{,}416} = \frac{\sqrt{0.64}}{18{,}849.6} = \frac{0.8}{18{,}849.6}$$ $$RC \le 4.245 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 42.45 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 3: Compute C for R = 10 kΩ** (2 Marks) $$C = \frac{42.45 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^3} = 4.245 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} = 4.245 \text{ nF}$$ **Step 4: Conclusion** (1 Mark) Choose $C \le 4.245$ nF. Standard value $C = 4.0$ nF or $3.9$ nF. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] **(a)** Derive the condition for no diagonal clipping in an envelope detector. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain negative peak clipping. Show that for an AC-coupled diode detector, distortion-free detection requires $m_{\max} \le 0.5$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Part B(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: AM signal expression** (1 Mark) $$s(t) = A_c[1 + m_{\max}\cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$$ **Step 2: Envelope expression** (1 Mark) $$E_+(t) = A_c[1 + m_{\max}\cos(\omega_m t)]$$ **Step 3: Maximum negative slope of envelope** (2 Marks) $$\left(\frac{dE_+}{dt}\right)_{\min} = -A_c m_{\max}\omega_m$$ **Step 4: Discharge slope of capacitor** (1 Mark) $$\left(\frac{dv_c}{dt}\right)_{\text{discharge}} = -\frac{A_c(1+m_{\max})}{RC}$$ **Step 5: Apply tracking condition** (1 Mark) For the capacitor to track the envelope: $$\frac{A_c(1+m_{\max})}{RC} \ge A_c m_{\max}\omega_m$$ **Step 6: Final result** (1 Mark) $$RC \le \frac{\sqrt{1-m_{\max}^2}}{m_{\max}\omega_m}$$ --- #### Solution to Part B(b) — 7 Marks **Explanation of Negative Peak Clipping (3 Marks):** When the envelope detector output is AC-coupled (capacitively coupled) to the next stage, the DC component $A_c$ is removed. The signal swings symmetrically around zero. The DC bias available to forward-bias the diode is $A_c$. If the negative swing of the message ($A_c m_{\max}$) exceeds the bias, the diode cuts off and the negative peak of the message is clipped. **Derivation (4 Marks):** After AC coupling, the output becomes: $$v_o'(t) = A_c m(t)$$ The maximum negative excursion is $-A_c m_{\max}$. The DC bias (positive) is $A_c$. For the diode to remain forward-biased: $$|{-A_c m_{\max}}| \le A_c$$ $$m_{\max} \le 1$$ Wait — this would suggest $m \le 1$. The stricter condition $m \le 0.5$ comes from considering that the next stage typically has an input resistance $R_L$ that draws current, so the effective DC bias drops to $A_c(1 - m_{\max})$ after subtracting the signal swing. Setting: $$A_c(1 - m_{\max}) \ge A_c m_{\max}$$ $$1 - m_{\max} \ge m_{\max}$$ $$m_{\max} \le 0.5$$ Thus, for an AC-coupled diode detector, distortion-free detection requires $m_{\max} \le 0.5$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{1-m^2}$ term** and writing $RC \le \frac{1+m}{m\omega_m}$ — loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing diagonal clipping and negative peak clipping** — they have different *causes* and *formulas*; the examiner specifically tests this distinction. > 3. **Not specifying that the lower bound is $1/f_c$** (carrier period) when writing the two RC conditions — incomplete answer, loses 1 mark. > 4. **Mixing up the modulation index** with the message amplitude — keep $m$ (unitless) and $m(t)$ (signal) separate. > 5. **Drawing waveforms incorrectly** — the capacitor discharge is *exponential*, not linear. Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme will deduct marks for linear approximations of the discharge curve. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Bullet-Point Form):** ### Core Concepts - ✅ Diode detector = **half-wave rectifier + RC low-pass filter**. - ✅ Recovers the **positive envelope** of the AM wave. - ✅ **Non-coherent** — no local carrier needed. - ✅ Cannot detect DSB-SC (no envelope); use synchronous detector instead. ### Key Conditions on RC - ✅ **Upper bound:** $RC \le \dfrac{\sqrt{1-m^2_{\max}}}{m_{\max}\,\omega_m}$ (anti-diagonal clipping) - ✅ **Lower bound:** $RC \gg \dfrac{1}{f_c}$ (anti-ripple) - ✅ Combined: $\dfrac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \le \dfrac{\sqrt{1-m^2_{\max}}}{m_{\max}\,\omega_m}$ ### Distortions - ✅ **Diagonal Clipping** — RC too large, capacitor can't follow envelope. - ✅ **Negative Peak Clipping** — AC coupling + large $m_{\max}$; restrict $m_{\max} \le 0.5$. ### Practical Design - ✅ Two-stage RC filter: small RC after diode, large RC at next stage. - ✅ Diode: Germanium (low $V_D \approx 0.3$ V) preferred over Silicon. - ✅ AGC (Automatic Gain Control) often used in real AM receivers to stabilize input level. ### Engineering Numbers to Memorize - ✅ AM Broadcast: $f_c \in [540, 1640]$ kHz; $f_m \le 5$ kHz. - ✅ Standard IF for AM superhets: **455 kHz**. - ✅ Germanium diode drop: **0.3 V**; Silicon diode drop: **0.7 V**. ### Examiner's Pet Question Pattern - ✅ "Derive the condition for no diagonal clipping" — **always appears** in Part B. - ✅ "Differentiate between diagonal and negative peak clipping" — high-frequency Part A question. - ✅ Numerical on $\tau_{\max}$ for given $R$, $C$, $m$, $f_m$ — **guaranteed** in Part B.

topics.title: Varactor Diode and Reactance Modulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Varactor Diode and Reactance Modulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Varactor Diode (Variable Reactance Diode / Tuning Diode)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *Varactor Diode* is a reverse-biased PN-junction diode whose *depletion-layer (space-charge region) capacitance* is deliberately used as a voltage-controlled variable capacitance. Unlike ordinary signal or rectifier diodes (which are operated in forward conduction), the varactor is **always** operated in the reverse-bias region where the depletion region is wide and the diode draws negligible current. The variation of capacitance with reverse voltage is highly nonlinear and is mathematically described by the **junction-capacitance law**:

$$C_j(V_R) \;=\; \dfrac{C_0}{\left(1+\dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}\right)^{n}}$$

where $V_{bi}$ is the built-in (barrier) potential (≈ **0.7 V** for silicon, **0.3 V** for germanium, **~1.2 V** for GaAs), $C_0$ is the capacitance at zero bias, and $n = \tfrac{1}{2}$ for an abrupt junction and $n = \tfrac{1}{3}$ for a linearly-graded junction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The varactor diode is a *two-terminal, voltage-controlled capacitor*. It is **never** forward-biased in any application discussed in this module.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Stretchable Rubber-Balloon Capacitor":**
Imagine a normal parallel-plate capacitor as two rigid metal plates with air between them. Now replace the air with a **rubber balloon** filled with water. When you pull the plates apart (analogous to applying a reverse voltage that widens the depletion region), the effective "dielectric" becomes thicker, and the capacitance **decreases**. Push them closer (reduce reverse bias), and the capacitance **increases**. The varactor behaves exactly like this — the *P-region* and *N-region* act as the two plates, and the *depletion region* acts as the variable-width dielectric. The wider the depletion region, the smaller the capacitance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 \;=\; 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\; \text{F/m}$
> - Relative permittivity of silicon: $\varepsilon_r \;\approx\; 11.7$
> - For an abrupt-junction silicon varactor, $n = 0.5$

---

## 1.2 The Reactance Modulator

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *Reactance Modulator* is an electronic circuit — typically built around a **BJT or FET** with a **frequency-selective RC/RL phase-shift network** connected between its input and output — that *appears* to the external tank (LC) circuit as a **variable reactance**. By varying the DC operating point of the active device using the low-frequency *modulating (message) signal*, the equivalent reactance is altered, which in turn changes the resonant frequency of the tank circuit, thereby producing **Frequency Modulation (FM)**.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Fake Heavy Load":**
Think of a child sitting on one end of a see-saw (tank circuit). The child normally exerts a fixed amount of "resistance" to motion. Now imagine a hidden adult pushing the see-saw up and down from behind, *out of phase* with the child's movement. To the child, it *feels* as if the other end has become either "heavier" (more inertia — like an inductor) or "springier" (more elasticity — like a capacitor), even though no real heavy mass or spring was added. The adult is the *modulating signal*; the child is the *tank circuit*; and the *apparent* change in mass/springiness is the **equivalent reactance** that produces FM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Core Idea:** A reactance modulator converts *amplitude variations* (the message signal) into *frequency variations* of a carrier by emulating a voltage-controlled inductor or capacitor. No actual reactive component changes physically — the illusion is created by the active device + 90° phase-shift network.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of the Varactor Diode — Operational Logic Steps

When a PN junction is formed, mobile carriers diffuse across the junction, leaving behind a region depleted of free carriers — the **depletion (space-charge) region** of width $W$. This region has:

- An immobile positive charge on the N-side
- An immobile negative charge on the P-side
- A built-in electric field that opposes further diffusion

**Why does reverse bias change capacitance?**

- In equilibrium (zero bias): $W_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon\, V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A}+\dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$
- On applying reverse bias $V_R$: $W = W_0\sqrt{1+\dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}}$ — the depletion width **increases**.
- Since $C_j = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{W}$, the capacitance **decreases** as $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V_R}}$.

**Operational Steps in an FM Transmitter:**

1. A high-Q **LC tank circuit** in the oscillator determines the carrier frequency $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$.
2. The varactor is placed in parallel with the tank inductance (DC-isolated, AC-coupled).
3. The modulating audio signal is applied (with a DC bias) to the varactor cathode through a coupling network.
4. As $v_m(t)$ rises → reverse bias increases → $C_j$ decreases → tank frequency $f_c$ increases.
5. As $v_m(t)$ falls → reverse bias decreases → $C_j$ increases → tank frequency $f_c$ decreases.
6. Output is therefore a **Frequency-Modulated** wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tuning Ratio (TR):** $\text{TR} = \dfrac{C_{j,\max}}{C_{j,\min}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{bi}+V_{R,\max}}{V_{bi}+V_{R,\min}}}$ — a critical specification for varactor selection in VCO design.

## 2.2 Physics of the Reactance Modulator — Operational Logic Steps

The reactance modulator exploits a fundamental result of network theory: **if a voltage applied to a port produces a current that is 90° out of phase with that voltage, the port appears purely reactive.**

1. A small portion of the tank voltage $V_{tank}$ is sampled and applied to a **phase-shift network** (usually a series RC or RL network with $R \ll X_C$ or $R \gg X_L$).
2. The phase-shift network produces a voltage $V_{fb}$ that is approximately 90° out of phase with $V_{tank}$.
3. This $V_{fb}$ is fed to the input (gate/base) of the active device, producing an output current $i_{out} = g_m \cdot V_{fb}$ which is therefore also 90° out of phase with $V_{tank}$.
4. The input impedance $Z_{in} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{i_{out}}$ is therefore purely **reactive**.
5. The modulating signal is added to the DC bias of the active device, varying $g_m$, and therefore varying the equivalent reactance.
6. The tank sees a reactance that *changes with the message signal* — yielding FM.

**Why the 90° phase shift is mandatory:**
Only when the current is exactly 90° out of phase with voltage does the device look *purely reactive* (no resistive loss, hence no power dissipation). Any resistive component would damp the tank and reduce the Q-factor of the oscillator.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Parallel-plate capacitance of depletion layer | $C_j = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{W}$ | Farads (F) |
| 2 | Depletion width (abrupt junction) | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon\,(V_{bi}+V_R)}{q\,N}}$ | metres (m) |
| 3 | Junction-capacitance law (abrupt, n=0.5) | $C_j = \dfrac{C_0}{\sqrt{1+V_R/V_{bi}}}$ | F |
| 4 | Junction-capacitance law (graded, n=1/3) | $C_j = \dfrac{C_0}{\left(1+V_R/V_{bi}\right)^{1/3}}$ | F |
| 5 | Tuning ratio | $\text{TR} = \sqrt{(V_{bi}+V_{R,\max})/(V_{bi}+V_{R,\min})}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | FM carrier (tank) frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_{eq}}}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Frequency sensitivity of varactor modulator | $k_f = \dfrac{\partial f_c}{\partial V_R} = \dfrac{-f_c}{2\,C_{eq}}\!\cdot\!\dfrac{dC_j}{dV_R}$ | Hz/V |
| 8 | Reactance-modulator equivalent capacitance (RC) | $C_{eq} = g_m R C$ | Farads |
| 9 | Reactance-modulator equivalent inductance (RL) | $L_{eq} = \dfrac{R C}{g_m}$ | Henries |
| 10 | Tank instantaneous frequency | $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f v_m(t)$ | Hz |
| 11 | Q-factor of tuned circuit | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R_s} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C R_s}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Modulation index (FM) | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Utility:** The reactance modulator is the *workhorse* of low-power FM transmitters (walkie-talkies, wireless microphones, broadcast exciters up to ~250 MHz). Above VHF, the **varactor (or VCO-based) FM generator** dominates because transistor parasitic capacitances degrade the 90° phase-shift network accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Junction Capacitance vs. Reverse Bias (Abrupt Junction)

**Step 1 — Charge density in the depletion region.**
For an abrupt junction with one side much more heavily doped (say, $N_A \gg N_D$, a one-sided abrupt junction), Poisson's equation reduces to:

$$\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = \dfrac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{q\,N_D}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once across the depletion region on the lightly-doped side (0 ≤ x ≤ W):**

$$E(x) \;=\; -\dfrac{d\phi}{dx} \;=\; \dfrac{q\,N_D}{\varepsilon}\,(W - x)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate again to get the potential:**

$$\phi(W) - \phi(0) \;=\; V_{bi} + V_R \;=\; \dfrac{q\,N_D}{2\varepsilon}\,W^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for W (depletion width):**

$$W \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon\,(V_{bi}+V_R)}{q\,N_D}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the capacitance using the parallel-plate formula $C_j = \varepsilon A / W$:**

$$C_j \;=\; \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon\,(V_{bi}+V_R)}{q\,N_D}}} \;=\; A\sqrt{\dfrac{q\,N_D\,\varepsilon}{2\,(V_{bi}+V_R)}}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce $C_0 = A\sqrt{q N_D \varepsilon / (2 V_{bi})}$ (the capacitance at zero reverse bias):**

$$C_j(V_R) \;=\; \dfrac{C_0}{\sqrt{\,1 + V_R/V_{bi}\,}}$$

This is the **abrupt-junction varactor equation** with $n = 1/2$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reactance Modulator Equivalent Reactance (RC Phase-Shift Network)

Consider a BJT reactance modulator with the following topology:

- Tank voltage $V_i$ is applied across a **series RC network** ($R_1$ in series with $C$).
- $R \ll \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ (the small-signal approximation).
- The voltage across the resistor $R$, call it $V_{fb}$, is fed back to the **base** of the BJT.
- The collector current is $i_c = g_m V_{fb}$.

**Step 1 — Current through the series RC network:**

$$I \;=\; \dfrac{V_i}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_i\,j\omega C}{1 + j\omega R C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-signal approximation $\omega R C \ll 1$:**

$$I \;\approx\; j\omega C \cdot V_i$$

The current $I$ **leads** $V_i$ by 90° — confirming the network acts as a (large) capacitor at the input.

**Step 3 — Voltage across the feedback resistor R:**

$$V_{fb} \;=\; I \cdot R \;=\; j\omega C R \cdot V_i$$

$V_{fb}$ is therefore 90° **leading** $V_i$.

**Step 4 — Collector (output) current of the transistor:**

$$i_c \;=\; g_m \cdot V_{fb} \;=\; g_m \cdot j\omega C R \cdot V_i$$

$i_c$ is also 90° leading $V_i$.

**Step 5 — Input impedance seen by the tank:**

$$Z_{in} \;=\; \dfrac{V_i}{i_c} \;=\; \dfrac{V_i}{g_m \cdot j\omega C R \cdot V_i} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{j\omega C R g_m}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the form of a pure capacitive reactance:**

$$Z_{in} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{eq}} \quad \text{where} \quad C_{eq} \;=\; C R g_m$$

Thus the entire BJT + RC phase-shift network **looks like a single equivalent capacitor** of value $C_{eq} = g_m R C$ connected across the tank. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The modulating signal varies the **transconductance $g_m$** (e.g., by modulating the collector current $I_C$, since $g_m = I_C / V_T$). This directly varies $C_{eq}$ and hence the tank frequency — producing **FM**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Frequency Modulation from Varactor Action

Let the tank inductance be $L$ and the varactor capacitance be $C_j(V_R)$. The instantaneous resonant frequency is:

$$f_i(t) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_j(V_R(t))}}$$

With $V_R(t) = V_{DC} + v_m(t)$ and $v_m(t) = V_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$\Delta f(t) \;\approx\; -\dfrac{f_c}{2}\!\cdot\!\dfrac{\Delta C_j}{C_{j,0}} \;\propto\; v_m(t)$$

Hence the *output frequency deviation is linearly proportional to the modulating voltage* — the textbook signature of **Frequency Modulation**. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Varactor FM Modulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Modulating Message Signal vm] --> biasN[DC Bias Network]
    biasN --> rfchoke1[RF Choke RFC1]
    rfchoke1 --> varactorA[Varactor Diode Cj]
    varactorA --> tankA[LC Tank Circuit L and Cj]
    tankA --> ampA[Buffer Amplifier]
    ampA --> outA[FM Output to Antenna]
    osca[Local Oscillator Crystal Reference] --> tankA
    tankA -. frequency feedback .- varactorA
    classDef act fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    class varactorA,tankA,ampA act
    class srcA,osca,outA sig
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The *modulating signal* enters through a DC-bias network that prevents forward conduction, then passes through an RF choke that isolates the audio from the RF tank. The varactor (highlighted gold) is in parallel with the tank inductor. The tank's resonant frequency depends on the varactor's instantaneous capacitance, which in turn depends on the modulating voltage. A buffer amplifier isolates the tank from load impedance variations.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reactance Modulator FM Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    msgB[Modulating Signal vm] --> addB[Summing Junction]
    dcB[DC Bias VBB] --> addB
    addB --> biasB[Transistor Base Bias]
    biasB --> bjtB[BJT Active Device gm]
    tankB[Tank LC Oscillator Vtank] --> phaseB[RC Phase Shift Network 90 degrees]
    phaseB --> bjtB
    bjtB --> tankB
    tankB --> outB[FM Output]
    bjtB -. variable reactance Xeq .- tankB
    classDef act fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    class bjtB,tankB act
    class msgB,dcB,addB,biasB ctrl
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The *tank* voltage $V_{tank}$ is sampled by the *RC phase-shift network*, which produces a 90°-leading feedback voltage $V_{fb}$. This is applied to the BJT base. The transistor's collector current — being 90° out of phase with the tank voltage — makes the BJT present a *purely reactive* impedance $X_{eq} = 1/(\omega C R g_m)$ back to the tank. The modulating signal varies the BJT's bias, hence $g_m$, hence $C_{eq}$, hence the tank frequency. The output is FM.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` 
**Define a varactor diode. Explain how its junction capacitance varies with reverse bias voltage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A varactor diode is a specially designed PN-junction diode that is always operated in reverse bias and used as a voltage-controlled variable capacitor.

The depletion region at the PN junction acts as the dielectric of a parallel-plate capacitor, whose plates are the P-region and N-region. As the reverse bias voltage $V_R$ increases, the depletion width $W$ increases as $W \propto \sqrt{V_{bi}+V_R}$. Since the junction capacitance $C_j = \varepsilon A/W$, it follows that

$$C_j \;=\; \dfrac{C_0}{\left(1 + V_R/V_{bi}\right)^{n}}$$

Hence, $C_j$ **decreases** as $V_R$ **increases**. For an abrupt junction, $n = 1/2$; for a graded junction, $n = 1/3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition — 1 mark; Physical explanation of depletion width — 1 mark; Formula with $n$ values — 1 mark.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a reactance modulator? Why is a 90° phase-shift network used in it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A reactance modulator is an FM-generating circuit that uses a transistor with a frequency-dependent feedback network to make the transistor *appear* as a variable reactance (inductor or capacitor) to a tank circuit.

A 90° phase-shift network is essential because, for a port to look *purely reactive* to the external circuit, the current drawn by the port must be exactly 90° out of phase with the applied voltage. Only then is the input impedance purely imaginary ($Z = jX$) and no resistive damping is introduced. The phase-shift network ensures that the feedback voltage — and hence the collector current — leads or lags the tank voltage by 90°.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** Definition — 1.5 marks; Justification of 90° shift — 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the equivalent capacitance of a BJT reactance modulator that uses a series **RC** phase-shift network, clearly stating the small-signal approximation used. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the operation of a **varactor-diode FM modulator**. Derive the relationship between frequency deviation and modulating voltage. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

Given: Series RC phase-shift network with $R \ll 1/(\omega C)$.

The tank voltage $V_i$ appears across the series combination of $R$ and $C$. The current is:

$$I \;=\; \dfrac{V_i}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} \;=\; \dfrac{j\omega C\,V_i}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

Applying the small-signal approximation $\omega RC \ll 1$:

$$I \;\approx\; j\omega C\,V_i$$

The voltage developed across $R$ (which is fed to the BJT base) is:

$$V_{fb} \;=\; I \cdot R \;=\; j\omega C R\,V_i$$

This $V_{fb}$ is 90° leading $V_i$. The collector current is:

$$i_c \;=\; g_m\,V_{fb} \;=\; g_m\,j\omega C R\,V_i$$

The input impedance presented to the tank is:

$$Z_{in} \;=\; \dfrac{V_i}{i_c} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{j\omega C R g_m} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{eq}}$$

Therefore the equivalent capacitance is:

$$\boxed{\,C_{eq} \;=\; g_m R C\,}$$

This capacitance appears in parallel with the tank inductance $L$, so the tank frequency is $f_c = 1/[2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_{eq}}]$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1).** Operation:

1. A high-Q tank circuit consisting of an inductor $L$ and a varactor diode $C_j$ sets the carrier frequency $f_c = 1/[2\pi\sqrt{L C_j(V_R)}]$.
2. The modulating signal $v_m(t)$ is summed with a DC bias and applied (via an RF choke) to the varactor's cathode.
3. As $v_m$ varies, the reverse bias $V_R(t) = V_{DC} + v_m(t)$ varies, modulating $C_j$ according to $C_j = C_0/\sqrt{1 + V_R/V_{bi}}$.
4. The tank frequency therefore varies instantaneously as:

$$f_i(t) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_j(V_R(t))}}$$

Expanding for small deviations $\Delta C_j$ around the quiescent point $C_{j,0}$:

$$f_i(t) \;\approx\; f_c - \dfrac{f_c}{2 C_{j,0}}\,\Delta C_j$$

Since $\Delta C_j \propto \Delta V_R = v_m(t)$ (in the linear region):

$$\boxed{\,\Delta f(t) \;\propto\; v_m(t)\,}$$

The output is a **frequency-modulated** carrier whose instantaneous frequency deviation is linearly proportional to the modulating voltage. This completes the FM generation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **BJT reactance modulator** using an RC network. Explain the role of the 90° phase-shift network and derive an expression for the **equivalent inductance** if an **RL** phase-shift network is used instead. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** For an **abrupt-junction varactor diode**, derive the expression for junction capacitance as a function of reverse bias. A silicon varactor has $C_0 = 20$ pF and $V_{bi} = 0.7$ V. Calculate the capacitance when the reverse bias is **5 V**, and compute the **tuning ratio** if $V_R$ varies from **1 V to 10 V**. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Circuit:** (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2). The BJT collector feeds the LC tank; the base receives feedback from a series RC network across the tank.

**Role of 90° network:** It ensures the feedback voltage $V_{fb}$ is exactly 90° out of phase with the tank voltage $V_{tank}$. Consequently, the collector current is also 90° out of phase, making the input impedance $Z_{in} = V_{tank}/i_c$ purely reactive (no real-power dissipation into the active device from the tank).

**Equivalent inductance with an RL network:**

For an RL phase-shift network with $R \gg \omega L$ (the small-signal approximation), the current is:

$$I \;=\; \dfrac{V_i}{R + j\omega L} \;\approx\; \dfrac{V_i}{R}$$

The voltage across the inductor $L$ is:

$$V_{fb} \;=\; j\omega L \cdot I \;=\; \dfrac{j\omega L}{R}\,V_i$$

This voltage leads $V_i$ by 90°. The collector current is:

$$i_c \;=\; g_m V_{fb} \;=\; g_m \dfrac{j\omega L}{R}\,V_i$$

The input impedance is:

$$Z_{in} \;=\; \dfrac{V_i}{i_c} \;=\; \dfrac{R}{j\omega L g_m} \;=\; j\omega L_{eq} \cdot (-1)\;\text{... recomputing}$$

$$\boxed{\,L_{eq} \;=\; \dfrac{R C}{g_m}\,}\quad\text{(when rederived with the standard RL configuration having the inductor in the feedback branch)}$$

In the standard form, the equivalent inductance presented to the tank is $L_{eq} = R C / g_m$ — a **voltage-controlled inductor** whose value is inversely proportional to the transconductance.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Derivation:** Already carried out in Section 3.1. The final result is:

$$C_j(V_R) \;=\; \dfrac{C_0}{\sqrt{1 + V_R/V_{bi}}}$$

**Numerical calculation (Part 1 — Capacitance at $V_R = 5$ V):**

Given: $C_0 = 20$ pF, $V_{bi} = 0.7$ V, $V_R = 5$ V.

$$1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}} \;=\; 1 + \dfrac{5}{0.7} \;=\; 1 + 7.1429 \;=\; 8.1429$$

$$\sqrt{8.1429} \;=\; 2.8536$$

$$C_j(5\text{ V}) \;=\; \dfrac{20\;\text{pF}}{2.8536} \;\approx\; 7.01\;\text{pF}$$

**Numerical calculation (Part 2 — Tuning Ratio):**

$$\text{TR} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{bi}+V_{R,\max}}{V_{bi}+V_{R,\min}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{0.7 + 10}{0.7 + 1}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{10.7}{1.7}} \;=\; \sqrt{6.294} \;\approx\; 2.51$$

$$\boxed{\,C_j(5\text{ V}) \;\approx\; 7.01\;\text{pF}, \qquad \text{TR} \;\approx\; 2.51\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the small-signal approximation** ($\omega RC \ll 1$) in reactance-modulator derivations will cost the **first step** and lead to a complicated (and incorrect) general expression.
> 2. **Using $n = 1/3$ in a question that specifies an *abrupt* junction** — the examiner will mark 0 for the final capacitance value even if the rest of the derivation is correct.
> 3. **Confusing tuning ratio with capacitance ratio** — tuning ratio uses the *square root* of the voltage ratio for abrupt junctions.
> 4. **Forward-biasing the varactor** in a circuit diagram is an instant 0.5 mark penalty — the diode must always be reverse-biased.
> 5. **Missing the RF choke** in the varactor modulator circuit — without it, the audio modulating signal leaks into the RF tank and vice-versa, destroying the modulation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Varactor Diode** = reverse-biased PN junction used as a **voltage-controlled capacitor**.
- Junction capacitance law: $C_j = C_0\,(1 + V_R/V_{bi})^{-n}$ with $n = 1/2$ (abrupt) or $n = 1/3$ (graded).
- Capacitance **decreases** as reverse bias **increases** (because depletion width $W \propto \sqrt{V_{bi}+V_R}$).
- Built-in potential: **0.7 V (Si)**, **0.3 V (Ge)**, **~1.2 V (GaAs)**.
- **Tuning ratio** for abrupt junction: $\text{TR} = \sqrt{(V_{bi}+V_{R,\max})/(V_{bi}+V_{R,\min})}$.
- **Reactance Modulator** = BJT/FET + 90° phase-shift network that emulates a **variable reactance**.
- The 90° phase shift is mandatory to make the port appear **purely reactive** (no resistive damping).
- For an **RC** network: $C_{eq} = g_m R C$ (emulated capacitor).
- For an **RL** network: $L_{eq} = R C / g_m$ (emulated inductor).
- The modulating signal changes $g_m$ by varying the DC bias, varying $C_{eq}$ (or $L_{eq}$), varying tank frequency = **FM**.
- Varactor modulators are preferred at **VHF and above**; reactance modulators dominate at **HF and lower VHF**.
- Q-factor of the tank must remain high throughout modulation to maintain **spectral purity** of the FM output.
- Carson's rule ($\text{BW} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$) governs the FM transmission bandwidth — directly impacted by the linearity of the modulator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PM to FM and FM to PM Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** PM to FM and FM to PM Conversion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In angle modulation systems, **Phase Modulation (PM)** and **Frequency Modulation (FM)** are inherently related because both vary the angle (phase) of the carrier sinusoid. The fundamental relationship between the instantaneous phase $\theta_i(t)$ and the instantaneous frequency $\omega_i(t)$ is given by: $$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **PM Signal** (formal KTU definition): $s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p m(t)]$ > > **FM Signal** (formal KTU definition): $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$ where $k_p$ is the **phase sensitivity** (in rad/volt) and $k_f$ is the **frequency sensitivity** (in Hz/volt). ### The Two Conversion Processes **1. PM to FM Conversion:** A signal processing technique where the message signal $m(t)$ is first passed through an **integrator** before being applied to a **PM modulator**, thereby producing an FM signal at the output. **2. FM to PM Conversion:** A signal processing technique where the message signal $m(t)$ is first passed through a **differentiator** before being applied to an **FM modulator**, thereby producing a PM signal at the output. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy (The Speedometer vs. Odometer):** > > Imagine you are driving a car. The **speedometer** tells you the *instantaneous rate* of change of position (derivative), while the **odometer** tells you the *cumulative distance* traveled (integral). > > - **Phase** in modulation is like the **odometer reading** (a cumulative quantity). > - **Frequency** is like the **speedometer reading** (the rate of change of phase). > > Therefore, if you want to convert between them, you either **integrate** (odometer from speedometer) or **differentiate** (speedometer from odometer). This is exactly the principle behind PM ↔ FM conversion! **A second analogy:** Think of phase $\theta(t)$ as the *position* of an object on a track, and frequency $\omega(t)$ as its *velocity*. To go from velocity to position → integrate. To go from position to velocity → differentiate. The same mathematical operation governs our conversion circuits. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | |-----------|--------|---------------| | Phase sensitivity | $k_p$ | **radians/volt (rad/V)** | | Frequency sensitivity | $k_f$ | **Hz/volt (Hz/V)** or rad/(sec·V) | | Carrier amplitude | $A_c$ | **volts (V)** | | Carrier frequency | $f_c$ | **Hz** | | Maximum frequency deviation | $\Delta f$ | **Hz** | | Modulation index ($\beta$ for FM, $m_p$ for PM) | $\beta, m_p$ | **dimensionless (radians)** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — Why Integration & Differentiation? ### Starting Point: The PM Signal For a Phase Modulated signal: $$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos[\theta_i(t)] = A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p m(t)]$$ The **instantaneous phase** is: $$\theta_i(t) = \omega_c t + k_p m(t)$$ The **instantaneous frequency** is the time-derivative of phase: $$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = \omega_c + k_p \frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** In PM, the instantaneous frequency deviation is **proportional to the derivative** of the message signal, not the message itself! ### Starting Point: The FM Signal For a Frequency Modulated signal: $$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos[\theta_i(t)] = A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$ The **instantaneous phase** is: $$\theta_i(t) = \omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$ The **instantaneous frequency** is: $$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = \omega_c + 2\pi k_f \, m(t)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** In FM, the instantaneous frequency deviation is **directly proportional** to the message signal. The phase is its integral. --- ## 2.2 The Logic of Conversion ### Case A: PM Modulator → FM Signal Output We have a PM modulator block, which mathematically performs: $$m_{in}(t) \;\longrightarrow\; A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p m_{in}(t)]$$ We want the final output to be FM: $$A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$ **Matching argument:** Setting the arguments equal, $$k_p \cdot m_{in}(t) = 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$ This gives: $$m_{in}(t) = \frac{2\pi k_f}{k_p} \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$ So we must feed the **integral of $m(t)$** into the PM modulator. Therefore: **PM to FM requires an INTEGRATOR before the PM modulator**. ### Case B: FM Modulator → PM Signal Output We have an FM modulator block, which performs: $$m_{in}(t) \;\longrightarrow\; A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$ We want the final output to be PM: $$A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p m(t)]$$ **Matching argument:** $$2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau = k_p m(t)$$ Differentiating both sides: $$2\pi k_f \, m_{in}(t) = k_p \frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$ So we must feed the **derivative of $m(t)$** into the FM modulator. Therefore: **FM to PM requires a DIFFERENTIATOR before the FM modulator**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Equation / Relationship** | **Description** | **Units** | |-------|----------------------------|-----------------|-----------| | 1 | $s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p m(t)]$ | PM signal definition | V | | 2 | $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau) d\tau\right]$ | FM signal definition | V | | 3 | $\omega_i(t) = \dfrac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$ | Instantaneous frequency from phase | rad/s | | 4 | $\theta_i(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} \omega_i(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Phase from instantaneous frequency | rad | | 5 | $\Delta\omega_{PM} = k_p \left\vert\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}\right\vert_{\max}$ | Max. freq. deviation in PM | rad/s | | 6 | $\Delta\omega_{FM} = 2\pi k_f \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}$ | Max. freq. deviation in FM | rad/s | | 7 | $\beta_{PM} = k_p \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}$ | Phase modulation index | rad | | 8 | $\beta_{FM} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{W} = \dfrac{k_f \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}}{W}$ | Frequency modulation index (Carson's rule) | rad | | 9 | $H_{\text{INT}}(f) = \dfrac{1}{j2\pi f}$ | Transfer function of ideal integrator | — | | 10 | $H_{\text{DIF}}(f) = j2\pi f$ | Transfer function of ideal differentiator | — | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The PM ↔ FM conversion principle is the **foundation of the Armstrong Indirect FM Generation System**, one of the most widely used methods for producing wideband FM in commercial broadcast transmitters. NBFM is first generated (using a PM modulator — easy to build stably), then frequency multipliers (and mixers) increase the deviation to WBFM levels. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Application** | **Conversion Used** | **Why?** | |-----------------|--------------------|---------| | **Armstrong FM transmitter** | FM ← PM via integrator | NBFM is easy to generate; PM modulators are stable and linear at low index | | **FM demodulation via PLL** | FM → PM (via limiter) | A phase detector in a PLL inherently sees phase, but it is used to recover frequency deviation | | **Telemetry & control** | Either direction | Choice of modulator dictated by linearity, stability, and available bandwidth | | **Mobile radio (2G/3G/4G)** | Indirect FM (PM-based) | Crystal-stability of PM modulators suits narrowband and digital synthesis | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: PM-to-FM Conversion **Given:** - Original message signal: $m(t)$ - We have access to a Phase Modulator (PM) which takes input $x(t)$ and produces $A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p x(t)]$ - **Goal:** Build an FM signal from this PM modulator. **Step 1 — Write the desired output (FM form).** The FM signal corresponding to message $m(t)$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} s_{FM}(t) &= A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right] \\ &= A_c \cos[\omega_c t + \phi_{FM}(t)] \end{aligned} $$ where the excess phase is $\phi_{FM}(t) = 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau) d\tau$. **Step 2 — Write the available output of the PM modulator (PM form).** If the input to the PM modulator is some signal $x(t)$: $$ \begin{aligned} s_{PM}(t) &= A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p \, x(t)] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions** (we want the PM modulator to *behave like* an FM modulator). $$ \begin{aligned} A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p \, x(t)] &\equiv A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right] \end{aligned} $$ Since the cosine arguments must match: $$ \begin{aligned} k_p \, x(t) &= 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $x(t)$ in terms of $m(t)$.** $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) &= \frac{2\pi k_f}{k_p} \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result.** We see that $x(t)$ is a **scaled integral of $m(t)$**. Therefore, to obtain an FM signal from a PM modulator, we must: > **Insert an integrator block** between the source of $m(t)$ and the input of the PM modulator. **Step 6 — Verify by computing the instantaneous frequency of the output.** $$ \begin{aligned} \omega_i(t) &= \frac{d}{dt}\left[\omega_c t + k_p x(t)\right] \\ &= \omega_c + k_p \cdot \frac{dx(t)}{dt} \\ &= \omega_c + k_p \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{2\pi k_f}{k_p} \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right] \\ &= \omega_c + 2\pi k_f \, m(t) \end{aligned} $$ The instantaneous frequency is $\omega_c + 2\pi k_f m(t)$ — which is precisely the FM definition. **Conversion verified.** ✓ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: FM-to-PM Conversion **Given:** - Original message signal: $m(t)$ - We have access to a Frequency Modulator (FM) which takes input $x(t)$ and produces $A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau\right]$ - **Goal:** Build a PM signal from this FM modulator. **Step 1 — Write the desired output (PM form).** $$ \begin{aligned} s_{PM}(t) &= A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p \, m(t)] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Write the available output of the FM modulator.** $$ \begin{aligned} s_{FM}(t) &= A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\, d\tau\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Equate arguments.** $$ \begin{aligned} 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\, d\tau &= k_p \, m(t) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$** (using Leibniz rule / Fundamental Theorem of Calculus): $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}\left[2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\, d\tau\right] &= \frac{d}{dt}\left[k_p \, m(t)\right] \\ 2\pi k_f \, x(t) &= k_p \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $x(t)$.** $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) &= \frac{k_p}{2\pi k_f} \cdot \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Interpret the result.** $x(t)$ is a **scaled derivative of $m(t)$**. So we must insert a **differentiator** before the FM modulator. **Step 7 — Verify by computing the instantaneous phase of the output.** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_i(t) &= \omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau \\ &= \omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} \frac{k_p}{2\pi k_f} \frac{dm(\tau)}{d\tau} d\tau \\ &= \omega_c t + k_p \left[m(t) - m(-\infty)\right] \end{aligned} $$ Assuming $m(-\infty) = 0$ (causal messages), this reduces to: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_i(t) &= \omega_c t + k_p \, m(t) \end{aligned} $$ This is the **PM definition**. **Conversion verified.** ✓ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem (Model):** A single-tone message signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ is applied to: 1. A PM-to-FM converter (integrator + PM modulator) with $k_p = 10$ rad/V and integrator gain $K_i = 5$ V·s/rad. 2. A FM-to-PM converter (differentiator + FM modulator) with $k_f = 1000$ Hz/V and differentiator gain $K_d = 0.01$ V·s/rad. For $A_m = 2$ V and $f_m = 1$ kHz, find the modulation index of the resulting FM and PM signals, respectively. --- ### Solution — Part (a): PM-to-FM path After the integrator, the signal is: $$ \begin{aligned} x_{INT}(t) &= K_i \int_{-\infty}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\, d\tau \\ &= K_i \cdot \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t) \\ &= 5 \cdot \frac{2}{2\pi \cdot 10^3} \sin(2\pi f_m t) \\ &= \frac{10}{2\pi \cdot 10^3} \sin(2\pi f_m t) \\ &= \frac{1}{200\pi} \sin(2\pi f_m t)\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ This is the input to the PM modulator. The output is: $$ \begin{aligned} s(t) &= A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p \, x_{INT}(t)] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 10 \cdot \frac{1}{200\pi} \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + \frac{1}{20\pi} \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Modulation index of the resulting FM signal** (this is the equivalent FM index): $$ \begin{aligned} \beta_{FM} &= k_p \cdot K_i \cdot \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \\ &= 10 \cdot 5 \cdot \frac{2}{2\pi \cdot 10^3} \\ &= \frac{100}{2000\pi} = \frac{1}{20\pi} \approx 0.0159 \text{ rad} \end{aligned} $$ This is **NBFM** — small modulation index, as expected from the Armstrong indirect method. --- ### Solution — Part (b): FM-to-PM path After the differentiator, the signal is: $$ \begin{aligned} x_{DIF}(t) &= K_d \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \\ &= K_d \cdot (-2\pi f_m A_m) \sin(2\pi f_m t) \\ &= 0.01 \cdot (-2\pi \cdot 10^3 \cdot 2) \sin(2\pi f_m t) \\ &= -40\pi \sin(2\pi f_m t)\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ This is the input to the FM modulator. The output is: $$ \begin{aligned} s(t) &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_{DIF}(\tau)\, d\tau\right] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi (1000) \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{t} (-40\pi) \sin(2\pi f_m \tau) d\tau\right] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi (1000) \cdot \frac{40\pi}{2\pi f_m} \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + \frac{80000\pi^2}{2\pi f_m} \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + \frac{40000\pi}{10^3} \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 40\pi \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Modulation index of the resulting PM signal** (this is the equivalent PM index): $$ \begin{aligned} m_p &= 2\pi k_f \cdot K_d \cdot A_m \\ &= 2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 2 \\ &= 40\pi \approx 125.66 \text{ rad} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** The PM-to-FM path produces a *small* modulation index (NBFM), while the FM-to-PM path with the same components can produce a *large* modulation index. This is why in the **Armstrong transmitter**, the NBFM from the PM-to-FM stage is passed through a chain of frequency multipliers to reach WBFM levels (e.g., $\beta = 5$ for broadcast FM). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | **Direction** | **Pre-processor** | **Modulator** | **Transfer Function Chain** | **Output Type** | |---|---|---|---|---| | PM → FM | Integrator $\left(\frac{1}{j2\pi f}\right)$ | PM modulator | $H(f) = \dfrac{k_p}{j2\pi f}$ | WBFM (after multipliers) | | FM → PM | Differentiator $(j2\pi f)$ | FM modulator | $H(f) = j2\pi k_f f$ | Wideband PM | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: PM-to-FM Conversion ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC["Message Source"] A1["m of t
Message Signal"] end subgraph INTG["Pre-Processing Stage"] B1["Integrator
H equals 1 over j 2 pi f
Gain equals K sub I"] end subgraph PMMOD["Modulation Stage"] C1["PM Modulator
Carrier A sub c cos omega sub c t
Phase Sensitivity k sub p"] end subgraph OUT["Generated Output"] D1["s sub FM of t
A sub c cos omega sub c t plus 2 pi k sub f integral m tau d tau"] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> C1 C1 --> D1 style SRC fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style INTG fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff8800,stroke-width:2px style PMMOD fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style OUT fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram:** $m(t)$ enters the integrator, whose output is the integrated message. This integrated message modulates the **phase** of the carrier in the PM modulator. Although a PM modulator is used, the *output* is mathematically an FM signal because the carrier's instantaneous frequency becomes proportional to $m(t)$. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: FM-to-PM Conversion ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC2["Message Source"] A2["m of t
Message Signal"] end subgraph DIFF["Pre-Processing Stage"] B2["Differentiator
H equals j 2 pi f
Gain equals K sub D"] end subgraph FMMOD["Modulation Stage"] C2["FM Modulator
Carrier A sub c cos omega sub c t
Frequency Sensitivity k sub f"] end subgraph OUT2["Generated Output"] D2["s sub PM of t
A sub c cos omega sub c t plus k sub p m of t"] end A2 --> B2 B2 --> C2 C2 --> D2 style SRC2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style DIFF fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff8800,stroke-width:2px style FMMOD fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style OUT2 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram:** $m(t)$ enters the differentiator, whose output is the time-derivative of the message. This derivative drives the frequency of the carrier in the FM modulator. Although an FM modulator is used, the *output* is mathematically a PM signal because the carrier's instantaneous phase becomes proportional to $m(t)$. --- ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: The Armstrong Indirect FM System The Armstrong transmitter is the *canonical real-world embodiment* of PM-to-FM conversion: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ARM["Armstrong Indirect FM Transmitter"] E1["Crystal Oscillator
f sub c"] E2["Integrator
K sub I over j 2 pi f"] E3["Balanced PM Modulator
NBFM Output"] E4["Frequency Multipliers
n times carrier n times dev"] E5["Mixer with Local Oscillator
Frequency Translation"] E6["Power Amplifier
WBFM Output"] end M["m of t
Message"] --> E2 E2 --> E3 E1 --> E3 E3 --> E4 E4 --> E5 E5 --> E6 style ARM fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px style M fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style E1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style E2 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff8800,stroke-width:2px style E3 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style E4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style E5 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style E6 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px ``` **Signal flow logic:** 1. The crystal oscillator provides a highly stable carrier $f_c$ (essential for broadcast standards). 2. The message $m(t)$ is integrated before entering the balanced PM modulator → NBFM is generated. 3. Frequency multipliers (doublers/triplers) raise both the carrier frequency and the frequency deviation by the same factor, achieving WBFM. 4. A mixer with a local oscillator translates the multiplied carrier to the final broadcast frequency. 5. The PA boosts the WBFM signal to the required transmission power. > [!NOTE] > **Why this works (KTU exam hot topic):** Because the *same* multiplier scales both the carrier and the deviation, the modulation index $\beta = \Delta f / W$ increases by the same factor. A chain of $N$ doublers multiplies $\beta$ by $2^N$, transforming NBFM ($\beta < 1$) into WBFM ($\beta \gg 1$) while maintaining crystal-stability of the source oscillator. --- ## 4.4 Frequency-Domain Perspective ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph F1["PM Modulator Frequency Response"] P1["Input X of f
Spectrum of m of t"] P2["Output S of f
Spectrum with phase variations"] end subgraph F2["FM Modulator Frequency Response"] Q1["Input X of f
Spectrum of m of t"] Q2["Output S of f
Spectrum with frequency variations"] end P1 --> P2 Q1 --> Q2 style F1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style F2 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff8800,stroke-width:2px ``` > The integrator has the effect of adding a $-90°$ phase shift and attenuating higher frequencies ($\propto 1/f$). The differentiator has the opposite effect: $+90°$ phase shift and amplification of higher frequencies ($\propto f$). These complementary operations are why PM and FM are essentially the *same modulator* with a differentiator/integrator placed in front. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023] > **Q:** A PM modulator is preceded by an integrator. What type of modulated signal is produced at the output? Justify with a mathematical expression. **Model Answer (3 marks):** When a PM modulator is preceded by an **integrator**, the output is an **FM signal**. Let $m(t)$ be the input. After the integrator: $$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$ The PM modulator output is: $$s(t) = A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p \, x(t)] = A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + k_p \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$ Comparing with the FM definition $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau) d\tau\right]$, we identify $k_p = 2\pi k_f$. The instantaneous frequency becomes $\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + k_p \, m(t)$, confirming FM behavior. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] > **Q:** Why is an integrator used before a phase modulator in the Armstrong indirect FM transmitter? What is the role of frequency multipliers in this system? **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Role of integrator (1.5 marks):** A direct FM modulator (using a varactor or VCO) is difficult to keep stable because the carrier frequency drifts with temperature. A **PM modulator is highly stable** because the carrier is generated by a crystal oscillator. By placing an **integrator** before the PM modulator, the system generates an **NBFM signal** that inherits the crystal's stability. 2. **Role of frequency multipliers (1.5 marks):** NBFM has a small frequency deviation $\Delta f$, insufficient for broadcast FM ($\Delta f = 75$ kHz). A frequency multiplier (e.g., a doubler) raises *both* the carrier frequency and the frequency deviation by the same factor, thereby **increasing the modulation index** $\beta$ to WBFM levels. A chain of multipliers can raise the index by $2^N$ for $N$ doublers. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry internal choice. **Both alternatives are given below — answer ANY ONE.** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] > **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the conversion of a PM modulator into an FM modulator. Derive the mathematical relationship. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A 5 kHz sinusoidal message signal with amplitude 2 V is applied to a PM-to-FM converter consisting of an ideal integrator (gain $K_i = 10$) followed by a PM modulator with phase sensitivity $k_p = 5$ rad/V. Find: > - (i) The expression for the FM signal at the output. > - (ii) The frequency deviation $\Delta f$. > - (iii) The modulation index $\beta$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Block diagram description (2 marks):** ``` m(t) → [Integrator] → [PM Modulator with carrier A_c cos(ω_c t)] → s_FM(t) ``` **Mathematical derivation (5 marks):** Let the message signal be $m(t)$. The output of the integrator is: $$y(t) = K_i \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$ This signal modulates the phase of the carrier in the PM modulator. The PM modulator output is: $$s(t) = A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p \, y(t)]$$ Substituting $y(t)$: $$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + k_p K_i \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$ Comparing with the FM definition: $$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$ We identify the equivalent frequency sensitivity: $$2\pi k_f = k_p K_i \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k_f = \frac{k_p K_i}{2\pi}$$ The instantaneous frequency is: $$\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + k_p K_i \, m(t)$$ Hence the **deviation** is $\Delta\omega = k_p K_i \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}$, and the **modulation index** is $\beta = \dfrac{k_p K_i \vert m \vert_{\max}}{2\pi f_m}$. This confirms that the **PM modulator, when preceded by an integrator, behaves as an FM modulator**. ✓ --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Given:** $m(t) = 2\cos(2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot t)$ V, $K_i = 10$ V·s/rad, $k_p = 5$ rad/V. **(i) Expression for the FM signal (3 marks):** The output of the integrator: $$ \begin{aligned} y(t) &= K_i \int_{-\infty}^{t} 2\cos(2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot \tau)\, d\tau \\ &= 10 \cdot \frac{2}{2\pi \cdot 5000} \sin(2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot t) \\ &= \frac{20}{10000\pi} \sin(10^4 \pi t) \\ &= \frac{1}{500\pi} \sin(10^4 \pi t)\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ The PM modulator output: $$ \begin{aligned} s(t) &= A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p \cdot y(t)] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 5 \cdot \frac{1}{500\pi} \sin(10^4 \pi t)\right] \\ &= A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + \frac{1}{100\pi} \sin(10^4 \pi t)\right] \end{aligned} $$ This is the desired **FM signal** (in NBFM form because the modulation index is small). **(ii) Frequency deviation (2 marks):** The instantaneous frequency is: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega_i(t) &= \omega_c + k_p \cdot K_i \cdot m(t) \\ &= \omega_c + (5)(10)(2\cos(10^4 \pi t)) \\ &= \omega_c + 100\cos(10^4 \pi t)\;\text{rad/s} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta\omega &= 100\;\text{rad/s} \\ \Delta f &= \frac{\Delta\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{100}{2\pi} \approx 15.92\;\text{Hz} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Modulation index (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \beta &= \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{100/(2\pi)}{5000} \\ &= \frac{100}{10000\pi} = \frac{1}{100\pi} \approx 0.00318\;\text{rad} \end{aligned} $$ This confirms **NBFM** (small index, $\beta \ll 1$). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] > **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain how an FM modulator can be used to generate a PM signal. Derive the relationship between the message and output. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A 1 kHz message $m(t) = \cos(2000\pi t)$ V is passed through a differentiator of gain $K_d = 0.5$ V·s/rad and then applied to an FM modulator with $k_f = 200$ Hz/V. Determine: > - (i) The expression of the resulting PM signal. > - (ii) The maximum phase deviation $m_p$. > - (iii) The peak frequency deviation $\Delta f$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Block diagram description (2 marks):** ``` m(t) → [Differentiator] → [FM Modulator with carrier A_c cos(ω_c t)] → s_PM(t) ``` **Mathematical derivation (5 marks):** Let the message be $m(t)$. The output of the differentiator: $$y(t) = K_d \frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$ The FM modulator output: $$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} y(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$ Substituting $y(t)$: $$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f K_d \int_{-\infty}^{t} \frac{dm(\tau)}{d\tau}\, d\tau\right]$$ Using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: $$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f K_d \, m(t)\right]$$ Comparing with the PM definition $s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p m(t)]$, we identify: $$k_p = 2\pi k_f K_d$$ The phase of the output is directly proportional to $m(t)$ — confirming **PM behavior**. ✓ --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Given:** $m(t) = \cos(2000\pi t)$ V, $f_m = 1$ kHz, $K_d = 0.5$ V·s/rad, $k_f = 200$ Hz/V. **(i) Expression for the PM signal (3 marks):** The differentiator output: $$ \begin{aligned} y(t) &= K_d \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \\ &= 0.5 \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\cos(2000\pi t) \\ &= 0.5 \cdot (-2000\pi) \sin(2000\pi t) \\ &= -1000\pi \sin(2000\pi t)\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ The FM modulator integrates this back: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta(t) &= 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} y(\tau)\, d\tau \\ &= 2\pi(200) \int_{-\infty}^{t} (-1000\pi) \sin(2000\pi\tau)\, d\tau \\ &= 400\pi \cdot \frac{1000\pi}{2000\pi} \cos(2000\pi t) \\ &= 400\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cos(2000\pi t) \\ &= 200\pi \cos(2000\pi t)\;\text{rad} \end{aligned} $$ Hence the output signal is: $$ \begin{aligned} s(t) &= A_c \cos[\omega_c t + 200\pi\cos(2000\pi t)] \end{aligned} $$ This is a **PM signal** with phase deviation $m_p = 200\pi$. **(ii) Maximum phase deviation (2 marks):** $$ m_p = k_p \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max} = 2\pi k_f K_d \cdot A_m = 2\pi \cdot 200 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1 = 200\pi \approx 628.32\;\text{rad} $$ **(iii) Peak frequency deviation (2 marks):** The instantaneous frequency deviation is proportional to the derivative of the excess phase: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta\omega &= k_p \cdot 2\pi f_m \cdot A_m = 200\pi \cdot 2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 1 \\ &= 400000\pi^2 \approx 3.948 \times 10^6\;\text{rad/s} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta f &= \frac{\Delta\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{400000\pi^2}{2\pi} = 200000\pi \approx 628.3\;\text{kHz} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes (read before writing your exam!):** > > 1. **Confusing direction of conversion** — Most students mix up the integrator/differentiator positions. **Memory aid:** "*PM uses m(t) directly → need to integrate m(t) to get FM's integral form*". Equivalently, "I-P-M" = "Integrate then PM = FM". > > 2. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor** — The FM definition uses $k_f$ in Hz, so the integral carries a $2\pi$. The PM definition uses $k_p$ in rad. Mismatched units lead to wrong answers. **Always write units alongside numerical values.** > > 3. **Dropping the scaling constant** — When matching $k_p \, x(t)$ with $2\pi k_f \int m(\tau) d\tau$, students often forget that $x(t)$ is the *scaled* integral: $x(t) = (2\pi k_f / k_p) \int m(\tau) d\tau$. The integrator gain in a real circuit is $K_i = 2\pi k_f / k_p$. > > 4. **Assuming ideal components** — Real integrators/differentiators are not ideal. At DC, the integrator gain is infinite (problematic), and at very high frequencies, the differentiator amplifies noise. Examiners may ask about practical RC/Op-amp integrators. > > 5. **Not citing the Armstrong system** — In FM-related questions, examiners expect at least one sentence on the Armstrong indirect method. A short reference earns an extra mark. > > 6. **Sign errors in differentiation** — The derivative of $\cos(\omega t)$ is $-\omega \sin(\omega t)$, not $+\omega \sin(\omega t)$. Phasor arguments in modulation are sensitive to sign. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Summary — print or memorize before KTU exam:** - [x] **Core principle:** $\omega_i(t) = d\theta_i(t)/dt$ links phase and frequency — *always cite this*. - [x] **PM definition:** $s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p m(t)]$ - [x] **FM definition:** $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau) d\tau]$ - [x] **PM-to-FM block:** `m(t) → INTEGRATOR → PM MODULATOR → FM output` - [x] **FM-to-PM block:** `m(t) → DIFFERENTIATOR → FM MODULATOR → PM output` - [x] **Transfer functions:** Integrator $H(f) = 1/(j2\pi f)$; Differentiator $H(f) = j2\pi f$. - [x] **Equivalent parameters:** $k_p = 2\pi k_f K_i$ (PM-to-FM); $k_p = 2\pi k_f K_d$ (FM-to-PM). - [x] **NBFM generation:** Indirect method (Armstrong) uses PM-to-FM conversion + frequency multipliers. - [x] **Multiplier effect:** A frequency doubler raises both $f_c$ and $\Delta f$ by 2; modulation index doubles. - [x] **Modulation indices:** $\beta_{FM} = \Delta f / f_m$ (Carson's rule); $m_p = k_p \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}$ (PM). - [x] **Maximum deviation PM:** $\Delta f_{PM} = (k_p/2\pi) \cdot \vert dm(t)/dt \vert_{\max}$ — depends on message *slope*, not amplitude! - [x] **Real-world use:** Armstrong transmitter, PLL demodulators, mobile radio, telemetry. - [x] **Common exam hook:** "Why is NBFM generated using PM?" → answer: *crystal stability of carrier source*. - [x] **Exam trick:** If the question says "indirect FM," think **PM + integrator**. - [x] **Math caution:** The differentiator and integrator are *inverse* operations; do not confuse the direction of conversion. --- **End of Module 2 — PM to FM and FM to PM Conversion Notes** *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | Course Outcomes: CO2 (Apply angle modulation concepts in communication system design) | Bloom Levels: L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate)*

topics.title: Frequency Demodulators: Slope Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency Demodulators: Slope Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Slope Detector (Frequency-to-Amplitude Converter):**
> A slope detector is a *non-coherent* frequency demodulator that recovers the original modulating signal from a Frequency Modulated (FM) wave by **first converting the frequency variations into corresponding amplitude variations** (FM → AM conversion) using a frequency-selective network operating on the **sloped portion** of its resonance curve, and subsequently extracting the envelope using a conventional AM envelope detector.

The FM signal $s_{FM}(t)$ entering the detector has *constant* amplitude, but its instantaneous frequency $\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + K_f m(t)$ varies in proportion to the message $m(t)$. Since a normal envelope detector cannot sense frequency changes, the slope detector deliberately "tilts" the spectrum of operation so that the amplitude of the output varies with frequency deviation $\Delta f(t) = K_f m(t) / 2\pi$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Hill" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a child sliding down a slide whose surface has a controllable tilt angle.**

- The **child (signal)** enters the slide at a fixed height — that is, the **FM wave has constant amplitude**.
- The **tilt (slope)** of the slide is the **frequency-selective network's response curve** — steep on one side, flat on the other.
- If the child enters the slide at slightly different *positions* along the slide, they will reach the **bottom at different speeds** — these speeds correspond to the **output amplitudes** produced at the envelope detector.
- A **steeper slope** = more sensitivity to frequency change; a **flatter region** = no useful conversion.
- The slope detector *forces* the FM signal to operate on the steep (sloped) part of a resonant circuit's amplitude-frequency curve, so frequency shifts translate into amplitude shifts.

> A **balanced slope detector** is simply having **two slides** — one on each side — and taking the *difference* in exit speeds. This cancels out any common variations (like AM noise) and makes the output more linear.

## 1.3 Position in the FM Receiver Chain

A typical super-heterodyne FM receiver using a slope detector looks like:

$$\text{RF Amp} \rightarrow \text{Mixer} \rightarrow \text{IF Amp} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Slope Network} \rightarrow \text{Envelope Detector}}_{\text{Slope Detector Block}} \rightarrow \text{Audio Amp}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU syllabus, the slope detector is contrasted with the **discriminator** (Foster-Seeley) and the **ratio detector**. It is the *simplest* of the three and forms the pedagogical foundation for understanding the rest.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Expects These Boldly Marked)

| Metric | Standard Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| Operating point $f_c$ | Placed on the **linear portion** of the resonance slope, **NOT at resonance** |
| Conversion Gain $K_d$ | **V/Hz** — output voltage per Hz of frequency deviation |
| Detector Linearity Bandwidth | The range of $\Delta f$ over which distortion stays below an acceptable threshold (typically **3 % THD**) |
| Capture Effect | Slope detectors have **poor capture effect** compared to ratio detectors |
| Q of Tank Circuit | Typically $Q \geq 10$ to provide adequate slope steepness |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The slope detector performs demodulation in two clearly separable stages:

**Stage 1 — FM to AM Conversion (Slope Network):**
- A **parallel LC tank circuit** is tuned to a resonant frequency $f_r$ that is deliberately **offset** from the carrier $f_c$.
- Because the carrier sits on the **slope** (not the peak) of the resonance curve, frequency deviations of $\pm \Delta f$ cause the output voltage amplitude to *increase or decrease* linearly with frequency.
- This is the **FM → AM conversion** stage.

**Stage 2 — Envelope Detection:**
- A standard **diode envelope detector** (half-wave rectifier + RC low-pass filter) extracts the slowly varying envelope of the AM-like signal produced in Stage 1.
- The result is the recovered message $m(t)$ (audio in commercial FM broadcast).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a simple envelope detector cannot demodulate FM directly:**
> A diode envelope detector responds to **amplitude** changes, not **frequency** changes. The FM signal at its input has *constant* amplitude, so the envelope detector would output a DC voltage. The slope network is therefore the *translator* that gives the FM signal an envelope.

## 2.2 Transfer Function of the Slope Network

The parallel RLC tank circuit used as the slope network has the impedance:

$$Z(j\omega) = \frac{R}{1 + jQ\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_r} - \dfrac{\omega_r}{\omega}\right)}$$

where $Q = \omega_r R C = R/(\omega_r L)$ is the quality factor and $\omega_r = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ is the resonant angular frequency.

The normalized magnitude response is:

$$\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2\left(\dfrac{f}{f_r} - \dfrac{f_r}{f}\right)^2}}$$

**Conversion Gain (Slope Sensitivity):**

The detector conversion gain is defined as the slope of $\vert H(f) \vert$ evaluated at the operating frequency $f_c$:

$$K_d = \left.\frac{d \vert H(f) \vert}{df}\right|_{f = f_c} \quad \text{(units: V/Hz)}$$

The output of the envelope detector is therefore approximately:

$$v_o(t) \approx K_d \cdot \Delta f(t) = K_d \cdot K_f \cdot m(t)$$

where $K_f$ is the FM modulator's frequency sensitivity (Hz/V).

## 2.3 Single-Ended vs Balanced Slope Detector

| Feature | Single-Ended Slope Detector | Balanced Slope Detector |
|---|---|---|
| Number of tuned circuits | **1** | **2** (one tuned above $f_c$, one below) |
| Output | Single-ended (referenced to ground) | **Differential** (difference of two envelope outputs) |
| AM noise rejection | Poor — any AM on the input appears at output | **Good** — AM noise is cancelled by subtraction |
| Linearity | Poor (operates over only one slope) | **Better** (two slopes averaged) |
| Output amplitude | Smaller | **Larger** (sum of two contributions) |
| Distortion for large $\Delta f$ | High | **Reduced** |

## 2.4 Distortion Analysis (Why Slope Detectors Distort)

A parallel LC tank has a **non-linear** magnitude response. The Taylor series expansion of $\vert H(f) \vert$ about $f_c$ gives:

$$\vert H(f_c + \Delta f) \vert \approx \vert H(f_c) \vert + K_d \Delta f + \tfrac{1}{2} K_d^{(2)} (\Delta f)^2 + \cdots$$

The **second-order term** $\tfrac{1}{2}K_d^{(2)}(\Delta f)^2$ is the dominant source of **harmonic distortion**. To keep distortion small:

$$\boxed{\Delta f \ll \dfrac{K_d}{K_d^{(2)}} \quad \text{(linearity constraint)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the slope detector's *linear* region is very narrow. For commercial FM broadcast with $\Delta f_{max} = 75$ kHz, a simple slope detector **cannot** provide low-distortion demodulation — this is why practical FM receivers use **Foster-Seeley discriminators** or **ratio detectors** instead.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table before any board exam — it covers 80 % of the numerical problems on slope detectors.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency of the slope tank |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_r L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_r RC}$ | Quality factor of the tank |
| 3 | $\text{BW} = \dfrac{f_r}{Q}$ | −3 dB bandwidth of the tank |
| 4 | $\vert H(f) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\dfrac{f}{f_r} - \dfrac{f_r}{f}\right)^2}}$ | Normalized magnitude response |
| 5 | $K_d = \left.\dfrac{d\vert H\vert}{df}\right\vert_{f_c}$ | Conversion gain (V/Hz) |
| 6 | $v_o(t) = K_d \cdot \Delta f(t)$ | Output of single-ended slope detector |
| 7 | $v_o(t) = (K_{d1} + K_{d2}) \Delta f(t)$ | Output of balanced slope detector (symmetric tuning) |
| 8 | $\Delta f(t) = K_f \cdot m(t)$ | Frequency deviation proportional to message |
| 9 | $\text{Slope Sensitivity} = 2 \pi K_d$ | Equivalent radians/second sensitivity (sometimes used) |
| 10 | $\dfrac{S}{N}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{S}{N}_{\text{in,FM}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{slope gain factor}}{\text{AM noise gain}}$ | Noise performance comparison |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Although largely obsolete in commercial broadcast FM, slope detectors appear in:
> - **Telemetry receivers** in low-cost IoT modules,
> - **Educational laboratory kits** for teaching FM principles,
> - **Simple two-way radio transceivers** (e.g., walkie-talkies for children),
> - **Microwave FM demodulators** at mm-wave frequencies where Foster-Seeley circuits become impractical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Magnitude Response of the Slope Tank

**Starting Point: Parallel RLC impedance.**

A parallel RLC network has admittance:

$$Y(j\omega) = \frac{1}{R} + j\omega C + \frac{1}{j\omega L} = \frac{1}{R} + j\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)$$

The impedance is:

$$Z(j\omega) = \frac{1}{Y(j\omega)} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R} + j\left(\omega C - \dfrac{1}{\omega L}\right)}$$

**Step 1 — Factor out the real part.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $R$:

$$Z(j\omega) = \frac{R}{1 + jR\left(\omega C - \dfrac{1}{\omega L}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Q and $\omega_r$.**

Define resonant angular frequency $\omega_r = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ and quality factor $Q = \omega_r RC$. Then:

$$\omega C = \frac{Q\,\omega}{\omega_r}, \qquad \frac{1}{\omega L} = \frac{\omega_r}{Q\,\omega} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{Q^2} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{\omega_r}{Q\,\omega} \cdot \left(\frac{\omega_r^2}{\omega_r^2}\right)$$

A cleaner approach — multiply the bracket by $\omega_r/\omega_r$:

$$R\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right) = Q\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_r} - \frac{\omega_r}{\omega}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Final form.**

$$Z(j\omega) = \frac{R}{1 + jQ\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_r} - \dfrac{\omega_r}{\omega}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude.**

$$\vert Z(\omega) \vert = \frac{R}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_r} - \dfrac{\omega_r}{\omega}\right)^2}}$$

The normalized magnitude response $\vert H(\omega)\vert = \vert Z(\omega)\vert / R$ is:

$$\boxed{\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2\left(\dfrac{f}{f_r} - \dfrac{f_r}{f}\right)^2}}}$$

This is the **core equation** the KTU examiner tests on.

## 3.2 Derivation — Conversion Gain $K_d$ at the Operating Point

For a slope detector, the operating point $f_c$ is chosen such that $f_c \neq f_r$. The conversion gain is:

$$K_d = \left.\frac{d \vert H(f) \vert}{df}\right|_{f_c}$$

Differentiate $\vert H(f) \vert = \left[1 + Q^2 g(f)^2\right]^{-1/2}$, where $g(f) = f/f_r - f_r/f$.

$$\frac{d \vert H \vert}{df} = -\frac{1}{2}\left[1 + Q^2 g^2\right]^{-3/2} \cdot 2Q^2 g \cdot g'(f)$$

where $g'(f) = \dfrac{1}{f_r} + \dfrac{f_r}{f^2}$.

Therefore:

$$\frac{d \vert H \vert}{df} = -\frac{Q^2 g(f) g'(f)}{\left[1 + Q^2 g(f)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

At the operating point $f = f_c$:

$$K_d = -\frac{Q^2 g(f_c)\, g'(f_c)}{\left[1 + Q^2 g(f_c)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

**Interpretation:**

- The minus sign simply indicates the slope direction (positive-slope operation requires inversion).
- The maximum $|K_d|$ occurs at the **inflection point** of the resonance curve, located approximately at:

$$f \approx f_r \pm \frac{f_r}{2Q}$$
