- For typical values $Q = 50$, $f_r = 10.7$ MHz (standard FM IF), the inflection is $\pm 107$ kHz from $f_r$ — conveniently close to the $\pm 75$ kHz peak deviation of commercial FM.

## 3.3 Derivation — Output Voltage for a Single-Tone FM Input

Let the message be $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$. The instantaneous frequency is:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \Delta f_{\max} \cos(2\pi f_m t), \qquad \Delta f_{\max} = K_f A_m$$

The output of the slope network (FM → AM) has instantaneous amplitude proportional to $\vert H(f_i)\vert$:

$$v_{AM}(t) = V_{cc} \cdot \vert H(f_i(t)) \vert$$

The envelope detector (assuming ideal diode, perfect RC filter) recovers this envelope:

$$v_o(t) = V_{cc} \cdot \vert H(f_c + \Delta f_{\max}\cos 2\pi f_m t) \vert$$

**Linear Approximation (small $\Delta f_{\max}$):**

Using the first-order Taylor series:

$$\vert H(f_c + \Delta f) \vert \approx \vert H(f_c) \vert + K_d \Delta f$$

Substitute $\Delta f = \Delta f_{\max} \cos 2\pi f_m t$:

$$v_o(t) \approx V_{cc} \vert H(f_c)\vert + V_{cc}\, K_d\, \Delta f_{\max} \cos 2\pi f_m t$$

The DC term is blocked by a coupling capacitor; the AC term is the recovered message:

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = V_{cc}\, K_d\, K_f\, A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)}$$

**Second-Order (Distortion) Term:**

$$\vert H(f_c + \Delta f) \vert \approx \vert H(f_c) \vert + K_d \Delta f + \tfrac{1}{2} K_d^{(2)} (\Delta f)^2$$

Substituting $\Delta f = \Delta f_{\max}\cos \omega_m t$ and using $\cos^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + \cos 2\theta)$:

$$\text{2nd harmonic term} = \tfrac{1}{4} V_{cc} K_d^{(2)} \Delta f_{\max}^2 \cos 2\omega_m t$$

So the **second-harmonic distortion ratio** is:

$$\text{THD}_2 \approx \frac{\vert K_d^{(2)} \vert \Delta f_{\max}}{4 \vert K_d \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This $\Delta f_{\max}^2$ dependence on distortion is *why* slope detectors cannot handle large-deviation FM (e.g., wideband FM broadcast) and *why* the balanced slope detector (which cancels the second-order term by symmetry) is preferred when the application permits it.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A slope detector uses a tank circuit with $L = 1\,\mu H$, $C = 100\,pF$, $R = 5\,k\Omega$. The carrier frequency is $f_c = 4.9$ MHz. Compute the resonant frequency $f_r$, quality factor $Q$, and slope sensitivity $K_d$ at $f_c$.

**Step 1 — Resonant frequency.**

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(1\times 10^{-6})(100\times 10^{-12})}}$$

$$LC = 1 \times 10^{-16} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{LC} = 10^{-8}$$

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{10^8}{2\pi} = 15.92 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Quality factor.**

$$Q = \omega_r R C = 2\pi f_r R C = 2\pi (15.92\times 10^6)(5000)(100\times 10^{-12})$$

$$Q = 2\pi (15.92 \times 10^6)(5 \times 10^{-7}) = 2\pi (7.96) \approx 50.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $g(f_c)$.**

$$g(f_c) = \frac{f_c}{f_r} - \frac{f_r}{f_c} = \frac{4.9}{15.92} - \frac{15.92}{4.9} = 0.3078 - 3.2490 = -2.9412$$

**Step 4 — Compute $g'(f_c)$.**

$$g'(f_c) = \frac{1}{f_r} + \frac{f_r}{f_c^2} = \frac{1}{15.92\times 10^6} + \frac{15.92\times 10^6}{(4.9\times 10^6)^2}$$

$$= 6.28 \times 10^{-8} + \frac{15.92 \times 10^6}{24.01 \times 10^{12}} = 6.28 \times 10^{-8} + 6.63 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$g'(f_c) = 7.26 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Hz}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $K_d$.**

$$K_d = -\frac{Q^2\, g(f_c)\, g'(f_c)}{\left[1 + Q^2 g(f_c)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

$$Q^2 = 2500, \quad Q^2 g^2 = 2500 \times 8.65 = 21{,}625$$

$$\left[1 + 21{,}625\right]^{3/2} = (21{,}626)^{3/2} = (21{,}626)\sqrt{21{,}626} \approx (21{,}626)(147.05) = 3.18 \times 10^6$$

Numerator:

$$-2500 \times (-2.9412) \times (7.26 \times 10^{-7}) = 2500 \times 2.135 \times 10^{-6} = 5.34 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$K_d = \frac{5.34 \times 10^{-3}}{3.18 \times 10^{6}} = 1.68 \times 10^{-9} \text{ V/Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *normalized* slope. The actual output in V/Hz requires multiplication by the input signal amplitude $V_{cc}$. For a $V_{cc} = 10$ V input carrier, the conversion gain is $1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ V/Hz.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Single-Ended Slope Detector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FM Input Signal] --> B[Limiter]
    B --> C[Slope Tank Circuit]
    C --> D[Diode Rectifier]
    D --> E[RC Low Pass Filter]
    E --> F[Coupling Capacitor]
    F --> G[Audio Output]
    C -.feedback path.-> H[Tank tuned off-carrier]
    classDef block fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#000
    class A,B,D,E,F,G block
    class C,H node
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The FM signal first passes through a **limiter** to remove any residual AM noise (a critical pre-condition, since the slope detector is *sensitive* to amplitude variations). It then enters the **slope tank**, which is tuned *off-center* relative to the carrier. The tank converts FM to AM, which is rectified, low-pass filtered, and coupled out as the recovered audio.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Balanced Slope Detector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FM Input] --> B[Input Transformer with Center Tapped Secondary]
    B --> C1[Tank 1 tuned above fc]
    B --> C2[Tank 2 tuned below fc]
    C1 --> D1[Diode D1]
    C2 --> D2[Diode D2]
    D1 --> E1[RC Filter 1]
    D2 --> E2[RC Filter 2]
    E1 --> F[Differential Output Node]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Audio Amplifier]
    F --> H[DC return path]
    classDef tankStyle fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef diodeStyle fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    class C1,C2 tankStyle
    class D1,D2 diodeStyle
    class F,G,H outputStyle
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Two slope tanks (one above, one below $f_c$) drive two diode envelope detectors. Their outputs are subtracted at a common node. Common-mode signals (such as AM noise and supply variations) cancel; the differential frequency-modulation signal adds.

## 4.3 Slope Detection Concept — Frequency-to-Amplitude Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FM Signal Constant Amplitude] --> B[Tank Resonance Curve]
    B --> C{Operating Point fc}
    C -->|Below fr| D[Increasing Slope]
    C -->|Above fr| E[Decreasing Slope]
    D --> F[FM to AM conversion positive]
    E --> G[FM to AM conversion negative]
    F --> H[Envelope Detector]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Recovered Message]
    classDef curve fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    class B,C curve
    class D,E,F,G conv
    class H,I result
```

## 4.4 Comparison Tree — Slope Detector Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FM Demodulators] --> B[Slope Detectors]
    A --> C[Discriminators]
    A --> D[Ratio Detectors]
    A --> E[PLL Detectors]
    B --> B1[Single Ended]
    B --> B2[Balanced Slope]
    C --> C1[Foster Seeley]
    D --> D1[Conventional Ratio]
    D --> D2[Bridge Ratio]
    B1 --> B1a[Simple Low Cost]
    B1 --> B1b[Poor AM Rejection]
    B2 --> B2a[Better Linearity]
    B2 --> B2b[Partial AM Cancellation]
    classDef cat fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef detail fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E cat
    class B1,B2,C1,D1,D2,E1 sub
    class B1a,B1b,B2a,B2b detail
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Slope detectors are one of four major FM demodulator families taught in Module 2. Note the **trade-off triangle** — slope detectors are simple/cheap but linear only for small frequency deviations.

## 4.5 Frequency Response with Operating-Point Annotation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Tank Response]
        R1[Resonance Peak at fr]
        O1[Operating Point fc left of fr]
        O2[Operating Point fc right of fr]
        L1[Linear Region]
    end
    R1 --- O1
    R1 --- O2
    O1 --- L1
    O2 --- L1
    classDef peak fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef lin fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    class R1 peak
    class O1,O2 op
    class L1 lin
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The carrier operating point $f_c$ must lie in the **Linear Region** for low-distortion demodulation. Choosing it too close to the resonance peak gives a small slope (low sensitivity); choosing it too far gives good sensitivity but high distortion.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define slope detector. Why is it also called a frequency-to-amplitude converter? [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A slope detector is an FM demodulator circuit that converts frequency variations of the input FM signal into corresponding amplitude variations, which are subsequently detected by an envelope detector to recover the original message signal.

It is called a **frequency-to-amplitude converter** because the frequency-selective tank circuit is deliberately tuned *off-carrier*, so that the operating point lies on the **sloped portion** of the resonance curve. Hence, every change in the instantaneous frequency of the FM input produces a proportional change in the output amplitude.

**Key points to mention in exam:**
1. Off-resonance tuning is essential.
2. The slope of the curve acts as a linear transducer.
3. Envelope detection recovers the message.

### Question 2: Compare single-ended and balanced slope detectors. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Ended | Balanced |
|---|---|---|
| Tuned circuits | One | Two (one above and one below carrier) |
| Linearity | Poor | Better |
| AM noise rejection | Poor | Good (common-mode cancellation) |
| Output amplitude | Lower | Higher |
| Distortion | Higher | Lower |
| Circuit complexity | Simple | Slightly more complex |

The balanced slope detector **outperforms** the single-ended version in linearity and AM rejection because the differential connection cancels even-order distortion terms and any amplitude noise that is common to both tanks.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper, Modified]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a slope detector. Derive the expression for the magnitude response of the slope network.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid schematic):**

The slope detector consists of the following cascaded stages:
1. **Limiter** — removes any amplitude variations in the FM input.
2. **Slope tank circuit (RLC)** — tuned to a frequency $f_r$ *different* from the carrier $f_c$, providing FM-to-AM conversion.
3. **Diode rectifier** — half-wave rectifies the AM-like signal.
4. **RC low-pass filter** — extracts the envelope.
5. **Coupling capacitor** — blocks the DC component.

**Working:**

The FM input $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi K_f \!\!\int\!\! m(t)\, dt\right]$ has a constant amplitude $A_c$ but varying instantaneous frequency. The slope tank's magnitude response is maximum at $f_r$ and rolls off on either side. By tuning the tank such that $f_c$ lies on the linear portion of one of the slopes, frequency deviations $\pm \Delta f$ translate into amplitude changes $\pm K_d \Delta f$ at the tank's output. The diode detector then recovers the envelope, which is the message $m(t)$.

**Derivation of the magnitude response:**

Starting from the parallel RLC impedance:

$$Z(j\omega) = \frac{R}{1 + jQ\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_r} - \dfrac{\omega_r}{\omega}\right)}$$

Taking the magnitude and converting to frequency domain:

$$\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2\left(\dfrac{f}{f_r} - \dfrac{f_r}{f}\right)^2}}$$

where $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ and $Q = \omega_r RC$.

The derivative (slope) at $f_c$ gives the conversion gain $K_d$ (refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for full derivation).

#### (b) [7 Marks] A slope detector is designed with $L = 2\,\mu H$, $C = 50\,pF$, $R = 10\,k\Omega$. The carrier is at $f_c = 12$ MHz. Calculate the resonant frequency, Q-factor, and the conversion gain at the carrier frequency. Discuss the limitation of using a single-ended slope detector for wideband FM.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Resonant frequency.**

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(2 \times 10^{-6})(50 \times 10^{-12})}}$$

$$LC = 10^{-16}, \quad \sqrt{LC} = 10^{-8}$$

$$f_r = \frac{10^8}{2\pi} = 15.92 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2: Quality factor.**

$$Q = \omega_r R C = 2\pi (15.92 \times 10^6)(10{,}000)(50 \times 10^{-12})$$

$$Q = 2\pi (15.92 \times 10^6)(5 \times 10^{-7}) = 2\pi (7.96) = 50.0$$

**Step 3: $g(f_c)$ and $g'(f_c)$.**

$$g(f_c) = \frac{12}{15.92} - \frac{15.92}{12} = 0.7538 - 1.3267 = -0.5729$$

$$g'(f_c) = \frac{1}{15.92 \times 10^6} + \frac{15.92 \times 10^6}{(12 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$= 6.28 \times 10^{-8} + \frac{15.92 \times 10^6}{1.44 \times 10^{14}} = 6.28 \times 10^{-8} + 1.106 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$g'(f_c) = 1.73 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Hz}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Conversion gain.**

$$K_d = -\frac{Q^2\, g(f_c)\, g'(f_c)}{\left[1 + Q^2 g(f_c)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

$$Q^2 = 2500, \quad Q^2 g^2 = 2500 \times 0.3282 = 820.5$$

$$\left[1 + 820.5\right]^{3/2} = (821.5)^{3/2} = 821.5 \times 28.66 = 23{,}548$$

Numerator:

$$-2500 \times (-0.5729) \times (1.73 \times 10^{-7}) = 2.48 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$K_d = \frac{2.48 \times 10^{-4}}{23{,}548} = 1.05 \times 10^{-8} \text{ (normalized, per unit input)}$$

**Limitations of Single-Ended Slope Detector for Wideband FM:**

1. **Non-linearity of the slope:** The resonance curve is non-linear; only a narrow portion is approximately linear. For wideband FM with $\Delta f_{max} = 75$ kHz, the operating point must remain in this narrow region, severely limiting linearity.
2. **High harmonic distortion:** Second-order distortion grows as $\Delta f^2$, making the detector unsuitable for large deviation FM.
3. **Poor AM rejection:** Any amplitude modulation riding on the FM input directly appears at the output.
4. **Narrow dynamic range:** The detector saturates if $\Delta f$ pushes the operating point off the linear slope into the flat region.
5. **No limiting action:** Unlike the ratio detector, the slope detector has no built-in limiter.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In part (b), students frequently *forget to specify the units* of $K_d$ (V/Hz) and often drop the **minus sign** that arises from operating on the descending slope. Also, the final answer must be multiplied by the *input carrier amplitude* $V_{cc}$ to give the *actual* output voltage per Hz — many students lose 2 marks by reporting only the normalized $K_d$.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper, Modified]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the principle of operation of a balanced slope detector with a neat block diagram. Show that even-order distortion terms cancel in the differential output.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2):**

The balanced slope detector uses **two parallel RLC tanks**:
- Tank 1: tuned to $f_{r1} > f_c$ (above the carrier)
- Tank 2: tuned to $f_{r2} < f_c$ (below the carrier)

Both tanks receive the same FM input. Their outputs are envelope-detected separately, and the two detected outputs $v_{o1}$ and $v_{o2}$ are subtracted at the final node.

**Mathematical Proof of Even-Order Cancellation:**

Let the operating points be symmetric: $f_{r1} = f_c + \delta$ and $f_{r2} = f_c - \delta$. Expand the magnitude responses about $f_c$:

$$\vert H_1(f_c + \Delta f) \vert \approx a_0 + a_1 \Delta f + a_2 (\Delta f)^2 + a_3 (\Delta f)^3 + \cdots$$

$$\vert H_2(f_c + \Delta f) \vert \approx a_0 - a_1 \Delta f + a_2 (\Delta f)^2 - a_3 (\Delta f)^3 + \cdots$$

(The sign of odd-order terms flips because the operating point is on opposite slopes.)

**Differential output:**

$$v_o = v_{o1} - v_{o2} = K_d(2 a_1 \Delta f + 2 a_3 (\Delta f)^3 + \cdots)$$

**Conclusion:** All **even-order** terms (the $a_2 (\Delta f)^2$ second-harmonic distortion term) **cancel**, leaving only odd-order distortion. The dominant distortion term is now third-order, which is much smaller than second-order for small $\Delta f$. The differential output also rejects **common-mode AM noise** present on the input FM signal.

#### (b) [7 Marks] An FM signal $s(t) = 10 \cos\!\left[2\pi(10^7)t + 5\sin(2000\pi t)\right]$ is applied to a single-ended slope detector with conversion gain $K_d = 0.1$ mV/Hz. Determine the frequency of the message signal, the peak frequency deviation, and the peak amplitude of the recovered message at the detector output. Assume ideal envelope detection.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the message parameters.**

The standard FM signal form is:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

Comparing: $2\pi f_c t = 2\pi(10^7)t$ → $f_c = 10$ MHz.

$5\sin(2000\pi t) = \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$ → $\beta = 5$ rad, and $2\pi f_m = 2000\pi$ → $f_m = 1000$ Hz = **1 kHz**.

**Step 2: Peak frequency deviation.**

$$\Delta f_{\max} = \beta \cdot f_m = 5 \times 1000 = 5000 \text{ Hz} = 5 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3: Peak amplitude of recovered message.**

The slope detector output is:

$$v_o(t) = K_d \cdot \Delta f(t) = K_d \cdot \Delta f_{\max} \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

Peak amplitude:

$$V_{o,\text{peak}} = K_d \cdot \Delta f_{\max} = (0.1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 5000 = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $f_m = 1$ kHz, $\Delta f_{\max} = 5$ kHz, $V_{o,\text{peak}} = 0.5$ V.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **frequency deviation** $\Delta f$ with **phase deviation** $\beta$. Remember: $\Delta f = \beta \cdot f_m$ (in Hz), and the units of $\beta$ are **radians**, not Hz. Also, $K_d$ must always be multiplied by the input carrier amplitude if it is given as a *normalized* value — in this problem it is given in absolute V/Hz, so direct multiplication is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Slope Detectors (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Slope detector = FM-to-AM converter + envelope detector.
- **Off-resonance tuning is mandatory:** $f_c$ must lie on the *slope* of the resonance curve, not at the peak.
- **Resonant frequency:** $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
- **Q factor:** $Q = \omega_r R C = R/(\omega_r L)$.
- **Magnitude response:** $\vert H(f) \vert = 1 / \sqrt{1 + Q^2(f/f_r - f_r/f)^2}$.
- **Conversion gain:** $K_d = d\vert H\vert/df$ evaluated at $f_c$, in V/Hz.
- **Single-ended:** one tank, simple, but poor AM rejection and high distortion.
- **Balanced:** two tanks (one above, one below $f_c$), even-order distortion cancelled, common-mode AM noise rejected.
- **Distortion source:** Second-order term $\tfrac{1}{2}K_d^{(2)} (\Delta f)^2$ in the Taylor series of $\vert H(f)\vert$.
- **Limitation:** Cannot handle large frequency deviation — unsuitable for wideband commercial FM.
- **Limiter must precede the slope detector** to remove input AM noise.
- **Output voltage:** $v_o(t) = K_d \cdot \Delta f(t) = K_d K_f m(t)$ (linear approximation).
- **Best operating point:** At the **inflection point** of the resonance curve, located approximately $f_r \pm f_r/(2Q)$ from resonance.
- **Real-world use:** Largely obsolete in broadcast FM; still used in low-cost telemetry and educational kits.
- **Comparison points to memorize for viva:**
  - vs **Foster-Seeley**: more distortion, simpler circuit.
  - vs **Ratio detector**: no built-in limiter, poorer AM rejection.
  - vs **PLL demodulator**: no active phase tracking, purely passive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for the exam hall:**
> *"A slope detector is the simplest FM demodulator — it leans a resonant tank off-carrier, lets the frequency variations ride up and down the slope as amplitude variations, and lets a diode envelope detector harvest the message — but it pays for its simplicity with poor linearity and no built-in AM rejection."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radio Transmitters – AM and FM Transmitter Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radio Transmitters – AM and FM Transmitter Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Radio Transmitter?

A **Radio Transmitter** is a collection of electronic circuits and subsystems that converts a low-frequency (audio/baseband) information signal into a modulated Radio Frequency (RF) signal suitable for radiation into free space via an antenna. In the context of KTU's Communication Techniques syllabus, a transmitter is the *front-end* of any wireless link — it performs three essential jobs:

1. **Generation** of a stable high-frequency carrier wave.
2. **Modulation** — impressing the baseband (audio/data) onto the carrier.
3. **Power Amplification & Radiation** — boosting the modulated signal to a level that can reach the receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "A radio transmitter is an electronic device that, with the aid of an antenna, transmits radiofrequency waves carrying information. It generates a carrier wave, modulates it using the message signal, amplifies it, and feeds it to the transmitting antenna."

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Loudspeaker on a Stick"

Imagine you are standing on a stage in a huge stadium and need to tell a story to 50,000 people. Your voice (the **baseband signal**) is weak — it cannot travel far. So you do the following:

| Stage | Real-Life Action | Transmitter Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| You shout into a microphone | Convert voice → small electrical signal | **Microphone / Audio Amplifier** |
| Microphone feeds a loudspeaker | Electrical signal → powerful sound | **Modulator + RF Power Amplifier** |
| Loudspeaker throws sound into the stadium | Distribute over long distance | **Transmitting Antenna** |

Now replace "loudspeaker" with "antenna," "sound" with "radio waves," and the "amplifier+speaker" with a **modulator + RF power amplifier**. That is, in essence, what every AM and FM transmitter does — it *throws* an information-bearing electromagnetic wave over a long distance.

### 1.3 AM vs FM — The Two Big Families

- **AM Transmitter** → varies the **Amplitude** of the carrier in step with the message; the frequency stays constant.
- **FM Transmitter** → varies the **Frequency** of the carrier in step with the message; the amplitude stays constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEBMT751 Module 3):**
> Students must master the block-level architecture of:
> (a) **AM transmitters** — both *High-Level* and *Low-Level* modulated designs.
> (b) **FM transmitters** — both *Direct* (Reactance / Varactor) and *Indirect / Armstrong* systems.
> The emphasis is on functional blocks, frequency up-conversion, and power efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal Block Diagram of a Radio Transmitter

Regardless of AM or FM, every transmitter is built from the following functional blocks:

1. **Carrier Oscillator (Signal Generator):** Produces a stable sinusoidal carrier at frequency $f_c$ (typically with a crystal oscillator for frequency stability).
2. **Buffer Amplifier:** Isolates the oscillator from the modulator to prevent *load pulling* (frequency shift due to loading).
3. **Modulator:** The heart of the transmitter. Impresses the message signal $m(t)$ onto the carrier.
4. **Frequency Multipliers / Mixers (Up-converters):** Step the modulated signal up to the final transmission frequency (very important in FM because direct generation at high frequencies is unstable).
5. **RF Power Amplifier (PA):** Boosts the modulated signal to antenna-level power (watts to kilowatts).
6. **Antenna Coupling Network / Matching Network:** Impedance matches the PA output to the antenna (typically 50 Ω).
7. **Transmitting Antenna:** Converts the guided RF current into a radiated electromagnetic wave.

### 2.2 AM Transmitter — Detailed Block Analysis

A standard AM transmitter (especially for broadcast band 540 kHz – 1700 kHz) consists of:

**Block Sequence:**
- **RF Carrier Oscillator (Crystal-controlled)** → produces $f_c$ at a low, stable frequency.
- **Buffer Amplifier** → isolation.
- **Audio Amplifier** → boosts microphone signal $m(t)$.
- **Modulator Stage (at low power level)** → mixes audio and carrier.
- **Frequency Multiplier Chain (if needed)** → multiplies $f_c$ to broadcast band.
- **Driver / Pre-driver PA** → intermediate amplification.
- **Final RF Power Amplifier (Class C)** → delivers antenna-level power.
- **Antenna Tuner + Antenna.**

#### 2.2.1 High-Level vs Low-Level AM Modulation

| Feature | Low-Level Modulation | High-Level Modulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Where modulation occurs** | At low power stage (right after modulator) | At the final RF Power Amplifier stage |
| **Modulated PA** | Modulator output is *amplified* later | The PA *itself* is the modulator |
| **Efficiency** | Lower (linear Class A/B PA needed to preserve envelope) | **Higher** (often uses Class C PA after the modulation is done) |
| **Power-handling** | Modulator handles small power | Modulator must handle antenna-level power (uses a *modulation transformer*) |
| **KTU usage** | Typically lower-power systems | **Standard broadcast AM transmitters** |

### 2.3 FM Transmitter — Detailed Block Analysis

FM transmitters are more complex because the *frequency* of the oscillator must be varied. There are two fundamental approaches:

#### 2.3.1 Direct FM Transmitter

The frequency of the carrier oscillator is directly varied by the modulating signal using a voltage-controlled device such as a **Reactance Modulator** or a **Varactor Diode** placed in the tank circuit of an LC oscillator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactance Modulator:** A transistor circuit whose equivalent impedance appears **capacitive or inductive** and whose magnitude is varied by the modulating voltage. This effectively changes the resonant frequency $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$.

#### 2.3.2 Indirect FM (Armstrong) Transmitter

Because direct FM suffers from poor frequency stability (the carrier drifts), **Edwin H. Armstrong** in 1936 proposed an elegant scheme:

1. Generate an **NBFM (Narrowband FM)** signal using a **Phase Modulator** — at a low carrier frequency, this is stable.
2. Convert the NBFM to **WBFM (Wideband FM)** by passing it through **Frequency Multipliers** (×2, ×3, etc.) — these multiply both the carrier frequency $f_c$ and the frequency deviation $\Delta f$.
3. Use **Mixers (Down-converters)** if necessary to bring the carrier to its final transmission value while *preserving* the multiplied deviation.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarises every equation that KTU examiners love to ask. Note the use of `\vert` for absolute values to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard AM signal | $s(t)=A_c\left[1+\mu\cos(2\pi f_m t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $\mu$ = modulation index (0 → 1 for no over-modulation) |
| 2 | Modulation index (AM) | $\mu=\dfrac{A_m}{A_c}=\dfrac{V_{\max}-V_{\min}}{V_{\max}+V_{\min}}$ | Dimensionless; also called *depth of modulation* |
| 3 | Carrier power | $P_c=\dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ | $R$ = antenna resistance, usually 50 Ω |
| 4 | Total AM sideband power | $P_{\text{SB}}=\dfrac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R}=\dfrac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}$ | Distributed equally in USB and LSB |
| 5 | Total AM transmitted power | $P_t=P_c\left(1+\dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | **KTU classic** for sinusoidal modulation |
| 6 | Transmission efficiency | $\eta=\dfrac{P_{\text{SB}}}{P_t}=\dfrac{\mu^2}{2+\mu^2}$ | $\eta_{\max}=1/3$ at $\mu=1$ (only 33 % of power carries info!) |
| 7 | AM Bandwidth | $BW_{\text{AM}}=2f_{m(\max)}$ | Same for DSB-SC, DSB-FC, and SSB uses $f_{m(\max)}$ |
| 8 | FM instantaneous frequency | $f_i(t)=f_c+k_f\,m(t)$ | $k_f$ = frequency sensitivity (Hz/volt) |
| 9 | Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = k_f \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}$ | Maximum swing of instantaneous freq. |
| 10 | FM modulation index | $\beta=\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | Dimensionless; $\beta \gg 1$ ⇒ WBFM |
| 11 | Single-tone FM | $s(t)=A_c\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t+\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$ | $\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)$ = phase deviation |
| 12 | Carson's Rule (FM BW) | $BW_{\text{FM}}=2(\Delta f+f_{m(\max)})=2f_{m(\max)}(\beta+1)$ | Captures ≥ 98 % of total FM power |
| 13 | Frequency Multiplier effect | $f_c^{\,\text{out}}=n f_c^{\,\text{in}}, \quad \Delta f^{\,\text{out}}=n \Delta f^{\,\text{in}}$ | $n$ = multiplication factor |
| 14 | Mixer (down-conversion) | $f_c^{\,\text{out}}=\vert f_{\text{LO}}-f_c^{\,\text{in}}\vert$ | Carrier shifts, deviation *unchanged* |
| 15 | Reactance Modulator eq. reactance | $X_{\text{eq}}=\dfrac{g_m}{\omega C}\;\text{or}\;\dfrac{\omega L}{g_m}$ | $g_m$ = transconductance of FET/BJT |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **AM Broadcast Transmitters:** Medium-wave broadcast (MW), short-wave broadcast, and aeronautical NDBs use the *high-level modulated Class-C* design because of its efficiency. Worldwide, AM broadcast still uses hundreds of kW transmitters.
- **FM Broadcast Transmitters (88–108 MHz):** Use **Armstrong indirect** or **direct varactor** designs, with $\Delta f = 75$ kHz (FCC standard) and $\beta \approx 5$ for audio of $f_m = 15$ kHz.
- **Mobile Phones (4G/5G):** Modern transmitters are essentially highly integrated ICs, but the *architectural principles* — oscillator, modulator, up-conversion, PA — remain identical to those taught at KTU level.
- **Two-way Radios (Walkie-Talkie, Police):** Use **NBFM** ($\beta < 1$) generated by Armstrong indirect method — small deviation, narrow bandwidth, and good noise immunity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the AM Signal and Power Relations

**Step 1 — The baseband signal.** Let the message be a single-tone sinusoid:

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2 — The carrier.** Generated by a crystal oscillator:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad f_c \gg f_m$$

**Step 3 — Amplitude modulation** multiplies the carrier by $[1 + k_a m(t)]$, where $k_a$ is the amplitude sensitivity of the modulator:

$$s(t) = A_c [1 + k_a m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 4 — Define modulation index.** To keep the envelope non-negative, $k_a A_m \le 1$. Define $\mu = k_a A_m$:

$$s(t) = A_c [1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 5 — Expand into carrier and sidebands** using the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B)+\cos(A+B)]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \tfrac{A_c\mu}{2}\cos(2\pi(f_c-f_m)t) + \tfrac{A_c\mu}{2}\cos(2\pi(f_c+f_m)t) \\
s(t) &= \underbrace{A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)}_{\text{Carrier (USB+LSB at }f_c)} + \underbrace{\tfrac{A_c\mu}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c-f_m)t\big)}_{\text{LSB}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{A_c\mu}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c+f_m)t\big)}_{\text{USB}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute total transmitted power** into a load $R$ (assume $R=1$ for simplicity, then re-introduce):

$$P_t = \langle s^2(t) \rangle = \underbrace{\tfrac{A_c^2}{2R}}_{P_c} + 2 \cdot \underbrace{\tfrac{A_c^2\mu^2}{8R}}_{P_{\text{USB}}=P_{\text{LSB}}}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_t = P_c\!\left(1 + \tfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)\,}$$

This shows the **KTU gem**: even at 100 % modulation ($\mu = 1$), only **one-third** of the total transmitted power carries information (the two sidebands); two-thirds is wasted in the carrier.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the FM Signal Equation and Carson's Rule

**Step 1 — Frequency modulation principle.** Let the instantaneous frequency be a linear function of $m(t)$:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f\, m(t)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate to get phase.** Since frequency is the time derivative of phase (divided by $2\pi$):

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi \!\int_0^t f_i(\tau)\, d\tau = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — FM signal form.** Substitute back into $A_c\cos\theta_i(t)$:

$$\boxed{\,s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]\,}$$

**Step 4 — Single-tone modulation.** With $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the integral is $\frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)$. Substituting:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\Big(2\pi f_c t + \underbrace{\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)}_{\text{phase deviation}}\Big)$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ — this is the **FM modulation index**.

**Step 5 — Bandwidth via Bessel function expansion.** Expanding using the Bessel identity $\cos(\beta\sin\theta) = J_0(\beta) + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}J_{2n}(\beta)\cos(2n\theta)$ and $\sin(\beta\sin\theta) = 2\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}J_{2n+1}(\beta)\sin((2n+1)\theta)$, the FM spectrum contains infinite sidebands spaced at $f_m$ with amplitudes $J_n(\beta)$.

**Step 6 — Carson's empirical rule.** Discarding negligible sidebands ($|J_n(\beta)| < 0.04$), the number of significant sidebands is approximately $(\beta + 1)$ on each side, giving:

$$\boxed{\,BW_{\text{Carson}} = 2(\beta+1)f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carson's rule vs Bessel:** Carson's rule is an *engineering approximation* that captures ≈ 98 % of the total power. The exact answer uses $J_n(\beta)$ sidebands where $|J_n| \ge 0.04$. For exam purposes, KTU accepts Carson's rule unless otherwise specified.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Matrix (Analytical Practice)

The following mini-matrix trains you to use the formulas in SECTION 2.4. Each row is a typical KTU 2024 calculation.

| Problem | Given | Find | Step-by-step | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3.3.1 AM modulation depth | $V_{\max}=18$ V, $V_{\min}=2$ V | $\mu$ | $\mu=\frac{18-2}{18+2}=\frac{16}{20}$ | $\mu = 0.8$ |
| 3.3.2 AM carrier power | $A_c=20$ V, $R=50$ Ω | $P_c$ | $P_c=\frac{20^2}{2\cdot 50}$ | $P_c = 4$ W |
| 3.3.3 Total AM power | $P_c=4$ W, $\mu=0.8$ | $P_t$ | $P_t=4(1+0.8^2/2)$ | $P_t = 5.28$ W |
| 3.3.4 AM efficiency | $\mu=0.8$ | $\eta$ | $\eta=0.64/(2+0.64)$ | $\eta \approx 24.2$ % |
| 3.3.5 FM deviation | $k_f = 25$ kHz/V, $A_m = 4$ V | $\Delta f$ | $\Delta f = 25 \times 4$ | $\Delta f = 100$ kHz |
| 3.3.6 FM modulation index | $\Delta f = 100$ kHz, $f_m = 10$ kHz | $\beta$ | $\beta = 100/10$ | $\beta = 10$ (WBFM) |
| 3.3.7 Carson's BW | $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m = 15$ kHz | $BW$ | $BW = 2(75+15)$ | $BW = 180$ kHz |
| 3.3.8 Frequency doubler effect | $f_c = 5$ MHz, $\Delta f = 1$ kHz | After ×6 | $f_c' = 30$ MHz, $\Delta f' = 6$ kHz | $\beta' = 6\beta$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define modulation index for an AM signal. An AM transmitter radiates 10 kW of carrier power and 2 kW of sideband power. Calculate the modulation index.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The modulation index $\mu$ of an AM signal is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the modulating signal $A_m$ to the peak amplitude of the carrier $A_c$:
> $$\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$$
> It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates the depth of modulation and must satisfy $\mu \le 1$ to prevent envelope distortion (over-modulation).
>
> **Calculation:**
> Given $P_c = 10$ kW, $P_{\text{SB}} = 2$ kW, the relation $P_{\text{SB}} = \mu^2 P_c / 2$ gives:
> $$\mu = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_{\text{SB}}}{P_c}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2}{10}} = \sqrt{0.4} \approx 0.632$$
> Hence $\mu \approx 0.632$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State Carson's rule for the bandwidth of an FM signal. A commercial FM broadcast station has $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and audio frequency range of 15 kHz. Find the bandwidth and modulation index.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Carson's rule** states that the approximate bandwidth of an FM signal is given by:
> $$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_{m(\max)})$$
> This rule ensures that approximately 98 % of the total FM signal power is contained within the specified bandwidth.
>
> **Calculation:**
> - Modulation index $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_{m(\max)}} = \dfrac{75}{15} = 5$
> - Bandwidth $BW = 2(75 + 15) = 180$ kHz
>
> Hence $\beta = 5$ and $BW = 180$ kHz. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **high-level AM broadcast transmitter**. Compare it with a low-level AM transmitter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the total transmitted power and the transmission efficiency of an AM wave. A certain AM transmitter radiates 20 kW of power when the carrier is unmodulated, and 22.5 kW when the carrier is sinusoidally modulated. Find the modulation index. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Block diagram (recall SECTION 4.2):** Crystal Oscillator → Buffer → RF Driver → Modulation Transformer (primary) ← Audio Amplifier Chain → Modulated Final Class-C PA → Harmonic Filter → Antenna Tuner → Antenna Tower.

**Working Principle:**
1. The **crystal oscillator** produces a stable low-power carrier at the desired broadcast frequency $f_c$.
2. The **buffer amplifier** prevents load variations on the oscillator from altering its frequency.
3. The **RF driver** provides a low-level carrier feed to the primary of the **modulation transformer**.
4. Simultaneously, the **audio chain** (mic → pre-amp → audio driver → audio PA) amplifies the modulating signal and applies it to the **secondary** of the modulation transformer.
5. The **modulated output** is developed across the secondary, varying the **collector supply voltage of the final Class-C PA** in step with the audio. This superimposes the audio envelope on the RF, producing a high-power modulated AM wave.
6. The **harmonic filter** removes harmonic distortion products from the Class-C PA; the **antenna tuner** matches impedance to the **antenna tower**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | High-Level AM | Low-Level AM |
|---|---|---|
| Where modulation occurs | Final PA (high power) | Modulator (low power) |
| PA type | Class C (efficient, but linear envelope needed) | Class A or AB (linear) |
| Modulation transformer required | Yes (handles full power) | No |
| Overall efficiency | High (≈ 70 %) | Lower (30–40 %) |
| Use case | Broadcast AM | Low-power two-way / lab systems |

**(7 marks – 2 marks block diagram, 3 marks working, 2 marks comparison)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Derivation of Total Power:**
Start from the single-tone AM signal:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \tfrac{A_c \mu}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c-f_m)t\big) + \tfrac{A_c \mu}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c+f_m)t\big)$$

The mean square value across resistance $R$ is the sum of mean squares of orthogonal sinusoids:

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle s^2(t) \rangle &= \left(\dfrac{A_c}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 \dfrac{1}{R} + 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_c\mu}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 \dfrac{1}{R} \\
P_t &= \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R} + \dfrac{A_c^2 \mu^2}{4R} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right) \\
P_t &= P_c \!\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation of Transmission Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{SB}}}{P_t} = \dfrac{\tfrac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}}{P_c\!\left(1+\tfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)} = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$$

Maximum efficiency at $\mu = 1$: $\eta_{\max} = 1/3 \approx 33.33$ %.

**Numerical:**
Given $P_c = 20$ kW (unmodulated) and $P_t = 22.5$ kW (modulated):

$$P_t = P_c\!\left(1+\dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right) \implies 22.5 = 20\!\left(1+\dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{\mu^2}{2} = \dfrac{22.5}{20} - 1 = 0.125 \implies \mu^2 = 0.25 \implies \mu = 0.5$$

**(7 marks – 3 marks derivation, 2 marks efficiency formula, 2 marks numerical)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of the **Armstrong indirect FM transmitter system**. Why is frequency multiplication needed in this system? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an FM system, the frequency deviation is 30 kHz when the modulating signal amplitude is 4 V and its frequency is 5 kHz. The carrier frequency is 100 MHz. Find: (i) frequency sensitivity $k_f$, (ii) modulation index, (iii) Carson's bandwidth, (iv) the new modulation index if the signal is passed through a frequency tripler. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Why indirect FM?**
A direct FM modulator (varactor/reactance) suffers from poor carrier frequency stability because the message perturbs the resonant tank. Armstrong's indirect method **separates frequency stability from deviation generation**: it generates a **NBFM** at a stable low carrier using a **phase modulator** (which is just an AM balanced modulator fed by a phase-shifted carrier), and then uses **frequency multipliers** to amplify the deviation.

**Block Diagram (recall SECTION 4.4):**
Crystal Oscillator → Buffer → 90° Phase Shift Network ← Audio Amplifier → Balanced Modulator → NBFM Output → Frequency Multiplier Chain ($\times n_1$) → Mixer with Local Oscillator → Frequency Multiplier Chain ($\times n_2$) → Final RF PA → Bandpass Filter → Antenna.

**Working Principle:**
1. The crystal oscillator generates a stable carrier $f_c$.
2. The carrier is split into two paths: one direct, one phase-shifted by 90°.
3. The 90° path is amplitude-modulated by the audio via a balanced modulator (which suppresses the carrier).
4. The direct and DSB-SC paths are summed to produce a **NBFM** at low $f_c$.
5. A frequency multiplier ($\times n_1$) raises both $f_c$ and $\Delta f$ by $n_1$.
6. A mixer with a local oscillator shifts the carrier frequency **without altering the deviation**.
7. Another multiplier ($\times n_2$) brings the deviation to its final value (e.g., 75 kHz for broadcast FM).
8. The final PA amplifies, the filter cleans the spectrum, and the antenna radiates.

**Why frequency multiplication is needed:**
- A phase modulator is inherently linear but produces only small phase deviation → small $\Delta f$.
- $\Delta f$ must reach 75 kHz for FM broadcast; multiplying by a chain of doublers/triplers simultaneously raises $f_c$ to the broadcast band (88–108 MHz) and $\Delta f$ to the broadcast deviation. **It is the trick that converts a small, stable NBFM into a wideband, stable WBFM.**

**(7 marks – 3 marks block diagram, 3 marks working, 1 mark justification)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

Given: $\Delta f = 30$ kHz, $A_m = 4$ V, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $f_c = 100$ MHz.

**(i) Frequency sensitivity:**

$$k_f = \dfrac{\Delta f}{A_m} = \dfrac{30\,\text{kHz}}{4\,\text{V}} = 7.5\,\text{kHz/V}$$

**(ii) Modulation index:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{30}{5} = 6 \;\; (\text{WBFM})$$

**(iii) Carson's bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(30 + 5) = 70\,\text{kHz}$$

**(iv) New modulation index after a frequency tripler ($n = 3$):**
A frequency multiplier scales both $f_c$ and $\Delta f$ by $n$, leaving $f_m$ unchanged:

$$\Delta f_{\text{new}} = n \cdot \Delta f = 3 \times 30 = 90\,\text{kHz}$$

$$\beta_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\Delta f_{\text{new}}}{f_m} = \dfrac{90}{5} = 18$$

**(7 marks – 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 marks split across the four parts)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $\mu$ (AM) with $\beta$ (FM).** Students often use AM formulas on FM problems. $\mu \le 1$ always; $\beta$ can be much greater than 1.
> 2. **Always specify $f_{m(\max)}$** when applying Carson's rule. Writing $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ without stating "for the *highest* modulating frequency" loses a mark.
> 3. **Frequency multipliers amplify BOTH the carrier and the deviation** by the same factor $n$. Mixers shift the carrier but **leave deviation unchanged**. Students routinely forget this and mix up the two blocks.
> 4. **AM transmission efficiency is at most 1/3**, not 1. Many answers mistakenly give "100 %" or "50 %."
> 5. **High-level modulation uses a Class-C PA**, but the modulated envelope must remain linear. Do *not* state "Class-C is linear" — it is not, but the modulation transformer trick preserves envelope linearity at the antenna.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Radio Transmitter = Oscillator + Modulator + RF PA + Antenna.** This chain is universal for AM and FM.
- **AM Transmitter:** Modulates the *amplitude* of the carrier. Spectrum has carrier + LSB + USB. **Bandwidth = $2f_{m(\max)}$.**
- **Low-Level AM:** Modulation done at small power → linear PA later → less efficient.
- **High-Level AM:** Modulation done at final Class-C PA via modulation transformer → most efficient → standard for AM broadcast.
- **AM total power:** $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$. **AM efficiency:** $\eta = \mu^2/(2+\mu^2)$, $\eta_{\max} = 1/3$.
- **Modulation index (AM):** $\mu = A_m/A_c = (V_{\max}-V_{\min})/(V_{\max}+V_{\min})$; range **0 to 1**.
- **FM Transmitter:** Modulates the *frequency* of the carrier. **Amplitude is constant.**
- **Direct FM:** Uses a varactor diode or reactance modulator to perturb the VCO tank. Simple but suffers from carrier drift.
- **Indirect (Armstrong) FM:** Generates stable NBFM via phase modulator → multiplies deviation by $\times n$ stages → produces WBFM. Standard for broadcast.
- **Frequency Multiplier:** Multiplies both $f_c$ and $\Delta f$ by $n$. **Mixer:** Shifts $f_c$ only; $\Delta f$ unchanged.
- **Frequency Deviation (FM):** $\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m$, in **Hz**.
- **Modulation Index (FM):** $\beta = \Delta f/f_m$; can be $> 1$ (WBFM) or $< 1$ (NBFM).
- **Carson's Rule:** $BW_{\text{FM}} = 2(\Delta f + f_{m(\max)}) = 2f_{m(\max)}(\beta + 1)$. Captures ~98 % of signal power.
- **Standard FM Broadcast:** $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_{m(\max)} = 15$ kHz, $BW = 180$ kHz, $\beta = 5$.
- **Reactance Modulator:** Presents a voltage-variable reactance (C or L) to the VCO tank → varies resonant frequency → produces FM.
- **Balanced Modulator (in Armstrong):** Generates DSB-SC by suppressing the carrier; combining with 90°-shifted direct carrier yields NBFM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Receivers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Communication Receivers

# Communication Receivers — Module 3 Study Notes

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Communication Receiver** is an electronic device or subsystem that accepts a weak, modulated Radio Frequency (RF) signal picked up by an antenna, processes it through a chain of amplification, frequency translation, and demodulation stages, and reproduces the original information (audio, video, or data) at its output. The receiver must accomplish three primary functions: **reception** (capturing the signal), **selection** (isolating the desired station from others), and **reproduction** (faithfully recovering the modulating signal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**
> The receiver portion of the syllabus focuses on the **Superheterodyne architecture**, the working of the **Mixer / Frequency Changer**, the concept of **Image Frequency**, **Intermediate Frequency (IF)** selection, and **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)**. Mastery of these topics is mandatory for the 14-mark analytical questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Post-Office Box"

Imagine the receiver as a **post-office in a noisy city**:
- The **antenna** is the main door — it accepts ALL letters (radio waves) flying through the air, useful and useless alike.
- The **RF Amplifier & Tuned Circuits** act like the **mail-sorting clerk** who looks at the address (frequency) and keeps only the letters from your friend (the desired station).
- The **Local Oscillator + Mixer** are like the **translator** who converts every foreign letter into a **standardized internal language** (the Intermediate Frequency), so the rest of the office can process them quickly and uniformly.
- The **Detector** is the **opener of the envelope** — it finally reads the content of the message.
- The **Audio Amplifier** is the **megaphone** that makes the message loud enough for you to hear.

Just as a post-office cannot deliver if the sorting clerk gets confused, a receiver cannot work if its frequency selection is poor — this gives us the concepts of **selectivity**, **sensitivity**, and **fidelity**.

### 1.3 Receiver Performance Metrics (KTU Standard Parameters)

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU receiver question is fundamentally a question about one of these three metrics. Memorize the definitions precisely.

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Selectivity** | $S$ | Ability to reject adjacent unwanted channels. |
| **Sensitivity** | $\Sigma$ | Ability to detect and amplify very weak input signals (usually in $\mu$V). |
| **Fidelity** | $F$ | Ability to reproduce all modulating frequencies with equal gain. |
| **Image Rejection Ratio** | $\alpha$ | Measure of how well the receiver rejects the image frequency. |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $\frac{S}{N}$ | Ratio of desired signal power to noise power at output. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Receivers

**(A) Tuned Radio Frequency (TRF) Receiver** — *The Old Way*
- Uses multiple RF amplifier stages, each stage tuned to the incoming signal frequency.
- Drawback: All tuned circuits must track together across the band — extremely difficult to align.
- Drawback: Bandwidth varies with frequency, causing non-uniform selectivity.
- **Verdict**: Obsolete; appears in KTU only as a contrast to the superheterodyne.

**(B) Superheterodyne Receiver** — *The Universal Standard*
- Patented by **Edwin Armstrong (1918)**. Almost every radio, TV, and radar uses this principle.
- The heart of the idea: convert EVERY incoming RF signal to a **fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF)**, then do all the heavy amplification and filtering at that constant IF.
- Result: Easy tuning, uniform selectivity, high gain, and excellent image rejection.

### 2.2 Block Diagram of the Superheterodyne Receiver

The signal flow is:
**Antenna → RF Amp → Mixer ← Local Oscillator → IF Amp → Detector → AF Amp → Loudspeaker**

- **RF Amplifier (Pre-selector)**: Provides initial gain and preliminary selectivity (rejects the image frequency as much as possible).
- **Local Oscillator (LO)**: Generates a sinusoid whose frequency is mechanically/electronically ganged to the RF tuning.
- **Mixer (Frequency Changer)**: A non-linear device that multiplies RF and LO signals, producing sum and difference frequencies. The **difference** is selected as the IF.
- **IF Amplifier**: A cascade of fixed-tuned amplifiers centered at $f_{IF}$. Provides most of the receiver's gain and adjacent-channel selectivity.
- **Detector (Demodulator)**: Recovers the original baseband signal from the IF modulated wave.
- **Audio Amplifier**: Boosts the baseband signal to drive a speaker.
- **AGC**: Samples the IF output, rectifies it, and feeds a DC control voltage back to the RF and IF stages to keep output amplitude constant.

### 2.3 The Superheterodyne Principle — Frequency Conversion

Let the incoming RF signal frequency be $f_s$ and the LO frequency be $f_{LO}$. After the mixer:

$$
f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert
$$

For **high-side injection** (the standard in broadcast AM):

$$
f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}
$$

The image frequency $f_{si}$ is the *unwanted* signal that, when mixed with the LO, also produces $f_{IF}$:

$$
f_{si} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **complete formula set** that KTU examiners expect you to reproduce. Every equation below has appeared in past University Examinations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ | Intermediate Frequency | **Hz** or **kHz** |
| 2 | $f_{si} = f_s + 2f_{IF}$ | Image Frequency (high-side LO) | **Hz** |
| 3 | $f_{si} = f_s - 2f_{IF}$ | Image Frequency (low-side LO) | **Hz** |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{f_c}{BW}$ | Quality factor of tuned circuit | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\rho = \dfrac{f_{si}}{f_s} - \dfrac{f_s}{f_{si}}$ | Frequency ratio for image rejection | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\alpha_{image} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}$ | Image Rejection Ratio (IRR) | ratio (or dB) |
| 7 | $\alpha_{image}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\big(\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}\big)$ | IRR in decibels | **dB** |
| 8 | $V_{o\,AGC} = k \cdot V_{IF(out)}$ | AGC control voltage equation | **Volts** |
| 9 | $f_{image} - f_s = 2 f_{IF}$ | Image-to-signal spacing | **Hz** |
| 10 | $S_{\text{adj}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{desired}}{P_{adjacent}}\right)$ | Adjacent-channel selectivity | **dB** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule for KTU:** When substituting numerical values into $\rho$, always compute the two fractions **separately first** to avoid decimal errors. Forgetting to convert kHz/MHz consistently is the #1 cause of wrong answers.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

The superheterodyne architecture is the **universal backbone** of:
- **AM broadcast radios** (standard IF = **455 kHz**)
- **FM broadcast radios** (standard IF = **10.7 MHz**)
- **Television receivers** (IF ≈ 38.9 MHz for video, 33.4 MHz for audio)
- **Radar and satellite communication front-ends**
- **Software Defined Radios (SDR)** — the digital equivalent of this block diagram

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Image Frequency

**Statement:** For a superheterodyne receiver with RF signal at $f_s$ and Intermediate Frequency $f_{IF}$, find the image frequency $f_{si}$ for a high-side local oscillator.

**Proof:**

Let the Local Oscillator operate at:

$$
f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} \quad \text{(high-side injection)}
$$

The mixer produces sum and difference outputs. The difference term is selected as the desired IF:

$$
f_{LO} - f_s = f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{desired output at } f_{IF} \;\checkmark
$$

Now consider a different input signal at frequency $f_{si}$ (the image). It is defined as the frequency which, when mixed with the same $f_{LO}$, also produces $f_{IF}$:

$$
f_{LO} - f_{si} = f_{IF}
$$

Substituting $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$:

$$
(f_s + f_{IF}) - f_{si} = f_{IF}
$$

$$
f_s - f_{si} = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{si} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}\;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The image is symmetric to the desired signal about $f_{LO}$. Since both signals map to the same IF, only the **RF pre-selector** (or image-reject mixer) can eliminate the image — which is why RF selectivity before the mixer is critical.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)

**Statement:** For a single-tuned RF pre-selector with quality factor $Q$ tuned to $f_s$, derive the attenuation offered at the image frequency $f_{si}$.

**Proof:**

The voltage transfer function of a parallel-resonant (single-tuned) circuit is:

$$
\dfrac{V_o}{V_i}(f) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\dfrac{f}{f_s} - \dfrac{f_s}{f}\right)^2}}
$$

At resonance $f = f_s$, the denominator is 1 (full gain). At the image $f = f_{si}$, the attenuation is given by the reciprocal:

$$
\alpha_{image} = \dfrac{1}{\vert V_o/V_i \vert_{f=f_{si}}}
= \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\dfrac{f_{si}}{f_s} - \dfrac{f_s}{f_{si}}\right)^2}
$$

Define the **frequency ratio factor**:

$$
\rho \equiv \dfrac{f_{si}}{f_s} - \dfrac{f_s}{f_{si}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha_{image} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}\;}
$$

In decibels:

$$
\alpha_{image}\,(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $Q$ appears here:** A higher $Q$ gives a steeper "skirt" of the tuned circuit, so the image (which lies $2f_{IF}$ away) is rejected more strongly. This is one of the reasons broadcast AM receivers use a **double-tuned RF stage** at the front-end.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (Module 3, 14 marks):**
A superheterodyne AM broadcast receiver is tuned to **1000 kHz**. The local oscillator is set to **1455 kHz**. The RF pre-selector has a quality factor $Q = 50$. Calculate:
1. The Intermediate Frequency.
2. The Image Frequency.
3. The Image Rejection Ratio in dB.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Intermediate Frequency:**

$$
f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_s = 1455 - 1000 = 455 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Image Frequency (high-side LO):**

$$
f_{si} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 1000 + 2(455) = 1910 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\rho$:**

$$
\dfrac{f_{si}}{f_s} = \dfrac{1910}{1000} = 1.910
$$

$$
\dfrac{f_s}{f_{si}} = \dfrac{1000}{1910} \approx 0.5236
$$

$$
\rho = 1.910 - 0.5236 = 1.3864
$$

**Step 4 — Image Rejection Ratio:**

$$
\alpha_{image} = \sqrt{1 + (50)^2 (1.3864)^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1 + 2500 \times 1.9221}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1 + 4805.25} = \sqrt{4806.25}
$$

$$
\alpha_{image} \approx 69.33
$$

**In decibels:**

$$
\alpha_{image}\,(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(69.33) \approx 36.81 \text{ dB}
$$

**Final Answer Block:**

$$
f_{IF} = 455 \text{ kHz}, \quad f_{si} = 1910 \text{ kHz}, \quad \alpha_{image} \approx 36.81 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Notice the spacing $f_{si} - f_s = 2 f_{IF}$. This is constant! If you know the IF, you can find the image by adding $2 f_{IF}$ to the tuned signal frequency — a quick mental check before writing the final answer.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: TRF vs. Superheterodyne

| Performance Aspect | TRF Receiver | Superheterodyne Receiver |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Selectivity | Poor (varies with $f$) | Excellent (fixed IF selectivity) |
| Sensitivity | Low | High |
| Bandwidth | Variable with $f$ | Constant (set by IF filters) |
| Image Rejection | Not applicable | Critical issue, IRR engineered |
| Tuning Complexity | Multi-gang, hard to align | Single-gang LO + fixed IF |
| Adjacent Channel Rejection | Poor | Excellent (ceramic/SAW IF filters) |
| Used in Modern Systems? | No | **Yes — universal** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Superheterodyne Receiver

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal flow, including the AGC feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANT["Antenna"] --> RFA["RF Amplifier\nPre-selector"]
    RFA --> MIX["Mixer\nFrequency Changer"]
    LO["Local Oscillator"] -- "f_LO = f_s + f_IF" --> MIX
    MIX --> IFA["IF Amplifier\nf_IF = 455 kHz"]
    IFA --> DET["Detector\nDemodulator"]
    DET --> AFA["Audio Amplifier"]
    AFA --> SPK["Loudspeaker\nOutput"]
    IFA -. "Sample output" .-> AGC["AGC Rectifier\nand Filter"]
    AGC -. "DC control voltage" .-> RFA
    AGC -. "DC control voltage" .-> IFA
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Image Frequency Generation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPECTRUM["Frequency Domain Spectrum"]
        FS["f_s\nDesired Signal\n1000 kHz"]
        FLO["f_LO\nLocal Oscillator\n1455 kHz"]
        FSI["f_si\nImage Frequency\n1910 kHz"]
    end
    MIXER["MIXER\nNon-linear Device"]
    IF_OUT["IF Stage Output\n455 kHz only"]
    FS --> MIXER
    FLO --> MIXER
    FSI --> MIXER
    MIXER -- "f_LO - f_s = 455 kHz\nDESIRED" --> IF_OUT
    MIXER -- "f_LO - f_si = -455 kHz\nUNWANTED - must be filtered" --> REJECT["Rejected by\nRF Pre-selector"]
```

### 4.3 AGC Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IF_OUT["IF Amplifier Output"] --> AGC_REC["AGC Detector\nHalf-wave Rectifier"]
    AGC_REC --> AGC_FILT["RC Low-pass Filter\nSlow DC Control"]
    AGC_FILT --> VCA["DC Gain Control\nto RF and IF stages"]
    VCA -.-> IF_OUT
    VCA -.-> RFA_NODE["RF Amplifier"]
    FIXED["Output Level remains\nnearly constant"]:::outputStyle
    VCA --> FIXED
    classDef outputStyle fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Frequency-Conversion Flow (How IF is Produced)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RF Input\nf_s = 1000 kHz\nplus Image\nf_si = 1910 kHz"] --> M["Mixer"]
    B["LO Input\nf_LO = 1455 kHz"] --> M
    M --> SUM["Sum:\nf_s + f_LO = 2455 kHz\nf_si + f_LO = 3365 kHz"]
    M --> DIFF["Difference:\nf_LO - f_s = 455 kHz\nf_LO - f_si = -455 kHz\n(abs) 455 kHz"]
    DIFF --> BPF["IF Bandpass Filter\nCenter = 455 kHz"]
    BPF --> OUT["Clean IF at 455 kHz"]
    SUM -. "Rejected" .-> X[End]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams in the exam:** Always label every block with its **function** and its **frequency** (where applicable). Examiners award a **1-mark bonus** for clear, complete block diagrams — never omit block labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
Define the term **Image Frequency** in a superheterodyne receiver. Why does it pose a problem, and how is it suppressed?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The image frequency $f_{si}$ is the unwanted signal frequency that lies **symmetric to the desired signal** $f_s$ about the local oscillator frequency. It is given by $f_{si} = f_s + 2f_{IF}$ (for high-side LO).
It poses a problem because, like the desired signal, it also mixes with $f_{LO}$ to produce the **same IF** ($455\,\text{kHz}$), and therefore appears at the detector output as interference. It is suppressed by a **highly selective RF pre-selector** (high-$Q$ tuned circuit) placed *before* the mixer, often implemented as a **double-tuned or band-pass** stage. The effectiveness of suppression is quantified by the **Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)**.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
What is the function of the **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** circuit in a superheterodyne receiver? List two of its advantages.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The AGC circuit **automatically adjusts the gain** of the RF and IF amplifier stages in inverse proportion to the strength of the received signal, so that the output volume remains nearly **constant** despite large variations in input signal strength (fading, near-far effect, etc.).
**Two advantages:**
1. Prevents overloading of later stages when receiving strong signals.
2. Compensates for **fading** during mobile reception.
*(Additional accepted advantage: Improves dynamic range and protects the detector from distortion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **superheterodyne receiver**. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The superheterodyne receiver converts every incoming RF signal to a fixed **intermediate frequency (IF)** before detection. The block diagram is shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. Functions of each block:

1. **Antenna:** Captures electromagnetic waves and delivers them as an RF voltage to the front-end.
2. **RF Amplifier (Pre-selector):** Provides initial voltage gain and rejects the **image frequency** by virtue of its tuned band-pass response.
3. **Local Oscillator (LO):** Generates a sinusoid $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$, mechanically or electronically ganged to the RF tuning.
4. **Mixer (Frequency Changer):** A non-linear device that combines RF and LO to produce sum and difference frequencies; the difference $f_{IF}$ is selected.
5. **IF Amplifier:** Multi-stage fixed-tuned amplifier at $f_{IF}$ (455 kHz for AM); provides **major gain and adjacent-channel selectivity** via ceramic/SAW/LC filters.
6. **Detector (Demodulator):** Recovers the audio baseband from the IF AM envelope (typically an envelope detector or product detector).
7. **Audio Amplifier:** Boosts the audio signal to drive a speaker.
8. **AGC:** Samples IF output, rectifies it, and uses the resulting DC voltage to control the gain of RF and IF stages.

The advantages are uniform selectivity, high gain, and excellent sensitivity.

---

**(b)** A superheterodyne receiver is tuned to **1200 kHz**. The local oscillator operates at **1655 kHz**, and the RF pre-selector has $Q = 80$. Calculate the **intermediate frequency**, the **image frequency**, and the **image rejection ratio in dB**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Intermediate Frequency:**

$$
f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_s = 1655 - 1200 = 455 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Image Frequency:**

$$
f_{si} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 1200 + 2(455) = 2110 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\rho$:**

$$
\dfrac{f_{si}}{f_s} = \dfrac{2110}{1200} = 1.7583
$$

$$
\dfrac{f_s}{f_{si}} = \dfrac{1200}{2110} = 0.5687
$$

$$
\rho = 1.7583 - 0.5687 = 1.1896
$$

**Step 4 — Image Rejection Ratio:**

$$
\alpha_{image} = \sqrt{1 + (80)^2 (1.1896)^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1 + 6400 \times 1.4152} = \sqrt{1 + 9057.0} = \sqrt{9058.0}
$$

$$
\alpha_{image} \approx 95.17
$$

**In decibels:**

$$
\alpha_{image}\,(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(95.17) \approx 39.57 \text{ dB}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{f_{IF} = 455 \text{ kHz}, \quad f_{si} = 2110 \text{ kHz}, \quad \alpha_{image} \approx 39.57 \text{ dB}}
$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Image Frequency Rejection Ratio (IRR)**. Derive the expression for IRR for a single-tuned RF stage with quality factor $Q$ and signal frequency $f_s$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)** is the ratio of the gain of the RF pre-selector at the desired signal frequency $f_s$ to its gain at the image frequency $f_{si}$. It is a dimensionless ratio (or expressed in dB) and quantifies how effectively the receiver front-end blocks the image.

**Derivation:** For a single-tuned parallel-resonant circuit:

$$
\dfrac{V_o}{V_i}\Big|_{f} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2\left(\dfrac{f}{f_s} - \dfrac{f_s}{f}\right)^2}}
$$

At $f = f_s$ the gain is maximum. At $f = f_{si}$ the gain drops. The IRR is:

$$
\alpha_{image} = \dfrac{(V_o/V_i)_{f_s}}{(V_o/V_i)_{f_{si}}} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\dfrac{f_{si}}{f_s} - \dfrac{f_s}{f_{si}}\right)^2}
$$

Let $\rho = \dfrac{f_{si}}{f_s} - \dfrac{f_s}{f_{si}}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha_{image} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}\;}
$$

In dB: $\alpha_{image}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(\alpha_{image})$.

---

**(b)** An AM superheterodyne receiver is tuned to **600 kHz**. Its IF is **455 kHz** and the LO is on the high side. The RF stage uses a single tuned circuit with $Q = 60$. Compute the **image frequency** and the **image rejection ratio** in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Image Frequency:**

$$
f_{si} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 600 + 2(455) = 600 + 910 = 1510 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\rho$:**

$$
\dfrac{f_{si}}{f_s} = \dfrac{1510}{600} = 2.5167
$$

$$
\dfrac{f_s}{f_{si}} = \dfrac{600}{1510} = 0.3974
$$

$$
\rho = 2.5167 - 0.3974 = 2.1193
$$

**Step 3 — IRR Calculation:**

$$
\alpha_{image} = \sqrt{1 + (60)^2 (2.1193)^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1 + 3600 \times 4.4914} = \sqrt{1 + 16169.0} = \sqrt{16170.0}
$$

$$
\alpha_{image} \approx 127.16
$$

**In decibels:**

$$
\alpha_{image}\,(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(127.16) \approx 42.09 \text{ dB}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{f_{si} = 1510 \text{ kHz}, \quad \alpha_{image} \approx 42.09 \text{ dB}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing!**
> 1. **Mixing high-side vs. low-side LO:** Always state which injection side your $f_{LO}$ is on. Using the wrong $f_{si}$ formula costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Converting kHz and MHz inconsistently in $\rho$ is the most common error. Convert everything to kHz **before** any calculation.
> 3. **Forgetting the 20·log factor:** KTU often asks IRR in dB. Computing the linear ratio and stopping there is treated as **incomplete answer (–2 marks)**.
> 4. **No block diagram for 7-mark theory questions:** A textual explanation *without* a labelled block diagram typically loses 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Communication Receivers**

- **Receiver = Reception + Selection + Reproduction.** Always keep this triad in mind.
- **Two main types:** TRF (obsolete) and **Superheterodyne (universal standard)**.
- **Superheterodyne key stages:** RF Amp → Mixer ← LO → IF Amp → Detector → AF Amp → Speaker, with **AGC feedback** to RF and IF.
- **Intermediate Frequency (IF) for AM broadcast = 455 kHz; for FM = 10.7 MHz; for TV video ≈ 38.9 MHz.**
- **Image frequency (high-side LO):** $f_{si} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$. Image is symmetric to $f_s$ about $f_{LO}$.
- **Image Rejection Ratio:** $\alpha_{image} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}$, where $\rho = f_{si}/f_s - f_s/f_{si}$.
- **dB conversion:** Multiply $20 \log_{10}$ of the linear ratio — **NOT** $10 \log_{10}$ (it is a *voltage* ratio, not a power ratio).
- **Higher $Q$ → better image rejection** because the pre-selector skirt is steeper.
- **Image rejection occurs *before* the mixer** — only the RF stage can reject the image. The IF stage cannot, because both $f_s$ and $f_{si}$ map to the same IF.
- **AGC** keeps output level constant by reducing gain for strong signals and increasing gain for weak signals.
- **Selectivity** is dominated by IF stage; **Sensitivity** is dominated by RF + IF combined gain and front-end noise figure.
- **Tracking error** between LO and RF ganged capacitors must be minimized to keep IF = 455 kHz exactly across the band.
- **Standard exam-trick formulas to memorize:** $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$, $f_{si} = f_s \pm 2f_{IF}$, $\alpha_{image} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}$.
- **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in any 7-mark theory question; it earns easy partial credit even if the rest is incomplete.
- **Practice at least 3 numerical problems on IRR** — KTU repeats this calculation style with varied numerical values every exam cycle.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AM and FM Superheterodyne Receivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** AM and FM Superheterodyne Receivers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Superheterodyne Receiver** is a radio receiver architecture in which the incoming **Radio Frequency (RF)** signal is mixed with a locally generated signal from a **Local Oscillator (LO)** to produce a fixed, lower frequency signal known as the **Intermediate Frequency (IF)**. This frequency translation is achieved using a non-linear device called a **Mixer**, followed by a highly selective **IF Amplifier** operating at the fixed IF. The technique, invented by **Edwin H. Armstrong in 1918**, has become the universal standard for both AM and FM commercial broadcast reception because it provides superior selectivity, sensitivity, and stability compared to the obsolete tuned-radio-frequency (TRF) receiver. For AM broadcast reception, the standard IF is **455 kHz**, while for FM broadcast reception, the standard IF is **10.7 MHz**. The "super-heterodyne" name literally means "above the heterodyne" — referring to the use of a *super* (additional) frequency conversion stage beyond the basic heterodyne principle. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multilingual Translator Booth" Imagine a busy international airport where announcements arrive in many different languages (different RF carrier frequencies) at varying loudness levels. Instead of hiring a different translator for every language and a different sound engineer for every volume, the airport uses a **single, standardized translation booth**: - A **traveler speaking language X** (the incoming RF signal at carrier frequency $f_s$). - A **specialized translator** who converts language X into a common, fixed language Y (the **mixer** + **local oscillator**). - A **single high-quality sound system** tuned perfectly to language Y (the **fixed IF amplifier** at 455 kHz or 10.7 MHz). - A **final output device** that delivers the announcement to your ear (the **demodulator + loudspeaker**). No matter what the original carrier frequency is, by the time the message reaches the final stage, it is always in the same "language" (the IF). The high-quality sound system only needs to be optimized for *one* frequency — that is the genius of the superheterodyne architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between the RF section (variable tuning, selects the station) and the IF section (fixed frequency, provides selectivity and gain). The mixer is the *bridge* between these two worlds. > [!NOTE] > **Historical Note:** Before the superheterodyne receiver, the **TRF (Tuned Radio Frequency) receiver** was used. It required multiple tuned RF stages ganged together — a mechanical nightmare. As the number of tuned stages increased, bandwidth narrowed, but alignment and tracking became practically impossible. The superheterodyne solves this elegantly by converting *all* signals to a single, fixed IF. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (in Bold) - **AM Intermediate Frequency (IF):** **455 kHz** - **FM Intermediate Frequency (IF):** **10.7 MHz** - **AM Broadcast Band:** **540 kHz to 1640 kHz** (medium wave, MW) - **FM Broadcast Band:** **88 MHz to 108 MHz** (VHF Band II) - **Image Frequency (general):** $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ (high-side LO injection) - **Local Oscillator Frequency (AM, high-side LO):** $f_{LO} = f_s + 455 \text{ kHz}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block-Level Operational Concept (Why & How) The superheterodyne receiver operates on a beautiful mathematical property of **non-linear mixing**. When two sinusoids are applied to a non-linear device (or a linear multiplier), the output contains **sum and difference frequencies** in addition to the original frequencies. **Why do we convert to a fixed IF?** 1. **Selectivity at a fixed frequency is much easier** — IF bandpass filters (typically ceramic or mechanical) can be designed with sharp skirts at one specific frequency. 2. **High gain is stable** — IF amplifiers operate at low, fixed frequencies where stray feedback is minimal and gain per stage is high. 3. **Image rejection is manageable** — by choosing an appropriate IF and pre-selector filtering. 4. **Demodulation is simpler** — both AM envelope detectors and FM discriminators work best at a controlled IF level. **How does the signal flow?** - **Step 1 (Antenna + RF Pre-selector):** Picks up the wanted station $f_s$ and rejects strong out-of-band signals. - **Step 2 (RF Amplifier):** Low-noise amplification of the weak RF signal. Often combined with the pre-selector. - **Step 3 (Mixer):** Multiplies the RF signal with the local oscillator signal $f_{LO}$. Output contains $f_{LO} - f_s$ and $f_{LO} + f_s$. - **Step 4 (IF Amplifier + Filter):** Selects the difference frequency (the IF) and rejects the sum and other products. - **Step 5 (Demodulator / Detector):** Extracts the original baseband (audio) information from the IF signal. - **Step 6 (Audio Amplifier + Loudspeaker):** Amplifies the recovered audio to drive the speaker. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All quantities below are **exam-essential**. Memorize the units, the boundary conditions, and the algebraic sign conventions. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Condition | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------| | 1 | Local Oscillator Frequency (high-side LO) | $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ | Hz | Standard for AM broadcast receivers | | 2 | Local Oscillator Frequency (low-side LO) | $f_{LO} = f_s - f_{IF}$ | Hz | Used in some TV/microwave receivers | | 3 | Image Frequency (high-side LO) | $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ | Hz | The "ghost" of $f_s$ on the other side of $f_{LO}$ | | 4 | Image Frequency (low-side LO) | $f_{im} = f_s - 2 f_{IF}$ | Hz | Mirror image when LO is below $f_s$ | | 5 | Intermediate Frequency (AM) | $f_{IF} = 455 \text{ kHz}$ | Hz | Fixed by KTU/global AM broadcast convention | | 6 | Intermediate Frequency (FM) | $f_{IF} = 10.7 \text{ MHz}$ | Hz | Fixed by global FM broadcast convention | | 7 | Image Rejection Ratio (IRR) | $\text{IRR} = \alpha_v = \dfrac{\text{Pre-selector attenuation at } f_{im}}{\text{Pre-selector attenuation at } f_s}$ | dimensionless (dB) | Measure of mirror-signal suppression | | 8 | RF-to-IF conversion | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ | Hz | Output of ideal mixer | | 9 | Conversion Gain (Mixer) | $G_c = \dfrac{P_{IF,\text{out}}}{P_{RF,\text{in}}}$ | dimensionless (dB) | Should ideally be $\geq 1$ (often expressed in dB) | | 10 | TRF selectivity (for comparison) | $BW_{TRF} = \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1} \cdot f_0/Q$ | Hz | $n$ = number of identical tuned stages | | 11 | AGC dynamic range | $DR_{AGC} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{in,\max}}{V_{in,\min}}\right)$ | dB | Receiver remains linear over this input range | | 12 | FM demodulation (slope detector) | $k_d = \dfrac{df}{dv}$ | Hz/Volt | Conversion sensitivity of discriminator | | 13 | De-emphasis time constant (FM) | $\tau = 75 \ \mu\text{s}$ (Americas/Kerala-India) | seconds | Single-pole RC low-pass after discriminator | | 14 | Pre-emphasis time constant (FM) | $\tau = 75 \ \mu\text{s}$ | seconds | Single-pole RC high-pass at transmitter | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The superheterodyne receiver is the **heart of nearly every wireless device** built since 1920: - **AM/FM broadcast radios** (home, car, portable) - **Television receivers** (legacy analog CRT TV, set-top boxes) - **Radar systems** (military, weather, aviation) - **Software Defined Radios (SDR)** use digital equivalents of the same architecture - **Cellular base stations** (multi-stage superheterodyne) - **Satellite communication ground terminals** - **Spectrum analyzers** and **network analyzers** in every RF lab The principle is so fundamental that even modern **fully digital radios** internally emulate the superheterodyne architecture using DSP — proving the timelessness of Armstrong's 1918 invention. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Image Frequency and the Heterodyne Process Let us mathematically derive the image frequency and the principle of frequency translation using a non-linear mixer.
