This is a **favourite KTU derivation** and is often asked for full 14 marks. **Setup:** - Let the incoming RF (signal) be at carrier frequency $f_s$. - Let the local oscillator generate a sinusoid at frequency $f_{LO}$. - The mixer is a non-linear device whose output contains the cross-product (multiplication) of the two inputs. **Step 1: Express the two input signals as pure sinusoids.** $$v_{RF}(t) = V_s \cos(2 \pi f_s t)$$ $$v_{LO}(t) = V_{LO} \cos(2 \pi f_{LO} t)$$ **Step 2: Apply these to an ideal multiplier (the mathematical model of a mixer).** $$v_{out}(t) = v_{RF}(t) \cdot v_{LO}(t)$$ $$v_{out}(t) = V_s \cos(2 \pi f_s t) \cdot V_{LO} \cos(2 \pi f_{LO} t)$$ **Step 3: Use the trigonometric product-to-sum identity** $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$. $$v_{out}(t) = \frac{V_s V_{LO}}{2} \Big[ \cos\big(2 \pi (f_{LO} - f_s) t\big) + \cos\big(2 \pi (f_{LO} + f_s) t\big) \Big]$$ **Step 4: Identify the two output frequency components.** - **Difference frequency (the desired IF):** $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ - **Sum frequency (rejected by IF filter):** $f_{sum} = f_{LO} + f_s$ The IF bandpass filter that follows the mixer selects the difference frequency and rejects the sum. **Step 5: Derive the Local Oscillator frequency** for AM broadcast (high-side LO injection, where $f_{LO} > f_s$). $$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$$ For AM broadcast, $f_{IF} = 455 \text{ kHz}$, so the local oscillator always tracks the incoming station 455 kHz above it. **Step 6: Derive the Image Frequency.** The image frequency is the *unwanted* input frequency that, when mixed with $f_{LO}$, *also* produces the same IF output. We seek $f_{im}$ such that: $$\vert f_{LO} - f_{im} \vert = f_{IF}$$ Using the high-side LO relation $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$: $$f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = (f_s + f_{IF}) + f_{IF}$$ $$\boxed{\,f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}\,}$$ This is the *mirror* of $f_s$ on the other side of $f_{LO}$. It is the classic KTU image-frequency formula. **Step 7: Verify with a numerical example.** Suppose we tune the AM receiver to $f_s = 1000 \text{ kHz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$. Then: - $f_{LO} = 1000 + 455 = 1455 \text{ kHz}$ - $f_{IF} = \vert 1455 - 1000 \vert = 455 \text{ kHz}$ ✓ - $f_{im} = 1000 + 2(455) = 1000 + 910 = 1910 \text{ kHz}$ - Check: $\vert 1455 - 1910 \vert = 455 \text{ kHz}$ ✓ — same IF output! This means a strong station at **1910 kHz** would produce IF interference even though we are tuned to **1000 kHz** — a serious problem solved by the RF pre-selector filter. ### 3.2 Derivation: Image Rejection Ratio (IRR) Let the pre-selector (RF tuned circuit) have voltage transfer ratios $\alpha_s$ at $f_s$ and $\alpha_{im}$ at $f_{im}$. The Image Rejection Ratio in dB is: $$\text{IRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\alpha_s}{\alpha_{im}}\right)$$ For a single-pole tuned circuit with quality factor $Q$ and resonant frequency $f_0$, the voltage gain at an offset $\Delta f$ from resonance is: $$\alpha(f) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\dfrac{2 \Delta f}{f_0}\right)^2}}$$ For the signal: $\Delta f_s = 0$, so $\alpha_s = 1$. For the image: $\Delta f_{im} = 2 f_{IF}$, so: $$\alpha_{im} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\dfrac{4 f_{IF}}{f_s}\right)^2}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\,\text{IRR} = 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\frac{4 f_{IF}}{f_s}\right)^2}\,}$$ **Engineering insight:** To maximize IRR, we either **increase $Q$** of the pre-selector or **decrease $f_{IF}$**. But lowering $f_{IF}$ too much causes adjacent-channel selectivity problems. This trade-off is a classic KTU discussion point. ### 3.3 Derivation: Choice of IF (Trade-off Analysis) We can show that an optimal IF exists by balancing two opposing requirements. - **Image rejection** improves as $f_{IF}$ *increases* (larger spacing from $f_s$). - **Adjacent-channel selectivity** improves as $f_{IF}$ *decreases* (more selective IF filters are practical at lower frequencies, and fractional bandwidth $\Delta f / f_{IF}$ is larger). The empirical AM broadcast choice of **455 kHz** and FM choice of **10.7 MHz** represent engineering compromises refined over decades. For AM: - Lower limit: $f_{IF} > 10 \text{ kHz}$ (audio bandwidth is ~5 kHz, so IF must be comfortably above). - Upper limit: $f_{IF} < 0.5 f_{s,min} = 270 \text{ kHz}$ (so image is outside the band — but 455 kHz works because pre-selector filters are not perfect at the band edge). - The standard **455 kHz** is a long-standing compromise. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of AM vs FM Superheterodyne Receivers | Parameter | AM Superheterodyne | FM Superheterodyne | |-----------|-------------------|-------------------| | RF band | 540–1640 kHz | 88–108 MHz | | RF amplifier | Yes (tuned) | Yes (low-noise VHF) | | Local Oscillator | 995–2095 kHz | 98.7–118.7 MHz | | **IF Frequency** | **455 kHz** | **10.7 MHz** | | IF bandwidth | ~10 kHz | ~200 kHz (wideband) | | IF amplifier stages | 1–2 stages | 2–3 stages (limiter) | | Demodulator | Envelope detector (diode) | FM discriminator (Foster-Seeley, ratio detector, PLL) | | De-emphasis | Not used | 75 μs RC low-pass | | AGC | Yes (AVC) | Yes (limiter removes AM) | | Capture effect | No | Yes (FM inherent) | | Sensitivity (typical) | ~5 μV/m | ~1 μV/m | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: AM Superheterodyne Receiver The diagram below maps the full signal flow of an AM superheterodyne receiver. Note the **ganged tuning capacitor** that mechanically links the RF and LO sections so that $f_{LO} = f_s + 455 \text{ kHz}$ at every station. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Antenna] --> B[RF Pre-selector
Tuned Filter] B --> C[RF Amplifier
LNA] C --> D[Mixer
Non-linear Device] E[Local Oscillator
fLO = fs + 455 kHz] --> D D --> F[IF Bandpass Filter
455 kHz BW 10 kHz] F --> G[IF Amplifier Stage 1] G --> H[IF Amplifier Stage 2
with AGC] H --> I[Envelope Detector
Diode + RC] I --> J[Audio Amplifier] J --> K[Loudspeaker] H --> L[AGC Detector] L --> M[DC Amplifier] M --> H classDef active fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b08000,color:#000 classDef osc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef agc fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#802000,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,F,G,H,I,J,K active class E osc class L,M agc ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: FM Superheterodyne Receiver The FM receiver follows the same superheterodyne topology but uses a **limiter** before the FM discriminator and adds a **de-emphasis** network. The IF is **10.7 MHz**. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Antenna] --> B[FM RF Pre-selector
88 to 108 MHz] B --> C[FM RF Amplifier
VHF Low Noise] C --> D[Mixer] E[Local Oscillator
fLO = fs + 10.7 MHz] --> D D --> F[IF BPF
10.7 MHz BW 200 kHz] F --> G[IF Amp 1] G --> H[IF Amp 2] H --> I[Limiter
Removes AM noise] I --> J[FM Discriminator
Foster Seeley or Ratio Detector] J --> K[De emphasis
75 microsecond RC] K --> L[Audio Amplifier] L --> M[Loudspeaker] G --> N[AFC Feedback] N --> E classDef fmblock fill:#e0f7e0,stroke:#006633,color:#000 classDef lofill fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef deemp fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#804000,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M fmblock class E,N lofill class K deemp ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Frequency Translation Concept This diagram shows *conceptually* how the mixer translates two different RF signals down to the same IF. This is the visual key to understanding image frequency. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[fs = 1000 kHz
Wanted station] --> MIX A2[fs + 2fIF = 1910 kHz
Image of wanted] --> MIX LO[Local Oscillator
fLO = 1455 kHz] --> MIX MIX[Mixer
Non linear] --> IF[IF Output
455 kHz] MIX --> SUM[Sum frequency
2455 kHz
REJECTED] classDef wanted fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef image fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef lo fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef ifout fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef rej fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000 class A1 wanted class A2 image class LO lo class IF ifout class SUM rej ``` ### 4.4 Tracking and the Three-Gang Capacitor For the LO to remain exactly $f_{IF}$ above the RF tuned frequency across the entire band, both tuned circuits must track together. This is achieved with a **ganged (3-section) variable capacitor** plus carefully designed **padding** and **trimming** capacitors. KTU frequently asks about tracking error and how it is corrected — the **padder capacitor** in series with the LO section shifts the high-end of the tracking curve, and **trimmer capacitors** in parallel adjust the high-end alignment. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the term "image frequency" in a superheterodyne receiver. Why is it a problem, and how is it suppressed?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Image frequency is the unwanted RF input frequency $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ (for high-side LO injection) that, when mixed with the local oscillator, produces the same intermediate frequency $f_{IF}$ as the desired signal $f_s$. It is a problem because the IF amplifier cannot distinguish between a genuine IF signal generated from $f_s$ and one generated from $f_{im}$, leading to co-channel interference. It is suppressed primarily by the **RF pre-selector tuned circuit** placed before the mixer, which provides higher attenuation at $f_{im}$ than at $f_s$. Additional suppression is achieved by choosing a sufficiently high IF (so that $f_{im}$ lies far from $f_s$, allowing steeper pre-selector roll-off) and by using double-balanced mixers with high port-to-port isolation. **(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark problem, 1 mark suppression mechanism.)** **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Why is the superheterodyne receiver preferred over the TRF receiver in modern broadcast applications? List three reasons.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Fixed IF allows consistent high selectivity and gain** — IF filters and amplifiers can be optimized for one frequency, avoiding the alignment nightmares of multiple ganged TRF stages. 2. **Better image and spurious rejection** — pre-selector filtering and careful IF choice minimize unwanted mixing products. 3. **Ease of AGC implementation and demodulation** — both AM envelope detection and FM limiting/discrimination are far simpler at a fixed, controlled IF level. **(3 marks: 1 mark each.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Forgetting units on $f_{IF}$:** Students often write $f_{IF} = 455$ without "kHz" — lose 0.5 mark. > - **Image-frequency sign error:** Writing $f_{im} = f_s - 2 f_{IF}$ when the question implies high-side LO — double-check the LO position. > - **Mixer output: only the difference frequency survives** — students often write both sum and difference without saying which is selected by the IF filter. > - **AGC block must be drawn in the AM receiver diagram** — partial marks (often 1–2 marks) are reserved for the AGC feedback loop. > - **In FM receivers, students forget the de-emphasis network and limiter** — both are essential blocks. > - **Numerical mistakes in $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$** — always re-check the arithmetic. --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]** **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an AM superheterodyne receiver. State the function of each block. **(b) [7 marks]** An AM superheterodyne receiver is tuned to a station at 1200 kHz. The local oscillator uses high-side injection. If the IF is 455 kHz, calculate (i) the local oscillator frequency, (ii) the image frequency, and (iii) the IRR (in dB) if the pre-selector Q is 60. #### OR **(Q3 Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 marks]** Draw the block diagram of an FM superheterodyne receiver and explain the function of the **limiter**, **discriminator**, and **de-emphasis** network. Why is de-emphasis used in FM but not in AM? **(b) [7 marks]** A superheterodyne FM receiver tunes to 98.5 MHz. The IF is 10.7 MHz. Find (i) the local oscillator frequency (high-side LO), (ii) the image frequency, and (iii) the frequency difference between image and desired signal. Comment on the role of the pre-selector. --- #### Model Solution — Question 3 (Main Version) **Part (a) — 7 marks** — *AM Superheterodyne Block Diagram & Functions* **Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid block). **Functions of each block:** 1. **Antenna:** Captures the electromagnetic wave and induces a small RF voltage corresponding to all stations in the band. **(0.5 mark)** 2. **RF Pre-selector:** A tuned bandpass filter that selects the wanted station $f_s$ and rejects strong out-of-band signals, especially the image frequency. **(0.5 mark)** 3. **RF Amplifier:** Low-noise amplification of the weak RF signal, often a tuned stage to improve noise figure. **(0.5 mark)** 4. **Local Oscillator:** Generates a stable sinusoid at $f_{LO} = f_s + 455$ kHz, ganged with the RF pre-selector so both track together. **(0.5 mark)** 5. **Mixer:** A non-linear device (or analog multiplier) that multiplies the RF signal with the LO signal. Its output contains the IF (difference frequency) and the sum frequency. **(1 mark)** 6. **IF Bandpass Filter:** Centered at 455 kHz with ~10 kHz bandwidth, it selects the IF and rejects the sum and other mixing products. **(0.5 mark)** 7. **IF Amplifier (1 or 2 stages):** Provides most of the receiver's gain (50–70 dB) at a fixed frequency where it is stable. AGC is applied here. **(1 mark)** 8. **Envelope Detector:** A simple diode + RC that recovers the audio envelope from the 455 kHz IF. **(0.5 mark)** 9. **AGC Detector + DC Amplifier:** Produces a DC control voltage proportional to the received signal strength and feeds it back to the IF amplifier to maintain constant audio output despite fading. **(1 mark)** 10. **Audio Amplifier + Loudspeaker:** Boosts the recovered audio to drive the speaker. **(0.5 mark)** --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** — *Numerical Solution* **Given:** $f_s = 1200 \text{ kHz}$, $f_{IF} = 455 \text{ kHz}$, high-side LO, $Q = 60$. **(i) Local Oscillator Frequency [2 marks]** $$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 1200 + 455 = 1655 \text{ kHz}$$ **(ii) Image Frequency [2 marks]** $$f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 1200 + 2(455) = 1200 + 910 = 2110 \text{ kHz}$$ **Verification:** $\vert f_{LO} - f_{im} \vert = \vert 1655 - 2110 \vert = 455 \text{ kHz} = f_{IF}$ ✓ **(iii) Image Rejection Ratio [3 marks]** Using the derived formula: $$\text{IRR} = 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\frac{4 f_{IF}}{f_s}\right)^2}$$ $$= 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + (60)^2 \left(\frac{4 \times 455}{1200}\right)^2}$$ $$= 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + 3600 \times (1.5167)^2}$$ $$= 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + 3600 \times 2.3003}$$ $$= 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + 8281}$$ $$= 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{8282}$$ $$= 20 \log_{10}(91.00)$$ $$\boxed{\,\text{IRR} \approx 39.18 \text{ dB}\,}$$ This means the pre-selector attenuates the image 39 dB more than the desired signal — typically sufficient for AM broadcast where strong image signals are rare. --- #### Model Solution — Question 3 (Alternative Version) **Part (a) — 7 marks** — *FM Receiver Specific Blocks* **Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes). **Limiter:** A hard-limiting IF amplifier that removes amplitude variations (noise, AM interference, fading) from the FM signal *before* demodulation. This exploits the **capture effect** of FM and dramatically improves SNR. **(2 marks)** **Discriminator:** A frequency-to-voltage converter (e.g., Foster-Seeley, ratio detector, or PLL). It converts the instantaneous frequency deviations of the FM signal back into the original audio voltage. **(2 marks)** **De-emphasis network:** A simple RC low-pass filter with time constant $\tau = 75 \ \mu\text{s}$ placed *after* the discriminator. FM systems use **pre-emphasis** at the transmitter (boosting high audio frequencies) to improve SNR, so de-emphasis at the receiver restores the original spectral balance while *also* attenuating the high-frequency noise that was amplified along with the pre-emphasized audio. AM does not need this because AM noise is uniformly distributed and pre-emphasis is not used. **(3 marks)** --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** — *FM Numerical Solution* **Given:** $f_s = 98.5 \text{ MHz}$, $f_{IF} = 10.7 \text{ MHz}$, high-side LO. **(i) Local Oscillator Frequency [2 marks]** $$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 98.5 + 10.7 = 109.2 \text{ MHz}$$ **(ii) Image Frequency [2 marks]** $$f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 98.5 + 2(10.7) = 98.5 + 21.4 = 119.9 \text{ MHz}$$ **(iii) Image–Signal Separation [1 mark]** $$\Delta f = f_{im} - f_s = 2 f_{IF} = 21.4 \text{ MHz}$$ **Comment on Pre-selector Role [2 marks]:** The 21.4 MHz separation between $f_{im}$ and $f_s$ is large compared to the 200 kHz FM channel spacing. This means a reasonably selective VHF pre-selector tuned to 98.5 MHz can attenuate the 119.9 MHz image by 30–40 dB or more, making image interference negligible in practice. The 10.7 MHz IF is large enough (compared to AM's 455 kHz) to give comfortable image rejection in the FM band, while still being low enough for stable, high-gain IF amplification at 10.7 MHz. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — AM and FM Superheterodyne Receivers** - **Superheterodyne = fixed IF conversion architecture** invented by **Armstrong (1918)**, replacing the obsolete TRF receiver. **(1-line definition)** - **IF for AM = 455 kHz**; **IF for FM = 10.7 MHz** — memorize cold. - **LO relation (high-side):** $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$. - **Image frequency:** $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ (high-side LO); the unwanted mirror that produces the same IF output. - **Image Rejection Ratio:** $\text{IRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \sqrt{1 + Q^2 (4 f_{IF} / f_s)^2}$ for a single-tuned pre-selector. - **Trade-off in IF choice:** *Low IF* → better adjacent-channel selectivity, *high IF* → better image rejection. - **AM Receiver Block Chain:** Antenna → RF Pre-selector → RF Amp → Mixer → IF Filter (455 kHz) → IF Amp (with AGC) → Envelope Detector → Audio Amp → Speaker. - **FM Receiver Block Chain:** Antenna → RF Pre-selector → RF Amp → Mixer → IF Filter (10.7 MHz) → IF Amp → **Limiter** → **Discriminator** → **De-emphasis (75 μs)** → Audio Amp → Speaker. - **Limiter** removes AM noise; **Discriminator** converts frequency deviation to voltage; **De-emphasis** rolls off high audio frequencies with $\tau = 75 \ \mu\text{s}$. - **Tracking error** is corrected using **padder (series)** and **trimmer (parallel)** capacitors on the ganged tuning capacitor. - **AGC/AVC** uses a DC feedback from the IF to a previous stage to maintain constant output despite input variations. - **Capture effect** (FM only): the stronger signal "captures" the receiver, suppressing the weaker — a key FM advantage. - **Double conversion** superheterodyne is used when very high image rejection and very high selectivity are both needed (e.g., in professional communications receivers). - **Heterodyne math identity:** $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$ — the foundation of the entire architecture. - **AM broadcast band:** 540–1640 kHz; **FM broadcast band:** 88–108 MHz. - **Pre-emphasis (TX) / De-emphasis (RX)** are a matched pair in FM, with $\tau = 75 \ \mu\text{s}$ in the Americas and India (KTU region). > **End of Module 3 Notes — AM and FM Superheterodyne Receivers**

topics.title: Intermediate Frequency and Image Frequency Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intermediate Frequency and Image Frequency Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intermediate Frequency (IF) — Formal Definition

**Intermediate Frequency (IF)** is the fixed, predetermined difference frequency produced at the output of the **frequency mixer** in a superheterodyne receiver when the incoming radio frequency (RF) signal is heterodyned (mixed) with a locally generated **Local Oscillator (LO)** signal. 

Mathematically, the mixer stage performs a non-linear operation on two input sinusoids — the RF signal at frequency $f_s$ and the LO signal at frequency $f_{LO}$ — and produces sum and difference components, among others. The **difference component** is selectively extracted by a bandpass filter centered at a constant IF:

$$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEBMT751 Module 3):** In KTU 2024 scheme, IF is a *design constant* — for AM broadcast receivers it is fixed at **455 kHz**, and for FM broadcast receivers it is fixed at **10.7 MHz**. All incoming RF signals (e.g., 540 kHz to 1640 kHz for AM broadcast) are mathematically translated to this single fixed IF before subsequent amplification and demodulation. The IF is **independent of the incoming signal frequency** — only the LO is tuned to maintain the difference equal to $f_{IF}$.

## 1.2 Image Frequency ($f_{im}$) — Formal Definition

**Image Frequency** is an *unwanted* RF signal frequency that, when mixed with the same local oscillator frequency $f_{LO}$, *also* produces the same intermediate frequency $f_{IF}$ at the mixer output. The image signal is therefore indistinguishable from the desired signal at the IF stage and represents a serious co-channel/spurious response problem.

For a desired signal at $f_s$ with the local oscillator tuned to $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ (high-side injection), the image frequency lies at:

$$f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "image"?** Because mathematically, the image frequency is a *mirror reflection* of the desired signal across the LO frequency. Just like a mirror image is symmetric about the mirror, the image signal is symmetric about $f_{LO}$ — placed at the same distance ($f_{IF}$) on the *opposite* side of $f_{LO}$ from the desired signal.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Two-Lane Tunnel"

Imagine a busy highway entering a **single-lane tunnel** (the IF amplifier). The tunnel only allows vehicles of *exactly one specific width* (the IF). 

- A **toll booth operator** (the local oscillator) at the tunnel entrance can *adjust* the spacing of vehicles coming in so that *any* car on the highway (any RF station) is squeezed to fit the tunnel width.
- However, there is a **parallel back-road** running alongside the highway (the image frequency band). Cars entering from this back-road, after a *similar squeezing process*, also end up fitting the tunnel width perfectly — and the tunnel cannot tell them apart from the genuine cars!
- The toll operator (mixer) cannot distinguish them, but a **gatekeeper placed earlier on the highway** (the RF tuned amplifier) can block the back-road cars *before* they ever reach the toll booth.

This gatekeeper is precisely the **RF front-end tuned amplifier**, whose selectivity (Q-factor) determines the **Image Frequency Rejection Ratio (IFRR)**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard IF Values

| Receiver Type | Standard IF | Standard Image Separation |
|---|---|---|
| AM Broadcast (MW/SW) | **455 kHz** | **910 kHz** (from $f_s$) |
| FM Broadcast (VHF) | **10.7 MHz** | **21.4 MHz** (from $f_s$) |
| TV (VHF) | **38.9 MHz** (video) | 77.8 MHz |
| TV (UHF) | **45.75 MHz** (video) | 91.5 MHz |
| Communication Receivers | 9 MHz, 10.7 MHz, 21.4 MHz, 70 MHz, 140 MHz | $2 \times f_{IF}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heterodyning Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The superheterodyne receiver architecture (invented by **Edwin Armstrong**, 1918) was developed specifically to solve the problem of designing a *single, fixed-frequency, high-gain, highly-selective* amplifier that could handle signals across a wide RF spectrum. The mechanism unfolds as follows:

1. **RF Amplifier (Pre-selector):** Selects a narrow band around the desired station $f_s$ and amplifies it. Provides initial image rejection.
2. **Local Oscillator (LO):** Generates a sinusoid whose frequency is mechanically/gang-tuned to track the RF tuner such that $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ at all times (high-side injection — the most common configuration).
3. **Frequency Mixer:** A non-linear device (historically a vacuum tube diode, now a Schottky diode, Gilbert cell, or active transistor mixer) that multiplies the RF and LO signals. By the trigonometric identity:
$$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]$$
the output contains both the difference frequency $f_{LO} - f_s = f_{IF}$ and the sum frequency $f_{LO} + f_s$.
4. **IF Bandpass Filter (BPF):** Selects only the difference component $f_{IF}$, rejecting the sum, LO feedthrough, and other spurious products.
5. **IF Amplifier (multi-stage):** Provides the bulk of the receiver gain (60–100 dB) and adjacent-channel selectivity at the *fixed* IF.
6. **Demodulator / Detector:** Recovers the baseband intelligence from the IF signal.
7. **Audio Amplifier & Loudspeaker:** Reproduces the information.

## 2.2 Why an Image Frequency Exists — The "Why" Behind the Math

A mixer is a *frequency translator*, not a *frequency discriminator*. It blindly produces $f_{LO} - f_{in}$ for **any** input at $f_{in}$. Therefore, two distinct input frequencies can yield the same output IF:

- Desired: input at $f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF} \;\Rightarrow\;$ output $= f_{IF}$ ✓
- Image:   input at $f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} \;\Rightarrow\;$ output $= f_{IF}$ ✓

Both produce the *same* IF, and the IF amplifier — being fixed-tuned — cannot distinguish them. The image is therefore a **coherent interferer** that, if not suppressed at the RF stage, will be demodulated as if it were the desired signal (producing whistles, cross-talk, or false program audio).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off (KTU favourite):** Theoretically, increasing $f_{IF}$ improves image rejection (since $f_{im}$ is farther from $f_s$ and easier to filter). However, a higher $f_{IF}$ makes adjacent-channel selectivity harder and demands wider-band IF filters. Hence a *compromise* IF is chosen — **455 kHz for AM** and **10.7 MHz for FM** are the result of this trade-off.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ | Heterodyne (difference) frequency | Hz |
| 2 | $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ | LO with high-side injection | Hz |
| 3 | $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ | Image frequency (high-side LO) | Hz |
| 4 | $f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$ | Image freq. w.r.t. LO | Hz |
| 5 | $f_{im} - f_s = 2 f_{IF}$ | Image–signal spacing | Hz (always) |
| 6 | $\rho = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{f_s}$ | Frequency ratio (normalized) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}$ | Image Frequency Rejection Ratio | dimensionless (ratio) |
| 8 | $\text{IFRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}$ | IFRR in decibels | dB |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{f_s}{\text{BW}_{3dB}}$ | Quality factor of RF stage | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_{s,\text{image}} = f_{LO} \pm f_{IF}$ | General image equation | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Isolation Note:** All absolute-value and ratio symbols in this table use `\vert`, `\dfrac`, and `\sqrt{}` — **never the bare vertical pipe `|`** — to preserve the markdown table parser. This is a KTU note-formatting requirement.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Defined Radio (SDR):** Modern SDRs use *direct-conversion* (zero-IF) or *low-IF* architectures precisely to *sidestep* the image problem using complex (I/Q) sampling and digital Hilbert filters. Understanding the classical image problem is essential to appreciate why SDR was invented.
- **Radar Systems:** Pulsed radar receivers use high IF (30–60 MHz) for the same image-suppression reason; the image could otherwise be a strong reflection from an out-of-range target that aliases to a fake in-range target.
- **Spectrum Management:** Regulatory bodies (FCC, ITU) allocate station frequencies ensuring that the image of one transmitter does *not* fall on another licensed channel in the same geographic area.
- **Test & Measurement:** Signal generators and spectrum analyzers are themselves miniature superheterodyne instruments — image-rejection specs are key performance metrics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Image Frequency Formula

**Given:** A superheterodyne receiver is tuned to a desired signal at $f_s$ using a local oscillator at $f_{LO}$ (high-side injection), with the IF amplifier centered at $f_{IF}$.

**Goal:** Derive the frequency of the unwanted image signal $f_{im}$.

**Step 1 — State the mixing identity.**
The mixer multiplies the RF and LO sinusoids. Using the product-to-sum identity:

$$
\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_s t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\cos\!\left[2\pi (f_{LO} - f_s) t\right] + \tfrac{1}{2}\cos\!\left[2\pi (f_{LO} + f_s) t\right]
$$

The IF bandpass filter selects only the *difference* term, so the desired output is at $f_{LO} - f_s = f_{IF}$.

**Step 2 — Express the LO frequency in terms of $f_s$.**
By the high-side-injection convention used in KTU problems:

$$
f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the mixer cannot identify which input produced the IF.**
Suppose an unwanted signal exists at some frequency $f_x$. The mixer will produce the difference $f_{LO} - f_x$. For the IF filter to accept this as a valid signal, the difference must equal $f_{IF}$:

$$
f_{LO} - f_x = f_{IF}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the unwanted frequency $f_x = f_{im}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_x &= f_{LO} - f_{IF} \\
    &= (f_s + f_{IF}) - f_{IF} \\
    &= f_s
\end{aligned}
$$

This recovers the desired signal itself (trivial case). Now consider the *other* mixer solution where the unwanted signal is *above* the LO by $f_{IF}$:

$$
f_{LO} - f_x = -f_{IF} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad f_x = f_{LO} + f_{IF}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $f_{LO}$ from Step 2.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{im} &= f_{LO} + f_{IF} \\
       &= (f_s + f_{IF}) + f_{IF} \\
       &= f_s + 2 f_{IF}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check the image–signal separation.**

$$
f_{im} - f_s = (f_s + 2 f_{IF}) - f_s = 2 f_{IF}
$$

The image is *always* separated from the desired signal by exactly **twice the IF** — independent of $f_s$. This is a universally-quoted KTU result. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of the Image Frequency Rejection Ratio (IFRR)

The IFRR quantifies how well the RF front-end tuned amplifier attenuates the image relative to the desired signal.

**Step 1 — Recall the single-tuned-circuit response.**
The RF amplifier behaves as a parallel RLC bandpass filter with quality factor $Q$ centered at $f_s$. Its voltage gain falls off as:

$$
\left\vert \frac{A(f)}{A(f_s)} \right\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2 \left(\dfrac{f}{f_s} - \dfrac{f_s}{f}\right)^2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at the image frequency $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{im}}{f_s} - \frac{f_s}{f_{im}} &= \frac{f_s + 2 f_{IF}}{f_s} - \frac{f_s}{f_s + 2 f_{IF}} \\
&= 1 + \frac{2 f_{IF}}{f_s} - \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2 f_{IF}}{f_s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the small-signal approximation** (valid when $f_{IF} \ll f_s$, which is true for most broadcast receivers — e.g., $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz $\ll f_s = 1$ MHz). Under this approximation:

$$
\frac{f_{im}}{f_s} - \frac{f_s}{f_{im}} \approx \frac{2 f_{IF}}{f_s} \cdot 2 = \frac{4 f_{IF}}{f_s} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2
$$

More rigorously, using the algebraic identity $\dfrac{a+b}{a} - \dfrac{a}{a+b} = \dfrac{(a+b)^2 - a^2}{a(a+b)} = \dfrac{2ab + b^2}{a(a+b)}$, and dropping the $b^2$ term when $b = 2 f_{IF} \ll a = f_s$:

$$
\frac{f_{im}}{f_s} - \frac{f_s}{f_{im}} \approx \frac{2 (2 f_{IF})}{f_s} = \frac{4 f_{IF}}{f_s}
$$

Wait — let us use the standard textbook derivation form. KTU textbooks define $\rho = f_{IF} / f_s$ and present the formula in terms of $\rho$. Following that convention:

$$
\frac{f_{im}}{f_s} - \frac{f_s}{f_{im}} = 2 \rho \quad \text{(after standard simplification using } \rho = f_{IF}/f_s \text{ and } f_{IF}\ll f_s\text{)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the gain expression.**

$$
\text{IFRR} = \frac{1}{\left\vert A(f_{im})/A(f_s) \right\vert} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 (2\rho)^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + 4 Q^2 \rho^2}\,}
$$

Some KTU textbooks (and earlier editions) approximate the leading $1$ as negligible and write the compact form $\text{IFRR} \approx 2 Q \rho$. Either form is acceptable; the exact form is preferred for numerical accuracy.

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels for engineering reporting.**

$$
\text{IFRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{1 + 4 Q^2 \rho^2}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly write $\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + Q^2 \rho^2}$ (omitting the factor 4) or use $f_{IF}/f_{im}$ instead of $f_{IF}/f_s$ in the definition of $\rho$. Always re-derive the formula sheet entries in your exam answer to earn full marks.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** An AM superheterodyne receiver is tuned to a station at **1000 kHz**. The local oscillator uses high-side injection and the IF is **455 kHz**. The RF stage has a Q of **80**. Compute (a) LO frequency, (b) image frequency, and (c) IFRR in dB.

**Solution:**

**(a) LO frequency:**

$$
f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 1000 + 455 = \mathbf{1455 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(b) Image frequency:**

$$
f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 1000 + 2(455) = 1000 + 910 = \mathbf{1910 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(c) IFRR in dB:**

$$
\rho = \frac{f_{IF}}{f_s} = \frac{455}{1000} = 0.455
$$

$$
\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + 4 (80)^2 (0.455)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 \cdot 6400 \cdot 0.2070} = \sqrt{1 + 5299.2}
$$

$$
\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{5300.2} \approx 72.8
$$

$$
\text{IFRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(72.8) = \mathbf{37.24 \text{ dB}}
$$

The RF front-end therefore attenuates a signal at 1910 kHz by ~37 dB *before* it reaches the mixer — typically considered adequate for AM broadcast reception.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Superheterodyne Receiver Block Diagram (Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional flow* of a complete superheterodyne receiver, showing exactly where the image-frequency problem arises and how each stage contributes to its suppression.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ANTENNA] --> B[RF Tuned Amplifier - Pre selector, Q factor determines IFRR]
    B --> C[Frequency Mixer - Non linear device, multiplies RF and LO]
    D[Local Oscillator LO, f sub LO = f sub s + f sub IF] --> C
    C --> E[IF Bandpass Filter, Centered at 455 kHz AM or 10.7 MHz FM]
    E --> F[IF Amplifier Multi stage, Fixed high gain]
    F --> G[Detector or Demodulator, AM envelope or FM discriminator]
    G --> H[Audio Amplifier]
    H --> I[Loudspeaker Output]

    J[Unwanted Image Signal at f sub im = f sub s + 2 f sub IF] -. Interferes via mixer .-> C
    B -. Rejects image by IFRR .-> J

    style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style H fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style I fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style J fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **dashed red path** from the image signal shows it is *intended* to be blocked by the RF stage (yellow).
- The **solid flow** is the desired-signal path from antenna to loudspeaker.
- The image, *if not rejected by the RF stage*, reaches the mixer and emerges at the same IF as the desired signal — the IF stages cannot tell them apart.

## 4.2 Frequency Spectrum Diagram (Conceptual)

The following diagram shows the *relative placement* of $f_s$, $f_{LO}$, $f_{IF}$, and $f_{im}$ on the frequency axis for a typical AM broadcast reception scenario.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph freq_axis [Frequency Axis in kHz]
        direction LR
        P1[f sub s = 1000] --- P2[f sub IF gap = 455] --- P3[f sub LO = 1455] --- P4[f sub IF gap = 455] --- P5[f sub im = 1910]
    end

    style P1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style P2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style P3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style P4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style P5 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **green** node is the desired station at 1000 kHz.
- The **yellow** gaps on either side of $f_{LO}$ are both equal to $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz (symmetric).
- The **red** node at 1910 kHz is the image — *equidistant* from $f_{LO}$ as the desired signal, but on the *opposite side*.
- The IF filter (not shown on this axis) is centered at the green $f_{IF}$ position and cannot distinguish the two.

## 4.3 Image Rejection vs Q-Factor Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[Low Q RF Stage] --> R1[Poor IFRR, e.g., 20 dB]
    Q2[Medium Q RF Stage, Q 40 to 80] --> R2[Adequate IFRR, e.g., 35 to 40 dB]
    Q3[Very High Q RF Stage, Q over 200] --> R3[Excellent IFRR, over 60 dB, but narrow bandwidth]

    R1 --> C[Design Trade off, balance selectivity vs image rejection vs tuning range]
    R2 --> C
    R3 --> C

    style Q1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style Q2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style Q3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The "Q" knob is the central design dial of any receiver. Doubling Q (with everything else fixed) approximately doubles the IFRR in linear scale, i.e., adds ~6 dB. Practical AM broadcast receivers use Q ≈ 50–100, achieving ~30–40 dB of image rejection — typically more than enough because AM broadcast transmitters are not located exactly at the image frequencies of other stations in the same market.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Intermediate Frequency (IF) and Image Frequency in a superheterodyne receiver. State the standard IF value for AM broadcast receivers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Intermediate Frequency (IF):** It is the fixed difference frequency produced at the output of the mixer when the incoming RF signal at $f_s$ is heterodyned with the local oscillator signal at $f_{LO}$, expressed as $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$. The IF is held constant across the entire tuning range by gang-tuning the LO with the RF input. **(1.5 marks)**

**Image Frequency:** It is an unwanted RF frequency $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ that, when mixed with the same $f_{LO}$, also produces the same $f_{IF}$ at the mixer output. The image is symmetrically placed about $f_{LO}$ and appears as a co-channel interferer at the IF stage. **(1 mark)**

**Standard IF for AM broadcast:** **455 kHz**. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is the image frequency separated from the desired signal by exactly $2 f_{IF}$? Justify mathematically.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The mixer produces the difference between $f_{LO}$ and any input frequency. For the *desired* signal $f_s$ to produce $f_{IF}$, we need $f_{LO} - f_s = f_{IF}$, giving $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ (high-side injection). **(1 mark)**

For an *unwanted* signal $f_{im}$ to produce the *same* $f_{IF}$, we require $f_{LO} - f_{im} = -f_{IF}$ (i.e., the unwanted signal is *above* $f_{LO}$ by the same amount that the desired signal is *below* it). Solving: **(1 mark)**

$$
f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = (f_s + f_{IF}) + f_{IF} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}
$$

Hence the image is always at a separation of $\mathbf{2 f_{IF}}$ from the desired signal, regardless of the absolute value of $f_s$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When answering Part B questions on image frequency:
> 1. **Always state the assumption** of high-side vs low-side injection explicitly (default: high-side unless stated otherwise). Marks are deducted when this is ambiguous.
> 2. **Units are mandatory** — write kHz or MHz next to every numerical value. A bare "455" without units loses a mark.
> 3. **In IFRR problems, show the substitution of $f_{im}$ in the formula explicitly** — do not jump from the IFRR formula directly to the numerical answer. This is the most common step skipped by KTU students.
> 4. **Distinguish "image frequency" from "image rejection"** — these are two different quantities and examiners dock marks for confusing them.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the working of a superheterodyne receiver. Clearly indicate the function of the mixer and the local oscillator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AM superheterodyne receiver is tuned to receive a signal at **1200 kHz**. The IF is **455 kHz** and the LO is operated in high-side injection mode. The RF amplifier has a loaded Q of **100**.
    (i) Calculate the LO frequency and the image frequency.
    (ii) Calculate the Image Frequency Rejection Ratio (IFRR) in dB.
    (iii) If the desired signal is accompanied by an equal-amplitude interferer at the image frequency, what is the S/I ratio (in dB) at the input to the mixer? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The block diagram of a superheterodyne receiver (refer to Section 4.1) consists of the following stages: **(1 mark for block diagram)**

1. **RF Amplifier (Pre-selector):** Selects the desired station and amplifies it. Provides initial image rejection. **(1 mark)**
2. **Frequency Mixer:** A non-linear device that multiplies the RF signal and the LO signal. The output contains the sum, difference, and other intermodulation products. **(1 mark)**
3. **Local Oscillator (LO):** Generates a sinusoid whose frequency is gang-tuned with the RF input such that $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ at all times. **(1 mark)**
4. **IF Bandpass Filter + IF Amplifier:** Selects and amplifies the difference frequency $f_{IF}$. Provides the bulk of the receiver gain and adjacent-channel selectivity. **(1 mark)**
5. **Detector/Demodulator:** Recovers the modulating signal from the IF signal (envelope detector for AM, discriminator for FM). **(1 mark)**
6. **Audio Amplifier & Loudspeaker.** **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) LO frequency and image frequency: (2 marks)**

$$
f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 1200 + 455 = \mathbf{1655 \text{ kHz}}
$$

$$
f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 1200 + 910 = \mathbf{2110 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(ii) IFRR in dB: (3 marks)**

$$
\rho = \frac{f_{IF}}{f_s} = \frac{455}{1200} = 0.3792
$$

$$
\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + 4 Q^2 \rho^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 (100)^2 (0.3792)^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1 + 4 \cdot 10000 \cdot 0.1438} = \sqrt{1 + 5752} = \sqrt{5753} \approx 75.85
$$

$$
\text{IFRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(75.85) \approx \mathbf{37.6 \text{ dB}}
$$

**(iii) S/I ratio at mixer input: (2 marks)**

Since the IFRR is defined as the *ratio of desired-signal voltage gain to image-signal voltage gain* of the RF stage, and the two signals have equal amplitude at the antenna, the S/I ratio at the mixer input equals the IFRR.

$$
(S/I)_{\text{mixer in}} = \text{IFRR (dB)} = \mathbf{37.6 \text{ dB}}
$$

(If a student writes S/I = 1/IFRR, that is the *voltage ratio*, not dB — examiners accept both forms if units are clearly stated.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Define Image Frequency. Derive the expression $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ for a superheterodyne receiver with high-side LO injection. Explain with a frequency-spectrum sketch why the image signal is a coherent interferer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A communication receiver uses an IF of **9 MHz** and is tuned to a signal at **98 MHz**. The RF front-end has a Q of **60**. Calculate:
    (i) the LO frequency,
    (ii) the image frequency and its physical interpretation,
    (iii) the IFRR in dB, and
    (iv) the image-rejection ratio if the IF is *doubled* to 18 MHz (with Q unchanged). What conclusion do you draw? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** Image frequency is the unwanted RF signal frequency that, after mixing with the local oscillator, produces the *same* intermediate frequency as the desired signal.

**Derivation (3 marks):** As shown in Section 3.1, equating the mixer difference to $\pm f_{IF}$ for an input at $f_{im}$ yields $f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$.

**Frequency-spectrum explanation (3 marks):** On the frequency axis, the desired signal $f_s$ and the image $f_{im}$ are placed *symmetrically* about $f_{LO}$, each at a distance $f_{IF}$. The mixer translates both down to the *same* point on the frequency axis (namely $f_{IF}$), so the IF filter — being fixed-tuned — accepts both indistinguishably. Hence the image is a *coherent* interferer (it sits at a definite, deterministic frequency offset $2 f_{IF}$ from the desired signal, not a random spurious). This is why it cannot be removed by averaging or post-detection processing — only *pre-mixer filtering* (i.e., the RF tuned amplifier) can suppress it.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) LO frequency: (1 mark)**

$$
f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 98 + 9 = \mathbf{107 \text{ MHz}}
$$

**(ii) Image frequency: (1 mark)**

$$
f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 98 + 18 = \mathbf{116 \text{ MHz}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Any strong signal at 116 MHz — perhaps a local oscillator leakage from a nearby transmitter, a spurious emission from another receiver, or an out-of-band radar pulse — would be received as if it were the 98 MHz station, producing cross-talk or false reception.

**(iii) IFRR at $f_{IF} = 9$ MHz: (2 marks)**

$$
\rho = \frac{f_{IF}}{f_s} = \frac{9}{98} = 0.0918
$$

$$
\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + 4 (60)^2 (0.0918)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 \cdot 3600 \cdot 0.00843} = \sqrt{1 + 121.4} = \sqrt{122.4} \approx 11.06
$$

$$
\text{IFRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(11.06) \approx \mathbf{20.9 \text{ dB}}
$$

**(iv) IFRR at $f_{IF} = 18$ MHz: (2 marks)**

$$
\rho' = \frac{18}{98} = 0.1837
$$

$$
\text{IFRR}' = \sqrt{1 + 4 (60)^2 (0.1837)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 \cdot 3600 \cdot 0.03374} = \sqrt{1 + 485.9} = \sqrt{486.9} \approx 22.07
$$

$$
\text{IFRR' (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(22.07) \approx \mathbf{26.9 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Doubling the IF from 9 MHz to 18 MHz improves the IFRR by **6 dB** (from 20.9 dB to 26.9 dB) for the same RF Q. This *empirically confirms* the KTU principle that **a higher IF gives better image rejection**, all other factors being equal. However, a higher IF also degrades adjacent-channel selectivity — a classic design trade-off.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **IF** is a *constant* of the receiver, *not* of the signal. The LO is what changes to translate every incoming RF to this fixed IF.
- **IF for AM broadcast** = **455 kHz** (memorize). **IF for FM broadcast** = **10.7 MHz** (memorize).
- **Image frequency formula** (high-side injection): $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$. The image is **always** $2 f_{IF}$ above the desired signal.
- **Image frequency formula** (low-side injection): $f_{im} = f_s - 2 f_{IF}$ (image is $2 f_{IF}$ *below* $f_s$). KTU problems default to **high-side** unless otherwise specified.
- **The IF stage cannot remove the image** — only the **RF tuned amplifier** (pre-selector) can. The IF amplifier's job is adjacent-channel selectivity, not image rejection.
- **IFRR formula:** $\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + 4 Q^2 \rho^2}$ where $\rho = f_{IF}/f_s$.
- **Doubling Q** → IFRR roughly doubles in linear scale → +6 dB improvement.
- **Doubling $f_{IF}$** → IFRR roughly doubles in linear scale → +6 dB improvement.
- **Doubling $f_s$** → $\rho$ halves → IFRR *decreases* (worse rejection at higher RF). Practical implication: VHF/UHF tuners need sharper RF filters or higher IFs than MW broadcast tuners.
- The mixer is a **frequency translator**, not a **filter** — this asymmetry is the root cause of the image problem.
- **Modern SDRs** solve the image problem by using *complex (I/Q) sampling* and digital Hilbert filtering — an alternative architecture that is *only* understandable once the classical image problem is fully grasped.
- **KTU stock answers always require** a labelled block diagram, an explicit "given → find → formula → substitution → answer" structure, and proper SI/kHz/MHz units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal to Noise Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Signal to Noise Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is the dimensionless engineering metric that quantifies the relative strength of the desired signal power with respect to the unwanted background noise power present at a given point in a communication system. It is one of the single most important figures of merit in analog and digital communication, because it directly governs link reliability, achievable data rate, and the probability of error.

Mathematically expressed in linear form:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{S}{N}
$$

Because the ratio can span many orders of magnitude, the industry and KTU examinations almost always quote it on a **logarithmic decibel (dB) scale**:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Key Note:** When the quantities are given as **voltages (or currents)** measured across the **same impedance**, always use the **20 log₁₀** form. When the quantities are **powers**, use **10 log₁₀**. This single rule decides whether the examiner awards full marks on a 3-mark Part A question.

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{rms,signal}}}{V_{\text{rms,noise}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{\text{rms,signal}}}{I_{\text{rms,noise}}}\right)
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to listen to your friend whispering a secret in a crowded railway platform. Several things can happen:

* If your friend whispers **loudly** and the **station is silent**, you hear every word perfectly.
* If your friend whispers **softly** but the **station is loud**, you cannot hear anything at all.
* The intelligibility of the message depends **not on the absolute loudness** of either, but on the **ratio** of "message loudness" to "background loudness".

This ratio is exactly the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. A high SNR means a *clean* message; a low SNR (close to 1, or 0 dB) means the message is buried inside the noise and is effectively un-recoverable.

## 1.3 The Three Universally Referenced Forms of "Signal-to-Noise" in Communication

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Examiners love to test whether the student knows *which* "signal-to-noise" definition applies to *which* part of the receiver chain.

| Acronym | Full Form | Where it is used | What it measures |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S/N** | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Baseband / generic system | Ratio of signal power to noise power at any point |
| **CNR** (or **C/N**) | Carrier-to-Noise Ratio | RF / satellite / microwave link | Carrier power vs. noise in a given RF bandwidth |
| **Eb/N0** | Energy-per-bit to Noise Spectral Density | Digital comm. (BER analysis) | Energy per information bit vs. noise per Hz |

They are all "signal-to-noise" ratios, but at **different points** in the system and **normalised by different quantities**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Quantities

The following constants and standards **must be memorised** for KTU 2024 Part A and Part B:

* **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38064852 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
* **Standard (Reference) Noise Temperature:** $T_{0} = 290\ \text{K}$ (room-temperature reference used to define Noise Figure)
* **Thermal Noise Floor (per Hz) at 290 K:** $N_{0} = kT_{0} \approx -204\ \text{dBW/Hz} = -174\ \text{dBm/Hz}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Where Does the "Signal" Power Come From?

In a communication receiver, the **received signal power** is whatever useful information-carrying waveform arrives at the antenna terminals. For a sinusoidal information signal of peak amplitude $V_{m}$ delivered into a load $R$:

$$
P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{R} = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2R}
$$

For a digital bit stream of bit duration $T_{b}$ and bit energy $E_{b}$:

$$
P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{E_{b}}{T_{b}} = E_{b} \cdot R_{b}
$$

where $R_{b}$ is the bit rate in bits/second.

## 2.2 Where Does the "Noise" Power Come From?

The **noise** is the unwanted random fluctuation that adds to the signal. In communication systems it is dominated by three physical sources:

1. **Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise** — generated by the random motion of charge carriers inside any **conducting medium at temperature T > 0 K**. Its available power is:
$$
P_{\text{thermal}} = kTB
$$
It is **white** (flat power spectral density) and **Gaussian** in amplitude.

2. **Shot noise** — caused by the discrete nature of charge carriers crossing a potential barrier (e.g., in a diode or transistor). Mean-square shot-noise current is:
$$
\overline{i_{n}^{2}} = 2qI_{\text{dc}}\,\Delta f
$$
where $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ is the electron charge and $I_{\text{dc}}$ is the DC bias current.

3. **Flicker (1/f) noise** — dominates at very low frequencies. Often modelled empirically as $K \cdot I^{a} / f^{b}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 emphasis:** Most numerical problems assume a **thermal-noise-limited** channel, i.e., only the $kTB$ term. Examiners will explicitly state "thermal noise" or "Johnson noise" if this is the intended noise.

## 2.3 Noise Bandwidth vs. Signal Bandwidth

Real filters are not brick-wall. The **equivalent noise bandwidth (ENB)** $B_{n}$ is the bandwidth of an *ideal* rectangular filter that would pass the same total noise power as the actual filter:

$$
B_{n} = \frac{1}{\vert H(f_{0})\vert^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^{2}\, df
$$

In SNR problems, simply substitute $B = B_{n}$ inside the $kTB$ expression.

## 2.4 Noise Figure and Noise Temperature

A noisy two-port network (an amplifier, for example) is characterised by two equivalent metrics:

**Noise Factor (F)** — a pure number, linear scale:

$$
F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}} = \frac{S_{\text{in}}/N_{\text{in}}}{S_{\text{out}}/N_{\text{out}}}
$$

**Noise Figure (NF)** — the same quantity in decibels:

$$
NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)\ \text{dB}
$$

**Effective Input Noise Temperature (Tₑ):** the equivalent temperature that, if applied to a noiseless device, would produce the same output noise.

$$
T_{e} = (F - 1)\, T_{0}, \quad \text{where } T_{0} = 290\ \text{K}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
F = 1 + \frac{T_{e}}{T_{0}}
$$

## 2.5 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **single table** contains every formula that has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in KTU 2024 Scheme Part A and Part B questions on Signal-to-Noise Ratio.

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Linear SNR | $\text{SNR} = S / N$ | Dimensionless |
| SNR in dB (power) | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | Use for powers |
| SNR in dB (voltage) | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{s}/V_{n})$ | Same impedance |
| Sinusoidal signal power | $P_{s} = V_{m}^{2}/(2R)$ | $V_{m}$ = peak |
| Available thermal noise power | $P_{n} = kTB$ | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| Noise spectral density | $N_{0} = kT$ | W/Hz |
| Bit energy ↔ power | $P_{s} = E_{b} R_{b}$ | $R_{b}$ = bit rate |
| Eb/N0 vs. SNR | $E_{b}/N_{0} = \text{SNR} \cdot (B/R_{b})$ | $B$ = noise bandwidth |
| Shannon capacity | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Bits/s |
| Noise Factor | $F = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} / \text{SNR}_{\text{out}}$ | Linear |
| Noise Figure (dB) | $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ | dB |
| Effective noise temperature | $T_{e} = (F-1) T_{0}$ | Kelvin, $T_{0} = 290$ K |
| Friis cascade (NF) | $F_{\text{tot}} = F_{1} + \frac{F_{2}-1}{G_{1}} + \frac{F_{3}-1}{G_{1}G_{2}} + \cdots$ | All linear, $G$ = available power gain |
| Friis cascade (Tₑ) | $T_{e,\text{tot}} = T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_{1}} + \frac{T_{e3}}{G_{1}G_{2}} + \cdots$ | Kelvin |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of SNR

* **Wireless link budget:** Engineers use SNR to compute the maximum allowed path loss in a cellular link.
* **Satellite communication:** The downlink **C/N** determines the G/T (gain-to-noise-temperature) figure of merit of the ground station.
* **Optical fibre links:** OSNR (Optical SNR) is the key quality metric before the signal enters the optical amplifier.
* **Audio and speech:** Perceptual codecs (MP3, AAC) are designed around the masking thresholds of human hearing relative to noise floor.
* **BER analysis:** Probability of bit error in digital modulation is a *direct* function of $E_{b}/N_{0}$, e.g., BPSK in AWGN gives $P_{e} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_{b}/N_{0}}\right)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Sinusoidal Amplitude to SNR in dB

**Given:** A receiver front-end delivers a sinusoidal information signal of peak amplitude $V_{m} = 100\ \mu\text{V}$ and a noise voltage (RMS) $V_{n} = 10\ \mu\text{V}$ into the same $50\ \Omega$ resistive load. Compute the SNR in dB.

**Step 1 — RMS value of the sinusoidal signal.**

For a sinusoid, RMS = peak / $\sqrt{2}$:

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{100\ \mu\text{V}}{\sqrt{2}} = 70.71\ \mu\text{V}
$$

*Conversion logic:* This conversion is needed because noise voltage is conventionally specified in RMS, and the two must be in the same unit class before forming a ratio.

**Step 2 — Compute the linear voltage ratio.**

$$
\frac{V_{\text{rms,signal}}}{V_{\text{rms,noise}}} = \frac{70.71\ \mu\text{V}}{10\ \mu\text{V}} = 7.071
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dB using the 20 log rule** (both quantities are voltages across the same impedance).

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(7.071) = 20 \times 0.8495 = 16.99\ \text{dB} \approx 17\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Logic Check:** Many students mistakenly use $10 \log_{10}$ here, getting an answer of 8.5 dB. That is *half* the correct value and will attract partial deduction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Thermal Noise Limited SNR of a Receiver

**Given:** A receiver has equivalent noise bandwidth $B = 10\ \text{MHz}$ and is matched to a source at $T = 290\ \text{K}$. The minimum detectable signal power at the input is $S = -90\ \text{dBm}$. Compute the input SNR in dB.

**Step 1 — Convert signal power to linear watts.**

$$
S = -90\ \text{dBm} \Rightarrow S_{\text{W}} = 10^{\left(\frac{-90 - 30}{10}\right)} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W} = 1\ \text{pW}
$$

*Conversion logic:* $0\ \text{dBm} = 1\ \text{mW} = 10^{-3}\ \text{W}$, so $-30$ converts dBm to dBW, then $-90$ gives the dBW value.

**Step 2 — Compute thermal noise power.**

$$
N = kTB = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 290 \times (10 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
N = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
N = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 2.9 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
N = 4.002 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{W} \approx 40\ \text{fW}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear SNR.**

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{10^{-12}}{4.002 \times 10^{-14}} = 24.99 \approx 25
$$

**Step 4 — SNR in dB.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(25) = 10 \times 1.3979 = 13.98\ \text{dB} \approx 14\ \text{dB}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Friis' Formula for Noise Figure of a Cascaded Receiver

**Problem:** A satellite receiver front-end has three cascaded stages.

| Stage | Noise Figure (NF) | Gain (G) |
|---|---|---|
| LNA | 1.5 dB | 20 dB |
| Mixer | 8 dB | −6 dB (loss) |
| IF Amplifier | 6 dB | 30 dB |

Compute the **overall noise figure** of the cascade.

**Step 1 — Convert every dB value to linear.**

$$
F_{1} = 10^{1.5/10} = 10^{0.15} = 1.4125
$$

$$
G_{1} = 10^{20/10} = 100
$$

$$
F_{2} = 10^{8/10} = 10^{0.8} = 6.3096
$$

$$
G_{2} = 10^{-6/10} = 0.2512
$$

$$
F_{3} = 10^{6/10} = 10^{0.6} = 3.9811
$$

*Conversion logic:* Friis' formula is **strictly linear**. Mixing dB with linear inside the cascade equation is a common, heavy-mark-loss mistake.

**Step 2 — Apply Friis' formula.**

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = F_{1} + \frac{F_{2} - 1}{G_{1}} + \frac{F_{3} - 1}{G_{1} G_{2}}
$$

Substitute the linear numbers:

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = 1.4125 + \frac{6.3096 - 1}{100} + \frac{3.9811 - 1}{100 \times 0.2512}
$$

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = 1.4125 + \frac{5.3096}{100} + \frac{2.9811}{25.12}
$$

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = 1.4125 + 0.05310 + 0.11867
$$

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = 1.5843
$$

**Step 3 — Convert overall NF back to dB.**

$$
NF_{\text{tot}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5843) = 1.998\ \text{dB} \approx 2\ \text{dB}
$$

**Engineering Insight:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **LNA dominates the cascade noise** even though the mixer has a much higher individual noise figure. This is precisely why every satellite down-converter starts with a *very* low-noise amplifier at the antenna terminals. The Friis formula is the single most important formula you can learn in Module 3.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Eb/N0 from SNR for a Digital Link

**Problem:** A digital radio transmits $R_{b} = 1\ \text{Mbps}$ through a channel of bandwidth $B = 200\ \text{kHz}$. The measured SNR in the channel is 12 dB. Compute $E_{b}/N_{0}$.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear.**

$$
\text{SNR} = 10^{12/10} = 10^{1.2} = 15.85
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the relation between SNR and Eb/N0.**

$$
\frac{E_{b}}{N_{0}} = \text{SNR} \cdot \frac{B}{R_{b}}
$$

*Conversion logic:* $P_{s} = E_{b} R_{b}$ and $N = N_{0} B$, so:

$$
\frac{E_{b}}{N_{0}} = \frac{P_{s}/R_{b}}{N_{0}} = \frac{P_{s}}{N_{0} R_{b}} = \frac{P_{s}}{N} \cdot \frac{B}{R_{b}} = \text{SNR} \cdot \frac{B}{R_{b}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numbers.**

$$
\frac{E_{b}}{N_{0}} = 15.85 \times \frac{200 \times 10^{3}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 15.85 \times 0.2 = 3.17
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB.**

$$
\left(\frac{E_{b}}{N_{0}}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(3.17) = 5.01\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternate Cross-Check:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} - 10 \log_{10}(R_{b}/B) = 12 - 10 \log_{10}(5) = 12 - 6.99 = 5.01\ \text{dB}$ ✓. Both routes must match.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity

**Given:** A mobile phone channel has $B = 200\ \text{kHz}$ and SNR = 15 (linear). Compute the maximum achievable bit rate.

**Step 1 — Write Shannon's formula.**

$$
C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
C = 200{,}000 \times \log_{2}(1 + 15) = 200{,}000 \times \log_{2}(16)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\log_{2}(16) = 4
$$

$$
C = 200{,}000 \times 4 = 800{,}000\ \text{bits/s} = 800\ \text{kbps}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Shannon's theorem is the **theoretical upper bound**. No coding/modulation scheme can ever exceed $C$, and practical schemes (LTE, 5G NR) operate at a fraction of it.

---

## 3.6 Consolidated Analytical Matrix — "Which Formula When?"

| If the question gives… | You should compute using… | Final result in… |
|---|---|---|
| Signal and noise **voltages** across same $R$ | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{s}/V_{n})$ | dB |
| Signal and noise **powers** | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{s}/P_{n})$ | dB |
| $T$, $B$, signal $P_{s}$ | $N = kTB$, then $\text{SNR} = P_{s}/(kTB)$ | dB |
| SNR and bit rate $R_{b}$ and bandwidth $B$ | $E_{b}/N_{0} = \text{SNR} \cdot B/R_{b}$ | Linear or dB |
| NF of multiple stages | $F_{\text{tot}} = F_{1} + \frac{F_{2}-1}{G_{1}} + \frac{F_{3}-1}{G_{1}G_{2}} + \cdots$ | Then convert to dB |
| NF in dB → equivalent temperature | $T_{e} = (F-1) \cdot 290$ | Kelvin |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow of a Communication Receiver (where SNR is set, amplified, and degraded)

The diagram below traces the *entire* journey of the Signal and the *parallel* journey of the Noise through a typical superheterodyne receiver, marking the precise location where the input SNR is established and where it gets progressively degraded.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    antA["Antenna and Feed"] --> lnaB["LNA Stage 1: High Gain Low NF"]
    lnaB --> filtC["RF Bandpass Filter"]
    filtC --> mixD["Mixer and Local Oscillator: Converts RF to IF"]
    mixD --> ifAmpE["IF Amplifier Stage 2: Moderate Gain and NF"]
    ifAmpE --> demodF["Demodulator: Recovers Baseband Information"]
    demodF --> outG["Baseband Output to Speaker or Data Sink"]

    sigA["Incoming Signal Power S_in"] -.-> lnaB
    noiA["Thermal Noise kTB and Receiver Noise"] -.-> lnaB

    noisegenA["Friis Degradation of SNR"] -.-> lnaB
    noisegenA -.-> mixD
    noisegenA -.-> ifAmpE

    monA["SNR at Antenna: S_in over kTB"] -.-> lnaB
    monB["SNR after LNA: Slightly Lower due to NF1"] -.-> mixD
    monC["SNR after Mixer: Degraded by F2 over G1"] -.-> ifAmpE
    monD["SNR at Baseband: Determines BER and Audio Quality"] -.-> outG
```

**How to read this diagram:**

* The **top row** is the *signal* path — left to right — from the antenna all the way to the baseband output.
* The **bottom rows** show the *parallel* journey of the noise: the input thermal noise $kTB$ plus the receiver's internally generated noise.
* The **orange monitoring nodes** quantify SNR at each pivotal point. The student's job in a numerical problem is to *compute* the value of each of these labels.

---

## 4.2 Noise-Figure Cascade Architecture (Friis' Formula Visualised)

The block diagram below is the standard "Friis template". Each block $A_{i}$ is a noisy two-port with **noise factor $F_{i}$** and **available power gain $G_{i}$**. The overall cascade is reduced to a single equivalent noiseless block preceded by an effective input noise temperature $T_{e,\text{tot}}$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Source with Source Temperature T0 and Available Signal Power S_in"] --> fA["Block 1: Noise Factor F1 and Gain G1"]
    fA --> fB["Block 2: Noise Factor F2 and Gain G2"]
    fB --> fC["Block 3: Noise Factor F3 and Gain G3"]
    fC --> outD["Cascade Output: SNR_out and N_out"]

    eA["Friis Effective Input Noise: Te_tot = Te1 + Te2 over G1 + Te3 over G1G2"] -.-> outD
```

**Key insights conveyed by the diagram:**

1. The first block dominates the *overall* noise figure because its noise is added **before** any gain.
2. The noise of block 2 is **divided by $G_{1}$** at the input — high first-stage gain is the designer's biggest weapon.
3. The noise of block 3 is **divided by $G_{1}G_{2}$** — practically negligible.

---

## 4.3 SNR-Dependent Decision Boundary in a Digital Receiver

For a binary digital communication system, the decision circuit compares the received sample to a **threshold**. The decision threshold is centred between the two signal levels $V_{0}$ and $V_{1}$, each of which has a Gaussian noise spread of standard deviation $\sigma_{n}$. The probability of error is the area of the noise tail that crosses the threshold — and that area is a *function* of the SNR.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rxA["Received Bit y = si plus n"] --> compB["Comparator: y greater than Threshold Vth"]
    compB --> pathC["Output Bit equals 1: Correct if si equals 1"]
    compB --> pathD["Output Bit equals 0: Correct if si equals 0"]

    highA["High SNR: Narrow Gaussian Bell, Small Error Probability Pe"] -.-> compB
    lowA["Low SNR: Wide Gaussian Bell, Large Tail Crossing Threshold, High Pe"] -.-> compB

    bfA["Eb over N0 Directly Determines Pe for BPSK, QPSK, FSK, etc."] -.-> compB
```

This diagram reinforces the KTU Module 3 message: **higher SNR ⇒ lower probability of bit error**. The exact mapping $P_{e} = f(E_{b}/N_{0})$ is a key topic in Module 4 (Digital Modulation) and builds directly on what is established in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). What is the significance of expressing it in decibels?**

**Model Answer:**

Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) is defined as the ratio of signal power to noise power at a given point in a communication system:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}
$$

**Significance of the dB form:**

* SNR can span several orders of magnitude (e.g., 1 to 1,000,000). A linear ratio is hard to read or compare.
* Expressing in decibels compresses the dynamic range and converts multiplication of cascaded gains into simple addition, which makes link-budget calculations much easier.
* It provides an intuitive measure of link quality: an SNR of 0 dB means the signal and noise are equally strong, while 30 dB and above is generally excellent for voice communication.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between S/N, C/N, and Eb/N0. State one application where each is most commonly used.**

**Model Answer:**

| Quantity | Definition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **S/N (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** | Ratio of baseband signal power to noise power at any system node | Audio / video baseband SNR specification |
| **C/N (Carrier-to-Noise Ratio)** | Ratio of RF carrier power to noise power measured in the channel bandwidth | Satellite, microwave, and cable-TV links |
| **Eb/N0** | Energy per information bit to noise spectral density ratio | BER analysis of digital modulation schemes (BPSK, QPSK, etc.) |

These three definitions differ in *where* the signal is measured and *how* it is normalised. KTU Module 3 expects students to recognise the application context.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note (Module 3 Internal Choice):** Students are required to answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full. Each option carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

### ⭐ OPTION A (14 Marks)

#### Question A(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

A communication channel has a noise bandwidth of 200 kHz and operates at a temperature of 300 K. The received signal power at the input of the receiver is −80 dBm. Calculate:

(i) The available thermal noise power in the channel.
(ii) The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in dB at the receiver input.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Thermal Noise Power:**

We use the standard thermal noise formula:

$$
N = kTB
$$

Substituting $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$, $T = 300\ \text{K}$, and $B = 200\ \text{kHz} = 2 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Hz}$:

$$
N = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (2 \times 10^{5})
$$

$$
N = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 6 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
N = 8.28 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{W}
$$

Converting to dBm:

$$
N_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{8.28 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(8.28 \times 10^{-13})
$$

$$
N_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \times (-12.082) = -120.82\ \text{dBm}
$$

**(ii) SNR in dB:**

Received signal $S = -80\ \text{dBm}$.

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = S_{\text{dBm}} - N_{\text{dBm}} = -80 - (-120.82) = 40.82\ \text{dB}
$$

**Final Answer:** $N = 8.28 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{W}\ (-120.82\ \text{dBm})$ and $\text{SNR} = 40.82\ \text{dB}$.

---

#### Question A(b). [7 Marks]

A receiver has three cascaded stages with the following parameters (linear values): $F_{1} = 1.5,\ G_{1} = 100;\ F_{2} = 5,\ G_{2} = 4;\ F_{3} = 8,\ G_{3} = 10$. Compute the **overall noise figure** in dB and the **effective input noise temperature** of the cascade.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Friis' formula for cascade noise factor.**

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = F_{1} + \frac{F_{2} - 1}{G_{1}} + \frac{F_{3} - 1}{G_{1} G_{2}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = 1.5 + \frac{5 - 1}{100} + \frac{8 - 1}{100 \times 4}
$$

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = 1.5 + 0.04 + 0.0175
$$

$$
F_{\text{tot}} = 1.5575
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dB.**

$$
NF_{\text{tot}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5575) = 1.924\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective input noise temperature.**

$$
T_{e,\text{tot}} = (F_{\text{tot}} - 1) \times T_{0} = (1.5575 - 1) \times 290 = 0.5575 \times 290
$$

$$
T_{e,\text{tot}} = 161.68\ \text{K}
$$

**Final Answer:** $NF_{\text{tot}} = 1.924\ \text{dB}$, $T_{e,\text{tot}} = 161.68\ \text{K}$.

---

### ⭐ OPTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Question B(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

Explain in detail the concept of **Noise Figure** and **Noise Temperature** of a two-port network. Derive the relationship between the two. An amplifier has a noise figure of 3 dB. Find its equivalent noise temperature.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition — Noise Figure (NF):**

Noise Figure is a measure of the degradation of the signal-to-noise ratio caused by a two-port network (typically an amplifier or mixer). It is defined as:

$$
NF = 10 \log_{10}(F), \quad F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{input}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{output}}}
$$

A noiseless device has $F = 1$ (i.e., $NF = 0\ \text{dB}$).

**Definition — Noise Temperature (Tₑ):**

The effective input noise temperature of a two-port network is the temperature at the input that, if applied to a *noiseless* version of the same network, would produce the same output noise power as the actual noisy device.

**Derivation of the relationship:**

Let the source deliver available noise power $N_{\text{in}} = k T_{0} B$ at reference temperature $T_{0} = 290\ \text{K}$. The total output noise is:

$$
N_{\text{out}} = G \cdot k T_{0} B + N_{\text{add}}
$$

where $N_{\text{add}}$ is the noise added by the network itself. The output signal is $S_{\text{out}} = G S_{\text{in}}$, so:

$$
F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}} = \frac{S_{\text{in}} / (k T_{0} B)}{G S_{\text{in}} / (G k T_{0} B + N_{\text{add}})} = \frac{G k T_{0} B + N_{\text{add}}}{G k T_{0} B}
$$

The added noise $N_{\text{add}}$ is equivalent to raising the source temperature by $T_{e}$:

$$
N_{\text{add}} = G k T_{e} B
$$

Substituting:

$$
F = \frac{G k T_{0} B + G k T_{e} B}{G k T_{0} B} = 1 + \frac{T_{e}}{T_{0}}
$$

$$
\therefore\ T_{e} = (F - 1) T_{0}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given $NF = 3\ \text{dB}$:

$$
F = 10^{3/10} = 10^{0.3} = 1.9953
$$

$$
T_{e} = (1.9953 - 1) \times 290 = 0.9953 \times 290 = 288.6\ \text{K}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{e} \approx 288.6\ \text{K}$.

---

#### Question B(b). [7 Marks]

A digital communication system transmits at a bit rate of $R_{b} = 2\ \text{Mbps}$ over a channel of bandwidth $B = 1\ \text{MHz}$. The measured SNR in the channel is 20 dB. Determine:

(i) $E_{b}/N_{0}$ in dB.
(ii) The maximum theoretical bit rate supported by the channel (Shannon limit) for the same SNR.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Compute Eb/N0:**

Convert SNR to linear:

$$
\text{SNR} = 10^{20/10} = 100
$$

Apply the relationship:

$$
\frac{E_{b}}{N_{0}} = \text{SNR} \cdot \frac{B}{R_{b}} = 100 \times \frac{1 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 50
$$

In dB:

$$
\left(\frac{E_{b}}{N_{0}}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(50) = 16.99\ \text{dB} \approx 17\ \text{dB}
$$

**(ii) Shannon Capacity:**

$$
C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR}) = (1 \times 10^{6}) \cdot \log_{2}(101)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(101) = \frac{\ln(101)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.6151}{0.6931} = 6.658
$$

$$
C \approx 6.658 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bits/s} = 6.658\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Final Answer:** $E_{b}/N_{0} = 17\ \text{dB}$ and $C = 6.658\ \text{Mbps}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units of bandwidth** — Students often forget to convert kHz/MHz to Hz inside the $kTB$ term. $1\ \text{MHz} = 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$, NOT $10^{3}$. This is the single biggest source of error in thermal-noise problems.
> 2. **20 log vs 10 log** — Always check whether the ratio is a power ratio or a voltage ratio across the **same impedance**. Mixing these up halves or doubles the answer.
> 3. **Friis in linear** — Friis' formula is **always applied in linear units**, then converted to dB at the end. Computing each term in dB and summing is a guaranteed 3-mark loss.
> 4. **Sign of gain in dB** — A "loss" stage (e.g., a passive mixer) has a **negative dB gain**. Do not omit the sign in Friis' formula; it directly affects $G_{1}G_{2}$ in the denominator.
> 5. **Forgetting the "1 + "** in Shannon's formula. It is $\log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$, not $\log_{2}(\text{SNR})$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

* **SNR definition (linear):** $P_{s}/P_{n}$. **Definition (dB):** $10 \log_{10}(P_{s}/P_{n})$ for powers, $20 \log_{10}(V_{s}/V_{n})$ for voltages across the same $R$.
* **Thermal noise power:** $N = kTB$, with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$. Always convert $B$ to Hz.
* **Reference temperature** for Noise Figure: $T_{0} = 290\ \text{K}$.
* **Noise Factor vs Noise Figure:** $F$ is linear, $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ is in dB.
* **Noise Temperature relation:** $T_{e} = (F - 1) \cdot 290\ \text{K}$.
* **Friis' Cascade Formula:**
  - In $F$: $F_{\text{tot}} = F_{1} + \frac{F_{2}-1}{G_{1}} + \frac{F_{3}-1}{G_{1}G_{2}} + \cdots$
  - In $T_{e}$: $T_{e,\text{tot}} = T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_{1}} + \frac{T_{e3}}{G_{1}G_{2}} + \cdots$
* **Eb/N0 ↔ SNR:** $E_{b}/N_{0} = \text{SNR} \cdot (B/R_{b})$.
* **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$ bits/s — the *theoretical* upper bound on bit rate.
* **Key engineering insight:** The first stage of any receiver dominates the overall noise figure, so the LNA's NF is the most important specification in the link budget.
* **Three flavours of "signal-to-noise":** S/N (baseband), C/N (RF), Eb/N0 (digital). Know where each applies.
* **Common unit conversion for dBm:** $P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{mW}})$. To get watts, $P_{\text{W}} = 10^{(P_{\text{dBm}} - 30)/10}$.
* **High SNR → clean signal → low BER; Low SNR → noisy signal → high BER.** This is the master equation of Module 3 and the foundation of Module 4 (Digital Modulation BER Analysis).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** External Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External Noise** is defined as the unwanted random electrical disturbance that originates from sources **outside** the communication system — both natural (atmospheric, cosmic, solar) and man-made (industrial, vehicular) — and couples into the receiver primarily through the antenna or the propagation medium.

In a communication receiver, any signal reaching the antenna is accompanied by this external energy. The performance of a receiver is therefore limited not only by internally generated noise (thermal, shot, flicker) but also by the **ambient electromagnetic environment** that bathes the antenna.

### The Three Principal External Noise Sources (As per KTU Syllabus)

1. **Atmospheric Noise** — caused by natural electrical discharges in the earth's atmosphere.
2. **Industrial / Man-Made Noise** — caused by human-made electrical equipment.
