3. **Extraterrestrial Noise** — noise arriving from outer space (Solar + Cosmic / Galactic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Favourite):** External noise is received by the **antenna** along with the desired signal. Internal noise, in contrast, is generated **inside** the receiver circuitry even when no signal is present.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Busy Market Place
Imagine you are standing in the middle of a crowded Indian market, trying to listen to your friend speak softly to you. The shouts of vendors, the honking of vehicles, the crackle of a passing autorickshaw's engine, and even the distant thunder of a storm are all **external disturbances** that enter your ears (your "antenna") and corrupt the desired voice message. 

In a similar way, the radio receiver's antenna picks up your friend's transmission, but along with it come *lightning-generated static*, *a sparking electric motor down the road*, and *the faint hiss of distant stars* — all of which together form the external noise.

### Standard Metrics Used

- **Noise Power Spectral Density:** $N_0 = kT$ in **W/Hz**
- **Total Noise Power:** $P_n = kTB$ in **watts (W)**
- **Effective Noise Temperature:** $T_e$ in **kelvin (K)**
- **Noise Figure:** $F$ (dimensionless, in dB)

where the constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** is **Boltzmann's constant**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Atmospheric Noise (Static / Spherics / Strays)

Atmospheric noise is generated by the **natural electrical discharge phenomena** occurring continuously in the earth's atmosphere — predominantly **lightning strokes** within thunderstorms.

- **Origin:** Lightning returns the atmosphere to electrical equilibrium. Each discharge radiates a short, sharp electromagnetic pulse.
- **Frequency of Occurrence:** There are, on average, **~100 lightning strikes per second** somewhere on the planet.
- **Spectrum:** The energy is spread over a wide range; the noise amplitude is **maximum below ~30 MHz** and decreases rapidly with increasing frequency.
- **Dependence:** 
  - Strongly dependent on **geographic location** (highest near the equator — *Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone*).
  - Strongly dependent on **time of day, season, and weather**.
  - Above ~30 MHz, atmospheric noise becomes negligible.
- **Mathematical Character:** Quasi-impulsive in the time domain.

$$P_{\text{atm}}(f) \propto \frac{1}{f^{\alpha}}$$

where $\alpha \approx 2$ to $3$ for typical storm centers. This inverse-frequency behaviour is the reason AM broadcast (535–1705 kHz) suffers badly from atmospheric noise, while satellite TV (GHz range) is essentially immune to it.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters for KTU:** Atmospheric noise is the **dominant noise** in the **HF, MF, and LF** bands — i.e., long-distance sky-wave and ground-wave communication.

### 2.2 Industrial / Man-Made Noise

Generated by **human-made electrical devices** — sparking, commutator noise, fluorescent lights, ignition systems, power lines, electric motors, digital switching circuits.

- **Spectrum:** Energy concentrated mostly in the **LF, MF, and VHF** ranges (1 MHz – 500 MHz roughly).
- **Dependence:** Strongly **localized** (high in industrial cities, low in rural areas).
- **Character:** Highly impulsive, similar to atmospheric noise in shape.

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial noise has decreased in some frequency bands due to better **EMC (Electromagnetic Compatibility) regulations** on consumer products.

### 2.3 Extraterrestrial Noise

Noise that arrives at the antenna from **outer space**. It has two distinct sub-categories:

#### (a) Solar Noise
- Emitted by the **Sun** due to the thermal radiation of its chromosphere and corona.
- Two operating conditions:
  - **Quiet Sun:** Sun is in a normal state — low noise.
  - **Disturbed Sun (Solar Flares / Sunspot Activity):** Noise level rises dramatically (can be 20–60 dB above quiet level) and may last for hours to days, disrupting HF communication.
- **Spectrum:** Most intense below **~300 MHz**; significant at frequencies below **100 MHz**.

#### (b) Cosmic / Galactic Noise
- Distributed sources of radio waves from **stars, nebulae, and other celestial objects** outside our solar system.
- Concentrated along the **plane of the Milky Way galaxy** — strongest when the antenna is pointed toward the galactic center (Sagittarius constellation).
- **Dominant Range:** Approximately **15 MHz to 100 GHz**.
- Above ~1–2 GHz, the cosmic background drops and is dominated by the **2.7 K Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a receiver antenna looking upward, the antenna "sees" a brightness temperature $T_{sky}(f,\theta,\phi)$ that **depends on direction** — the basis of radio astronomy and satellite TV.

### 2.4 Concept of Effective Noise Temperature (External View)

Antennas do not have a single physical temperature when receiving external noise — the noise they collect is best characterized by an **equivalent noise temperature** $T_{\text{ant}}$ such that:

$$P_{\text{noise,ant}} = k \, T_{\text{ant}} \, B$$

Typical order of magnitudes:

| Source | Dominant Frequency | Effective Noise Temp. (K) |
|---|---|---|
| Earth (ground) at antenna | HF/VHF | $\approx 290$ |
| Quiet Sky (zenith) at 1 GHz | 1 GHz | $\approx 3$–$10$ |
| Quiet Sun (towards sun) | 100 MHz – 10 GHz | $\approx 10^4$ – $10^5$ |
| Galactic Center | 100 MHz | $\approx 10^5$ |
| Cosmic Microwave Background | All | $\approx 2.7$ |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` in formulas below to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Sl. | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_n = kTB$ | Available noise power in bandwidth $B$ at temperature $T$ |
| 2 | $N_0 = kT$ | Noise power spectral density (W/Hz) |
| 3 | $P_{\text{ant}} = kT_{\text{ant}}B$ | External noise power delivered by antenna |
| 4 | $T_{\text{ant}} = \frac{P_{\text{noise,ant}}}{kB}$ | Definition of antenna noise temperature |
| 5 | $T_{sys} = T_{\text{ant}} + T_{\text{eqv,rec}}$ | Cascaded system noise temperature |
| 6 | $F = 1 + \frac{T_{\text{eqv,rec}}}{T_0}$ | Noise figure from equivalent temperature, with $T_0 = 290$ K |
| 7 | $T_{\text{ant}} \approx T_{\text{sky}} + T_{\text{ground}} \cdot (1 - \eta)$ | Antenna sees both sky and ground depending on its pattern |
| 8 | $P_{\text{atm}}(f) \propto 1/f^{2}$ | Atmospheric noise rolls off with frequency |
| 9 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{in}} = \frac{S}{kTB}$ | Input SNR set by external noise |
| 10 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{F}$ | Output SNR degraded by receiver noise figure |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **HF Radio Broadcasting:** Atmospheric noise dictates the minimum usable frequency.
- **Satellite TV (DTH):** Cosmic background and ground temperature limit the downlink $C/N_0$.
- **Radio Astronomy:** Galactic noise is the *signal* — antennas are designed to maximize it.
- **Deep-Space Communication:** Cosmic Microwave Background (2.7 K) is the ultimate noise floor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Antenna Noise Temperature from Multiple Sources

Consider an antenna that is simultaneously observing **N independent external noise sources**, each characterized by an equivalent temperature $T_i$ and subtending a fraction of the antenna's radiation pattern.

The **total noise power** collected by the antenna within a bandwidth $B$ is the sum of contributions from every direction weighted by the antenna's gain pattern $G(\theta,\phi)$:

$$P_{\text{noise,ant}} = kB \int_{0}^{4\pi} T_{b}(\theta,\phi) \cdot \frac{G(\theta,\phi)}{4\pi} \, d\Omega$$

The quantity in front of $kB$ is, by definition, the **antenna noise temperature**:

$$T_{\text{ant}} = \int_{0}^{4\pi} T_{b}(\theta,\phi) \cdot \frac{G(\theta,\phi)}{4\pi} \, d\Omega$$

For **discrete sources** (e.g., a strong radio source of brightness temperature $T_1$ inside a beam solid angle $\Omega_1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ant}} &= T_1 \cdot \frac{\Omega_1}{\Omega_A} + T_2 \cdot \frac{\Omega_2}{\Omega_A} + \dots \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i \cdot \frac{\Omega_i}{\Omega_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Omega_A$ is the antenna's **beam solid angle**, and $\Omega_i$ is the solid angle subtended by the $i$-th source.

> **Conversion Logic:** Since $G(\theta,\phi) \, d\Omega / 4\pi$ is the *fraction of total radiated power* going in that direction, multiplying by the source brightness temperature and integrating gives the effective thermodynamic temperature seen at the antenna terminals.

### 3.2 Derivation: System (Cascaded) Noise Temperature

A typical receiver consists of the antenna feeding a low-noise amplifier (LNA) with gain $G_1$ and equivalent noise temperature $T_{e1}$, followed by a second stage with gain $G_2$ and $T_{e2}$, etc.

The total noise power at the output, normalized to the input, is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{n,\text{total}} &= k B \left[ T_{\text{ant}} + T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1} + \frac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2} + \dots \right] \\
\Rightarrow T_{\text{sys}} &= T_{\text{ant}} + T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1} + \frac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2} + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first stage's noise is added *directly* to the antenna noise. Every subsequent stage's noise is *divided by the cumulative gain* of the stages before it, because the noise injected later gets attenuated as it travels back to the antenna. This is **Friis' formula for noise**.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Line:** Always place a **high-gain, low-noise first stage** (LNA) at the antenna terminals so that the antenna's external noise dominates the system noise budget.

### 3.3 Derivation: Atmospheric Noise Spectral Roll-off

A single lightning stroke is essentially a step-like current pulse. Its frequency-domain representation is rich at low frequencies.

For a statistical model with stroke density $n_s$ (strokes/sec/km²) and average energy per stroke $E_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{atm}}(f) &\propto n_s E_0 \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{1 + (f/f_c)^2} \right] \\
&\approx \frac{n_s E_0}{(f/f_c)^2} \quad \text{for } f \gg f_c
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f_c$ is the corner frequency of a typical stroke (~1–10 kHz). This explains the **20 dB/decade** fall-off seen in atmospheric noise measurements.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An antenna pointing at the quiet sky has an effective noise temperature of $T_{\text{ant}} = 35$ K. It feeds a receiver with $T_e = 150$ K. Compute the total system noise temperature and the available noise power in a 10 MHz bandwidth.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sys}} &= T_{\text{ant}} + T_e \\
&= 35 + 150 \\
&= 185 \, \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= k \, T_{\text{sys}} \, B \\
&= (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 185 \times (10 \times 10^6) \\
&= 1.38 \times 185 \times 10^{-16} \\
&= 255.3 \times 10^{-16} \\
&\approx 2.55 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

In dBm: $10 \log_{10}(2.55 \times 10^{-14} / 10^{-3}) = 10 \log_{10}(2.55 \times 10^{-11}) \approx -105.9 \, \text{dBm}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** From W to dBm, use $P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_W / 10^{-3})$.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Three External Noise Sources

| Parameter | Atmospheric Noise | Industrial Noise | Extraterrestrial Noise |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Lightning, thunderstorms | Man-made devices (motors, ignitions) | Sun, stars, galaxies |
| **Dominant Band** | $\leq 30$ MHz (LF/MF/HF) | 1 MHz – 500 MHz (MF/VHF) | 15 MHz – 100 GHz (HF/UHF/SHF) |
| **Frequency Dependence** | $1/f^{2}$ to $1/f^{3}$ | Variable, mostly localized | Increases at low frequencies |
| **Time Variability** | High (weather, diurnal) | High (human activity) | Sun: very high; Galaxy: low |
| **Geographic Dependence** | Equatorial regions worst | Urban/industrial areas | Antenna pointing direction |
| **Predictability** | Statistical (CCIR curves) | Hard to model | Astronomical models |
| **Primary Countermeasure** | Limiter, error coding, HF bypass | Shielding, filtering, site selection | Cooling, narrow beam, pointing |
| **Polarization Effect** | Mostly vertical E-field | Depends on source | Depends on source orientation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — External Noise Coupling

The figure below shows how external noise enters a communication system through three different paths and how the receiver processes it.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Atmospheric Noise Source] --> ANT[Receiving Antenna]
    A2[Industrial Noise Source] --> ANT
    A3[Extraterrestrial Noise Source] --> ANT
    ANT --> TRK[Antenna Feeder and Tuning Network]
    TRK --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier Stage]
    LNA --> MIX[Mixer and IF Amplifier]
    MIX --> DET[Detector and Baseband Stage]
    DET --> OUT[Audio or Data Output]
    A1 -. T_ant_atm .-> TN[Antenna Noise Temperature T_ant]
    A2 -. T_ant_ind .-> TN
    A3 -. T_ant_ext .-> TN
    TN --> LNA
    DET -. S plus N .-> SNR[Signal to Noise Ratio SNR Calculation]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Frequency Spectrum Allocation of External Noise

```mermaid
graph LR
    LF[LF Band 30 to 300 kHz] --> A1[Atmospheric Dominant]
    MF[MF Band 300 kHz to 3 MHz] --> A2[Atmospheric plus Industrial]
    HF[HF Band 3 to 30 MHz] --> A3[Atmospheric plus Galactic Dominant]
    VHF[VHF Band 30 to 300 MHz] --> A4[Industrial plus Galactic plus Solar Disturbed]
    UHF[UHF Band 300 MHz to 3 GHz] --> A5[Galactic and Solar Quiet plus Sky]
    SHF[SHF Band 3 to 30 GHz] --> A6[Sky and CMB Dominant]
    EHF[EHF Band above 30 GHz] --> A7[Sky and Atmospheric Absorption]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cascaded Noise Temperature Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANT[Antenna External Noise T_ant] --> AD1[Stage 1 LNA Gain G1 and Noise T_e1]
    AD1 --> AD2[Stage 2 Mixer Gain G2 and Noise T_e2]
    AD2 --> AD3[Stage 3 IF Gain G3 and Noise T_e3]
    AD3 --> TSYS[Total System Noise T_sys]
    TANT -. direct contribution .-> TSYS
    AD1 -. divided by 1 .-> TSYS
    AD2 -. divided by G1 .-> TSYS
    AD3 -. divided by G1 G2 .-> TSYS
```

> [!TIP]
> **Read this as:** the closer the LNA is to the antenna, the more it dominates the noise budget. Antennas in space (low $T_{\text{ant}}$) plus high-$G_1$, low-$T_{e1}$ LNA yield the lowest $T_{\text{sys}}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define external noise. List any three sources of external noise in a communication system.**

**Model Answer:**
External noise refers to the unwanted random electrical signals that originate from **sources external** to the communication system and enter the receiver primarily through the antenna. The three main sources are:
1. **Atmospheric noise** — caused by lightning and thunderstorm activity.
2. **Industrial (man-made) noise** — generated by electrical equipment, motors, and ignition systems.
3. **Extraterrestrial noise** — comprising solar noise and cosmic/galactic noise from space.

> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):** Definition = 1 mark, List = 2 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between atmospheric noise and industrial noise in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. | Atmospheric Noise | Industrial Noise |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural origin (lightning) | Man-made origin (machinery) |
| 2 | Dominant in LF, MF, HF bands (below 30 MHz) | Dominant in MF, VHF bands (1–500 MHz) |
| 3 | Strongest near equatorial regions and during storms | Strongest in industrial/urban areas |
| 4 | Time variation tied to weather and diurnal cycle | Time variation tied to human activity hours |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students often confuse **internal noise** with **external noise**. Remember: external noise enters through the *antenna* and includes atmospheric, industrial, and extraterrestrial. Internal noise is generated *inside* the receiver.
> 2. Many students forget to **convert bandwidth** into Hz when using $P_n = kTB$. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. In numericals, do not forget to include **antenna noise temperature** when computing the *total* system noise. Students often compute only receiver noise and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. In the derivation of cascaded noise, the terms $T_{e2}/G_1, T_{e3}/(G_1 G_2), \dots$ are often missed — KTU specifically looks for **Friis' formula** in full form.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choice 1)*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the different sources of **external noise** affecting a communication receiver. Discuss in detail the **atmospheric noise** and its frequency characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **total system noise temperature** of a cascaded receiver with three stages having gains $G_1, G_2, G_3$ and equivalent noise temperatures $T_{e1}, T_{e2}, T_{e3}$ respectively. A satellite receiver operates at an antenna temperature of 80 K. The first stage LNA has $G_1 = 1000$ and $T_{e1} = 50$ K, followed by a mixer with $G_2 = 0.5$ and $T_{e2} = 500$ K. Compute the system noise temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The external noise sources coupling into a receiver are:

1. **Atmospheric noise** — generated by lightning discharges and electrical storms in the troposphere and ionosphere. The energy is spread across a wide spectrum with a peak in the LF, MF, and HF bands. Its spectral density falls as $1/f^2$ above the corner frequency. It is maximum in tropical regions and during the rainy season.

2. **Industrial noise** — caused by man-made electrical devices such as commutator motors, neon signs, ignition systems, and power switches. It is most severe in urban areas and lies in the 1 MHz – 500 MHz band. It is impulsive in nature.

3. **Extraterrestrial noise**:
   - **Solar noise** — emitted by the sun. Quiet sun is low; disturbed sun (solar flares) raises the noise by 20–60 dB.
   - **Cosmic / galactic noise** — distributed along the Milky Way; dominates 15 MHz – 100 GHz.

**Atmospheric noise frequency characteristics:**
- Maximum amplitude below 30 MHz.
- Average noise field strength follows $P(f) \propto 1/f^2$.
- Characterized statistically by **CCIR curves** showing the expected noise factor $F_a$ vs. frequency for different geographic regions.
- Negligible above ~30 MHz because the ground-wave attenuation rises and the noise falls below receiver thermal noise.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation of cascaded noise temperature:**

For two cascaded stages, the output noise power referred to the input is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{n,\text{out,ref}} &= k B T_{e1} \cdot G_1 + \frac{k B T_{e2}}{G_1} \\
\Rightarrow T_{e,\text{total}} &= T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Extending to three stages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{e,\text{total}} &= T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1} + \frac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the antenna contribution, the **total system noise temperature** is:

$$T_{\text{sys}} = T_{\text{ant}} + T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1} + \frac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2}$$

**Numerical computation:**

Given: $T_{\text{ant}} = 80$ K, $T_{e1} = 50$ K, $G_1 = 1000$, $T_{e2} = 500$ K, $G_2 = 0.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sys}} &= 80 + 50 + \frac{500}{1000} + 0 \\
&= 80 + 50 + 0.5 \\
&= 130.5 \, \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Term $T_{e3}$ not given, so neglected.)

**Answer:** $T_{\text{sys}} = 130.5$ K.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Choice 2)*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **solar noise** and **cosmic noise** under extraterrestrial noise. Compare their spectral characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An antenna pointing at a clear sky has an effective noise temperature of 25 K. It feeds a receiver with a noise figure of 2.5 dB and a bandwidth of 200 kHz. Compute: (i) the equivalent noise temperature of the receiver, (ii) the total system noise temperature, and (iii) the available noise power in dBm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Solar noise:** Emitted by the sun as broadband radio radiation due to the thermal motion of charged particles in its chromosphere and corona. Two states:
- *Quiet sun* — provides a low, slowly varying noise floor.
- *Disturbed sun* — during sunspot activity and solar flares, the noise flux rises sharply (20–60 dB above quiet), causing HF radio blackouts.

**Cosmic (Galactic) noise:** Radio waves from stars, nebulae, and the interstellar medium outside our solar system. The noise is concentrated along the galactic plane and strongest toward the galactic center. The galactic noise temperature falls roughly as $1/f^{2.5}$ between 100 MHz and 1 GHz, then levels off at a few kelvin (dominated by the 2.7 K cosmic microwave background above ~2 GHz).

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Solar Noise | Cosmic Noise |
|---|---|---|
| Source | The Sun | Stars, galaxies, interstellar gas |
| Variability | Highly variable (flares) | Slowly varying |
| Frequency peak | Below 300 MHz | 15 MHz – 100 GHz |
| Antenna pointing | Towards the Sun | Towards galactic plane |
| Practical impact | Disrupts HF communication | Limits satellite and radio-astronomy SNR |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $T_{\text{ant}} = 25$ K, $F = 2.5$ dB, $B = 200$ kHz.

**(i) Equivalent noise temperature of receiver:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{lin}} &= 10^{2.5/10} = 10^{0.25} \approx 1.778 \\
T_e &= (F - 1) \times T_0 = (1.778 - 1) \times 290 \\
&= 0.778 \times 290 \approx 225.7 \, \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) System noise temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sys}} &= T_{\text{ant}} + T_e \\
&= 25 + 225.7 = 250.7 \, \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Available noise power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= k \, T_{\text{sys}} \, B \\
&= (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 250.7 \times (200 \times 10^3) \\
&= 1.38 \times 250.7 \times 2 \times 10^{-17} \\
&\approx 6.92 \times 10^{-16} \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to dBm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n \text{ (dBm)} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{6.92 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-3}}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(6.92 \times 10^{-13}) \\
&\approx -121.6 \, \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $T_e \approx 225.7$ K, $T_{\text{sys}} \approx 250.7$ K, $P_n \approx -121.6$ dBm.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External Noise — Must-Know Points for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ⭐ External noise originates **outside** the receiver and enters through the **antenna**.
- ⭐ The **three** sources are: **Atmospheric, Industrial, Extraterrestrial (Solar + Cosmic)**.
- ⭐ **Atmospheric noise** is dominant below **30 MHz**; falls as $1/f^2$.
- ⭐ **Industrial noise** is dominant in urban/industrial areas; lies in **1–500 MHz**.
- ⭐ **Solar noise** has quiet and disturbed phases; flares can raise noise by 20–60 dB.
- ⭐ **Cosmic / galactic noise** is strongest along the **Milky Way**; 2.7 K CMB above ~2 GHz.
- ⭐ Master the formula: $P_n = k T_{\text{sys}} B$, with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- ⭐ **Friis' cascaded noise formula:** $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{e1} + T_{e2}/G_1 + T_{e3}/(G_1 G_2) + \dots$
- ⭐ Noise figure ↔ Noise temperature: $F = 1 + T_e/T_0$, with $T_0 = 290$ K.
- ⭐ Place the **LNA closest to the antenna** — first-stage gain suppresses later-stage noise.
- ⭐ Always convert **bandwidth to Hz** and **noise figure to linear scale** before numerical computation.
- ⭐ Distinguish **internal noise** (thermal, shot, flicker, transit-time) from **external noise** in two-mark questions.
- ⭐ For satellite links, the antenna noise temperature is usually **low (≈ 25–50 K)** — this is why satellite TV works so well despite weak signals.
- ⭐ Remember the conversion: $P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_W \times 1000)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internal Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Internal Noise — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of communication engineering, **Noise** is defined as any unwanted signal that interferes with the desired information-bearing signal, corrupting it and degrading the quality of communication. It is a random, unpredictable electrical disturbance that cannot be completely eliminated but can only be minimized through careful system design.

**Internal Noise** refers to the class of electrical noise that is *generated within the components and devices* of a communication system itself, due to the inherent physical properties of conductors, semiconductor devices, and active components. It is fundamentally different from external noise (atmospheric noise, man-made noise, cosmic noise, etc.) in that it cannot be mitigated by shielding, filtering of the environment, or antenna orientation — it is a thermodynamic and quantum-level consequence of the materials used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The 2024 scheme specifically categorizes internal noise into four canonical types — **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise)**, **Shot Noise**, **Flicker Noise (1/f noise)**, and **Transit-Time Noise**. Each of these must be studied in terms of its physical origin, mathematical model, and effect on receiver sensitivity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Picture

Imagine you are trying to have a quiet conversation with a friend in a crowded marketplace. Even if your friend is standing right next to you, the shouts of vendors, honking vehicles, and background chatter interfere with your conversation — this is analogous to **external noise**. 

Now, suppose you and your friend move to a perfectly silent soundproof room. Yet, if you both have heavy colds and are breathing noisily, you will still struggle to hear each other clearly because *your own bodies* are producing the disturbance. This residual, unavoidable disturbance — originating from the very source of communication itself — is **internal noise**.

In a resistor, electrons are in constant thermal agitation; in a semiconductor junction, charges are quantized and arrive at random times; in a transistor at high frequencies, carriers take a finite time to cross the junction. All of these microscopic random processes sum up to produce a macroscopic noisy voltage or current at the terminals.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants You Must Memorize for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Boltzmann's Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
> - **Electronic Charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ **Coulomb**
> - **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 290$ **K** (17 °C, the IEEE standard)
> - **Room Temperature Reference:** $T = 300$ **K** (often used for quick approximations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Noise Metrics in Communication Engineering:**
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Ratio of signal power to noise power, dimensionless, often expressed in dB.
> - **Noise Figure (NF):** Degradation factor of SNR as the signal passes through a network, expressed in dB.
> - **Equivalent Noise Temperature ($T_e$):** Hypothetical temperature at which a noisy device would generate the same noise as an ideal resistor, expressed in Kelvin.
> - **Noise Bandwidth ($\Delta f$ or $B_n$):** Equivalent rectangular bandwidth over which noise is integrated, in Hertz.

## 1.4 Why Internal Noise is the Limiting Factor in Modern Receivers

In contemporary communication systems, especially satellite, deep-space, and cellular systems, the received signal power at the antenna is extraordinarily small (often in the order of picowatts or femtowatts). At such low power levels, the **internal noise generated by the very first amplifying stage of the receiver** becomes the dominant limiting factor. This is why the study of internal noise is not merely academic — it directly determines the **minimum detectable signal (MDS)**, the **bit error rate (BER)**, and ultimately the **range and reliability** of any real-world communication link.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Types of Internal Noise — A Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise)

- **Physical Origin:** Random thermal agitation (Brownian motion) of charge carriers (electrons) inside a conductor or resistor at any temperature above absolute zero.
- **Nature:** Gaussian, stationary, white (flat power spectral density) up to ~1000 GHz.
- **Key Property:** Exists in *all* passive resistive components even without any applied voltage or current — it is purely a function of temperature and resistance.
- **Why It Matters:** Sets the absolute noise floor of any electronic system. The *minimum possible* noise in any receiver is the thermal noise of its input matching network.

**Mathematical Model:**

The mean-square noise voltage across a resistor $R$ over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ is given by the **Johnson-Nyquist Equation**:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$$

The available noise power (maximum power that can be delivered to a matched load) is:

$$P_n = k_B T \Delta f$$

Note that this *available power is independent of the value of $R$* — a counterintuitive but profoundly important result.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because thermal noise power is directly proportional to bandwidth $\Delta f$, reducing the receiver bandwidth (e.g., by using narrower filters) is the single most effective way to reduce thermal noise. This is why narrowband communication systems have better sensitivity than wideband ones.

### 2.1.2 Shot Noise

- **Physical Origin:** Discrete nature of charge carriers. In a semiconductor junction (diode, BJT, FET), electrons and holes cross the junction in discrete, random packets, leading to fluctuations in current even when the average current is constant.
- **Nature:** Poisson-distributed, white noise (flat PSD) for frequencies up to about the device's transit time.
- **Key Property:** Exists only in *active* devices with DC current flow; absent in pure resistors at zero bias.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$$

where $I_{DC}$ is the average DC current flowing through the device.

### 2.1.3 Flicker Noise (1/f Noise or Pink Noise)

- **Physical Origin:** Imperfections in the semiconductor crystal lattice, surface traps, contamination at junctions, and slow variations in carrier density. The exact mechanism is not fully understood theoretically.
- **Nature:** Power spectral density is inversely proportional to frequency — hence the name **1/f noise**.
- **Key Property:** Dominant at **low frequencies** (typically below 1 kHz to 10 kHz), and decreases as frequency increases. For this reason, it is sometimes called *pink* noise.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$\overline{v_n^2} \propto \frac{\Delta f}{f^{\alpha}}$$

where $\alpha$ is approximately 1 (typically 0.8 to 1.5 depending on the device).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Flicker Noise Matters:** In oscillators, 1/f noise is up-converted to phase noise near the carrier. In low-frequency instrumentation amplifiers, it can dominate and must be reduced using **chopper stabilization** or **auto-zero techniques**.

### 2.1.4 Transit-Time Noise

- **Physical Origin:** When a carrier takes a finite, non-zero time to traverse the active region of a device (e.g., from emitter to collector in a BJT, or from source to drain in a FET), the current generated at the output terminal is not an instantaneous replica of the input. At very high frequencies, this delay becomes comparable to the period of the signal, causing random phase variations that manifest as additional noise.
- **Nature:** Becomes significant only at **microwave and millimeter-wave frequencies** (typically > 1 GHz).
- **Key Property:** Negligible at low frequencies; increasingly dominant as $f \to f_T$ (the cutoff frequency of the device).

> [!NOTE]
> **Carrier Transit Time:** $\tau = \frac{d}{v_{sat}}$, where $d$ is the active region thickness and $v_{sat}$ is the carrier saturation velocity.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a high-yield revision resource. Memorize all of these formulas — they appear regularly in KTU module tests and university examinations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Mean-square thermal noise voltage | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$ | $\text{V}^2$ | Across a resistor $R$ |
| 2 | Mean-square thermal noise current | $\overline{i_n^2} = \dfrac{4 k_B T \Delta f}{R}$ | $\text{A}^2$ | Norton equivalent |
| 3 | Available thermal noise power | $P_n = k_B T \Delta f$ | W (Watts) | Independent of $R$ |
| 4 | Mean-square shot noise current | $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$ | $\text{A}^2$ | Active devices only |
| 5 | Noise Figure (definition) | $F = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}$ | dimensionless | $F \geq 1$ always |
| 6 | Noise Figure in dB | $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ | dB | Used in specifications |
| 7 | Equivalent Noise Temperature | $T_e = T_0 (F - 1)$ | K | $T_0 = 290$ K |
| 8 | Friis' Formula (Cascaded NF) | $F = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$ | dimensionless | First stage dominates |
| 9 | Total System Noise Temperature | $T_{\text{sys}} = T_e + T_{\text{ant}}$ | K | $T_{\text{ant}} \approx 290$ K for ground |
| 10 | Minimum Detectable Signal | $\text{MDS} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(\Delta f) + NF$ | dBm | At room temperature |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not abstract — they directly govern the design of:

- **Satellite Ground Station Receivers:** The LNA (Low Noise Amplifier) at the front-end of a satellite receiver is chosen primarily for its *noise figure* (typically 0.3–1.0 dB for cryogenically cooled systems). Every fraction of a dB of NF translates directly into a larger communication range.
- **Software Defined Radios (SDR):** The noise bandwidth of the analog front-end filter sets the noise floor, which in turn sets the bit-error-rate performance for a given modulation scheme.
- **Medical Instrumentation:** ECG and EEG amplifiers operate at very low frequencies where 1/f noise dominates — special low-noise chopper-stabilized amplifiers are used.
- **Optical Communication Receivers:** APD and PIN photodiodes exhibit shot noise that limits the minimum detectable optical power, dictating the maximum fiber length between repeaters.

## 2.4 Noise Bandwidth vs. Signal Bandwidth

A subtle but exam-relevant point: the noise bandwidth $B_n$ is *not* the same as the 3-dB signal bandwidth $B_{3dB}$. They are related by:

$$B_n = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert H(f) \vert^2}{H_{\max}^2} \, df$$

For a single-pole RC low-pass filter, $B_n = \dfrac{\pi}{2} B_{3dB} \approx 1.57 \, B_{3dB}$. This factor often appears in noise calculations in KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Available Thermal Noise Power (Johnson-Nyquist Result)

We derive the result $P_n = k_B T \Delta f$ using the **principle of equipartition of energy** from statistical thermodynamics.

### Step 1: Model the Resistor as a Noisy Source

A real (noisy) resistor $R$ at temperature $T$ can be modeled as an ideal (noise-free) resistor in series with a noise voltage source $v_n(t)$, where the mean-square value of $v_n(t)$ over bandwidth $\Delta f$ is $\overline{v_n^2}$.

### Step 2: Maximum Power Transfer Condition

To extract *all* available noise power, we connect a matched load $R_L = R$ to the source. The mean-square voltage across the load is half of the source open-circuit voltage:

$$v_{\text{load}}^{2} = \frac{\overline{v_n^2}}{4} = \frac{4 k_B T R \Delta f}{4} = k_B T R \Delta f$$

### Step 3: Compute the Delivered Power

The power dissipated in the matched load is:

$$P_n = \frac{v_{\text{load}}^{2}}{R} = \frac{k_B T R \Delta f}{R} = k_B T \Delta f$$

Thus we have rigorously derived the famous result:

$$P_n = k_B T \Delta f \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

This is independent of $R$ — a remarkable result with deep physical meaning related to the **second law of thermodynamics**.

### Step 4: Verification via Planck's Law (Nyquist's Original Approach)

Nyquist's original 1928 derivation uses the one-dimensional transmission line model. A transmission line of length $L$ terminated in resistance $R$ supports standing waves. The energy per mode of an electromagnetic wave in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$ is, by equipartition, $k_B T$ (for each degree of freedom — one for voltage, one for current, averaging to $k_B T$ per mode in matched conditions). The number of modes in bandwidth $\Delta f$ in a line of length $L$ is $\dfrac{2 L \Delta f}{c}$ (factor 2 for two directions of propagation).

Total energy $U = k_B T \times \dfrac{2 L \Delta f}{c}$. This energy travels to the load and is absorbed in time $t = \dfrac{L}{c}$, so power delivered is:

$$P = \frac{U}{t} = \frac{k_B T \times (2 L \Delta f / c)}{L / c} = 2 k_B T \Delta f$$

The factor of 2 comes from matching — splitting equally between the source and the line. After proper matched termination, the delivered power to the load is:

$$P_n = k_B T \Delta f$$

which matches the resistor-model derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Examinations:** You are only required to derive the result using the resistor model (Steps 1–3). The Planck/transmission-line derivation (Step 4) is bonus depth for full marks on the *Derive* or *Show that* type of 14-mark questions.

## 3.2 Derivation of Noise Figure and Equivalent Noise Temperature

### Step 1: Define Total Output Noise Power

For a two-port network with gain $G$ and bandwidth $\Delta f$, the total output noise power is:

$$N_{\text{out}} = G \cdot N_{\text{in}} + N_{\text{added}}$$

where $N_{\text{added}}$ is the noise power added by the network itself.

### Step 2: Apply the Definition of Noise Figure

By definition:

$$F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}} = \frac{S_{\text{in}}/N_{\text{in}}}{S_{\text{out}}/N_{\text{out}}} = \frac{S_{\text{in}}/N_{\text{in}}}{G S_{\text{in}}/(G N_{\text{in}} + N_{\text{added}})} = \frac{G N_{\text{in}} + N_{\text{added}}}{G N_{\text{in}}}$$

$$\boxed{F = 1 + \frac{N_{\text{added}}}{G \cdot N_{\text{in}}}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $N_{\text{in}} = k_B T_0 \Delta f$ at Reference Temperature

When the input is connected to a matched source at the standard reference temperature $T_0 = 290$ K:

$$F = 1 + \frac{N_{\text{added}}}{G \cdot k_B T_0 \Delta f}$$

### Step 4: Express Added Noise as an Equivalent Temperature

We model the noisy network as an *ideal noise-free* network with an additional input noise source corresponding to an equivalent temperature $T_e$:

$$N_{\text{added}} = G \cdot k_B T_e \Delta f$$

Substituting into the expression for $F$:

$$F = 1 + \frac{G \cdot k_B T_e \Delta f}{G \cdot k_B T_0 \Delta f} = 1 + \frac{T_e}{T_0}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{T_e = T_0 (F - 1)}$$

This is the fundamental relation between noise figure and equivalent noise temperature, used in every satellite and microwave receiver design.

## 3.3 Derivation of Friis' Formula for Cascaded Noise Figure

Consider three cascaded stages with gains $G_1, G_2, G_3$ and noise figures $F_1, F_2, F_3$. The total output noise is:

$$N_{\text{out}} = G_1 G_2 G_3 \cdot N_{\text{in}} + G_2 G_3 \cdot N_1 + G_3 \cdot N_2 + N_3$$

where $N_i = (F_i - 1) G_i \cdot k_B T_0 \Delta f$ is the noise added by stage $i$.

Dividing through by $G_1 G_2 G_3 \cdot N_{\text{in}}$ to get the total system noise figure $F$:

$$F = 1 + \frac{N_1}{G_1 N_{\text{in}}} + \frac{N_2}{G_1 G_2 N_{\text{in}}} + \frac{N_3}{G_1 G_2 G_3 N_{\text{in}}}$$

Using $F_i - 1 = \dfrac{N_i}{G_i N_{\text{in}}}$, we obtain the celebrated **Friis' formula for noise figure**:

$$F = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The first stage's noise figure $F_1$ contributes *fully* to the system noise figure. The contribution of subsequent stages is divided by the cumulative gain *before* them. This is why the LNA (Low Noise Amplifier) at the front-end of a receiver is by far the most critical component for noise performance.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Cascaded Amplifier Noise Figure

**Problem:** A receiver has three cascaded stages with the following parameters (measured at $T_0 = 290$ K, $\Delta f = 10$ kHz):

- Stage 1 (LNA): $G_1 = 20$ dB, $F_1 = 1.5$ dB
- Stage 2 (Mixer): $G_2 = -6$ dB, $F_2 = 6$ dB
- Stage 3 (IF Amplifier): $G_3 = 30$ dB, $F_3 = 10$ dB

Compute the overall noise figure in dB and the equivalent noise temperature.

**Step 1: Convert dB to Linear Values**

Using $G_{\text{lin}} = 10^{G_{dB}/10}$ and $F_{\text{lin}} = 10^{F_{dB}/10}$:

$$G_1 = 10^{20/10} = 100$$

$$G_2 = 10^{-6/10} = 0.2512$$

$$G_3 = 10^{30/10} = 1000$$

$$F_1 = 10^{1.5/10} = 1.4125$$

$$F_2 = 10^{6/10} = 3.9811$$

$$F_3 = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

**Step 2: Apply Friis' Formula**

$$F_{\text{sys}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$

$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1.4125 + \frac{3.9811 - 1}{100} + \frac{10 - 1}{100 \times 0.2512}$$

$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1.4125 + \frac{2.9811}{100} + \frac{9}{25.12}$$

$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1.4125 + 0.02981 + 0.3583$$

$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1.8006$$

**Step 3: Convert to dB**

$$NF_{\text{sys}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.8006) = 2.55 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: Compute Equivalent Noise Temperature**

$$T_e = T_0 (F_{\text{sys}} - 1) = 290 \times (1.8006 - 1) = 290 \times 0.8006 = 232.17 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Compute Total Output Noise Power**

$$N_{\text{out}} = G_1 G_2 G_3 \cdot k_B T_0 \Delta f \cdot F_{\text{sys}}$$

$$G_1 G_2 G_3 = 100 \times 0.2512 \times 1000 = 25120$$

$$k_B T_0 \Delta f = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 10^4 = 4.002 \times 10^{-17} \text{ W}$$

$$N_{\text{out}} = 25120 \times 4.002 \times 10^{-17} \times 1.8006 = 1.81 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W} = -117.4 \text{ dBm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice:** Even though stages 2 and 3 have very poor noise figures (6 dB and 10 dB), the system noise figure is only 2.55 dB — close to the LNA's noise figure of 1.5 dB. This vividly demonstrates Friis' formula's central lesson: the *first stage dominates the system noise performance*.

## 3.5 Shot Noise Derivation (Schottky's Result)

For a saturated diode or a forward-biased p-n junction, electrons are emitted (or traverse the junction) randomly. The current is therefore the sum of random current pulses.

### Step 1: Model Each Carrier as a Dirac Delta

Each charge carrier of charge $q$ crossing the junction in time $\tau$ contributes a current pulse of area $q$. The current is:

$$i(t) = \sum_{k} q \, \delta(t - t_k)$$

### Step 2: Compute the Autocorrelation Function

The autocorrelation of a Poisson impulse train with mean rate $\bar{n} = I_{DC}/q$ is:

$$R_i(\tau) = \bar{n} q^2 \cdot \delta(\tau) + (I_{DC})^2$$

The first term is the fluctuating part; the second is the DC term.

### Step 3: Take the Fourier Transform (Wiener-Khinchin Theorem)

$$S_i(f) = \bar{n} q^2 = q I_{DC} \quad \text{(constant, white)}$$

### Step 4: Integrate Over Bandwidth

$$\overline{i_n^2} = \int_0^{\Delta f} 2 S_i(f) \, df = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$$

The factor of 2 arises from the two-sided PSD being folded into the one-sided representation for positive frequencies.

$$\boxed{\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f}$$

This is **Schottky's Shot Noise Formula**, experimentally verified in 1918.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Electrical Noise in Communication Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ELECTRICAL NOISE IN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS] --> B[INTERNAL NOISE]
    A --> C[EXTERNAL NOISE]
    
    B --> B1[THERMAL NOISE]
    B --> B2[SHOT NOISE]
    B --> B3[FLICKER NOISE 1/F]
    B --> B4[TRANSIT TIME NOISE]
    
    B1 --> B1A[Johnson-Nyquist Noise]
    B1 --> B1B[White Gaussian]
    B1 --> B1C[Independent of Current]
    B1 --> B1D[Mean-Square Voltage: 4kTR df]
    
    B2 --> B2A[Schottky Noise]
    B2 --> B2B[Poisson Statistics]
    B2 --> B2C[Requires DC Current]
    B2 --> B2D[Mean-Square Current: 2qIdc df]
    
    B3 --> B3A[Pink Noise]
    B3 --> B3B[Low Frequency Dominant]
    B3 --> B3C[Crystal Defects Origin]
    B3 --> B3D[PSD Inversely Proportional to f]
    
    B4 --> B4A[High Frequency Effect]
    B4 --> B4B[Microwave and mm-wave]
    B4 --> B4C[Finite Carrier Transit Time]
    B4 --> B4D[Significant near Cutoff ft]
    
    C --> C1[Atmospheric Noise]
    C --> C2[Man-Made Industrial Noise]
    C --> C3[Cosmic and Solar Noise]
    C --> C4[Extraterrestrial Noise]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Receiver with Internal Noise Sources

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Antenna Source with Thermal Noise kT df] --> lna[Stage 1 LNA: Gain G1, Noise Figure F1]
    lna --> mixer[Stage 2 Mixer: Gain G2, Noise Figure F2]
    mixer --> ifamp[Stage 3 IF Amplifier: Gain G3, Noise Figure F3]
    ifamp --> demod[Demodulator and Output]
    
    src -.-> n1[Input Noise N1]
    lna -.-> n2[Internal Noise N2]
    mixer -.-> n3[Internal Noise N3]
    ifamp -.-> n4[Internal Noise N4]
    
    n1 --> ntotal[Total Output Noise]
    n2 --> ntotal
    n3 --> ntotal
    n4 --> ntotal
    
    ntotal --> friis[Friis Formula Application]
    friis --> sysnf[System Noise Figure Fsys]
    sysnf --> snrout[Output SNR Calculation]
    
    lna -.->|First stage dominates| sysnf
    mixer -.->|Divided by G1| sysnf
    ifamp -.->|Divided by G1 G2| sysnf
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Noise Calculation in Exam Problems

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Given Problem with Multiple Stages] --> Q{Identify Resistors or Active Devices}
    Q -->|Resistor or Passive| R[Use Thermal Noise Formula 4kTR df]
    Q -->|Active Device with DC Current| S[Use Shot Noise Formula 2qIdc df]
    Q -->|Cascaded Network| T[Apply Friis Formula]
    
    R --> X[Compute Mean Square Voltage or Power]
    S --> X
    T --> Y[Convert All dB to Linear Scale First]
    Y --> Z[Apply F equals F1 plus F2-1 by G1 plus...]
    Z --> W[Convert Final F to dB using 10 log10 F]
    W --> U[Optionally Convert to Equivalent Noise Temperature]
    U --> V[Te equals T0 times F minus 1]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define thermal noise. Derive an expression for the available thermal noise power from a resistor.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Thermal noise, also known as Johnson-Nyquist noise, is the random voltage or current generated across a resistor or any passive conductor due to the continuous thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) at any temperature above absolute zero. It exists in all passive components, is Gaussian and white in nature, and is independent of any applied voltage or current.

**Derivation of Available Noise Power:**

Consider a noisy resistor $R$ at absolute temperature $T$ K. It can be modeled as a noise-free resistor in series with a noise voltage source whose mean-square value over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ is $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$.

When a matched load $R_L = R$ is connected, the mean-square voltage delivered to the load is:

$$v_{\text{load}}^{2} = \frac{\overline{v_n^2}}{4} = k_B T R \Delta f$$

The noise power dissipated in the matched load is:

$$P_n = \frac{v_{\text{load}}^{2}}{R_L} = \frac{k_B T R \Delta f}{R} = k_B T \Delta f$$

Thus, the available thermal noise power depends only on the temperature and bandwidth, **not** on the resistance value.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is shot noise? Write the mathematical expression for shot noise current and explain its dependence on DC current.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Shot noise is the random fluctuation in the current of an active semiconductor device (such as a diode, BJT, or FET) caused by the discrete nature of electric charge. Since current is the flow of discrete charge carriers (electrons and holes) which arrive at random times, the instantaneous current exhibits random variations around its mean DC value. This effect was first analyzed by Walter Schottky in 1918.

**Mathematical Expression:**

The mean-square shot noise current over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ is given by:

$$\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$$

where $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C is the electronic charge, $I_{DC}$ is the average DC current through the device, and $\Delta f$ is the measurement bandwidth in Hz.

**Dependence on DC Current:** Shot noise is **directly proportional** to the DC bias current. This means that biasing a transistor at a higher quiescent current increases its shot noise, which is why low-noise amplifiers are designed to operate at the *minimum* DC current consistent with the required gain and bandwidth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of noise figure and equivalent noise temperature of a two-port network. Derive the relation between noise figure $F$ and equivalent noise temperature $T_e$.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Three cascaded amplifiers have gains and noise figures as follows:

- Amplifier 1: $G_1 = 10$ dB, $F_1 = 3$ dB
- Amplifier 2: $G_2 = 6$ dB, $F_2 = 5$ dB
- Amplifier 3: $G_3 = 20$ dB, $F_3 = 8$ dB

Determine the overall noise figure of the system in dB and the equivalent noise temperature at $T_0 = 290$ K.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**Concept Explanation:**

A two-port network (amplifier, mixer, or filter) is considered *noisy* if it adds its own internal noise to the signal passing through it, in addition to amplifying the input noise. The **Noise Figure (F)** is a figure of merit that quantifies how much the signal-to-noise ratio degrades as the signal traverses the network.

**Block Diagram:**

```
       Input                           Output
    +--------+    +-------------------+   +--------+
    |        |    |                   |   |        |
S_i |--->(+)--->|  Noisy Two-Port    |---|->(S_out)|--->
    |     (+)   |  Gain = G          |   |        |
N_i |---(+)---->|  Adds N_added      |---|->(N_out)|--->
    |     (+)   |                   |   |        |
    +--------+    +-------------------+   +--------+
                  |                   |
                  | Noise Generation  |
                  +-------------------+
```

**Derivation of $F - T_e$ Relation:**

For a two-port network with power gain $G$ and equivalent noise bandwidth $\Delta f$:

**Input signal power:** $S_i$
**Input noise power:** $N_i = k_B T_0 \Delta f$ (at reference temperature $T_0 = 290$ K)

**Output signal power:** $S_o = G \cdot S_i$
**Output noise power:** $N_o = G \cdot N_i + N_{\text{added}}$ where $N_{\text{added}}$ is the noise generated internally.

By definition of noise figure:

$$F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}} = \frac{S_i / N_i}{S_o / N_o} = \frac{S_i / N_i}{G S_i / (G N_i + N_{\text{added}})} = \frac{G N_i + N_{\text{added}}}{G N_i} = 1 + \frac{N_{\text{added}}}{G N_i}$$

To express $N_{\text{added}}$ in terms of an equivalent temperature $T_e$, we set:

$$N_{\text{added}} = G \cdot k_B T_e \Delta f = G \cdot T_e / T_0 \cdot N_i$$

Therefore:

$$F = 1 + \frac{T_e}{T_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{T_e = T_0 (F - 1)}$$

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Step 1: Convert dB to Linear Scale**

$$G_1 = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

$$G_2 = 10^{6/10} = 3.981$$

$$G_3 = 10^{20/10} = 100$$

$$F_1 = 10^{3/10} = 1.9953$$

$$F_2 = 10^{5/10} = 3.1623$$

$$F_3 = 10^{8/10} = 6.3096$$

**Step 2: Apply Friis' Formula for Cascaded Noise Figure**

$$F_{\text{sys}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 \cdot G_2}$$

$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1.9953 + \frac{3.1623 - 1}{10} + \frac{6.3096 - 1}{10 \times 3.981}$$

$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1.9953 + \frac{2.1623}{10} + \frac{5.3096}{39.81}$$

$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1.9953 + 0.21623 + 0.13337$$

$$F_{\text{sys}} = 2.3449$$

**Step 3: Convert to dB**

$$NF_{\text{sys}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.3449) = 3.703 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: Equivalent Noise Temperature**

$$T_e = T_0 (F_{\text{sys}} - 1) = 290 \times (2.3449 - 1) = 290 \times 1.3449 = 390.02 \text{ K}$$

**Final Answer:** $NF_{\text{sys}} = 3.70$ dB; $T_e = 390$ K.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**(a) (7 Marks)** With necessary equations, explain the different types of internal noise in communication systems. Compare thermal noise and shot noise.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A receiver has an equivalent noise bandwidth of 200 kHz and is connected to an antenna with an equivalent noise temperature of 150 K. The receiver itself has an equivalent noise temperature of 250 K. Calculate:
- (i) The total system noise temperature
- (ii) The noise figure of the receiver
- (iii) The available noise power at the receiver input in dBm

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Types of Internal Noise:**

**(1) Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise):**

Thermal noise arises from the random thermal agitation of electrons in conductors and resistors. It is present in all passive components at any temperature above 0 K. The mean-square noise voltage is:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$$

It is Gaussian, white (constant PSD), and present even without bias.

**(2) Shot Noise:**

Shot noise occurs in active semiconductor devices due to the discrete, random nature of charge carriers crossing a p-n junction. It requires a DC bias current. The mean-square noise current is:

$$\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$$

It follows Poisson statistics, is white, and depends on the device type.

**(3) Flicker Noise (1/f Noise):**

Flicker noise is caused by crystal imperfections, surface traps, and carrier density fluctuations in semiconductors. It is dominant at low frequencies (typically below 1 kHz) and has a power spectral density inversely proportional to frequency:

$$S_v(f) \propto \frac{1}{f^{\alpha}}, \quad \alpha \approx 1$$

**(4) Transit-Time Noise:**

At very high frequencies (microwave and mm-wave), the finite time taken by carriers to cross the active region of a device becomes comparable to the signal period, introducing random phase variations. This noise becomes significant when $f \to f_T$ (the device cutoff frequency).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Thermal Noise | Shot Noise |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Origin | Thermal agitation of electrons | Discrete charge carrier emission |
| Occurs in | Passive resistors, conductors | Active devices (diodes, BJTs, FETs) |
| DC bias required? | No | Yes |
| Mathematical form | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$ | $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$ |
| Statistics | Gaussian | Poisson |
| Spectrum | White | White (up to transit-time limit) |
| Discoverer | Johnson (1927) and Nyquist (1928) | Schottky (1918) |

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Given:** $T_{\text{ant}} = 150$ K, $T_e = 250$ K, $\Delta f = 200$ kHz $= 2 \times 10^5$ Hz, $T_0 = 290$ K.

**(i) Total System Noise Temperature:**

$$T_{\text{sys}} = T_{\text{ant}} + T_e = 150 + 250 = 400 \text{ K}$$

**(ii) Noise Figure of the Receiver:**

Using the relation $F = 1 + T_e / T_0$:

$$F = 1 + \frac{250}{290} = 1 + 0.8621 = 1.8621$$

In dB:

$$NF = 10 \log_{10}(1.8621) = 2.70 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Available Noise Power at the Receiver Input:**

$$N_{\text{in}} = k_B T_{\text{sys}} \Delta f$$

$$N_{\text{in}} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 400 \times 2 \times 10^5$$

$$N_{\text{in}} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 8 \times 10^7 = 1.104 \times 10^{-15} \text{ W}$$

Converting to dBm:

$$N_{\text{in(dBm)}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1.104 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.104 \times 10^{-12})$$

$$N_{\text{in(dBm)}} = 10 \times (-11.957) = -119.57 \text{ dBm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $T_{\text{sys}} = 400$ K
- (ii) $NF = 2.70$ dB
- (iii) $N_{\text{in}} = -119.57$ dBm

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear scale:** Friis' formula requires linear values. Using dB values directly in the addition $\frac{F_2-1}{G_1}$ will give a wrong answer and zero credit.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Boltzmann's constant $k$ with the small letter $k$ used in amplifiers:** Always write $k_B$ for Boltzmann's constant in noise formulas to avoid ambiguity.
> 
> 3. **Using $T = 300$ K instead of $T_0 = 290$ K in noise figure calculations:** The standard reference temperature for noise figure is *always* 290 K (IEEE standard), not room temperature 300 K. The 10 K difference can change the answer by ~0.3 dB.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 4 in the matched load derivation:** When computing available noise power, students often write $P = v_n^2 / R$ directly using the *open-circuit* noise voltage, giving a result 4× too large. The correct procedure is $P = v_n^2 / (4R)$ for a matched load.
> 
> 5. **Mixing one-sided and two-sided PSDs:** Shot noise formula uses one-sided PSD, so the factor of 2 (for negative and positive frequencies) is already included. Adding another factor of 2 gives an answer 2× too large.
> 
> 6. **Not stating units in the final answer:** Always specify whether the answer is in dB, dBm, K, or linear. The KTU marking scheme deducts up to 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Internal Noise:**
> 
> ✅ **Four Types of Internal Noise to Memorize:** Thermal, Shot, Flicker (1/f), Transit-Time
> 
> ✅ **Memorize the two master formulas:** $P_n = k_B T \Delta f$ and $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{DC} \Delta f$
> 
> ✅ **Boltzmann's constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K and $T_0 = 290$ K** must be at your fingertips.
> 
> ✅ **Friis' formula** is the single most important cascaded system formula — know it in both linear and dB form.
> 
> ✅ **The first stage dominates the system noise figure** — emphasize this in any cascade problem.
> 
> ✅ **Relation $T_e = T_0 (F - 1)$** links the two noise metrics and is exam-favorite.
> 
> ✅ **Available noise power is independent of $R$** — a counterintuitive but frequently tested fact.
> 
> ✅ **Shot noise requires DC current; thermal noise does not** — this is the most common short-answer question.
> 
> ✅ **Flicker noise dominates at LOW frequencies; transit-time noise at HIGH frequencies** — the frequency dependence is a key differentiator.
> 
> ✅ **Minimum Detectable Signal formula:** MDS (in dBm) $= -174 + 10\log_{10}(\Delta f) + NF$ — memorize this for direct numerical problems.
> 
> ✅ **For matched (maximum power transfer) condition, always use the divided-by-4 version** of the open-circuit noise voltage.
> 
> ✅ **Convert dB to linear *first*, apply Friis' formula, then convert the result back to dB** — this is the most error-free method.

---

**End of Module 3 — Internal Noise Notes**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Modulation and Communication Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulation** is the process of varying one or more properties of a periodic **carrier signal** (high-frequency waveform) with a **modulating signal** (the information-bearing message signal) to encode and transmit information efficiently over a communication channel.

In the context of KTU's Communication Techniques syllabus (2024 Scheme), the topic **"Principles and Types"** under Module 4 establishes the foundational framework for understanding **modulation** — the cornerstone of all electronic communication systems. Modulation is the process by which a low-frequency message signal (containing voice, data, or video information) is superimposed onto a high-frequency carrier wave so that the signal becomes suitable for long-distance transmission via antennas, cables, or optical fibers.

### 1.1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

Mathematically, if the carrier signal is expressed as:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

and the modulating (message) signal is $m(t)$, then the modulated signal $s(t)$ is obtained by systematically varying one of the carrier's parameters — **Amplitude ($A_c$)**, **Frequency ($f_c$)**, or **Phase ($\phi$)** — in proportion to $m(t)$.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Block Carrying" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you have a tiny, valuable diamond (your message signal) that needs to travel from Kerala to Delhi. You cannot throw the diamond by itself — it will get lost. Instead, you place it inside a large, sturdy delivery truck (the carrier signal) that travels along the highway (the channel). The truck's color, size, or speed (amplitude, frequency, phase) tells the receiver about the diamond. The diamond never travels alone — it is **modulated onto** the truck. At the destination, the truck is opened and the diamond is extracted (**demodulation**).

This simple analogy captures the entire purpose of modulation: a low-frequency, low-energy message is placed onto a high-frequency, high-energy carrier to enable efficient, reliable, and long-distance transmission.

### 1.1.3 Why Modulation is Necessary (The "Why" Behind the Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Engineering Reasons for Modulation:**
> 1. **Antenna Size Reduction** — Efficient radiation requires antennas of length $\lambda/4$, where $\lambda = c/f$. Voice signals ($\approx 3\text{ kHz}$) would need antennas of length $\approx 25\text{ km}$, which is physically impossible.
> 2. **Multiplexing Capability** — Multiple independent signals can share a single channel using frequency-division or time-division techniques.
> 3. **Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — High-frequency carriers are less affected by 1/f noise and atmospheric disturbances.
> 4. **Bandwidth Allocation** — Regulatory bodies (FCC, ITU) allocate specific frequency bands; modulation places signals in these bands.
> 5. **Long-Distance Propagation** — Higher frequencies propagate efficiently through space, waveguides, and optical fibers.

### 1.1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Standard voice frequency range:** $300\text{ Hz} - 3.4\text{ kHz}$
- **Commercial AM broadcast band:** $540\text{ kHz} - 1.6\text{ MHz}$
- **Commercial FM broadcast band:** $88\text{ MHz} - 108\text{ MHz}$
- **Carrier frequency (general):** $f_c \gg f_m$ (where $f_m$ is the maximum message frequency)
- **Modulation index range (AM):** $0 \le m \le 1$
- **Modulation index range (FM):** $\beta_f \ge 0$ (typically $\le 5$ for narrowband)

---

## 1.2 Fundamental Principle of Modulation

The **principle of modulation** rests on the **Superposition Theorem** and the **Frequency Translation Property** of the Fourier Transform. When a message signal $m(t)$ modulates a carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, the spectrum of the message is **shifted** from a low-frequency band (baseband) to a high-frequency band centered around $f_c$ (passband).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle — Frequency Translation:** Modulation is essentially a **frequency-shifting operation** performed by a multiplier (modulator) circuit, followed by filtering. This is a universal truth — regardless of whether we vary amplitude, frequency, or phase, the underlying mathematical operation is multiplication in the time domain, which corresponds to convolution in the frequency domain.

The general modulator model is shown conceptually below:

**Baseband Signal** $m(t)$ → **Multiplier (Modulator)** ← **Carrier Oscillator** $c(t)$ → **Bandpass Filter** → **Modulated Output** $s(t)$

### 1.3 The General Equation of a Modulated Signal

Any modulated signal can be written in the universal form:

$$s(t) = A(t) \cos[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)]$$

where:
- $A(t)$ = instantaneous **amplitude** (time-varying if AM-type modulation)
- $f_c$ = **carrier frequency** (constant for AM, time-varying for FM)
- $\phi(t)$ = instantaneous **phase** (constant for AM/FM, time-varying for PM)

This single equation elegantly describes all modulation types — only the choice of *which parameter varies* changes between AM, FM, and PM.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Modulation Principles

The principle of modulation is grounded in three independent, mutually exclusive variations of the carrier signal:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Modulation Primitives:**
> 1. **Amplitude Variation** → The amplitude $A_c$ of the carrier is varied in proportion to $m(t)$ → **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**
> 2. **Frequency Variation** → The frequency $f_c$ of the carrier is varied in proportion to $m(t)$ → **Frequency Modulation (FM)**
> 3. **Phase Variation** → The phase $\phi$ of the carrier is varied in proportion to $m(t)$ → **Phase Modulation (PM)**

These three primitives combine and extend to form the complete family of modulation techniques.

## 2.2 Classification Tree of Modulation Types

Modulation is broadly classified into two families based on the nature of the modulating signal:

| **Criterion** | **Class 1** | **Class 2** |
|---|---|---|
| Modulating signal nature | **Analog Modulation** | **Digital Modulation** |
| Sub-category A | Continuous-wave (CW) Modulation | Pulse Modulation (intermediate) |
| Sub-category B | AM, FM, PM | ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM |

### 2.2.1 Detailed Taxonomy

**A) Analog / Continuous-Wave Modulation (CW Modulation)** — Modulating signal $m(t)$ is continuous and analog:

1. **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**
   - Double Sideband Full Carrier (DSB-FC) — Standard AM
   - Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)
   - Single Sideband Suppressed Carrier (SSB-SC)
   - Vestigial Sideband (VSB)
2. **Angle Modulation**
   - Frequency Modulation (FM)
   - Phase Modulation (PM)
   - Narrowband FM (NBFM)
   - Wideband FM (WBFM)

**B) Pulse Modulation** — Carrier is a pulse train:

1. **Analog Pulse Modulation**
   - Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)
   - Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) / Pulse Duration Modulation (PDM)
   - Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)
2. **Digital Pulse Modulation**
   - Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)
   - Delta Modulation (DM)
   - Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)

**C) Digital Modulation (Carrier Modulation)** — Modulating signal is digital (binary):

1. **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)** — On-Off Keying (OOK)
2. **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** — Binary FSK, M-ary FSK
3. **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** — BPSK, QPSK, M-ary PSK
4. **Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)** — 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all critical equations, boundary conditions, and units required for solving KTU 2024 Scheme problems on Principles and Types of Modulation.

| **Parameter** | **AM (DSB-FC)** | **FM (Angle Modulation)** | **PM (Angle Modulation)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modulated Signal $s(t)$ | $[A_c + A_c k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(t)dt]$ | $A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)]$ |
| Modulation Index $\mu$ or $\beta$ | $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c} = k_a \cdot \max\vert m(t)\vert$ | $\beta_f = \dfrac{k_f \cdot \max\vert m(t)\vert}{f_m}$ | $\beta_p = k_p \cdot \max\vert m(t)\vert$ (radians) |
| Bandwidth (Carson Rule) | $BW = 2 f_m$ (for standard AM) | $BW = 2(\beta_f + 1)f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | $BW = 2(\beta_p + 1)f_m$ |
| Transmission Power | $P_t = P_c\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ (constant) | $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ (constant) |
| Carrier Power $P_c$ | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2 R}$ | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2 R}$ | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2 R}$ |
| Sideband Power $P_{SB}$ | $P_c \cdot \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}$ | $0$ (no discrete sidebands) | $0$ (no discrete sidebands) |
| Overmodulation condition | $\mu > 1$ → distortion | $\beta_f$ can be > 1 (no distortion) | $\beta_p$ can be > 1 (no distortion) |
| Demodulation Method | Envelope detector / Coherent | Frequency discriminator / PLL | Coherent + differentiator → FM |
| Noise Immunity | Poor (susceptible) | Excellent (best among analog) | Good |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | Low (wasteful) | Moderate to High | Moderate to High |
| Power Efficiency | $\le 33\%$ (max for $\mu=1$) | $100\%$ (all power is useful) | $100\%$ (all power is useful) |

### 2.3.1 Digital Modulation Parameter Summary

| **Parameter** | **ASK (BPSK)** | **BFSK** | **BPSK** | **QPSK** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol Error Probability (coherent) | $\dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $\text{erfc}\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)\cdot$(approx.) |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | $\leq 1 \text{ bit/s/Hz}$ | $\geq 1 \text{ bit/s/Hz}$ | $\leq 1 \text{ bit/s/Hz}$ | $2 \text{ bit/s/Hz}$ |
| Spectral Efficiency | Lowest | Moderate | Lowest of digital | Highest of M-ary PSK |
| Bit Rate $R_b$ vs Bandwidth $B$ | $R_b \leq B$ | $R_b \leq B/2$ | $R_b \leq B$ | $R_b \leq 2B$ |

### 2.3.2 Pulse Modulation Summary

| **Parameter** | **PAM** | **PWM/PDM** | **PPM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variable Parameter | Amplitude of pulses | Width of pulses | Position of pulses |
| Bandwidth Requirement | Low | High | Moderate to High |
| Noise Immunity | Poor | Good | Better than PWM |
| SNR Performance | Worst | Moderate | Best among pulse analog |
| Sampling Theorem Required? | Yes ($f_s \geq 2f_m$) | Yes | Yes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Utility of Modulation Principles:**
> - **AM (DSB-FC):** AM radio broadcasting (540 kHz – 1.6 MHz), aviation communication
> - **SSB-SC:** Two-way radio, military communication, HF point-to-point links
> - **FM:** FM radio (88–108 MHz), two-way police communication, VHF television sound
> - **PM:** Digital cellular (GSM uses GMSK — a form of phase/frequency modulation), WiFi
> - **PAM:** First stage of PCM in digital telephony
> - **PWM:** Motor speed control, Class-D audio amplifiers, switched-mode power supplies
> - **PPM:** Optical communication, remote control systems, IR data transmission
> - **PCM:** Digital telephone backbone (TDM-based), CD audio, voice over IP (VoIP)
> - **ASK:** Low-cost RFID systems, optical fiber links (simple on-off light)
> - **FSK:** Modems (early dial-up), Bluetooth, walkie-talkies
> - **PSK / QPSK:** Satellite communication, WiFi (802.11), cellular LTE, GPS
> - **QAM:** Digital television (DVB), cable modems, 4G/5G LTE, WiFi (higher orders)
