> 3. **Wrong sampling rate:** The Nyquist rate is $2 f_m$, not $f_m$. If $f_m = 4$ kHz, then $f_s = 8$ kHz. Mixing these up changes the bit rate by a factor of 2.
> 4. **Bandwidth Confusion:** For polar NRZ line coding, the minimum bandwidth is $R_b/2$, not $R_b$. For unipolar NRZ, it is $R_b$. KTU often tests this distinction.
> 5. **Confusing signal power formulas:** Sine input gives $P_s = V_{max}^2/2$; uniform-distribution input gives $P_s = V_{max}^2/3$. Mixing them gives the wrong SNR by 1.76 dB.
> 6. **Not specifying the bit rate units:** Always state the answer in **kbps or Mbps** explicitly, not just "bits/second".

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PCM Modulation (Module 4):**

### Core Concepts
- **PCM = Sampling → Quantization → Encoding → Line Coding**
- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s = 2 f_m$ (minimum to avoid aliasing)
- **Quantization Levels:** $L = 2^n$
- **Step Size:** $\Delta = 2 V_{max} / 2^n$
- **Bit Rate:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s$
- **Bandwidth (NRZ polar):** $BW = n f_s / 2$
- **Bandwidth (NRZ unipolar):** $BW = n f_s$

### Key Numerical Formulas
- **Quantization Noise Power:** $\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}$
- **SNR (sine input):** $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB
- **SNR (uniform input):** $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n$ dB
- **6-dB Rule:** Every additional bit improves SNR by ~6 dB

### Companding
- **μ-law (US/Japan):** μ = 255, ~24 dB SNR boost for weak signals
- **A-law (Europe):** A = 87.6, similar performance
- **Effect:** Near-constant SNR across wide dynamic range

### PCM vs. Analog Modulation
- **PCM:** Noise-immune (regenerable), secure, multiplexable, **requires higher bandwidth**
- **Analog (AM/FM):** Bandwidth-efficient, but **noise accumulates** with distance

### Standard Telephony PCM (G.711)
- $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits, $R_b = 64$ kbps, BW ≈ 32 kHz
- This is the "default" KTU exam configuration

### Block Diagram Order (Must Memorize)
- **Tx:** AAF → Sampler → Hold → Quantizer → Encoder → P/S → Line Coder
- **Rx:** Regenerator → S/P → Decoder → Reconstruction LPF → Output

### Frequently Tested Numerical Combos
1. Given $n$, $V_{max}$, $f_m$ → Find $\Delta$, SNR, $R_b$, BW.
2. Given target SNR → Find required $n$ (use $n = (\text{SNR} - 1.76)/6.02$).
3. Given $n$ channels of TDM-PCM → Find total bit rate = $N \cdot n \cdot f_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these three formulas by heart — they cover 80% of KTU numerical questions on PCM:**
> 1. $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$
> 2. $R_b = n \cdot f_s$
> 3. $\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplexing and Demultiplexing – FDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multiplexing and Demultiplexing – FDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** is a technique of multiplexing that permits the simultaneous transmission of multiple independent baseband signals over a single communication channel by assigning each signal a **distinct, non-overlapping frequency band** within the total channel bandwidth, achieved through the process of modulation (translation in frequency domain).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, FDM is classified as an **analog multiplexing technique** belonging to the **frequency-domain sharing** family. Each input message signal $m_i(t)$ (where $i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, N$) is modulated onto a distinct sub-carrier frequency $f_{ci}$ such that the spectra of the modulated signals are mutually disjoint in the frequency axis.

The composite transmitted signal $s_{FDM}(t)$ is a linear superposition:

$$s_{FDM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$$

At the receiver, **demultiplexing** is performed using a bank of **band-pass filters (BPF)** tuned to each sub-carrier frequency $f_{ci}$, followed by coherent demodulators to recover the original baseband messages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** Students must clearly distinguish between **Multiplexing** (combining multiple signals) and **Demultiplexing** (separating them). The reverse process at the receiver is called **Frequency Division Demultiplexing**, and the FDM system is therefore inherently symmetric in its design philosophy.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **multi-lane highway** where:
- Each **vehicle** represents an independent message signal (voice, video, data).
- Each **lane** represents a specific frequency band allocated to that signal.
- The **highway divider lines** act as **guard bands** that prevent vehicles from colliding.
- The **highway toll booth (entrance)** is the **multiplexer** that places each vehicle into its assigned lane.
- The **highway toll booth (exit)** is the **demultiplexer** that separates vehicles back into their respective streams.

In real life, this is exactly what **FM radio broadcasting** does. When you tune your car radio to **92.7 MHz** (All India Radio) or **104.2 MHz** (Radio Mirchi), you are essentially performing demultiplexing — selecting one of many frequency-multiplexed audio channels riding on the same physical transmission tower.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric:** The voice signal bandwidth in telephony is standardized at **$\mathbf{B_m = 4 \text{ kHz}}$** (3 kHz usable + 1 kHz guard portion). This is the universal KTU assumption unless the problem statement specifies otherwise.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Baseband Signal | $m_i(t)$ | Original low-frequency information signal |
| Sub-Carrier Frequency | $f_{ci}$ | Carrier used to translate $m_i(t)$ to a higher band |
| Message Bandwidth | $B_m$ | Highest frequency component in $m_i(t)$ |
| Guard Band | $B_g$ | Unused spectrum between adjacent channels to prevent interference |
| Total FDM Bandwidth | $B_T$ | Sum of all channel bandwidths plus guard bands |
| Multiplexer | MUX | Device that combines multiple signals |
| Demultiplexer | DEMUX | Device that separates combined signals |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps in an FDM Transmitter

The FDM transmitter operates in a strictly sequential manner. Each step is **mandatory** for KTU 2024 scheme derivations:

1. **Signal Conditioning (Low-Pass Filtering):** Each baseband signal $m_i(t)$ is band-limited to $B_m$ Hz using a low-pass filter (LPF) to prevent aliasing and inter-symbol interference.
2. **Modulation (Frequency Translation):** The conditioned signal is multiplied by a sub-carrier $\cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$, producing a Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC) signal centered at $f_{ci}$.
3. **Band-Pass Filtering:** A BPF removes out-of-band harmonics, isolating the desired sidebands.
4. **Frequency Translation (Mixing — Optional):** If required, the signal is shifted to its final allocated slot in the multiplexed spectrum using a mixer and local oscillator.
5. **Combining (Summing):** All $N$ modulated signals are linearly summed in a **linear adder** to form the composite FDM signal $s_{FDM}(t)$.
6. **Final Modulation (Multiplexing to Carrier):** The composite FDM signal modulates a high-frequency RF carrier for transmission over the channel (this is the FDM-FDM cascade used in commercial radio).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Linear Summing?** Because the spectra of individual modulated signals are non-overlapping, superposition holds without cross-talk. If the spectra overlapped, the linear sum would cause **co-channel interference (CCI)** and the receiver could not separate the signals.

## 2.2 Operational Steps in an FDM Receiver (Demultiplexer)

The receiver reverses the process, and is the focus of the word "Demultiplexing" in the module title:

1. The received composite FDM signal is first **RF-demodulated** using the main carrier to recover the baseband FDM spectrum.
2. The signal is passed through a **bank of Band-Pass Filters (BPF)**, each tuned to one of the sub-carrier frequencies $f_{c1}, f_{c2}, \dots, f_{cN}$ with bandwidth $B_{ch} = 2B_m + B_g$.
3. Each BPF output is fed to a **coherent demodulator** (product detector) using a locally generated carrier synchronized to $f_{ci}$.
4. The demodulated output is **low-pass filtered** to extract the original message $m_i(t)$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains **every formula** a student requires to solve FDM problems in the KTU 2024 University Examination:

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Message Bandwidth | $B_m$ (Hz) | Highest baseband frequency component |
| Sub-carrier Condition | $f_{c(i+1)} - f_{ci} \geq 2B_m + B_g$ | Prevents spectral overlap |
| Channel Bandwidth | $B_{ch} = 2B_m + B_g$ | Allocated spectrum per channel |
| Guard Band | $B_g$ | Mandatory safety margin |
| Total FDM Bandwidth (N channels) | $B_T = N \cdot B_{ch} = N(2B_m + B_g)$ | Sum of all channel allocations |
| Sub-carrier Frequency of $i$-th channel | $f_{ci} = f_{c1} + (i-1)(2B_m + B_g)$ | Center frequency of $i$-th BPF |
| DSB-SC Modulated Signal | $s_i(t) = m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$ | Standard DSB-SC form |
| Composite FDM Signal | $s_{FDM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$ | Linear sum of all DSB-SC signals |
| Coherent Demodulation Output | $v_i(t) = m_i(t) \cos(0) + \text{high frequency terms}$ | After LPF: $m_i(t) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$ |
| Carrier Amplitude | $A_c$ (assumed unity if not specified) | Default value in KTU problems |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Exam Trap:** Many students confuse the **message bandwidth $B_m$** with the **channel bandwidth $B_{ch}$**. Remember: the **message** lives in $B_m$ Hz, but the **FDM slot** requires $2B_m$ Hz (for DSB) **plus** the guard band $B_g$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Analog Telephone Trunk Lines (Carrier Systems):** The classic AT&T L-carrier, N-carrier, and modern hierarchical multiplexing systems use FDM.
- **FM Stereo Broadcasting:** Stereo audio is multiplexed using FDM — the (L+R) sum is the baseband, while (L−R) is DSB-SC modulated on a 38 kHz sub-carrier.
- **Television Broadcasting:** The video signal and audio signal of TV channels are FDM-multiplexed within a single 6 MHz channel.
- **Satellite Communication (Early Generation):** Pre-DVB era satellites used FDM-FM-FDMA.
- **Cable TV Distribution (CATV):** Each channel occupies a 6 MHz slot in the coaxial cable spectrum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Composite FDM Signal Expression

Let there be $N$ baseband signals $m_1(t), m_2(t), \dots, m_N(t)$, each band-limited to $B_m$ Hz. Each signal is multiplied by its dedicated sub-carrier $\cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$ to generate the DSB-SC output.

**Step 1:** The $i$-th modulated signal is:

$$s_i(t) = m_i(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the product-to-sum identity, where multiplication in time domain corresponds to frequency shift of $\pm f_{ci}$:

$$S_i(f) = \frac{1}{2} M_i(f - f_{ci}) + \frac{1}{2} M_i(f + f_{ci})$$

This places upper and lower sidebands symmetrically around $\pm f_{ci}$.

**Step 3:** The composite FDM signal is the sum of all individually modulated signals:

$$s_{FDM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$$

**Step 4:** The frequency spectrum of the composite signal is non-overlapping provided the sub-carrier spacing satisfies:

$$f_{c(i+1)} - f_{ci} \geq 2B_m + B_g$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Bandwidth Requirement for N Channels

Consider $N$ DSB-SC channels. Each channel occupies $2B_m$ Hz (upper and lower sidebands).

**Step 1:** Bandwidth of a single channel (DSB-SC + guard band):

$$B_{ch} = 2B_m + B_g$$

**Step 2:** For $N$ such channels arranged adjacently:

$$B_T = N \cdot B_{ch}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the expression for $B_{ch}$:

$$B_T = N(2B_m + B_g)$$

**Step 4:** In terms of $B_m$ only (assuming $B_g$ is often taken as 0 for ideal problems):

$$B_T = 2N B_m \quad \text{(ideal case, no guard band)}$$

**Step 5:** With practical guard band (e.g., $B_g = 0.5 B_m$ for KTU problems):

$$B_T = N(2B_m + 0.5 B_m) = 2.5 N B_m$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sub-Carrier Frequency for the $i$-th Channel

**Step 1:** Let the first sub-carrier be at $f_{c1}$ (given or assigned in the problem). The required spacing between adjacent sub-carriers is:

$$\Delta f = 2B_m + B_g$$

**Step 2:** The $i$-th sub-carrier is therefore located at:

$$f_{ci} = f_{c1} + (i-1) \Delta f$$

**Step 3:** Expanding the expression:

$$f_{ci} = f_{c1} + (i-1)(2B_m + B_g)$$

**Step 4:** The lower sideband (LSB) of the $i$-th channel begins at:

$$f_{LSB,i} = f_{ci} - B_m - \frac{B_g}{2}$$

**Step 5:** The upper sideband (USB) of the $i$-th channel ends at:

$$f_{USB,i} = f_{ci} + B_m + \frac{B_g}{2}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Demultiplexing at the Receiver

**Step 1:** The received composite signal at the receiver is $r(t) = s_{FDM}(t) + n(t)$, where $n(t)$ is AWGN.

**Step 2:** For the $i$-th channel, the BPF output is:

$$r_i(t) = m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t) + \text{(other channel residuals)}$$

**Step 3:** The BPF isolates only the $i$-th channel, giving:

$$y_i(t) = m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$$

**Step 4:** Coherent demodulation multiplies $y_i(t)$ with a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$:

$$z_i(t) = m_i(t) \cos^2(2\pi f_{ci} t)$$

**Step 5:** Using the identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}(1 + \cos(2\theta))$:

$$z_i(t) = \frac{1}{2} m_i(t) + \frac{1}{2} m_i(t) \cos(4\pi f_{ci} t)$$

**Step 6:** Passing through an LPF with cutoff $B_m$ removes the high-frequency term:

$$v_i(t) = \frac{1}{2} m_i(t)$$

**Step 7:** A unity-gain amplifier (factor of 2) restores the original amplitude:

$$\hat{m}_i(t) = m_i(t)$$

This completes the demultiplexing process.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Format)

> **Problem:** An FDM system multiplexes **5 voice channels**, each with **$B_m = 4$ kHz** and a guard band of **$B_g = 0.5$ kHz**. The first sub-carrier is at **$f_{c1} = 60$ kHz**. Find: (a) Channel bandwidth, (b) Total FDM bandwidth, (c) Sub-carrier frequencies of all 5 channels.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Channel bandwidth:

$$B_{ch} = 2B_m + B_g = 2(4) + 0.5 = 8.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**(b)** Total FDM bandwidth:

$$B_T = N \cdot B_{ch} = 5 \times 8.5 = 42.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**(c)** Sub-carrier frequencies:

$$f_{c1} = 60 \text{ kHz}$$
$$f_{c2} = 60 + 8.5 = 68.5 \text{ kHz}$$
$$f_{c3} = 60 + 2(8.5) = 77 \text{ kHz}$$
$$f_{c4} = 60 + 3(8.5) = 85.5 \text{ kHz}$$
$$f_{c5} = 60 + 4(8.5) = 94 \text{ kHz}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FDM Transmitter (Multiplexer) Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the full FDM multiplexer chain, following the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** standard:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Source 1 m1t] --> lpf1[LPF Bandwidth Bm]
    src2[Source 2 m2t] --> lpf2[LPF Bandwidth Bm]
    src3[Source 3 m3t] --> lpf3[LPF Bandwidth Bm]
    srcN[Source N mNt] --> lpfN[LPF Bandwidth Bm]
    lpf1 --> mod1[Multiplier with sub-carrier fc1]
    lpf2 --> mod2[Multiplier with sub-carrier fc2]
    lpf3 --> mod3[Multiplier with sub-carrier fc3]
    lpfN --> modN[Multiplier with sub-carrier fcN]
    mod1 --> bpf1[BPF centered at fc1]
    mod2 --> bpf2[BPF centered at fc2]
    mod3 --> bpf3[BPF centered at fc3]
    modN --> bpfN[BPF centered at fcN]
    bpf1 --> sum[Linear Summing Adder]
    bpf2 --> sum
    bpf3 --> sum
    bpfN --> sum
    sum --> rfMod[RF Modulator Final Carrier]
    rfMod --> channel[Transmission Channel]
```

## 4.2 FDM Receiver (Demultiplexer) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    channel[Received Composite Signal] --> rfDemod[RF Demodulator]
    rfDemod --> bpfbank[Bank of N Band-Pass Filters]
    bpfbank --> bpf1out[BPF1 tuned to fc1]
    bpfbank --> bpf2out[BPF2 tuned to fc2]
    bpfbank --> bpf3out[BPF3 tuned to fc3]
    bpfbank --> bpfNout[BPFN tuned to fcN]
    bpf1out --> dem1[Coherent Demodulator fc1]
    bpf2out --> dem2[Coherent Demodulator fc2]
    bpf3out --> dem3[Coherent Demodulator fc3]
    bpfNout --> demN[Coherent Demodulator fcN]
    dem1 --> lpf1out[LPF Bm]
    dem2 --> lpf2out[LPF Bm]
    dem3 --> lpf3out[LPF Bm]
    demN --> lpfNout[LPF Bm]
    lpf1out --> out1[Recovered m1t]
    lpf2out --> out2[Recovered m2t]
    lpf3out --> out3[Recovered m3t]
    lpfNout --> outN[Recovered mNt]
```

## 4.3 Frequency Domain Spectral Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph spectrum[FDM Composite Spectrum]
        axis[Frequency Axis in Hz]
        block1[Channel 1 LSB and USB around fc1]
        gb1[Guard Band Bg]
        block2[Channel 2 LSB and USB around fc2]
        gb2[Guard Band Bg]
        block3[Channel 3 LSB and USB around fc3]
        blockN[Channel N LSB and USB around fcN]
        axis --- block1
        block1 --- gb1
        gb1 --- block2
        block2 --- gb2
        gb2 --- block3
        block3 --- blockN
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the spectral layout:** Each "Channel $i$" block contains both the **Lower Sideband (LSB)** and **Upper Sideband (USB)** symmetrically placed around the sub-carrier $f_{ci}$. The total width of the block is $2B_m$ Hz. Guard bands of width $B_g$ Hz sit between adjacent channel blocks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Frequency Division Multiplexing. List any two advantages of FDM over TDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** FDM is a multiplexing technique in which multiple signals are transmitted simultaneously over a single channel by allocating each signal a distinct, non-overlapping frequency band using sub-carrier modulation.
>
> **Advantages (1.5 marks — any two):**
> 1. Suitable for **analog signals** without requiring analog-to-digital conversion.
> 2. Allows **simultaneous real-time transmission** of multiple continuous signals.
> 3. **No synchronization** between transmitter and receiver is required at the bit level (unlike TDM).
> 4. Lower **latency** because no time-slot allocation is needed.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a guard band? Why is it necessary in an FDM system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A guard band is a small, unused portion of the frequency spectrum deliberately left vacant between two adjacent FDM channels to act as a buffer.
>
> **Necessity (1.5 marks):** Practical Band-Pass Filters at the receiver have **finite roll-off** and cannot perfectly separate adjacent channels. The guard band absorbs the spectral leakage and prevents **adjacent channel interference (ACI)**, ensuring clean demultiplexing.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **block diagram of an FDM system** with **N input signals**. Clearly show the multiplexer and demultiplexer sections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FDM system combines **12 voice channels** with each channel having a message bandwidth of **$B_m = 3.5$ kHz** and a guard band of **$B_g = 0.5$ kHz**. The first sub-carrier is at **$f_{c1} = 50$ kHz**. Calculate:
- (i) Bandwidth of each channel
- (ii) Total FDM bandwidth
- (iii) Sub-carrier frequencies of channels 1, 6, and 12
- (iv) The total FDM bandwidth if DSB-SC modulation is replaced by SSB-SC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

**Multiplexer Section (3.5 marks):**
Each of the $N$ baseband signals $m_1(t), m_2(t), \dots, m_N(t)$ is first **low-pass filtered** to limit its bandwidth to $B_m$. Each filtered signal is then **multiplied** with a sub-carrier $\cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$ where $f_{ci}$ is unique for the $i$-th channel. The output of each multiplier is passed through a **Band-Pass Filter** to remove harmonics and spurious components. All the BPF outputs are summed in a **linear adder** to form the composite FDM signal $s_{FDM}(t)$, which is then modulated onto a final RF carrier for transmission.

**Demultiplexer Section (3.5 marks):**
At the receiver, the composite FDM signal is first RF-demodulated. It is then passed through a **bank of $N$ Band-Pass Filters**, each tuned to one of the sub-carrier frequencies $f_{ci}$. The output of the $i$-th BPF contains only the $i$-th modulated signal. This is fed to a **coherent demodulator** that multiplies it with a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$. A final **Low-Pass Filter** recovers the original baseband message $m_i(t)$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $N = 12$, $B_m = 3.5$ kHz, $B_g = 0.5$ kHz, $f_{c1} = 50$ kHz.

**(i) Bandwidth of each channel (DSB-SC):**

$$B_{ch} = 2B_m + B_g = 2(3.5) + 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Total FDM bandwidth:**

$$B_T = N \cdot B_{ch} = 12 \times 7.5 = 90 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Sub-carrier frequencies using $f_{ci} = f_{c1} + (i-1)(2B_m + B_g)$:**

Channel 1: $f_{c1} = 50$ kHz

Channel 6: $f_{c6} = 50 + 5(7.5) = 50 + 37.5 = 87.5$ kHz

Channel 12: $f_{c12} = 50 + 11(7.5) = 50 + 82.5 = 132.5$ kHz

**(iv) Total FDM bandwidth with SSB-SC:**

For SSB-SC, each channel needs only $B_m$ Hz instead of $2B_m$ Hz. Thus:

$$B_{ch,SSB} = B_m + B_g = 3.5 + 0.5 = 4 \text{ kHz}$$

$$B_{T,SSB} = N \cdot B_{ch,SSB} = 12 \times 4 = 48 \text{ kHz}$$

**Conclusion:** SSB-SC reduces the total bandwidth by **42 kHz (≈ 47 %)** compared to DSB-SC.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With the help of a **frequency spectrum diagram**, explain the concept of FDM. Discuss the role of guard bands. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FDM system has **8 channels** with each having a message bandwidth of **$B_m = 4$ kHz**. The system uses DSB-SC modulation with a guard band of **$B_g = 1$ kHz**.
- (i) Calculate the total bandwidth.
- (ii) Find the sub-carrier frequencies if the lowest sub-carrier is at **20 kHz**.
- (iii) What is the bandwidth efficiency improvement if SSB-SC is used? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):** In FDM, $N$ baseband signals are translated to distinct frequency bands using sub-carriers, then combined for transmission. The frequency spectrum of the composite signal shows $N$ pairs of sidebands (LSB and USB), each pair centered at its sub-carrier frequency $f_{ci}$.

**Spectrum Diagram (2 marks):** The diagram shows the baseband spectrum at the origin, then $N$ translated spectra at $f_{c1}, f_{c2}, \dots, f_{cN}$, with guard bands of width $B_g$ separating adjacent translated spectra.

**Role of Guard Bands (2 marks):** Guard bands absorb spectral spillover from non-ideal filters, prevent inter-channel interference, and allow receiver BPFs to be designed with realistic roll-off characteristics.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $N = 8$, $B_m = 4$ kHz, $B_g = 1$ kHz, $f_{c1} = 20$ kHz.

**(i) Total bandwidth (DSB-SC):**

$$B_{ch} = 2B_m + B_g = 2(4) + 1 = 9 \text{ kHz}$$

$$B_T = N \cdot B_{ch} = 8 \times 9 = 72 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Sub-carrier frequencies:**

$$f_{ci} = 20 + (i-1)(9) \text{ kHz}$$

$f_{c1} = 20$ kHz, $f_{c2} = 29$ kHz, $f_{c3} = 38$ kHz, $f_{c4} = 47$ kHz, $f_{c5} = 56$ kHz, $f_{c6} = 65$ kHz, $f_{c7} = 74$ kHz, $f_{c8} = 83$ kHz.

**(iii) Bandwidth with SSB-SC:**

$$B_{ch,SSB} = B_m + B_g = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ kHz}$$

$$B_{T,SSB} = 8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ kHz}$$

**Bandwidth reduction:**

$$\Delta B = 72 - 40 = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

**Efficiency improvement percentage:**

$$\eta = \frac{32}{72} \times 100 \approx 44.44 \text{ \%}$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing $B_m$ and $B_{ch}$:** Students often use $B_m$ directly as channel bandwidth, missing the doubling factor of 2 from DSB-SC. This costs **2 marks minimum**.
> 2. **Forgetting the guard band:** When $B_g$ is mentioned, it must be added to $2B_m$. Skipping it is a **deduction of 1-2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong sub-carrier indexing:** The formula is $f_{ci} = f_{c1} + (i-1) \cdot B_{ch}$, **not** $f_{c1} + i \cdot B_{ch}$. Off-by-one error costs **1-2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the demultiplexer section:** In long-answer questions, students often describe the multiplexer but skip the demultiplexer. KTU valuation key explicitly asks for **both halves** — losing 3-4 marks otherwise.
> 5. **No synchronization justification:** For coherent detection, students must mention **carrier synchronization** (PLL or Costas loop) at the receiver to obtain full marks in the demultiplexing explanation.
> 6. **Units not mentioned:** Always write kHz/MHz/Hz explicitly. Bare numbers lose **0.5 mark** per occurrence.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **FDM** = Frequency Division Multiplexing — multiple signals share a channel by allocating distinct **frequency bands**.
> - Each input signal is modulated using **DSB-SC** (default) or **SSB-SC** onto a **sub-carrier** $f_{ci}$.
> - The **multiplexer** = LPF + Modulator + BPF + Linear Adder.
> - The **demultiplexer** = Bank of BPFs + Coherent Demodulators + LPFs.
> - **Channel bandwidth** (DSB-SC): $B_{ch} = 2B_m + B_g$.
> - **Channel bandwidth** (SSB-SC): $B_{ch} = B_m + B_g$.
> - **Total FDM bandwidth**: $B_T = N \cdot B_{ch}$.
> - **Sub-carrier formula**: $f_{ci} = f_{c1} + (i-1)(2B_m + B_g)$.
> - **Guard band** $B_g$ prevents adjacent channel interference due to non-ideal filters.
> - **Composite signal**: $s_{FDM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$.
> - **Coherent detection** is mandatory at the receiver; phase synchronization with $f_{ci}$ is required.
> - **Advantages**: Real-time transmission, no need for ADC, simple analog implementation.
> - **Disadvantages**: Inter-modulation distortion, requires linear amplifiers, susceptible to nonlinear noise, guard bands waste spectrum.
> - **Standard KTU assumptions**: $B_m = 4$ kHz for voice, $B_g$ given in problem, $f_{c1}$ given in problem.
> - **Practical applications**: Telephone trunk carrier systems, FM stereo, TV broadcasting, early satellite links, CATV.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cell Phone Technologies – Cellular Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cell Phone Technologies – Cellular Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cellular Concept:** The *Cellular Concept* is a fundamental radio system design philosophy in mobile communication wherein a large geographical service area (the **coverage zone**) is subdivided into smaller, non-overlapping, low-power radio zones called **cells**. Each cell is served by a dedicated **Base Station (BS)** operating on a controlled set of radio frequencies. By intelligently reusing these frequencies in non-adjacent cells (frequency reuse), the system achieves near-unlimited spectral efficiency, superior Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR), and high user capacity.

**Key Vocabulary Anchors:**
- **Cell:** The smallest hexagonal/radio footprint served by one Base Transceiver Station (BTS).
- **Cluster:** A group of $N$ cells in which every available frequency is used exactly once.
- **Frequency Reuse:** Re-assigning the same carrier frequency to geographically separated cells belonging to different clusters.
- **Co-channel Cells:** Cells belonging to different clusters that share the same set of RF channels.
- **Handoff / Handover:** The process of transferring an active mobile call from one cell's base station to another without service interruption.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Pizza Delivery Problem":**
> Imagine a single pizza shop trying to deliver to an entire city. As the city grows, delivery times become unacceptable because the rider has to travel too far, and only one chef (one radio tower) can bake only so many pizzas (channels) at once. The smart solution? **Open small branch kitchens in every neighborhood** (micro base stations). Each branch only serves its own neighborhood using a small menu of pizzas (frequencies). However, you can reuse the "Margherita" pizza recipe in branches that are far enough apart so that customers don't get confused about which branch is delivering to them. This is *exactly* the cellular concept: small cells, low-power transmitters, and clever frequency reuse across distance.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Operating Frequency Bands (Legacy & Current):** **800 MHz (AMPS)**, **900 MHz (GSM 900)**, **1800 MHz (DCS 1800)**, **1900 MHz (PCS 1900)**, and modern 4G/5G bands at **2.1 GHz, 2.6 GHz, 3.5 GHz**.
- **Cell Radius ($R$):** Typically **1 km to 35 km** (macrocell); **< 1 km** for microcells/picocells.
- **Speed of Light ($c$):** $3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (used in propagation delay/wavelength calculations).
- **Path Loss Exponent ($n$):** Free space $= 2$; Urban cellular environment $\approx 3$ to $4$.

---

### 1.4 Why "Hexagonal" Cells?

> [!NOTE]
> A circle leaves unilluminated gaps; a square produces overlapping ambiguous regions. A **regular hexagon** tiles perfectly without overlap, closely approximates circular radiation patterns, and provides the **largest coverage area per unit radius**, making it the canonical analytical model in cellular engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Frequency Reuse — The Heart of the Cellular Concept

To increase capacity, the available spectrum is partitioned into $K$ channel sets. These sets are allocated to cells within a **cluster of size $N$**, and the entire pattern is repeated across the geography. The cluster size $N$ is determined by:

$$
N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}
$$

where $i$ and $j$ are non-negative integers defining the relative cell separation. Common configurations: $N = 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 19, 21$.

The **Co-channel Reuse Ratio $Q$** — the ratio of the distance $D$ between two co-channel cells to the cell radius $R$ — is:

$$
Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}
$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Frequency Reuse

1. **Spectrum Partitioning:** Divide the total $K$ available channels into $N$ distinct channel groups.
2. **Cluster Assignment:** Allocate one channel group to each of the $N$ cells inside a cluster.
3. **Cluster Repetition:** Tile the service area with adjacent, non-overlapping clusters. Cells sharing the same channel group number (e.g., cell #1 in every cluster) are called **co-channel cells**.
4. **Interference Control:** The minimum distance $D$ between co-channel cells must be large enough to keep co-channel interference (CCI) below an acceptable threshold.

### 2.3 Interference Analysis

#### A. Co-Channel Interference (CCI)
Caused by co-channel cells transmitting on identical frequencies. The **Signal-to-Interference Ratio (S/I)** for the worst-case mobile (located at the boundary of its serving cell) is given by:

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^{n}}{i_{0}} = \frac{(Q)^{n}}{i_{0}}
$$

where $n$ is the path loss exponent and $i_0$ is the **number of co-channel interferers in the first tier** (typically **6** for omnidirectional antennas; **2 or 3** with sectoring).

**Standard Form (KTU Examiners' Favourite):** For $n = 4$ and $i_0 = 6$:

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{4}}{6} = \frac{9N^{2}}{6} = 1.5 \, N^{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Acceptable S/I:** For voice quality, KTU/industry benchmarks require $S/I \geq 18$ dB. Solving $1.5 N^{2} \geq 63$ (linear for 18 dB) yields **$N \geq 7$**.

#### B. Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI)
Caused by imperfect receiver filters allowing energy from adjacent frequencies to leak in. Mitigated via careful **frequency planning** and **guard bands**.

### 2.4 Capacity Expansion Techniques

| Technique | Mechanism | Capacity Multiplier |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Splitting** | Replace large cells with smaller ones, reusing same frequencies more densely | $4 \times$ per split (area) |
| **Sectoring** | Replace omnidirectional antenna at BS with 3 directional antennas ($120^{\circ}$) | $\approx 3 \times$ |
| **Microcells / Picocells** | Reduce cell radius to fill coverage gaps (e.g., 100 m urban street cells) | Variable |
| **Frequency Borrowing** | Congested cell temporarily borrows frequencies from a low-traffic neighbor | Marginal |

### 2.5 Handoff (Handover)

> [!NOTE]
> **Handoff** is triggered when the received signal strength (RSS) from the current BS drops below a **handoff threshold**, while RSS from a neighboring BS becomes stronger. Two main categories:
> - **Hard Handoff:** "Break-before-make" (used in GSM, LTE).
> - **Soft Handoff:** "Make-before-break" (used in CDMA/3G, where the mobile connects to multiple BS simultaneously).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE 2024 Scheme.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cluster Size | $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | $i, j \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | Co-channel Reuse Ratio | $Q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | S/I (Omnidirectional, $n=4$) | $\dfrac{S}{I} = 1.5 \, N^{2}$ | Linear scale |
| 4 | S/I (Sectored 120°, $n=4$) | $\dfrac{S}{I} = 6 \, N^{2}$ | Linear scale (1 interferer) |
| 5 | Capacity (Channels/Cell) | $C = \dfrac{K}{N}$ | $K$ = total channels |
| 6 | S/I in dB | $\text{S/I(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)$ | dB |
| 7 | Worst-case path loss exponent | $n \in [3, 4]$ | Urban macrocell |
| 8 | Cell Splitting Radius Factor | $R_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{old}}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Halves area |
| 9 | Number of interferers (omnidirectional) | $i_0 = 6$ | First tier |
| 10 | Number of interferers (3-sector $120^{\circ}$) | $i_0 = 2$ | First tier |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The cellular concept is the operational backbone of **2G (GSM)**, **3G (WCDMA)**, **4G (LTE/LTE-A)**, and **5G NR (New Radio)**. Mastering $N$, $Q$, and $S/I$ allows RF planning engineers to optimize **spectral efficiency**, **cell-site density**, and **interference margins** in operator networks such as Jio, Airtel, Vi, and BSNL in India.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Co-channel Reuse Ratio $Q = \sqrt{3N}$

**Setup:** Consider a hexagonal cell layout. Let a mobile be at the **center of a reference cell**. The six co-channel cells in the first tier are arranged at angular separations of $60^{\circ}$ around a circle of radius $D$.

Let the co-channel cell centers be located using a "shift vector" defined by integer lattice coordinates $(i, j)$. By geometry of the regular hexagon tiling, the distance from the origin to a co-channel cell is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= R \cdot \sqrt{i^{2} + ij + j^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{3} \\
  &= R \cdot \sqrt{3(i^{2} + ij + j^{2})} \\
  &= R \cdot \sqrt{3N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Q \equiv D/R$:

$$
Q = \sqrt{3N}
$$

**Justification:** The hexagon lattice constants cause the distance between cell centers along the primary axes to be $\sqrt{3}\,R$. The Pythagorean combination of $i$ and $j$ shifts yields $i^{2}+ij+j^{2}$, scaled by $3$. This is the foundational equation of frequency reuse geometry.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: S/I Ratio (Worst-Case Omnidirectional)

**Assumptions:**
- Path loss model: $P_{r} \propto r^{-n}$ (power law).
- Mobile is at the **farthest point** from its own BS (distance $\approx R$).
- Mobile is at the **closest point** to the six co-channel BS interferers (distance $\approx D$).

**Signal Power:**
$$
S = k \cdot R^{-n}
$$

**Total Interference Power (6 co-channel interferers at distance $D$):**
$$
I = \sum_{k=1}^{6} k \cdot D^{-n} = 6 k \cdot D^{-n}
$$

**Taking the Ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{I} &= \frac{k \cdot R^{-n}}{6 k \cdot D^{-n}} \\
           &= \frac{1}{6} \left( \frac{D}{R} \right)^{n} \\
           &= \frac{1}{6} \left( \sqrt{3N} \right)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting $n = 4$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{I} &= \frac{1}{6} \left( \sqrt{3N} \right)^{4} \\
           &= \frac{1}{6} \cdot 9 N^{2} \\
           &= 1.5 \, N^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the form KTU examiners expect in part-(a) of every frequency-reuse numerical problem.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

> **Problem:** A cellular system uses a cluster size of $N = 7$. The path loss exponent is $n = 4$, and the system employs omnidirectional antennas. Compute the S/I ratio in **dB**.

**Step 1 — Substitute $N = 7$ into the linear S/I formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{I} &= 1.5 \cdot (7)^{2} \\
           &= 1.5 \cdot 49 \\
           &= 73.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dB:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{I}\,(\text{dB}) &= 10 \log_{10}(73.5) \\
                         &= 10 \times 1.866 \\
                         &= 18.66 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
Since $18.66 \text{ dB} \geq 18 \text{ dB}$ (the KTU/voice-quality benchmark), **$N = 7$ is acceptable** for omnidirectional voice systems. ✅

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Sectoring on S/I

| Configuration | $i_0$ | S/I (linear, $n=4$) | S/I (dB) | Improvement vs Omni |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Omnidirectional | 6 | $1.5 N^{2}$ | $10 \log_{10}(1.5 N^{2})$ | 0 dB |
| 3-sector ($120^{\circ}$) | 2 | $6 N^{2}$ | $10 \log_{10}(6 N^{2})$ | +6 dB |
| 6-sector ($60^{\circ}$) | 1 | $12 N^{2}$ | $10 \log_{10}(12 N^{2})$ | +9 dB |

**Formula in general form:**
$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{i_{0}}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Example — Sectoring Trade-off

> **Problem:** A cellular operator using $N = 7$ switches from omnidirectional to 3-sector antennas. By how many dB does the S/I improve?

**Step 1 — Compute S/I for omnidirectional ($i_0 = 6$, $n = 4$):**
$$
(S/I)_{\text{omni}} = 1.5 \cdot 7^{2} = 73.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute S/I for 3-sector ($i_0 = 2$, $n = 4$):**
$$
(S/I)_{\text{sector}} = \frac{(\sqrt{3 \cdot 7})^{4}}{2} = \frac{(4.583)^{4}}{2} = \frac{441.6}{2} = 220.8
$$

Wait — let me recompute $(\sqrt{3 \cdot 7})^{4}$:
$$
\sqrt{3 \cdot 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583
$$
$$
(4.583)^{4} \approx 441.6
$$
$$
(S/I)_{\text{sector}} = 441.6 / 2 = 220.8
$$

**Step 3 — Improvement in dB:**
$$
\Delta(\text{S/I}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{220.8}{73.5}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3.004) \approx 4.78 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Sectoring cuts first-tier interferers from 6 → 2, yielding approximately **+4.8 dB** of S/I gain — equivalent to **doubling cluster reuse density** without violating the 18 dB benchmark.

---

### 3.6 Capacity Expansion via Cell Splitting

If traffic density increases by a factor of 4, a cell of radius $R$ is replaced by 4 cells of radius $R_{\text{new}}$, with:

$$
R_{\text{new}} = \frac{R_{\text{old}}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

This preserves the **same S/I ratio** (because $D/R$ and $N$ remain unchanged), but quadruples the channel reuse density, hence **quadruples capacity**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cellular System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS1[Mobile Station A] -->|Radio Uplink| BTS1[BTS Cell 1]
    MS2[Mobile Station B] -->|Radio Uplink| BTS2[BTS Cell 2]
    MS3[Mobile Station C] -->|Radio Uplink| BTS3[BTS Cell 3]

    BTS1 -->|T1 E1 Link| BSC1[Base Station Controller BSC]
    BTS2 -->|T1 E1 Link| BSC1
    BTS3 -->|T1 E1 Link| BSC2[Base Station Controller BSC]

    BSC1 -->|A Interface| MSC1[Mobile Switching Center MSC]
    BSC2 -->|A Interface| MSC1

    MSC1 -->|SS7 Signaling| HLR[Home Location Register HLR]
    MSC1 -->|SS7 Signaling| VLR[Visitor Location Register VLR]
    MSC1 -->|PSTN Gateway| PSTN[Public Switched Telephone Network]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** MSs in each cell communicate with their local BTS. BTSs are aggregated by the BSC, which manages radio resource allocation. The MSC coordinates call routing, location management (via HLR/VLR), and connectivity to the public telephone network. This is the **GSM 2G reference architecture**.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cluster and Frequency Reuse Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph C1[Cluster of N equals 7 cells]
        A1[Cell 1] --> A2[Cell 2] --> A3[Cell 3] --> A4[Cell 4] --> A5[Cell 5] --> A6[Cell 6] --> A7[Cell 7] --> A1
    end

    C1 -->|Reuse same frequencies| C2[Adjacent Cluster N equals 7]
    C2 -->|Reuse same frequencies| C3[Adjacent Cluster N equals 7]

    A1 -. Co-channel .-> C2_Cell1[Cell 1 of Cluster 2]
    A1 -. Co-channel .-> C3_Cell1[Cell 1 of Cluster 3]

    style A1 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C2_Cell1 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C3_Cell1 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Yellow cells (Cell 1 from each cluster) are co-channel cells. They are separated by distance $D$, which must be large enough to ensure $S/I \geq 18$ dB. The hexagonal pattern repeats in a tessellation.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Handoff Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Mobile in active call]) --> Measure[Measure RSS from current BS and candidate BS]
    Measure --> Compare{RSS of candidate BS greater than RSS of current BS by hysteresis margin H}
    Compare -->|No| Stay[Stay on current BS]
    Compare -->|Yes| Trigger[Trigger Handoff Request to BSC]
    Trigger --> Allocate[BSC allocates channel in target cell]
    Allocate --> Switch[Mobile retunes to new BS frequency]
    Switch --> Update[HLR VLR updated with new cell ID]
    Update --> Continue([Call continues seamlessly])

    Stay --> Measure
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The handoff process is event-driven by RSS comparison plus a **hysteresis margin $H$** (typically 3–6 dB) to prevent "ping-pong" handoffs at the cell boundary.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the cellular concept. Why are hexagonal cells preferred over square or circular cells?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **cellular concept** divides a large service area into smaller cells, each served by a low-power Base Station (BS), with frequencies reused in geographically separated cells to maximize capacity.

Hexagonal cells are preferred because:
1. **Perfect tessellation** — no overlapping or uncovered gaps (unlike circles).
2. **Largest area per unit radius** — among three regular tilings (triangular, square, hexagonal), the hexagon encloses the maximum area for a given circumradius.
3. **Close approximation of omnidirectional radiation patterns** in real BS antennas.
4. **Mathematically tractable geometry** — the lattice supports the elegant relation $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ and $Q = \sqrt{3N}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker Allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + any two reasons (2 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is frequency reuse? Write the formula for co-channel reuse ratio.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Frequency Reuse** is the technique of using the same set of RF channels in cells that are geographically separated by a minimum distance $D$ (called the co-channel reuse distance), so that co-channel interference remains below an acceptable threshold.

The **co-channel reuse ratio** is defined as:

$$
Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}
$$

where $D$ is the distance between centers of two co-channel cells, $R$ is the cell radius, and $N$ is the cluster size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker Allocation:** Definition (2 marks) + formula (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Signal-to-Interference (S/I) ratio** in a cellular system using omnidirectional antennas. Assume a path loss exponent of $n = 4$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A cellular operator uses $N = 7$, omnidirectional antennas, and a total of $K = 420$ channels.
   (i) Calculate the **S/I ratio in dB**.
   (ii) Calculate the **number of channels per cell**.
   (iii) If the operator upgrades to **3-sector ($120^{\circ}$) antennas**, compute the new S/I in dB and the improvement. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of S/I for Omnidirectional Antennas:**

Let a mobile be at the boundary of its serving cell, at distance $\approx R$ from its own BS. The **six co-channel interferers** in the first tier are each at distance $\approx D$.

Assuming the path loss model $P_r \propto r^{-n}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Signal Power:}\quad S &= k \cdot R^{-n} \\
\text{Interference Power (6 cells):}\quad I &= \sum_{m=1}^{6} k \cdot D^{-n} = 6 k \cdot D^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{k R^{-n}}{6 k D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{n} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3N}\right)^{n}
$$

For $n = 4$:

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3N)^{2}}{6} = \frac{9N^{2}}{6} = 1.5 \, N^{2}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations:**

**(i) S/I for $N = 7$, omnidirectional:**

$$
\frac{S}{I} = 1.5 \times (7)^{2} = 1.5 \times 49 = 73.5
$$

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) = 18.66 \, \text{dB}
$$

**(ii) Channels per cell:**

$$
C = \frac{K}{N} = \frac{420}{7} = 60 \text{ channels/cell}
$$

**(iii) With 3-sector antennas ($i_0 = 2$, $n = 4$):**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{sector}} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{4}}{2} = \frac{(3 \cdot 7)^{2}}{2} = \frac{441}{2} = 220.5
$$

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{sector, dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(220.5) \approx 23.43 \, \text{dB}
$$

**Improvement:**

$$
\Delta = 23.43 - 18.66 = 4.77 \, \text{dB}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams:
   (i) **Cluster formation** and the formula $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$.
   (ii) **Cell splitting** as a capacity expansion technique. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A hexagonal cellular system has:
   - Cell radius $R = 4$ km.
   - Co-channel reuse ratio $Q = 4.583$.
   - Path loss exponent $n = 3$.
   - Omnidirectional antennas.
   
   Compute:
   (i) The **cluster size $N$**.
   (ii) The **co-channel cell separation $D$**.
   (iii) The **S/I ratio in dB** and verify whether it meets the **18 dB benchmark**. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory:**

**(i) Cluster formation:** A **cluster** is the smallest grouping of cells that collectively uses every available channel exactly once before the pattern repeats. The cluster size is governed by:

$$
N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}, \quad i, j \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}
$$

Common valid combinations: $(i, j) = (2, 0) \Rightarrow N = 4$; $(i, j) = (2, 1) \Rightarrow N = 7$; $(i, j) = (3, 0) \Rightarrow N = 9$; $(i, j) = (2, 2) \Rightarrow N = 12$; $(i, j) = (3, 2) \Rightarrow N = 19$.

**(ii) Cell splitting:** When user traffic in a region grows beyond capacity, the congested cells are subdivided into smaller cells. Each large cell of radius $R$ is replaced by smaller cells of radius $R_{\text{new}} = R/\sqrt{2}$. The transmit power of the BS is also reduced by approximately 12 dB (since power scales as $R^{n}$, $n = 4 \Rightarrow (1/\sqrt{2})^{4} = 1/4 \Rightarrow -6 \text{ dB}$ per dimension, total $-12$ dB). **Capacity increases by a factor of 4** without changing $N$ or $S/I$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations:**

**(i) Cluster size from $Q$:**

$$
Q = \sqrt{3N} \implies Q^{2} = 3N \implies N = \frac{Q^{2}}{3} = \frac{(4.583)^{2}}{3} = \frac{21.0}{3} = 7
$$

**(ii) Co-channel separation:**

$$
D = Q \cdot R = 4.583 \times 4 = 18.33 \, \text{km}
$$

**(iii) S/I for $n = 3$, omnidirectional ($i_0 = 6$):**

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{3}}{6} = \frac{(3N)^{1.5}}{6} = \frac{(21)^{1.5}}{6}
$$

$$
(21)^{1.5} = 21 \times \sqrt{21} = 21 \times 4.583 = 96.24
$$

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{96.24}{6} = 16.04
$$

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(16.04) \approx 12.05 \, \text{dB}
$$

**Benchmark Check:**
$$
12.05 \, \text{dB} < 18 \, \text{dB} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{❌ FAILS benchmark}
$$

**Conclusion:** With $n = 3$ and $N = 7$, the S/I is only 12.05 dB, which is below the 18 dB voice quality benchmark. The operator must either (a) increase $N$, (b) apply sectoring, or (c) use directional antennas to reduce $i_0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Always report S/I in **dB** for the final answer when asked, but show the linear value first.
> 2. **Forgetting $i_0$:** Using 6 vs 2 vs 1 (for omni vs 3-sector vs 6-sector) changes the answer drastically.
> 3. **Wrong Path Loss Exponent:** $n = 2$ (free space) vs $n = 3$ to $4$ (urban) — read the question carefully!
> 4. **Mixing up $N$ and $D$:** $N$ is dimensionless (cluster size); $D$ has units of length (km/m).
> 5. **Skipping the benchmark check:** Examiners reward the engineering judgment of comparing against **18 dB**.
> 6. **Not writing the formula first:** Always write $\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{i_0}$ *before* substituting values.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Cellular Concept:** Divide service area → small cells → reuse frequencies.
- **Hexagonal Geometry:** Standard analytical model; tiles perfectly; matches omnidirectional patterns.
- **Cluster Size:** $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ → feasible values: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27…
- **Co-channel Reuse Ratio:** $Q = \sqrt{3N}$; geometric foundation of the entire system.
- **S/I Ratio (Omni, $n=4$):** $\frac{S}{I} = 1.5 N^{2}$.
- **S/I Ratio (3-Sector, $n=4$):** $\frac{S}{I} = 6 N^{2}$.
- **Minimum S/I benchmark:** **18 dB** for voice; ~7 dB for CDMA.
- **Number of first-tier interferers:** $i_0 = 6$ (omni), $i_0 = 2$ (3-sector), $i_0 = 1$ (6-sector).
- **Capacity per cell:** $C = K / N$.
- **Cell Splitting:** $R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{old}} / \sqrt{2}$, capacity $\times 4$.
- **Handoff:** Triggered by RSS + hysteresis margin; Hard (GSM) vs Soft (CDMA).
- **Frequency Planning Tools:** Cluster size, sectoring, cell splitting, frequency borrowing.
- **Path Loss Exponent:** $n = 2$ (free space), $n = 3$–$4$ (typical urban macrocell).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Base Stations and Small Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Base Stations and Small Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Base Station (BS) — Formal Definition

A **Base Station (BS)** is a fixed terrestrial transceiver infrastructure node that acts as the central wireless access point connecting User Equipment (UE) / Mobile Stations (MS) to the core network of a cellular communication system. It performs critical Layer 1 (PHY) and Layer 2 (MAC/RLC) functions such as modulation/demodulation, channel coding/decoding, multiplexing, power control, handover management, and radio resource allocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Terminology Mapping (Must Memorize):**
> - **GSM (2G):** Base Transceiver Station (BTS) + Base Station Controller (BSC)
> - **UMTS (3G):** **Node B** + Radio Network Controller (RNC)
> - **LTE (4G):** **eNodeB (eNB)** — flat architecture, no central controller
> - **5G NR:** **gNodeB (gNB)** — distributed unit (DU) + centralized unit (CU)

## 1.2 Small Cell — Formal Definition

A **Small Cell** is a low-power, short-range cellular radio access node that operates in licensed spectrum to complement the macro-cellular network. Small cells are characterized by **transmit power typically in the range of 250 mW to 5 W** and **coverage radii from 10 m to 1–2 km**, providing targeted capacity and coverage enhancement in dense urban, indoor, or hotspot environments.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Classification Rule:** Small cells are categorized by the **3GPP** based on the minimum coupling loss (MCL) between the user and the access point, and by their typical deployment scenario (indoor/outdoor, operator/customer deployed).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy: The Post Office Network**

Imagine a city:
- A **Macro Base Station** is like the **main General Post Office (GPO)** on the city outskirts — it has a huge service area, large staff (antennas), and handles massive traffic, but it can be far from your house.
- A **Small Cell (Pico/Femto)** is like a **neighbourhood letter box / local post office** — small, located in your building or street, gives you instant access with no long walk.
- As the city grows, simply enlarging the GPO doesn't help — you need many tiny neighbourhood post offices to serve dense areas. **This is exactly why 4G/5G networks deploy Heterogeneous Networks (HetNets) with macro + small cells.**

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Maximum Macro BS Power:** **20 W (43 dBm)** to **40 W (46 dBm)** per carrier
- **Typical Small Cell Power:** **250 mW (24 dBm)** to **2 W (33 dBm)**
- **Coverage Radius:** Macro ≈ **1–35 km**; Small cells ≈ **10 m – 1 km**
- **Operating Frequency Bands (KTU focus):** 700 MHz – 6 GHz (sub-6 GHz for 5G), mmWave 24–52 GHz
- **Backhaul Latency Target (5G):** **< 1 ms** for URLLC
- **Capacity Enhancement with Small Cells:** **3x to 10x** improvement in hotspot zones

> [!NOTE]
> The **3GPP TS 36.104** standardizes four base station classes: **Wide Area BS, Medium Range BS, Local Area BS, and Home BS** — corresponding directly to Macro, Micro, Pico, and Femto respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Modern Base Station (eNodeB / gNodeB)

A modern base station consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **RF Front-End:** Antennas (sectorized or MIMO arrays), Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA), Power Amplifier (PA), Duplexers (FDD) or switches (TDD).
2. **Baseband Processing Unit (BBU):** Performs digital signal processing — FFT/IFFT, channel coding (Turbo, LDPC, Polar), modulation (QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM).
3. **Digital Unit (DU):** Handles MAC scheduling, HARQ, and real-time L1/L2 processing.
4. **Centralized Unit (CU):** In 5G, separated into CU-CP (control plane) and CU-UP (user plane) for cloud deployment.
5. **Backhaul Interface:** Fiber, microwave, or mmWave link to the core/EPC/5GC.

## 2.2 Why Small Cells? The "Why" Behind HetNets

- **Spectrum Reuse Factor:** Reducing cell radius $R$ increases the number of cells $N_c$ per area, and the cluster size reuse factor $\frac{N_c}{K}$ (where $K$ is the reuse pattern) increases system capacity.
- **Cell Splitting Gain:** Capacity scales approximately as $\left(\frac{R_{macro}}{R_{small}}\right)^2$.
- **Indoor Penetration Loss:** Walls cause **15–25 dB** penetration loss; a femto indoors eliminates this.
- **Hotspot Offload:** 80% of mobile traffic originates from **20% of locations** (Pareto principle).

## 2.3 Classification of Cells by Coverage and Power

| Cell Type | Tx Power (dBm) | Coverage Radius | Users Supported | Deployment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Macrocell | 46 dBm (40 W) | 1 – 35 km | 100s – 1000s | Tower / rooftop, outdoor |
| Microcell | 33–38 dBm (2–6 W) | 200 m – 2 km | 50 – 100 | Urban street / outdoor wall |
| Picocell | 23–30 dBm (250 mW – 1 W) | 50 – 200 m | 20 – 50 | Indoor public spaces (malls) |
| Femtocell | 13–20 dBm (20 – 100 mW) | 10 – 30 m | 1 – 8 | Home / small office (HeNB) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU Numerical Problems will be solved using the formulas in this table.** Memorize the **units** alongside each equation.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_c = \dfrac{A_{total}}{\pi R^2}$ | Number of cells to cover area $A_{total}$ with radius $R$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{cell} = \dfrac{W}{N} \cdot N_c$ | Per-cell capacity; $W$ = total BW, $N$ = reuse cluster | bps |
| 3 | $G = \left(\dfrac{R_{macro}}{R_{small}}\right)^2$ | Cell splitting gain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $SINR = \dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot PL^{-1}}{N_0 + \sum I_i}$ | Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 5 | $PL_{Hata}(d) = 69.55 + 26.16\log_{10}(f) - 13.82\log_{10}(h_b) - a(h_m) + [44.9 - 6.55\log_{10}(h_b)]\log_{10}(d)$ | Okumura-Hata path loss (urban macro) | dB; $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km |
| 6 | $PL_{FSPL}(d) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | Free-space path loss | dB; $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 7 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SINR)$ | Shannon capacity limit | bps |
| 8 | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - PL_{total}$ | Link budget equation (received power) | dBm |
| 9 | $EIRP = P_{tx} + G_{antenna} - L_{cable}$ | Effective Isotropic Radiated Power | dBm |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Area served by small cells}}{\text{Total area}} \times 100$ | HetNet deployment density metric | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for KTU exams:** When a question gives transmit power in **Watts** and asks for a result in **dBm**, ALWAYS use the conversion: $P_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}(P_{mW})$. For example, **2 W = 2000 mW = 33 dBm**.

## 2.5 Operational Architecture — Why the Flat Architecture in 4G/5G?

In **3G UMTS**, the **RNC** handled radio resource management, which introduced latency of **10–20 ms** between Node B and core. The **4G/5G flat architecture** removes this central controller (eNodeB/gNodeB directly connects to EPC/5GC), reducing latency to **< 5 ms** in LTE and **< 1 ms** in 5G URLLC.

> [!NOTE]
> The macro BS handles **coverage and mobility**; the small cell handles **capacity and hotspot throughput** — this division of labor is the foundation of **Heterogeneous Networks (HetNet)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cell Splitting Gain

**Problem Setup:** A macrocell with radius $R_1$ is split into 4 smaller cells, each of radius $R_2 = R_1/2$. Prove the cell splitting gain formula.

**Step 1 — Area of macrocell:**
The macrocell covers a hexagonal area approximated by a circle:

$$A_1 = \pi R_1^2$$

**Step 2 — Area of each small cell:**

$$A_2 = \pi R_2^2 = \pi \left(\frac{R_1}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi R_1^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Number of small cells required:**

$$N_{small} = \frac{A_1}{A_2} = \frac{\pi R_1^2}{\frac{\pi R_1^2}{4}} = 4$$

**Step 4 — Capacity per unit area:**
If the spectral efficiency $\eta$ (bps/Hz) per cell is constant, total capacity per unit area scales as:

$$C_{area} \propto \frac{N_{small} \cdot \eta \cdot W}{A_1}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the gain ratio:**

$$G = \frac{C_{area,small}}{C_{area,macro}} = \frac{N_{small} \cdot A_2}{A_1} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_{1}}$$

$$G = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{A_1}{4}}{A_1} = \frac{A_1}{A_1} \cdot \frac{4}{4} = 1 \text{ (per cell basis)}$$

**Step 6 — Reframe as per-unit-area gain (the correct KTU form):**
On a per-unit-area basis, the gain is:

$$G = \left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{R_{macro}}{R_{small}}\right)^2$$

**Numerical Verification:**
If $R_{macro} = 1$ km and $R_{small} = 100$ m = 0.1 km:

$$G = \left(\frac{1}{0.1}\right)^2 = (10)^2 = 100$$

This means **100× capacity per unit area** is achievable — the basis of dense small-cell deployment in 5G.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Link Budget for a Small Cell Deployment

**Given KTU-style problem data:**
- Transmit power of pico BS: $P_{tx} = 1$ W
- Antenna gains: $G_t = G_r = 8$ dBi
- Cable/connector loss: $L_c = 2$ dB
- Frequency: $f = 2.6$ GHz
- Distance: $d = 200$ m
- Receiver noise figure: $NF = 5$ dB
- Bandwidth: $B = 10$ MHz
- Thermal noise: $N_0 = -174$ dBm/Hz

**Step 1 — Convert transmit power to dBm:**

$$P_{tx} = 1 \text{ W} = 1000 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{tx,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(1000) = 30 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute EIRP:**

$$EIRP = P_{tx} + G_t - L_c = 30 + 8 - 2 = 36 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 3 — Free-space path loss at 200 m, 2.6 GHz:**

$$f \text{ in MHz} = 2600 \text{ MHz}; \quad d \text{ in km} = 0.2$$

$$PL_{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(0.2) + 20\log_{10}(2600) + 32.45$$

$$\log_{10}(0.2) = -0.699$$

$$\log_{10}(2600) = 3.415$$

$$PL_{FSPL} = 20(-0.699) + 20(3.415) + 32.45$$

$$PL_{FSPL} = -13.98 + 68.30 + 32.45$$

$$PL_{FSPL} = 86.77 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Received signal power:**

$$P_{rx} = EIRP + G_r - PL = 36 + 8 - 86.77 = -42.77 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 5 — Thermal noise power in 10 MHz:**

$$N_{thermal} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(10 \times 10^6)$$

$$N_{thermal} = -174 + 70 = -104 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 6 — Total noise + NF:**

$$N_{total} = N_{thermal} + NF = -104 + 5 = -99 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 7 — SNR at receiver:**

$$SNR = P_{rx} - N_{total} = -42.77 - (-99) = 56.23 \text{ dB}$$

This extremely high SNR shows that small cells achieve excellent link quality, justifying their use for high-throughput 5G hotspots.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Macro vs Small Cell

| Parameter | Macro BS | Micro BS | Pico BS | Femto BS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tx Power | 40 W (46 dBm) | 2–6 W (33–38 dBm) | 250 mW–1 W (24–30 dBm) | 20–100 mW (13–20 dBm) |
| Antenna Height | 25–70 m | 5–15 m | 4–6 m | 1–2 m |
| Backhaul | Fiber / Microwave | Fiber | Fiber / DSL | DSL / Cable / Fiber |
| Access Control | Open (all subscribers) | Open | Open / Closed | Closed Subscriber Group (CSG) |
| Handover Type | Inter-frequency | Inter-frequency | Intra-frequency | Intra-frequency / HNB |
| Typical 3GPP Class | Wide Area | Medium Range | Local Area | Home |
| Cost per Node | High (>\$50k) | Medium (\$10k) | Low (\$1–3k) | Very Low (\$100–300) |
| Deployment Owner | Operator | Operator | Operator | End User |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Modern Base Station (gNodeB / eNodeB)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[UE Mobile Terminal]:::ue
    nodeB[RF Antenna Array 4x4 or 64x64 MIMO]:::rf
    nodeC[Power Amplifier PA 46 dBm]:::rf
    nodeD[Duplexer FDD or TDD Switch]:::rf
    nodeE[ADC DAC Conversion Block]:::proc
    nodeF[Baseband Processing FFT CP Removal]:::proc
    nodeG[Channel Coding LDPC Polar]:::proc
    nodeH[Modulation QAM 256 64 16]:::proc
    nodeI[MAC Scheduler HARQ ARQ]:::ctrl
    nodeJ[DU Distributed Unit L2 Real Time]:::ctrl
    nodeK[CU Centralized Unit CP and UP]:::ctrl
    nodeL[Backhaul Fiber X2 F1 NG]:::ext
    nodeM[5G Core Network AMF UPF]:::ext

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeK --> nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeK -. control plane .-> nodeJ
    nodeJ -. scheduling .-> nodeI

    classDef ue fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef rf fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff5e5,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#f0e5ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **5G NR** architecture splits the base station into **CU (Centralized Unit) + DU (Distributed Unit) + RU (Radio Unit)**, enabling **C-RAN (Cloud-Radio Access Network)** deployment where multiple DUs share a single CU for efficient resource pooling.

## 4.2 Heterogeneous Network (HetNet) Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph macroLayer[MACRO LAYER Coverage Tier]
        nodeMA[Macro BS eNB gNB Tower 46 dBm]
    end
    subgraph microLayer[SMALL CELL LAYER Capacity Tier]
        nodeMI[Micro BS Urban 33 dBm]
        nodePI[Pico BS Indoor Mall 27 dBm]
        nodeFE[Femto BS HeNB Home 17 dBm]
    end
    subgraph coreNet[CORE NETWORK 5GC EPC]
        nodeCN[Core Network Cloud Server]
    end

    nodeFE --> nodePI --> nodeMI
    nodePI --> nodeMA
    nodeMI --> nodeMA
    nodeMA --> nodeCN
    nodeFE -. offload traffic .-> nodeCN

    classDef macro fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef small fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Handover Flow: Macro to Small Cell (5G NR)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UE as User Equipment
    participant Macro as Macro gNB
    participant Small as Small Cell gNB
    participant Core as 5G Core AMF

    UE->>Macro: Connected in RRC CONNECTED state
    UE->>UE: RSRP from Small Cell crosses A2-A4 threshold
    UE->>Macro: Measurement Report MR
    Macro->>Core: Handover Request via N2 interface
    Core->>Small: Handover Preparation Xn or N2
    Small->>Core: Handover Acknowledge
    Core->>UE: RRC Reconfiguration with target cell
    UE->>Small: Random Access Preamble
    Small->>UE: RRC Reconfiguration Complete
    UE->>Core: Path Switch Request
    Note over UE,Small: UE now served by Small Cell for capacity boost
```

## 4.4 Interference Management in HetNet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeUE[UE Near Small Cell]
    nodeSC[Small Cell Pico 27 dBm]
    nodeMC[Macro Cell 46 dBm]
    nodeINT[Interference Region]

    nodeMC -. strong DL interference to UE .-> nodeUE
    nodeSC --> nodeUE
    nodeUE --> nodeSC
    nodeINT[Solution: Almost Blank Subframes ABS, eICIC, FeICIC]

    classDef ue fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    classDef sc fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600
    classDef mc fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#000099
    classDef int fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#996600
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a macrocell and a femtocell in terms of transmit power, coverage area, and typical deployment scenario.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Macrocell:** Transmit power ≈ **40 W (46 dBm)**, coverage radius **1–35 km**, deployed on **tall towers or rooftops** by the network operator to provide wide-area outdoor coverage.
- **Femtocell:** Transmit power ≈ **20–100 mW (13–20 dBm)**, coverage radius **10–30 m**, deployed by **end users** in homes or small offices to enhance indoor coverage using DSL/cable backhaul. It uses a **Closed Subscriber Group (CSG)** access mechanism.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the role of an eNodeB in the LTE architecture? Mention two key differences between Node B (3G) and eNodeB (4G).**

**Model Answer:**
The **eNodeB (Evolved Node B)** is the base station in LTE that handles all radio interface functions: scheduling, HARQ, modulation/coding, and radio resource management. It directly interfaces with the EPC (Evolved Packet Core) without any central controller.

**Two key differences:**
1. **Flat Architecture:** eNodeB integrates the functions of 3G's **Node B + RNC** into a single node, eliminating the RNC and reducing latency.
2. **All-IP Network:** eNodeB uses **S1 interface** (S1-MME for control, S1-U for user plane) with full IP connectivity, unlike 3G which used ATM-based transport.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a 4G LTE base station (eNodeB) with a functional block diagram. List the key functions performed at the PHY, MAC, and RRC layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LTE macrocell BS transmits at 20 W with an antenna gain of 18 dBi at 2.1 GHz. The cable loss is 3 dB, and the receiver is at a distance of 5 km with a gain of 0 dBi. Calculate:
   (i) EIRP of the transmitter.
   (ii) Free-space path loss.
   (iii) Received power at the UE.
   **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) eNodeB Architecture (7 marks):**
The eNodeB consists of three functional layers:
- **Physical Layer (PHY):** OFDM modulation, FFT/IFFT, channel coding (Turbo), MIMO processing, synchronization, power control.
- **MAC Layer:** HARQ retransmissions, scheduling (proportional fair, round-robin), logical/transport channel mapping, random access.
- **RRC Layer:** Radio resource control, connection establishment, handover decisions, broadcast of system information (SIBs), measurement reporting.

**Functional Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Antenna Array] --> nodeB[RF Front End LNA PA]
    nodeB --> nodeC[OFDM Modulator Demodulator]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Channel Coding Turbo]
    nodeD --> nodeE[MAC Scheduler HARQ]
    nodeE --> nodeF[RRC Layer Handover]
    nodeF --> nodeG[S1 Interface to EPC]
```

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) EIRP:**
- $P_{tx} = 20$ W = 20,000 mW
- $P_{tx,dBm} = 10\log_{10}(20000) = 10 \times 4.301 = 43.01$ dBm
- $EIRP = P_{tx} + G_t - L_c = 43.01 + 18 - 3 = 58.01$ dBm

**(ii) Free-Space Path Loss:**
- $f = 2.1$ GHz = 2100 MHz
- $d = 5$ km
- $PL_{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(2100) + 32.45$
- $PL_{FSPL} = 20(0.699) + 20(3.322) + 32.45$
- $PL_{FSPL} = 13.98 + 66.44 + 32.45 = 112.87$ dB

**(iii) Received Power:**
- $P_{rx} = EIRP + G_r - PL = 58.01 + 0 - 112.87 = -54.86$ dBm

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of a Heterogeneous Network (HetNet). Discuss the role of small cells (pico, femto) in 5G and the challenges in their deployment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5G gNodeB (macro) covers a circular area of radius 2 km. To increase capacity, the area is split into small cells of radius 200 m. Calculate:
   (i) The number of small cells required.
   (ii) The cell splitting gain.
   (iii) If each small cell uses 20 MHz bandwidth and achieves 4 bps/Hz spectral efficiency, calculate the total capacity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HetNet Architecture (7 marks):**
A **Heterogeneous Network (HetNet)** is a cellular network architecture that combines **macro cells** (for coverage) with **low-power small cells** (pico, femto, micro) for capacity enhancement. 

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Macro Tier Wide Area Coverage] --> nodeC[HetNet Combined Deployment]
    nodeB[Small Cell Tier Hotspot Capacity] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD[UE Experience Higher Throughput]
```

**Role in 5G:**
- Offloading traffic from macro BS
- 1000× capacity gain targeted in 5G
- Indoor coverage penetration (15–25 dB loss through walls)

**Challenges:**
1. **Interference:** Cross-tier interference (macro → femto). Solved by **eICIC/FeICIC**.
2. **Handover complexity:** Frequent handover as UE moves between small cells.
3. **Backhaul:** Requires fiber / high-speed links.
4. **Synchronization:** Time/frequency sync across tiers.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of small cells:**
- $A_{macro} = \pi R_{macro}^2 = \pi (2)^2 = 4\pi$ km²
- $A_{small} = \pi R_{small}^2 = \pi (0.2)^2 = 0.04\pi$ km²
- $N_{small} = A_{macro} / A_{small} = 4\pi / 0.04\pi = 100$ cells

**(ii) Cell splitting gain:**
- $G = (R_{macro} / R_{small})^2 = (2 / 0.2)^2 = 100$

**(iii) Total capacity:**
- Per-cell capacity: $C = B \cdot \eta = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 4 = 80$ Mbps
- Total: $C_{total} = 100 \times 80 = 8000$ Mbps = **8 Gbps**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Mixing dBm and dBW without converting. Always check: $P_{dBm} = P_{dBW} + 30$.
> 2. **Forgetting to add 32.45** in the FSPL formula when distance is in km. This is a top-3 error in KTU answer sheets.
> 3. **Confusing Hata and FSPL:** FSPL is for **line-of-sight free-space**; Hata is for **macrocell urban/suburban** with empirical correction factors.
> 4. **Wrong Shannon usage:** $C = B\log_2(1+SINR)$ gives an **upper bound**, not achievable throughput. The **5G NR practical peak** is around 70% of Shannon capacity.
> 5. **In HetNet diagrams:** Students often forget to show **backhaul connections** to the small cells. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for missing backhaul.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Base Station Evolution:** BTS (2G) → Node B (3G) → eNodeB (4G) → gNodeB (5G). Each generation reduced the hierarchy and centralized control.
- **Small Cell Categories:** Femto (home, ≤100 mW) < Pico (indoor public, ≤1 W) < Micro (outdoor urban, ≤6 W) < Macro (tower, ≤40 W).
- **Heterogeneous Networks (HetNet):** The cornerstone of 5G capacity scaling — combines macro coverage with small-cell capacity.
- **CU-DU Split (5G):** Enables C-RAN, centralizing L3/L4 in CU and distributing real-time L1/L2 in DU.
- **Key Formulas:** FSPL = $20\log(d) + 20\log(f) + 32.45$, Cell splitting gain = $(R_1/R_2)^2$, Shannon capacity = $B\log_2(1+SINR)$.
- **Interference Solutions:** eICIC, FeICIC, Almost Blank Subframes (ABS), CoMP (Coordinated Multi-Point).
- **SON (Self-Organizing Networks):** Auto-configuration, auto-optimization, auto-healing for small cells.
- **Backhaul:** Fiber (gold standard), mmWave, microwave, or DSL/cable for femtocells.
- **3GPP Class Mapping:** Wide Area = Macro, Medium Range = Micro, Local Area = Pico, Home = Femto.
- **Handover Types:** Intra-frequency (within same frequency), inter-frequency, inter-RAT (e.g., LTE → 5G NR).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GSM: Principle and Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GSM: Principle and Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications):** GSM is a **second-generation (2G)** digital cellular communication standard developed by the **European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)** to provide voice, data, and supplementary services over a public switched telephone network (PSTN) using **digital modulation, Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)**, and **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD)** techniques. It operates in the **900 MHz** and **1800 MHz** bands (with 850/1900 MHz variants in the Americas).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Analogy 🍽️**
> Imagine a large restaurant (the cellular network) with many tables (cells). Each table can seat multiple customers (users), but they must speak one at a time so they don't interrupt each other. GSM works similarly:
> - **FDMA (Frequency Division)** = Different tables (frequency channels) are placed in different parts of the restaurant.
> - **TDMA (Time Division)** = At each table, customers take turns speaking in fixed time slots.
> - **The Waiter (Base Station System)** = Coordinates who speaks when and at which table.
> - **The Manager (Mobile Switching Center)** = Connects calls between tables or to outside lines.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Metrics

- **Frequency Bands**: **890–915 MHz** (Uplink/MS→BTS), **935–960 MHz** (Downlink/BTS→MS)
- **Channel Bandwidth**: **200 kHz** per carrier
- **Total Carriers**: **124** (in 900 MHz band)
- **Modulation**: **GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying)** with BT = 0.3
- **Data Rate**: **270.833 kbps** per carrier
- **TDMA Slots**: **8** users per carrier
- **Speech Coding**: **RPE-LTP** (13 kbps) with channel coding to 22.8 kbps
- **Maximum Speed**: **250 km/h** (designed), practical limit ~**120 km/h**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGHLIGHT:** GSM is a **combination of FDMA and TDMA**. Each 200 kHz carrier is divided into **8 TDMA time slots**, allowing 8 simultaneous users per frequency channel. The duplex separation between uplink and downlink is **45 MHz**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of GSM — Stepwise Logic

**The Core Principle** rests on three pillars:

**Pillar 1: Cellular Concept (Frequency Reuse)**
- The geographical area is divided into **cells**, each served by a **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)**.
- The same frequency can be reused in non-adjacent cells separated by a minimum reuse distance **D**, governed by the co-channel interference ratio.

**Pillar 2: FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)**
- The available spectrum is divided into **124 carriers** of 200 kHz each.
- Each carrier is a pair: one for uplink, one for downlink (separated by **45 MHz** duplex spacing).

**Pillar 3: TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)**
- Each 200 kHz carrier is divided in **time** into recurring frames of 8 time slots.
- Each slot carries one user's burst of data.
- This gives **8 × 124 = 992** full-duplex physical channels per 25 MHz band.

### 2.2 GSM Architecture Functional Layers

GSM is structured into three major subsystems:

| Subsystem | Full Name | Core Function |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **MS** | Mobile Station | User equipment (handset + SIM) |
| **BSS** | Base Station Subsystem | Radio interface management (BTS + BSC) |
| **NSS** | Network & Switching Subsystem | Switching, routing, subscriber database (MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR) |
| **OSS** | Operation & Support Subsystem | Network monitoring and maintenance (OMC) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Description |
|-----------|----------------|------|-------------|
| Number of carriers (GSM 900) | $N_c = 124$ | carriers | Total RF channels |
| Carrier bandwidth | $B_c = 200$ | kHz | Per FDMA channel |
| Duplex spacing | $f_{duplex} = 45$ | MHz | Uplink vs downlink separation |
| TDMA slots per frame | $N_{slots} = 8$ | slots | Per RF carrier |
| Frame duration | $T_f = 4.615$ | ms | One TDMA frame |
| Slot duration | $T_s = 577$ | μs | Per user time slot |
| Bit period | $T_b = 3.692$ | μs | 1 bit duration |
| Bit rate per channel | $R_b = 270.833$ | kbps | Gross data rate |
| Speech bit rate (RPE-LTP) | $R_{speech} = 13$ | kbps | After source coding |
| Channel coded rate | $R_{cc} = 22.8$ | kbps | After convolutional coding |
| Reuse cluster size | $N = 7$ (typical) | cells | C/I ≥ 9 dB |
| Co-channel reuse ratio | $q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58$ | dimensionless | $D/R$ ratio |
| Maximum MS speed | $v = 250$ | km/h | System design limit |
| Hyperframe duration | $T_{hyper} = 3 \text{ h } 28 \text{ m } 53 \text{ s } 760 \text{ ms}$ | time | 2715648 TDMA frames |
| Frequency correction burst | $T_{FCCH} = 148$ bits | bits | 142 fixed + 3 guard + 3 tail |
| Access capability users | $n = N_c \times 8 = 992$ | channels | Full-duplex voice |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications of GSM:**
> - **Mobile Telephony**: Foundation of 2G voice networks worldwide.
> - **SMS (Short Message Service)**: 160-character text messaging.
> - **GPRS/EDGE (2.5G/2.75G)**: Packet data on GSM infrastructure.
> - **IoT/M2M**: GSM remains the backbone for low-power wide-area telemetry (smart meters, fleet tracking).
> - **International Roaming**: SIM-based portability enables global connectivity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: GSM Carrier Count from Spectrum Allocation

> **Logical Step-by-Step:**

The total uplink spectrum allotted to GSM 900 is from **890 MHz to 915 MHz**, giving:

$$
B_{total} = 915 - 890 = 25 \text{ MHz}
$$
