Each FDMA channel requires a bandwidth of 200 kHz (including a 200 kHz guard spacing at the band edges). Therefore, the number of carriers is:

$$
N_c = \frac{B_{total}}{B_c} = \frac{25 \text{ MHz}}{200 \text{ kHz}} = \frac{25 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^3} = 125
$$

However, a **guard band of 100 kHz** is reserved at each edge of the spectrum, effectively leaving only:

$$
B_{usable} = 25 \text{ MHz} - (2 \times 100 \text{ kHz}) = 24.8 \text{ MHz}
$$

$$
N_c = \frac{24.8 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^3} = 124 \text{ carriers}
$$

**Result:** GSM 900 supports **124 RF carriers** ✅ (matches KTU standard answer)

### 3.2 Derivation 2: TDMA Frame Hierarchy

The GSM frame structure follows a strict hierarchy. Let us derive each level:

**Level 1 — Burst (1 Time Slot Duration):**

One time slot contains **156.25 bits**, with a duration of:

$$
T_{slot} = \frac{156.25 \text{ bits}}{270.833 \text{ kbps}} = \frac{156.25}{270833} \approx 577 \text{ μs}
$$

**Level 2 — TDMA Frame:**

A TDMA frame consists of **8 time slots** and lasts:

$$
T_{frame} = 8 \times 577 \text{ μs} = 4.615 \text{ ms}
$$

**Level 3 — Multiframe (26 frames):**

A traffic multiframe contains **26 frames** and represents one complete TCH (Traffic Channel) cycle:

$$
T_{multiframe} = 26 \times 4.615 \text{ ms} = 120 \text{ ms}
$$

**Level 4 — Superframe (51 × 26 or 26 × 51):**

A superframe is **1326 frames** long:

$$
T_{superframe} = 1326 \times 4.615 \text{ ms} = 6.12 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Level 5 — Hyperframe (2048 Superframes):**

$$
T_{hyperframe} = 2048 \times 6.12 \text{ s} = 12533.76 \text{ s} = 3\text{h } 28\text{m } 53.76\text{s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM TRAP:** The hyperframe has **2715648 TDMA frames** total, used for **frequency hopping** and **ciphering algorithms (A3/A5/A8)**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Co-Channel Reuse Ratio for GSM

> **Logical Step-by-Step:**

For a hexagonal cellular system with cluster size **N**, the co-channel reuse ratio is:

$$
q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}
$$

For GSM's standard reuse pattern of **N = 7**:

$$
q = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583
$$

This guarantees a **Carrier-to-Interference ratio (C/I)** of at least:

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(q^6 \times i_0^{-1}) + (q^6 \times i_1^{-1}) + (q^6 \times i_2^{-1}) + (q^6 \times i_3^{-1}) + (q^6 \times i_4^{-1}) + (q^6 \times i_5^{-1})}{6}
$$

For an ideal hexagonal layout with 6 first-tier co-channel interferers and $i_0 = 1, i_1 = \dots$ at integer multiples of the shift parameter, the simplified C/I for N=7 yields:

$$
\frac{C}{I} \geq 17 \text{ dB}
$$

which satisfies the GSM requirement of **C/I ≥ 9 dB** with ample margin.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Number of Users per Cell

> **Logical Step-by-Step:**

For a cell with **k carriers** assigned and **8 time slots** per carrier:

$$
\text{Users per cell} = k \times 8
$$

If the cluster has 7 cells and the system has 124 carriers total:

$$
\text{Carriers per cell} = \frac{N_c}{N} = \frac{124}{7} \approx 17.71
$$

Rounding down to **17 carriers per cell**:

$$
\text{Users per cell} = 17 \times 8 = 136 \text{ simultaneous full-rate voice users}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GSM Block Diagram — System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS["MS: Mobile Station\nUser + SIM Card"]
    BTS["BTS: Base Transceiver Station\nRadio Modem - Antennas"]
    BSC["BSC: Base Station Controller\nHandover, Radio Resource Mgmt"]
    MSC["MSC: Mobile Switching Center\nSwitching - Call Routing"]
    HLR["HLR: Home Location Register\nSubscriber Database"]
    VLR["VLR: Visitor Location Register\nRoaming User Database"]
    AUC["AUC: Authentication Center\nSecurity - Ki Keys"]
    EIR["EIR: Equipment Identity Register\nIMEI Validation"]
    PSTN["PSTN / ISDN\nPublic Network"]
    OMC["OMC: Operations Maint Center\nNetwork Monitoring"]

    MS -->|"Um Air Interface\nTDMA Frames"| BTS
    BTS -->|"Abis Interface\nLapD Protocol"| BSC
    BSC -->|"A Interface\nSS7 Signaling"| MSC
    MSC --> PSTN
    MSC --> HLR
    MSC --> VLR
    HLR --- AUC
    MSC --- EIR
    OMC --- BSC
    OMC --- MSC
```

### 4.2 GSM Frame Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HF["HYPERFRAME\n2048 Superframes = 3h 28m 53.76s\n2715648 TDMA Frames\nEncryption + Frequency Hopping"]
    SF["SUPERFRAME\n1326 TDMA Frames = 6.12 s\nContains 51 Traffic Multiframes\nOR 26 Control Multiframes"]
    MF["MULTIFRAME\n26 Frames = 120 ms\nTraffic Channels"]
    FR["TDMA FRAME\n8 Time Slots = 4.615 ms"]
    TS["TIME SLOT / BURST\n156.25 Bits = 577 μs\nUser Data Unit"]
    BIT["BIT DURATION\n3.692 μs per bit\n270.833 kbps"]

    HF --> SF
    SF --> MF
    MF --> FR
    FR --> TS
    TS --> BIT
```

### 4.3 GSM Transmission Chain Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Analog Voice\n300-3400 Hz"] --> B["A/D Converter\n8 kHz Sampling"]
    B --> C["RPE-LTP Speech Coder\n13 kbps"]
    C --> D["Channel Coding\nConvolutional 1/2 rate\n22.8 kbps"]
    D --> E["Interleaving\n8 Bursts Spread"]
    E --> F["Ciphering\nA5 Algorithm"]
    F --> G["Burst Formatting\n148 Bits + Training Seq"]
    G --> H["GMSK Modulation\nBT = 0.3"]
    H --> I["RF Upconversion\n890-915 MHz UL"]
    I --> J["Antenna Transmission\nUm Interface"]
```

### 4.4 GSM Channel Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CH["GSM PHYSICAL CHANNELS\n124 Carriers x 8 Slots = 992"]
    LOG["LOGICAL CHANNELS Mapped to Slots"]
    TCH["TRAFFIC CHANNELS TCH\nCarry Voice and Data"]
    CCH["CONTROL CHANNELS CCH\nSignaling and Sync"]
    BCH["BCH Broadcast CH\nFCCH SCH BCCH"]
    CCCH["CCCH Common Control\nPCH RACH AGCH"]
    DCCH["DCCH Dedicated Control\nSDCCH SACCH FACCH"]

    CH --> LOG
    LOG --> TCH
    LOG --> CCH
    CCH --> BCH
    CCH --> CCCH
    CCH --> DCCH
```

### 4.5 GSM Logical Channel Mapping (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TrafficMultiframe["Traffic Multiframe (26 Frames)"]
        T0["Frame 0\nIdle"] --> T1["Frame 1\nTCH"] --> T2["Frame 2\nTCH"]
        T2 --> T3["Frame 3\nTCH"] --> T4["Frame 4\nTCH"] --> T5["Frame 5\nTCH"]
        T5 --> T6["Frame 6\nTCH"] --> T7["Frame 7\nTCH"] --> T8["Frame 8\nTCH"]
        T8 --> T9["Frame 9\nTCH"] --> T10["Frame 10\nTCH"] --> T11["Frame 11\nTCH"]
        T11 --> T12["Frame 12\nSACCH"] --> T25["...Frame 25\nTCH"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** Out of 26 frames in a traffic multiframe, **24 carry TCH**, **1 carries Slow Associated Control Channel (SACCH)**, and **1 is idle**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define GSM. List any four salient features of GSM.**

**Model Answer:**

> **GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications)** is a 2G digital cellular standard developed by ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute) for mobile voice and data communication.

**Four salient features:**
1. **Digital modulation** using GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) with BT = 0.3.
2. **FDMA + TDMA** hybrid multiple access: 124 carriers × 8 time slots = 992 channels.
3. **SIM-based subscriber identity** enabling international roaming and personal mobility.
4. **Encryption and authentication** via A3/A5/A8 algorithms stored on the SIM card.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the difference between FDMA and TDMA as used in GSM?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | FDMA | TDMA |
|--------|------|------|
| **Basis** | Frequency separation | Time separation |
| **Division** | 25 MHz spectrum → 124 carriers of 200 kHz | Each carrier → 8 time slots |
| **Simultaneous users per carrier** | 1 (per direction) | 8 |
| **Guard band** | 200 kHz between carriers | 8.25 bit periods (≈30.4 μs) between slots |
| **Function in GSM** | Allocates the RF channel | Allocates the time slot to a specific user |

In GSM, **both** FDMA and TDMA are used together — FDMA provides the carrier, and TDMA shares each carrier among 8 users.

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]

(a) Draw and explain the block diagram of the GSM system architecture, identifying the major subsystems. **(7 marks)**

(b) Explain the GSM TDMA frame hierarchy from burst to hyperframe with their durations. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A:**

**Part (a) — GSM Architecture:**

The GSM system is divided into **three major subsystems** plus one support subsystem. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) consists of:

**1. Mobile Station (MS) — User End:**
- **Mobile Equipment (ME):** The physical handset with unique **IMEI** (International Mobile Equipment Identity) number.
- **Subscriber Identity Module (SIM):** Smart card holding **IMSI, Ki, and authentication algorithms**.
- Functions: Radio transmission/reception, user interface, and subscriber identification.

**2. Base Station Subsystem (BSS) — Radio Access Network:**

**(a) Base Transceiver Station (BTS):**
- Contains radio transceivers, antennas, and signal processing units.
- Handles the **Um air interface** (radio link with MS).
- Performs GMSK modulation, channel coding/decoding, and equalization.

**(b) Base Station Controller (BSC):**
- Manages multiple BTSs.
- Performs **radio resource allocation, handover decision**, and **power control**.
- Uses **TRAU (Transcoding and Rate Adaptation Unit)** for speech codec.

**3. Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS) — Core Network:**

**(a) Mobile Switching Center (MSC):**
- Heart of GSM network; performs call switching, routing, and connection to PSTN/ISDN.
- Coordinates inter-BSC handovers.

**(b) Home Location Register (HLR):**
- Permanent database of subscribers belonging to its network.
- Stores IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, and service subscription.

**(c) Visitor Location Register (VLR):**
- Temporary database of subscribers currently roaming in its area.
- Mirrors selected HLR data for fast access.

**(d) Authentication Center (AUC):**
- Stores authentication keys (Ki) and generates SRES triplets for verifying subscriber identity.

**(e) Equipment Identity Register (EIR):**
- Maintains a list of valid, stolen, or faulty mobile equipment using IMEI.

**4. Operation Support Subsystem (OSS):**
- **OMC (Operations and Maintenance Center):** Handles network monitoring, fault management, and configuration.

> **Marking Note:** A labeled block diagram is worth **3 marks**; explanation of each block is **4 marks**.

---

**Part (b) — GSM TDMA Frame Hierarchy:**

Refer to Section 4.2 for the complete hierarchy. The structure from smallest to largest is:

**1. Bit Period:**
$$
T_b = \frac{1}{270.833 \text{ kbps}} \approx 3.692 \text{ μs}
$$

**2. Time Slot (Burst):** **156.25 bits** = 0.546 ms gross, 0.577 ms including guard.
- Contains: 3 tail + 57 info + 26 training + 57 info + 3 tail = 148 data bits + 8.25 guard bits.

**3. TDMA Frame:** **8 time slots** = 4.615 ms duration.

**4. Multiframe:** Two types:
- **Traffic Multiframe:** 26 frames = 120 ms
- **Control Multiframe:** 51 frames = 235.38 ms

**5. Superframe:** 1326 TDMA frames = 6.12 seconds.

**6. Hyperframe:** 2048 superframes = 3 h 28 m 53.76 s = 2,715,648 frames.

> **Function of Hyperframe:** Provides the cycle length for **frequency hopping** sequences and **ciphering** (encryption) algorithms.

> **Marking Note:** Mentioning **all 5 levels with duration values** = **5 marks**; stating the **function of each level** = **2 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]

(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the GSM transmission chain from speech input to RF output. **(7 marks)**

(b) Explain the GSM logical channel structure, distinguishing between traffic and control channels. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B:**

**Part (a) — GSM Transmission Chain:**

Refer to Section 4.3 (GSM Transmission Chain Block Diagram). The step-by-step process is:

**Step 1: Source Encoding (Speech)**
- Input: Analog voice (300 Hz–3400 Hz) sampled at **8 kHz**, 13-bit linear PCM.
- **RPE-LTP (Regular Pulse Excitation – Long Term Prediction)** speech coder compresses to **13 kbps**.

**Step 2: Channel Coding**
- Block convolutional coding (rate 1/2, constraint length 5) plus 4-bit CRC.
- Output: **22.8 kbps** protected data.
- Purpose: Error protection against fading and interference.

**Step 3: Interleaving**
- The 456 bits of one 20 ms speech frame are spread over **8 consecutive bursts** (interleaving depth = 8).
- This protects against burst errors from Rayleigh fading.

**Step 4: Ciphering**
- A5 algorithm XORs the data with a pseudo-random sequence derived from the Kc ciphering key.

**Step 5: Burst Formatting**
- Data is packed into a normal burst of **148 bits** + 8.25 bit guard period = 156.25 bits.

**Step 6: GMSK Modulation**
- Gaussian-filtered MSK with BT = 0.3.
- Constant envelope, narrow spectrum, good spectral efficiency.

**Step 7: RF Up-conversion**
- Carrier frequency in the 890–915 MHz band.
- Power amplifier output ~0.1–2 W for handsets, up to 20 W for vehicles.

**Step 8: Antenna Transmission**
- Signal radiated over the **Um interface** to the BTS.

**Part (b) — GSM Logical Channels:**

Logical channels are mapped onto the **8 physical time slots** of each TDMA frame. They are divided into two broad categories:

**A. Traffic Channels (TCH):** Carry user information (speech/data).
- **TCH/F (Full Rate):** 22.8 kbps gross, 13 kbps speech.
- **TCH/H (Half Rate):** 11.4 kbps gross, 6.5 kbps speech (doubles capacity).

**B. Control Channels (CCH):** Carry signaling and synchronization.

**B.1 Broadcast Channels (BCH) — Downlink only:**
- **FCCH (Frequency Correction CH):** Carries a fixed bit pattern for MS to lock onto frequency.
- **SCH (Synchronization CH):** Carries TDMA frame number and BSIC (Base Station Identity Code).
- **BCCH (Broadcast Control CH):** Carries system information (cell ID, location area code, frequency list).

**B.2 Common Control Channels (CCCH):**
- **PCH (Paging CH):** BTS pages MS for incoming calls (downlink).
- **RACH (Random Access CH):** MS requests channel assignment (uplink).
- **AGCH (Access Grant CH):** BSC assigns channel to MS (downlink).

**B.3 Dedicated Control Channels (DCCH):**
- **SDCCH (Stand-alone Dedicated Control CH):** Used for call setup, location updating, SMS transfer.
- **SACCH (Slow Associated Control CH):** Carries measurement reports (8 frames/multiframe = 480 ms).
- **FACCH (Fast Associated Control CH):** Steals TCH frames for urgent signaling like handover.

> **Marking Note:** Distinguishing between **BCH, CCCH, DCCH** = 4 marks; explaining **TCH/F vs TCH/H** = 2 marks; example of FACCH stealing = 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Frame Hierarchy Trap:** Students often confuse **26-frame (Traffic)** vs **51-frame (Control)** multiframes. Always specify which type.
> 2. **Block Diagram Marks:** A diagram without labels is **0 marks**. Always label: Um, Abis, A, and B/C/D/E interfaces.
> 3. **Speech Rate Confusion:** Don't write 9.6 kbps (that is GSM data) for voice. RPE-LTP is **13 kbps**, channel coded to **22.8 kbps**.
> 4. **Hyperframe vs Frame Number:** The hyperframe number (0 to 2,715,647) is used for ciphering. TDMA frame number alone is **0–26 × 51 × 2048**.
> 5. **GSM ≠ 3G:** Do not mix GSM and UMTS architecture; UMTS uses WCDMA and a completely different core (SGSN/GGSN).
> 6. **FCCH vs SCH:** FCCH has **fixed 142 bits** of all-zeros (frequency burst); SCH carries the frame number — they are separate bursts, not the same.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **🔥 HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS:**

- **GSM = 2G, ETSI standard, 1991**, first deployed in Finland.
- **Access Method:** FDMA + TDMA hybrid. **124 carriers × 8 slots = 992** channels.
- **Uplink:** 890–915 MHz, **Downlink:** 935–960 MHz. **Duplex spacing = 45 MHz.**
- **Modulation:** GMSK with **BT = 0.3**, data rate **270.833 kbps**.
- **Speech Coder:** RPE-LTP at **13 kbps**, channel coded to **22.8 kbps** (rate 1/2 convolutional).
- **Frame Hierarchy:** Burst (0.577 ms) → Frame (4.615 ms) → Multiframe (120 ms / 235.38 ms) → Superframe (6.12 s) → Hyperframe (3h 28m 53.76s).
- **Hyperframe length:** **2,715,648** TDMA frames.
- **Architecture:** MS + BSS (BTS, BSC) + NSS (MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR) + OSS (OMC).
- **SIM** holds IMSI, Ki, and ciphering algorithms (A3, A5, A8).
- **IMEI** is the equipment's identity, stored in **EIR** (white/grey/black list).
- **Logical Channels:** TCH (traffic) and CCH (BCH + CCCH + DCCH).
- **FACCH** = "fast" steal of TCH for urgent signaling (e.g., handover).
- **Cluster size N = 7** is the most common reuse pattern; reuse ratio $q = \sqrt{3N}$.
- **Interfaces:** **Um** (MS↔BTS), **Abis** (BTS↔BSC), **A** (BSC↔MSC).
- **Handover types:** Intra-cell, Inter-cell (same BSC), Inter-BSC, Inter-MSC.
- **Power classes:** Class 1 = 20 W (vehicle), Class 2 = 8 W, ..., Class 4 = 2 W (handheld), Class 5 = 0.8 W.
- **Security algorithms:** A3 (authentication), A8 (key generation), A5 (ciphering).
- **GSM is a circuit-switched network**; GPRS adds packet switching on top.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 4: Wireless Technologies – Wireless LAN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless LAN (WLAN)** is a flexible data communication system that uses **Radio Frequency (RF)** or **Infrared (IR)** waves as the physical transmission medium to provide networked connectivity between devices within a limited geographic area (typically a building or campus), implementing the **IEEE 802.11** family of standards (commonly branded as **Wi-Fi**).

The term **Wi-Fi** is a trademark of the Wi-Fi Alliance and refers to devices that have been certified as interoperable under the **IEEE 802.11** standard family. WLANs extend or replace traditional wired LANs in environments where cabling is impractical, expensive, or where mobility is a primary requirement.

**WLAN Standard Evolution (IEEE 802.11 Family):**

| Standard | Year | Band | Max Data Rate | Modulation |
|----------|------|------|---------------|------------|
| 802.11 (Legacy) | 1997 | 2.4 GHz | **2 Mbps** | DSSS / FHSS |
| 802.11a | 1999 | **5 GHz** | **54 Mbps** | OFDM |
| 802.11b | 1999 | 2.4 GHz | **11 Mbps** | DSSS / CCK |
| 802.11g | 2003 | 2.4 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 GHz | **600 Mbps** | OFDM / MIMO |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | 2013 | **5 GHz** | **6.93 Gbps** | OFDM / MU-MIMO |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E) | 2019/2020 | 2.4/5/**6 GHz** | **9.6 Gbps** | OFDMA / 1024-QAM |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Radio Cafe"**
>
> Imagine a crowded coffee shop where customers (devices) want to talk to the cashier (Access Point). Since everyone is moving around and no one wants to sit near a power outlet to plug in a network cable, the shop installs a small radio station at the counter. Customers can now walk anywhere inside the shop and talk to the cashier over the air.
>
> - The **cashier** = **Access Point (AP)**
> - The **customers** = **Stations (STAs) / Mobile devices**
> - The **radio channel** = **shared wireless medium (2.4/5/6 GHz RF)**
> - The **"please wait, I'm talking" rule** = **CSMA/CA protocol**
> - The **coffee shop walls** = **Coverage area / BSS**

In a wired Ethernet network, devices listen to the cable for collisions. In wireless, this is impossible because a station cannot simultaneously transmit and listen on the same frequency (the transmitted signal is millions of times stronger than any received signal). This is the **"Near-Far Problem"** and is the central reason **WLANs use CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** instead of CSMA/CD (Collision Detection) used in Ethernet.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Metrics

The fundamental physical parameters governing WLAN performance are:

- **Operating Frequencies (ISM Bands):** 2.4 GHz (Industrial, Scientific, Medical), 5 GHz (UNII), 6 GHz (Wi-Fi 6E)
- **Typical Indoor Range:** 35 m (2.4 GHz) to 15 m (5 GHz)
- **Typical Outdoor Range:** Up to 100–200 m
- **Channel Bandwidth:** 20 MHz, 40 MHz, 80 MHz, 160 MHz, 320 MHz
- **Channel Count (2.4 GHz):** Up to **3 non-overlapping channels** (1, 6, 11)
- **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):** Determines effective range, given by the **Friis Transmission Equation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 2.4 GHz penetrates walls better than 5 GHz?**
> Lower frequencies have longer wavelengths, which diffract around and penetrate physical obstructions more effectively. However, lower frequencies support lower data rates due to the Shannon-Hartley limit (Bandwidth × log₂(1 + SNR)).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 WLAN Architecture (IEEE 802.11 Building Blocks)

The IEEE 802.11 architecture is built on a hierarchy of components:

**A) Station (STA):** Any device containing an IEEE 802.11 conformant MAC and PHY interface (laptops, phones, IoT sensors).

**B) Basic Service Set (BSS):** The fundamental building block of a WLAN — a group of STAs that communicate with each other.
- **Independent BSS (IBSS) / Ad-hoc Mode:** STAs communicate **directly** without an AP (peer-to-peer). Used for temporary networks.
- **Infrastructure BSS:** STAs communicate **only through an AP**. This is the most common mode in homes and offices.

**C) Distribution System (DS):** The backbone (typically wired Ethernet) that connects multiple BSSs together via their APs, allowing roaming between them.

**D) Extended Service Set (ESS):** A collection of one or more infrastructure BSSs interconnected by a DS. This appears as a single logical LAN to upper layers.

**E) Portal:** The logical integration point where the DS meets a non-802.11 network (e.g., the Internet via a router).

### 2.2 The IEEE 802.11 Protocol Stack

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Upper Layers (TCP/IP, Applications)   │  ← LLC sublayer
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│      IEEE 802.2 LLC (Logical Link)      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│      IEEE 802.11 MAC Sublayer           │  ← CSMA/CA, Fragmentation, Security
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   IEEE 802.11 PHY (a/b/g/n/ac/ax)       │  ← OFDM, DSSS, MIMO
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.3 The MAC Sublayer — CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Avoidance)

This is the **heart of WLAN operation** and a guaranteed KTU question topic.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Listen (Physical Carrier Sensing):** A station that wants to transmit first senses the medium. If idle for a period called **DIFS (Distributed Inter-frame Space)**, it proceeds.
2. **Backoff Counter:** The station picks a random integer from [0, CW], where **CW = Contention Window**. The station decrements this counter for every idle slot. It freezes when the medium becomes busy and resumes after DIFS.
3. **Transmit:** When the counter hits 0, the station transmits the entire frame (no CD possible).
4. **Acknowledge:** The receiver waits **SIFS (Short Inter-frame Space)** and returns an **ACK (Acknowledgment)** to confirm successful reception.
5. **Collision Handling:** If no ACK is received within a timeout, the transmitter assumes a collision occurred and **doubles CW** (up to CW_max) and retries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no Collision Detection?**
> - Wireless transceivers cannot transmit and receive simultaneously (the TX power dwarfs the received signal strength).
> - Hidden node problem: A station may not "hear" another transmitter's signal due to obstacles.
> - Therefore, the protocol is designed to **avoid** collisions rather than detect them.

### 2.4 Inter-Frame Spaces (IFS) — The Priority Hierarchy

$$
\text{SIFS} < \text{PIFS} < \text{DIFS} < \text{EIFS}
$$

| IFS | Duration (802.11b) | Used For | Priority |
|-----|-------------------|----------|----------|
| **SIFS** | 10 µs | ACK, CTS, Fragment | Highest |
| **PIFS** | 30 µs | PCF (Contention-free) | High |
| **DIFS** | 50 µs | DCF Data frames | Normal |
| **EIFS** | ~364 µs | Error recovery | Lowest |

### 2.5 Solving the Hidden Node Problem — RTS / CTS Handshake

The **Hidden Node Problem** occurs when stations A and C cannot hear each other but both transmit to B, causing a collision at B. The solution is the **RTS/CTS mechanism**:

1. Sender transmits **RTS (Request To Send)** — short, contains duration field.
2. Receiver replies with **CTS (Clear To Send)** after SIFS.
3. All stations within range (sender's and receiver's) set their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** based on the duration in RTS/CTS and defer transmission.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---------|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | $d$ = distance (km), $f$ = frequency (MHz) | Predicts received signal strength in open air |
| **Received Power (Friis)** | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | $P_t$ = TX power, $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains, $\lambda$ = wavelength | Range planning for APs |
| **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $B$ = bandwidth, SNR = signal-to-noise ratio | Theoretical max data rate |
| **DCF Throughput (approx.)** | $S \approx \dfrac{P_s \cdot E[L]}{T_s}$ | $P_s$ = success probability, $E[L]$ = avg payload, $T_s$ = slot time | WLAN capacity planning |
| **Contention Window (Binary Exp.)** | $CW_{i+1} = \min(2 \cdot CW_i, CW_{\max})$ | $CW_0 = 31$ (802.11b), $CW_{\max} = 1023$ | Backoff behavior |
| **SIFS Duration (802.11b)** | $\text{SIFS} = 10\ \mu s$ | DSSS PHY | Priority timing |
| **DIFS Duration** | $\text{DIFS} = \text{SIFS} + 2 \cdot \text{SlotTime}$ | SlotTime = 20 µs (802.11b) | Standardized timing |
| **RTS / CTS duration field** | $\text{NAV}_\text{set} = T_{\text{data}} + 3 \cdot \text{SIFS} + 2 \cdot \text{ACK} + T_{\text{CTS}}$ | Frame timing parameters | Hidden node resolution |
| **Number of Channels (2.4 GHz)** | $N = \lfloor (83.5 - 2.412) / 5 \rfloor + 1 \approx 11$ | Band: 2.412 – 2.484 GHz | Channel planning |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

WLANs underpin nearly all modern enterprise, residential, and public internet access. Engineers apply this theory when:
- **Designing campus networks:** Estimating AP placement using FSPL
- **Capacity planning:** Using Shannon's limit to select channel bandwidths
- **QoS provisioning:** Using 802.11e (WMM) — which itself extends the IFS hierarchy with **AIFS, TXOP**
- **Security architecture:** Implementing WPA3-Enterprise (802.1X + AES-256-GCMP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The four standard IFS values and their uses are the most commonly tested 2-mark concept. Memorize the priority ordering **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS** and the typical 802.11b values (10, 30, 50, ~364 µs).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) Using Friis Transmission Equation

The Friis transmission equation gives the received power of a signal transmitted over free space (line-of-sight, no obstacles).

**Step 1:** Start with the geometric spreading of an isotropic radiator. A transmitter radiating power $P_t$ uniformly distributes energy across a sphere of radius $d$.

$$
\text{Power Flux Density} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 2:** The receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures a fraction of this density.

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

where $G_r$ is the receive antenna gain and $\lambda$ is the wavelength of the carrier.

**Step 3:** Combine the two equations. The received power is the product of flux density and effective aperture.

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot G_t \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange into the canonical Friis equation.

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert wavelength to frequency using $\lambda = c / f$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{c}{4\pi d f}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Express path loss in decibels (dB), which is a logarithmic ratio and is more practical for engineering.

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_r}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

**Step 7:** Apply the identity $10 \log_{10}(x^2) = 20 \log_{10}(x)$.

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 8:** Substitute the constants in engineering units ($d$ in km, $f$ in MHz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s = $3 \times 10^5$ km/s) and evaluate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} &= 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot d_{\text{km}} \cdot 1000 \cdot f_{\text{MHz}} \cdot 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{3} \cdot d_{\text{km}} \cdot f_{\text{MHz}} \cdot 10^{-3}\right) \\
&\approx 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Range Estimation for a 2.4 GHz AP

**Problem:** An Access Point transmits at **20 dBm (100 mW)** with antenna gain **+2 dBi**. The receiver's minimum sensitivity is **-82 dBm** with a 0 dBi antenna. Find the maximum operating range in free space at **2.4 GHz**.

**Step 1:** Convert all values to dB and compute the maximum allowable path loss.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= 20\ \text{dBm} \\
G_t &= +2\ \text{dBi} \\
G_r &= 0\ \text{dBi} \\
P_{r,\text{min}} &= -82\ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the link budget equation.

$$
P_{r,\text{min}} = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for FSPL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}_{\max} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{r,\text{min}} \\
&= 20 + 2 + 0 - (-82) \\
&= 104\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the FSPL formula with $f = 2400$ MHz.

$$
104 = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(2400)
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the frequency term.

$$
20 \log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.3802 = 67.604
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute and solve for $d$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
104 &= 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 67.604 \\
20 \log_{10}(d) &= 104 - 32.44 - 67.604 = 3.956 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= 0.1978 \\
d &= 10^{0.1978} \approx 1.578\ \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Apply a practical fade margin. In real deployments, we subtract **20 dB** to account for multipath fading, walls, and interference.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}_{\text{practical}} &= 104 - 20 = 84\ \text{dB} \\
20 \log_{10}(d) &= 84 - 32.44 - 67.604 = -16.044 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= -0.8022 \\
d &= 10^{-0.8022} \approx 0.157\ \text{km} = 157\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Realistically, the AP will cover approximately **150 m in open air** or **30–40 m indoors** when factoring in walls, multipath, and human absorption.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DCF Backoff Evolution

| Attempt (i) | Contention Window $CW_i$ | Backoff Range | Reasoning |
|-------------|--------------------------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 (initial) | 31 | [0, 31] | First transmission attempt |
| 2 (after 1st fail) | 63 | [0, 63] | $2 \times 31$ |
| 3 (after 2nd fail) | 127 | [0, 127] | $2 \times 63$ |
| 4 (after 3rd fail) | 255 | [0, 255] | $2 \times 127$ |
| 5 (after 4th fail) | 511 | [0, 511] | $2 \times 255$ |
| 6 (after 5th fail) | 1023 | [0, 1023] | $CW_{\max}$ (802.11b) |
| 7+ (retransmits) | 1023 | [0, 1023] | Stays at maximum |
| **Retry limit (m)** | 1023 | — | Up to 7 attempts (aCWmax) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic for the Backoff:** After every failed transmission (no ACK), the backoff window doubles to spread the re-attempts across a larger random window. This is **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** and is mathematically equivalent to a discrete-time stochastic process with adaptive collision probability.

### 3.4 Analytical Proof: Why CSMA/CA Cannot Use Collision Detection

**Given:** A station transmitting at power $P_t = 100$ mW (20 dBm) and receiving a signal at distance $d = 10$ m.

**Step 1:** Calculate received power using Friis equation at 2.4 GHz (assume isotropic antennas, $G_t = G_r = 1$).

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125\ \text{m}
$$

$$
P_r = 0.1 \times 1 \times 1 \times \left(\frac{0.125}{4\pi \times 10}\right)^{2} = 0.1 \times (0.000994)^{2} = 9.89 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W} \approx -40\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with transmitted power.

$$
\frac{P_t}{P_r} = \frac{0.1}{9.89 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1{,}011{,}000 \approx 60\ \text{dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** The transmitted signal is **over one million times stronger** than any incoming signal at the antenna. The receiver's ADC would be saturated by the station's own transmission, making collision detection impossible. This is the engineering reason CSMA/CD is replaced with CSMA/CA in WLANs.

```mermaid
    Note over Medium: Physical carrier sense triggered
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: DCF Backoff State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> WaitDIFS: Frame Ready to Transmit
    WaitDIFS --> SenseCW: DIFS Expired
    SenseCW --> Decrement: Slot Time Idle
    Decrement --> SenseCW: Counter greater than 0
    Decrement --> TransmitFrame: Counter equals 0
    TransmitFrame --> WaitACK: Frame Sent
    WaitACK --> Idle: ACK Received
    WaitACK --> DoubleCW: Timeout No ACK
    DoubleCW --> WaitDIFS: Retry Limit Not Exceeded
    DoubleCW --> DropFrame: Retry Limit Exceeded
    SenseCW --> Freeze: Medium Busy
    Freeze --> WaitDIFS: Medium Idle plus DIFS
    DropFrame --> [*]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term **WLAN**. List any two advantages of WLAN over wired LAN.

**Model Answer:**
A Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) is a network that interconnects devices within a limited geographic area using wireless (RF or IR) communication, implementing the **IEEE 802.11** standard family (Wi-Fi).

**Advantages:**
1. **Mobility** — Users can move freely within the coverage area while staying connected.
2. **Easy and flexible installation** — No cabling required, reducing deployment cost and time in difficult-to-wire locations (heritage buildings, outdoor campuses).
3. **Scalability** — Adding a new station requires no new cabling infrastructure.
4. **Reduced cost of ownership** in dynamic environments.

*(Any two well-explained points earn full 2 marks.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** Differentiate between **BSS** and **ESS** in IEEE 802.11 architecture.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BSS (Basic Service Set) | ESS (Extended Service Set) |
|-----------|-------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Smallest building block; a group of stations controlled by a single AP (or in ad-hoc mode, no AP) | Collection of one or more BSSs interconnected via a Distribution System (DS) |
| **AP Required** | Yes (infrastructure) / No (ad-hoc IBSS) | Yes (each BSS has its own AP) |
| **Coverage** | Limited to a single cell (typically 30–100 m radius) | Large area, spanning multiple cells (whole building/campus) |
| **Roaming Support** | No | Yes — STAs can roam between BSSs without losing connectivity |
| **Topology** | Star (infrastructure) or Mesh (ad-hoc) | Multi-cellular with seamless handoff |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Never confuse CSMA/CA with CSMA/CD.** Examiners specifically test this. WLAN = CSMA/CA (no collision detection), Ethernet = CSMA/CD.
> - **Always state units** in numerical answers (µs, dBm, dB, m, MHz) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> - When asked "explain CSMA/CA", **draw the timing diagram** (DIFS → Backoff → Data → SIFS → ACK). A diagram is worth ~3 marks by itself.
> - **The 4 IFS values** must be memorized in correct order with their uses. Confusing DIFS with SIFS is a common error.

---

#### **Part B – QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1**

> **Q. A) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the **CSMA/CA** protocol used in IEEE 802.11 with a clear timing diagram. Discuss how the **RTS/CTS** mechanism solves the **hidden node problem**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **maximum free-space range** of a 5 GHz Wi-Fi AP that transmits at **30 dBm** with a transmit antenna gain of **+5 dBi** and a receiver sensitivity of **-78 dBm** (receiver antenna gain = 0 dBi). Apply a fade margin of **25 dB**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**1. CSMA/CA Operation:**

CSMA/CA is the medium access control protocol used in IEEE 802.11 WLANs to coordinate transmissions on the shared wireless medium. Unlike wired Ethernet's CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA is needed because wireless stations **cannot detect collisions** (their own TX signal drowns out any incoming signal at the antenna).

**2. Step-by-Step Process:**

1. A station with a frame to send **first senses the medium (physical carrier sensing)**.
2. If the medium is **busy**, the station defers and waits.
3. If the medium is **idle for a period of DIFS** (Distributed Inter-Frame Space = 50 µs for 802.11b), the station enters the **backoff phase**.
4. The station picks a **random integer backoff counter** uniformly from [0, CW], where **CW (Contention Window)** starts at $CW_{\min} = 31$.
5. The counter is **decremented by 1 for every idle slot**. If the medium becomes busy, the counter is **frozen** and resumed only after the next DIFS.
6. When the counter reaches **zero**, the station transmits the entire frame.
7. The receiver waits **SIFS (10 µs)** — which is shorter than DIFS — and replies with an **ACK**.
8. If the sender **does not receive ACK** within the timeout, it **doubles the CW** (Binary Exponential Backoff) and retries.

**3. RTS/CTS and Hidden Node Solution:**

The **Hidden Node Problem:** Stations A and C cannot hear each other (a wall is between them) but both reach AP B. A transmits, C senses idle, also transmits → collision at B.

**RTS/CTS Solution:**
- Before sending data, A sends a short **RTS (Request-To-Send)** containing a duration field indicating the time needed for the entire exchange.
- B replies (after SIFS) with **CTS (Clear-To-Send)**, also carrying a duration field.
- C, although out of A's range, **hears the CTS from B** and sets its **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** to defer transmissions for the indicated duration.
- All stations within radio range of either A or B are now aware of the upcoming transmission, preventing collision.

**4. Timing Diagram (drawn by student):**

```
        DIFS          Backoff
   ┌────┐  ┌────────┐  ┌────┐
   │    │  │  CW=31 │  │Data│
───┘    └──┘        └──┘    └─── SIFS ─┐
   Idle  Idle   Busy   Free           │ ACK
                                      │
                                   SIFS
```

*(The full timing diagram in the answer sheet should show: DIFS → Backoff slots → RTS → SIFS → CTS → SIFS → Data → SIFS → ACK → DIFS → next backoff.)*

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks — Steps of CSMA/CA
- 2 marks — IFS explanation (DIFS, SIFS, PIFS)
- 1 mark — Diagram
- 2 marks — RTS/CTS + hidden node problem

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $P_t = 30\ \text{dBm}$
- $G_t = +5\ \text{dBi}$
- $G_r = 0\ \text{dBi}$
- $P_{r,\min} = -78\ \text{dBm}$
- $f = 5000\ \text{MHz}$
- Fade margin = $25\ \text{dB}$

**Step 1:** Compute the maximum allowable FSPL (without fade margin).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}_{\max} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{r,\min} \\
&= 30 + 5 + 0 - (-78) \\
&= 113\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the fade margin to get practical FSPL.

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{practical}} = 113 - 25 = 88\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the FSPL formula.

$$
88 = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(5000)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute frequency term.

$$
20 \log_{10}(5000) = 20 \times 3.6990 = 73.98
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $d$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \log_{10}(d) &= 88 - 32.44 - 73.98 = -18.42 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= -0.921 \\
d &= 10^{-0.921} \approx 0.1198\ \text{km} \approx 119.8\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The maximum practical range is **≈ 120 m** in free space (line-of-sight). Real indoor coverage will be much less (~30 m) due to walls and multipath.

---

#### **Part B – QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 2**

> **Q. B) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **IEEE 802.11 protocol stack** and the functions of the **MAC sublayer**. Discuss the **four Inter-Frame Spaces (IFS)** and their priority hierarchy. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the salient features of **IEEE 802.11a, 802.11b, 802.11g, and 802.11n** standards. Mention the modulation scheme and maximum data rate of each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**1. IEEE 802.11 Protocol Stack:**

The IEEE 802.11 standard specifies only the two lower layers of the OSI model — the **Physical (PHY) layer** and the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer** of the Data Link layer.

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Higher Layers (TCP/IP, Network Apps) │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   LLC Sublayer (IEEE 802.2)            │  ← Bridges to upper layers
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   MAC Sublayer (IEEE 802.11)           │  ← CSMA/CA, Addressing, Security
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   PHY Layer (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac)   │  ← Modulation, RF transmission
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**2. MAC Sublayer Functions:**
- **Channel access** — Distributed Coordination Function (DCF) using CSMA/CA
- **Addressing** — 48-bit MAC addresses (4 address fields in the frame)
- **Frame fragmentation and reassembly**
- **Acknowledgment and retransmission** of erroneous frames
- **Security** — Encryption/authentication (WEP/WPA/WPA2/WPA3)
- **Power management** — Sleep and wake-up coordination
- **Roaming** — Handoff between BSSs

**3. Inter-Frame Spaces (IFS):**

| IFS | Duration (802.11b) | Function | Priority |
|-----|-------------------|----------|----------|
| **SIFS** | 10 µs | Used for highest-priority transmissions: ACK, CTS, frame fragments | Highest |
| **PIFS** | 30 µs | Used for PCF (Point Coordination Function) — contention-free period | High |
| **DIFS** | 50 µs | Standard DCF data frame transmission — default for normal stations | Normal |
| **EIFS** | ~364 µs | Used after a frame error to allow recovery | Lowest |

**Priority Hierarchy:** SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS (numerically larger = lower priority)

The **physical reasoning** is that a station waiting a shorter IFS gets first access to the medium, allowing time-critical responses (like ACK) to interleave with regular data traffic.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks — Protocol stack diagram
- 2 marks — MAC functions
- 3 marks — IFS table + priority explanation

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

| Parameter | 802.11a | 802.11b | 802.11g | 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|-------------------|
| **Year Released** | 1999 | 1999 | 2003 | 2009 |
| **Operating Band** | **5 GHz** (UNII) | **2.4 GHz** (ISM) | **2.4 GHz** (ISM) | **2.4 / 5 GHz** (dual-band) |
| **Channel BW** | 20 MHz | 20 MHz | 20 MHz | 20 / 40 MHz |
| **Modulation** | **OFDM (BPSK to 64-QAM)** | **DSSS / CCK** | **OFDM (BPSK to 64-QAM)** | **OFDM + MIMO** |
| **Max Data Rate** | **54 Mbps** | **11 Mbps** | **54 Mbps** | **600 Mbps** |
| **Range (typical indoor)** | ~25 m | ~35 m | ~35 m | ~50 m |
| **Interference Susceptibility** | Low (less crowded 5 GHz) | High (microwaves, Bluetooth) | High | Medium (dual-band option) |
| **Compatibility** | — | — | Backward compatible with 802.11b | Backward compatible with a/b/g |
| **Key Innovation** | First OFDM WLAN | Low-cost mass adoption | Speed of 'a' + range of 'b' | MIMO + channel bonding |

**Key Engineering Observations:**

- **802.11a** was the first to use **OFDM**, which splits the channel into 52 subcarriers, providing robustness against multipath fading.
- **802.11b** uses **CCK (Complementary Code Keying)**, a DSSS variant allowing 11 Mbps — but it crowds the 2.4 GHz band along with microwaves, Bluetooth, and ZigBee.
- **802.11g** brought OFDM's 54 Mbps to the 2.4 GHz band while maintaining backward compatibility with 802.11b.
- **802.11n** introduced **MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output)** and **channel bonding** (40 MHz), yielding up to 600 Mbps. With $4 \times 4$ MIMO, it provides 4 spatial streams, each at 150 Mbps.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 5 marks — Detailed comparison table
- 2 marks — Modulation explanation and key innovations

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **WLAN** = IEEE 802.11 standard family (Wi-Fi), uses 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz ISM/UNII bands.
- **Why CSMA/CA, not CSMA/CD:** Wireless stations cannot detect collisions (TX power ≫ RX signal strength).
- **IFS Priority (smallest = highest priority):** **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS** (802.11b values: 10, 30, 50, ~364 µs).
- **DIFS** = SIFS + 2 × SlotTime. **SIFS** is used for ACK/CTS/Fragment replies.
- **Hidden Node Problem** is solved by **RTS/CTS** handshake. The **CTS** propagates the reservation to the hidden node.
- **NAV (Network Allocation Vector):** A virtual carrier-sense timer set from RTS/CTS duration fields.
- **Binary Exponential Backoff:** CW doubles after each failed transmission (31 → 63 → 127 → ... → 1023).
- **802.11 Architecture Levels:** **STA → BSS → DS → ESS → Portal**.
- **BSS** = single AP + stations; **ESS** = multiple BSSs connected via DS; **IBSS** = ad-hoc (no AP).
- **FSPL Formula:** $\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$.
- **Higher frequency (5 GHz) = shorter range** but higher data rate (less wall penetration, less interference).
- **802.11 standards comparison:**
  - a: 5 GHz, 54 Mbps, OFDM
  - b: 2.4 GHz, 11 Mbps, DSSS/CCK
  - g: 2.4 GHz, 54 Mbps, OFDM
  - n (Wi-Fi 4): dual-band, 600 Mbps, OFDM + MIMO
  - ac (Wi-Fi 5): 5 GHz, 6.93 Gbps, MU-MIMO
  - ax (Wi-Fi 6): 9.6 Gbps, OFDMA
- **Security evolution:** WEP (broken) → WPA (TKIP) → WPA2 (AES-CCMP) → WPA3 (SAE, forward secrecy).
- **WLAN Frame** has 4 address fields (vs. 2 in Ethernet) to support the DS.
- **Channel allocation in 2.4 GHz:** Use channels **1, 6, 11** to avoid overlap (each channel = 5 MHz, but signal spans ~22 MHz).
- **Shannon Limit:** Max data rate $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — this is the theoretical ceiling for any modulation scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** For the ESE (End Semester Exam), expect at least one question on **CSMA/CA timing diagram** and one on **standards comparison**. Always draw the diagram and always include a comparison table — both are auto-grabbers of easy 3-5 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wi-Fi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wi-Fi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wi-Fi (Wireless Fidelity)** is a family of wireless local area networking (WLAN) technologies defined by the **IEEE 802.11 standard family**, which enables devices to exchange data over short-to-medium distances using radio waves in the unlicensed Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio bands — primarily **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, and (recently) **6 GHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Wi-Fi is a WLAN technology based on the IEEE 802.11 specifications, providing shared access to the wireless medium using CSMA/CA at the MAC sub-layer and operating in half-duplex broadcast mode within a Basic Service Set (BSS).*

In a formal KTU/ECE treatment, Wi-Fi is positioned as the practical realization of the lower two layers (PHY + MAC) of the OSI model over an unlicensed radio channel, with the **Distribution System (DS)** and **Access Point (AP)** providing the bridge to the wired Ethernet world.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a large, open-air **town-hall meeting** where many people want to speak, but only one microphone exists:

- If two people speak at the same time, no one is understood → this is a **collision** in radio.
- So, every person **waits for silence (DIFS)**, then picks a **random secret number (backoff counter)** and counts down before speaking.
- The receiver immediately says a short **"got it" (ACK)** to confirm.
- The **moderator (Access Point)** keeps the meeting organized, assigns the room, and translates words for the outside world (wired network).

That "town-hall etiquette" is exactly what **CSMA/CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) does in Wi-Fi.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Physical Constants (KTU Board Essentials)

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed-of-light propagation constant** used in all Wi-Fi timing/frequency-to-wavelength conversions:
> $$c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$
> 
> **Wavelength of a 2.4 GHz carrier:**
> $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ cm}$$
> 
> **Wavelength of a 5 GHz carrier:** $\lambda = 6$ cm.

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operating Bands | **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz** | Unlicensed ISM / U-NII |
| Channel Bandwidth | 20 / 40 / 80 / 160 / 320 MHz | OFDM channel width |
| Half-Duplex | Yes (no simultaneous TX/RX) | Forces CSMA/CA |
| Range (indoor) | ~**35 m** (802.11n), ~**50 m** (802.11ac) | Limited by FSPL |
| Typical PHY Rate | 54 Mbps (802.11a/g) → 9.6 Gbps (802.11ax) | Theoretical raw rate |
| MAC Throughput | ~**60-70%** of PHY rate | Protocol overhead |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board answers, **always state the standard number, frequency band, and modulation type** in a single introductory line — examiners award marks for precise IEEE 802.11 nomenclature.

---

## 1.4 The IEEE 802.11 Family Tree (Evolution)

| Standard | Year | Band | Max PHY Rate | Modulation | KTU Hot Keyword |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 802.11 (legacy) | 1997 | 2.4 GHz | **2 Mbps** | DSSS, FHSS | Birth of Wi-Fi |
| 802.11a | 1999 | 5 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM (BPSK→64-QAM) | OFDM debut |
| 802.11b | 1999 | 2.4 GHz | **11 Mbps** | HR-DSSS (CCK) | Long range |
| 802.11g | 2003 | 2.4 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM | Backward-compat b |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 GHz | **600 Mbps** | MIMO + OFDM | MIMO enters |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | 2014 | 5 GHz | **6.93 Gbps** | MU-MIMO, 256-QAM | Beamforming |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E) | 2019/2020 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | **9.6 Gbps** | OFDMA, 1024-QAM | Dense networks |
| 802.11be (Wi-Fi 7) | 2024 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | **46 Gbps** | 320 MHz, 4096-QAM | MLO, EHT |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Wi-Fi Network Architecture (IEEE 802.11 Topology)

The 802.11 architecture defines **four logical components** and **two physical addressing groupings**.

### 2.1.1 Core Logical Components

1. **Station (STA)** — Any device containing an 802.11 conformant MAC + PHY (laptop, phone, IoT sensor).
2. **Basic Service Set (BSS)** — The fundamental coverage unit: a set of STAs communicating with one another through a common MAC coordination function.
3. **Access Point (AP)** — A special STA that acts as a bridge between the wireless BSS and the wired Distribution System.
4. **Distribution System (DS)** — The "backhaul" (typically Ethernet) that interconnects multiple BSSs into a larger network.
5. **Extended Service Set (ESS)** — Two or more BSSs interconnected via a DS, sharing a common SSID (the home/office Wi-Fi network).
6. **Independent BSS (IBSS / Ad-hoc)** — Direct STA-to-STA without an AP (peer-to-peer Wi-Fi Direct).

### 2.1.2 Why does the BSS need an AP?

In infrastructure mode, the AP:
- **Beacons** (every ~100 ms) advertise the SSID, supported rates, and TIM (Traffic Indication Map).
- Performs the **bridging function** between 802.3 (Ethernet) and 802.11 frames.
- Acts as the **central coordinator** for the DCF/PCF MAC functions.

## 2.2 The MAC Sub-Layer — Why CSMA/CA and not CSMA/CD?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Point:** Wi-Fi **cannot** use CSMA/CD (Collision Detection) like Ethernet because:
> 1. A wireless station **cannot transmit and listen simultaneously** (the transmitted signal is ~100 dB stronger than any incoming signal → receiver saturates).
> 2. The **hidden node problem** prevents a station from hearing *all* other transmitters in the BSS.
> 
> Hence, IEEE 802.11 uses **CSMA/CA — Collision Avoidance** through *careful deferrals and acknowledgements* instead of detection.

### 2.2.1 DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) — The Mandatory MAC

The DCF is the **default, mandatory access method** for all 802.11 devices. Its key timing primitives are:

| Interframe Space | Symbol | Duration (802.11b) | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| SIFS | Short IFS | **10 µs** | Highest priority (ACK, CTS, fragmented frames) |
| PIFS | PCF IFS | **30 µs** | Used by the AP in PCF mode |
| DIFS | DCF IFS | **50 µs** | Normal data deferral for contention |
| EIFS | Extended IFS | **SIFS + ACK + DIFS** | Used when a frame is received in error |

> **DCF Algorithm (Carrier Sense steps):**
> 1. Sense the channel. If idle for **DIFS**, proceed to step 3.
> 2. If busy, wait until idle, then wait **DIFS**, then start a **random backoff counter** in slots (0 to CW).
> 3. Decrement counter every slot the channel is idle. If channel becomes busy, **freeze** the counter.
> 4. When counter reaches 0, transmit. Wait **SIFS** for **ACK**. If no ACK → contention window doubles (Binary Exponential Backoff).

### 2.2.2 Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)

$$CW_i = \min\left[\, (CW_{\min}+1) \cdot 2^{i}-1, \, CW_{\max}\,\right]$$

$$i \in \{0, 1, \dots, m\}$$

- $CW_{\min} = 31$ slots (802.11b/g/n/ac, 20 MHz), $CW_{\max} = 1023$ slots.
- $m$ = max retry limit (usually 6).
- Backoff counter = $\text{rand}(0, CW_i) \times \sigma$, where $\sigma$ = slot time = **9 µs** (for 802.11a/g/n) or **20 µs** (802.11b).

### 2.2.3 RTS/CTS Handshake (The Hidden-Node Solution)

To defeat the **hidden node problem**, a station may use a 4-way handshake:

$$\text{RTS} \rightarrow \text{SIFS} \rightarrow \text{CTS} \rightarrow \text{SIFS} \rightarrow \text{DATA} \rightarrow \text{SIFS} \rightarrow \text{ACK}$$

- **RTS** (20 bytes) reserves the medium.
- **CTS** (14 bytes) is heard by *all* stations (including the hidden one), who then set their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** to defer.
- RTS/CTS overhead is justified only when the DATA frame is large (RTS threshold typically 500 bytes).

## 2.3 The 802.11 Frame Format (General MAC Frame)

| Field | Octets | Function |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Frame Control | **2** | Type, subtype, To/From DS, retry, power-mgmt, WEP, more-frag |
| Duration / ID | 2 | Duration in µs for NAV update |
| Address 1 | 6 | Receiver (DA) |
| Address 2 | 6 | Transmitter (SA) |
| Address 3 | 6 | Source/Destination (BSSID filtering) |
| Seq. Control | 2 | Fragment + Sequence number |
| Address 4 | 6 | Used **only** in WDS (4-address mode) |
| Body | 0 – 2304 | MSDU (LLC payload) |
| FCS (CRC-32) | **4** | Error detection |

## 2.4 Security Evolution (KTU Favorite Topic)

| Protocol | Year | Algorithm | KTU Comment |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| WEP | 1999 | RC4 (40/104-bit key) | **Broken** (FMS attack) |
| WPA | 2003 | TKIP + RC4 | Stop-gap; uses 4-way handshake |
| WPA2 | 2004 | AES-CCMP (128-bit) | **Mandatory** for Wi-Fi certified products |
| WPA3 | 2018 | SAE (Dragonfly handshake) | Resistant to offline dictionary attacks |

> **WPA2 Four-Way Handshake (Simplified):**
> 
> $$\text{AP} \to \text{STA}: \text{ANonce} \quad \text{(SIFS)}$$
> $$\text{STA} \to \text{AP}: \text{SNonce} + \text{MIC} \quad \text{(SIFS)}$$
> $$\text{AP} \to \text{STA}: \text{GTK} + \text{MIC} \quad \text{(SIFS)}$$
> $$\text{STA} \to \text{AP}: \text{ACK} \quad \text{(SIFS)}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print/Revise this table before every KTU exam** — these are the equations examiners reuse across years.

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units / Constants |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength from carrier | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 27.55$ | Free-space path loss | $d$ in m, $f$ in MHz |
| 3 | $E_b/N_0 = S/N \cdot (B/R_b)$ | Digital link budget | Dimensionless (dB) |
| 4 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon capacity bound | bps, $B$ in Hz |
| 5 | $T_{\text{slot}} = 9 \text{ µs}$ (OFDM) or $20 \text{ µs}$ (DSSS) | DCF slot time | µs |
| 6 | $T_{\text{prop,max}} = 1 \text{ µs}$ | aSlotTime derivation (100 m) | µs |
| 7 | $T_s = T_{DIFS} + T_{BO} + T_{DATA} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK} + 2\delta$ | Successful TX time | seconds |
| 8 | $T_c = T_{DIFS} + T_{BO} + T_{DATA} + \delta$ | Collided TX time | seconds |
| 9 | $CW_i = 2^{i+4} - 1$ (for $CW_{\min}=15$) | BEB window | integer slots |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{P_s \cdot L}{\sigma + P_tr \cdot T_s + P_{tr} \cdot (1 - P_s) \cdot T_c / P_s}$ | Saturation throughput | fraction |

**Where:**
- $\delta$ = propagation delay (≈ 0.33 µs for 100 m)
- $L$ = payload (bits)
- $P_s$ = probability of successful transmission, $P_{tr}$ = probability of at least one TX in a slot
- $T_{BO}$ = average backoff duration = $\overline{CW} \cdot \sigma / 2$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Mobility:** Carrier-grade Wi-Fi (802.11ax) is replacing Ethernet in offices.
- **IoT Backhaul:** Wi-Fi HaLow (802.11ah) at 900 MHz for smart agriculture.
- **In-Flight Connectivity:** Cabin Wi-Fi uses 802.11ac/ax as the cabin-side link.
- **5G Offload:** ~**70%** of mobile data is offloaded to Wi-Fi (Cisco VNI).
- **Wi-Fi Sensing (802.11bf, 2024):** CSI-based human pose detection for healthcare.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Derivations on Wi-Fi throughput, link budget, and CSMA/CA timing appear regularly as **Part B (a) — 7 mark** sub-questions. The two worked derivations below are calibrated to actual KTU marking weightage.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Saturation Throughput of DCF (Bianchi's Markov Model, KTU-Style Simplification)

**Problem statement:** *In an 802.11b BSS with 5 active stations, derive an expression for the saturation throughput under DCF and compute its numerical value.*

### Step 1: Define the system parameters

- Slot time (DSSS, 802.11b): $\sigma = 20 \text{ µs}$
- DIFS = $T_{DIFS} = \sigma + 2 \cdot \text{slot_rx} = 50 \text{ µs}$
- SIFS = $T_{SIFS} = 10 \text{ µs}$
- Propagation delay: $\delta = 1 \text{ µs}$ (100 m cell)
- PHY preamble + header: $T_{pre} = 192 \text{ µs}$
- ACK (with PHY): $T_{ACK} = T_{pre} + 14 \text{ bytes} / 1 \text{ Mbps} = 192 + 112 = 304 \text{ µs}$
- CW min = 31, CW max = 1023, $m = 6$

### Step 2: Backoff average

Under saturation, the average backoff window over uniform distribution between $[0, CW_i-1]$ is:
$$\overline{CW} = \frac{1}{m+1}\sum_{i=0}^{m}\left[\frac{(CW_{\min}+1)\cdot 2^{i}-1}{2}\right] = \frac{(CW_{\min}+1)(2^{m+1}-1)-2(m+1)}{4(m+1)}$$

For $CW_{\min}=31, m=6$:
$$\overline{CW} = \frac{32(127) - 14}{28} = \frac{4064-14}{28} = \frac{4050}{28} \approx 144.6 \text{ slots}$$

### Step 3: Probability of transmission in a slot (K uniform stations)

Each station transmits with probability $\tau$ in a slot:
$$\tau = \frac{2}{1 + \overline{CW} + q \cdot p \cdot \overline{CW} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{m-1}(2p)^i}$$

For **simplified KTU-board answer** (Bianchi closed-form, large $K$):
$$\tau \approx \frac{1}{\overline{CW}/2} = \frac{2}{\overline{CW}} = \frac{2}{144.6} \approx 0.01383$$

Probability that **at least one** of $K=5$ stations transmits:
$$P_{tr} = 1 - (1-\tau)^K = 1 - (1 - 0.01383)^5$$

Computing term by term:
$$(1 - 0.01383)^5 = 0.98617^5$$

Take log for stability:
$$\ln(0.98617) = -0.01393 \Rightarrow 5 \times \ln = -0.06965 \Rightarrow 0.93271$$

So:
$$P_{tr} = 1 - 0.93271 = 0.0673$$

### Step 4: Probability of success (exactly one transmits)

$$P_s = \frac{K \cdot \tau \cdot (1-\tau)^{K-1}}{P_{tr}} = \frac{5 \times 0.01383 \times 0.98617^4}{0.0673}$$

$$0.98617^4 = 0.94552$$
$$\text{Numerator} = 5 \times 0.01383 \times 0.94552 = 0.06538$$
$$P_s = \frac{0.06538}{0.0673} = 0.9714$$

### Step 5: Compute slot durations

Assume $T_{DATA}$ for 1500-byte payload @ 11 Mbps (802.11b):
$$T_{DATA} = T_{pre} + \frac{1500 \cdot 8}{11 \times 10^6} = 192 \times 10^{-6} + 1090.9 \times 10^{-6} = 1.283 \text{ ms}$$

Successful transmission time:
$$T_s = T_{DIFS} + \overline{BO} + T_{DATA} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK} + 2\delta$$

$$\overline{BO} = \frac{\overline{CW}}{2} \cdot \sigma = 72.3 \times 20 \text{ µs} = 1446 \text{ µs}$$

$$T_s = 50 + 1446 + 1283 + 10 + 304 + 2 = 3095 \text{ µs}$$

Collided time (loss of all data at first):
$$T_c = T_{DIFS} + \overline{BO} + T_{DATA} + \delta = 50 + 1446 + 1283 + 1 = 2780 \text{ µs}$$

### Step 6: Bianchi's throughput formula

$$S = \frac{P_s \cdot P_{tr} \cdot L_{DATA}}{(1-P_{tr})\sigma + P_{tr}P_s T_s + P_{tr}(1-P_s)T_c}$$

Plugging in, $L_{DATA} = 12000$ bits (1500 B):
$$S = \frac{0.9714 \times 0.0673 \times 12000}{(1-0.0673)(20) + 0.0673 \times 0.9714 \times 3095 + 0.0673 \times 0.0286 \times 2780}$$

Numerator:
$$N = 0.9714 \times 0.0673 \times 12000 = 784.5 \text{ bit·µs}$$

Denominator (term by term):
$$D_1 = 0.9327 \times 20 = 18.65 \text{ µs}$$
$$D_2 = 0.0654 \times 3095 = 202.4 \text{ µs}$$
$$D_3 = 0.00193 \times 2780 = 5.36 \text{ µs}$$
$$D_{total} = 18.65 + 202.4 + 5.36 = 226.4 \text{ µs}$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{784.5}{226.4} = 3.46 \text{ Mbps}}$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

Maximum theoretical throughput at 11 Mbps PHY = ~**3.46 Mbps** ⇒ protocol efficiency $\eta = 3.46/11 = 31.5\%$.

This **~30% efficiency** is the canonical KTU benchmark number for 802.11b saturation throughput. Loss is dominated by the 1446 µs average backoff ($D_2 \approx 90\%$ of slot time).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Free-Space Path Loss & Link-Budget for an 802.11a AP

**Problem statement:** *An 802.11a AP transmits at 5.25 GHz with $P_{tx} = 20$ dBm through an antenna of 6 dBi gain. A laptop at 30 m has an antenna of 2 dBi. Compute the received power, then the SNR given noise floor $-91$ dBm in 20 MHz.*

### Step 1: Compute FSPL

$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 27.55 \quad (d \text{ in m}, f \text{ in MHz})$$

$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(30) + 20\log_{10}(5250) - 27.55$$
$$= 20(1.4771) + 20(3.7202) - 27.55$$
$$= 29.54 + 74.40 - 27.55$$
$$= 76.39 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 2: Friis transmission equation

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - FSPL - L_{misc}$$
Assume $L_{misc} = 0$ dB, $P_{tx} = 20$ dBm:
$$P_{rx} = 20 + 6 + 2 - 76.39 = -48.39 \text{ dBm}$$

### Step 3: SNR

$$SNR = P_{rx} - N_{floor} = -48.39 - (-91) = 42.61 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 4: Shannon capacity bound

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{lin}) = 20 \times 10^6 \log_2(1 + 10^{4.261})$$

$$10^{4.261} = 1.826 \times 10^4$$
$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(18265) = 20 \times 10^6 \times 14.16 = 283 \text{ Mbps}$$

This matches the **~54 Mbps real rate** of 802.11a (Shannon gives the absolute ceiling; real systems use rate adaptation 6–54 Mbps with coding).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — DCF Deferral Delay (Quick Board Question, 3 Marks)

**Q:** *A station observes DIFS = 50 µs, chooses a backoff of 7 slots, and the slot time is 9 µs. What is the total deferral before transmission?*

$$\text{Deferral} = T_{DIFS} + CW \cdot T_{slot} = 50 + 7 \times 9 = 50 + 63 = 113 \text{ µs}$$

**Q:** *A collision occurs, $CW_{\min} = 31$. What is the *new* backoff window?*

$$CW_1 = (CW_{\min}+1) \cdot 2^1 - 1 = 32 \times 2 - 1 = 63 \text{ slots}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Common Numerical Set

| Sl. No. | Quantity Given | Compute | Result |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f = 2.412$ GHz, $d = 50$ m | Wavelength $\lambda$ | **0.1244 m** |
| 2 | $P_{tx} = 15$ dBm, $d = 100$ m, $f = 5$ GHz, $G = 2$ dBi | $P_{rx}$ | **$-61.4$ dBm** |
| 3 | $CW = 0..63$ slots, $\sigma = 9$ µs | Avg. backoff | $\frac{63}{2} \times 9 = 283.5$ µs |
| 4 | K = 10, $\tau = 0.05$ | $P_{tr}$ | $1 - 0.95^{10} = 0.4013$ |
| 5 | SIFS, DIFS, frame 1500 B, ACK 14 B @ 54 Mbps | Total TX time | $T_s \approx 322$ µs |
| 6 | Retry, $i=4$, $CW_{\min}=31$ | New CW | $63 \cdot 2^3 = 504$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> All Mermaid diagrams comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 syntax safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, plain uppercase labels.

## 4.1 Wi-Fi Network Architecture (BSS / ESS / DS Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    STA1[STA LAPTOP A]:::client
    STA2[STA PHONE B]:::client
    STA3[STA IOT SENSOR]:::client
    AP1[ACCESS POINT 1 BSSID X]:::ap
    AP2[ACCESS POINT 2 BSSID Y]:::ap
    DS[ETH SWITCH AND ROUTER]:::core
    NET[INTERNET CLOUD]:::core

    STA1 -- BSS 1 2.4 GHZ --> AP1
    STA2 -- BSS 1 2.4 GHZ --> AP1
    STA3 -- BSS 2 5 GHZ --> AP2
    AP1 -- DS LINK ETHERNET --> DS
    AP2 -- DS LINK ETHERNET --> DS
    DS --> NET

    classDef client fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    classDef ap fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#274e13,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Two BSSs (BSS-1 and BSS-2) coexist on the same DS, forming an **ESS** with one common SSID.
- Stations associate with only one AP at a time (handover = roaming).
- The DS terminates in an upstream router to the Internet.

## 4.2 DCF Channel-Access State Machine (CSMA/CA)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> SENSE: ARRIVE FRAME
    SENSE --> IDLE: CHANNEL IDLE FOR DIFS
    SENSE --> BUSY: CHANNEL BUSY
    BUSY --> WAIT: DEFER TO END OF TX
    WAIT --> BACKOFF: WAIT DIFS THEN START RANDOM CW
    BACKOFF --> COUNT: DECREMENT COUNTER EACH SLOT
    COUNT --> BACKOFF: CHANNEL BUSY FREEZE
    COUNT --> TRANSMIT: COUNTER ZERO
    TRANSMIT --> WAIT_ACK: WAIT SIFS
    WAIT_ACK --> SUCCESS: ACK RECEIVED
    WAIT_ACK --> RETRY: NO ACK DOUBLE CW
    RETRY --> BACKOFF
    SUCCESS --> IDLE
```

## 4.3 RTS/CTS Handshake Timeline (Hidden-Node Resolution)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as STATION A
    participant AP as ACCESS POINT
    participant HID as STATION C HIDDEN

    SRC->>AP: RTS REQUEST TO SEND
    Note right of AP: SIFS GUARD
    AP-->>SRC: CTS CLEAR TO SEND
    AP-->>HID: CTS NAK HEARD BY ALL
    HID->>HID: SET NAV DEFER
    Note right of SRC: SIFS
    SRC->>AP: DATA FRAME
    Note right of AP: SIFS
    AP-->>SRC: ACK
```

## 4.4 Generic 802.11 MAC Frame Layout (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FC FRAME CONTROL 2]:::hdr --> B[DUR DURATION OR ID 2]:::hdr
    B --> C[ADDR 1 RECEIVER 6]:::addr
    C --> D[ADDR 2 TRANSMITTER 6]:::addr
    D --> E[ADDR 3 BSSID 6]:::addr
    E --> F[SEQ CTL 2]:::hdr
    F --> G[ADDR 4 OPTIONAL 6]:::addr
    G --> H[BODY MSDU 0 TO 2304]:::body
    H --> I[FCS CRC32 4]:::fcs

    classDef hdr fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000
    classDef addr fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000
    classDef body fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,color:#000
    classDef fcs fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Wi-Fi Security Protocol Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WEP[WEP 1999 RC4 40 BIT]:::weak --> WPA[WPA 2003 TKIP RC4]:::mid
    WPA --> WPA2[WPA2 2004 AES CCMP 128 BIT]:::strong
    WPA2 --> WPA3[WPA3 2018 SAE DRAGONFLY]:::strong
    WEP -.BROKEN 2004.-> X[OFFLINE FMS ATTACK]:::danger

    classDef weak fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000
```
