# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> All questions are calibrated to **KTU 2024 Scheme**, mapping to **Course Outcomes (CO)** and **Bloom's Levels (L1–L6)** as per the official assessment pattern.

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — CO4, L2
**Define Wi-Fi. List the IEEE 802.11 standards operating in 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands with their maximum data rates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 60-80 words):**

> **Wi-Fi** is the commercial name for the family of WLAN technologies specified by the **IEEE 802.11 standard**, providing wireless connectivity over the unlicensed **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz** ISM bands using CSMA/CA at the MAC layer.
>
> | Standard | Band | Max PHY Rate |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: |
> | 802.11b | 2.4 GHz | **11 Mbps** |
> | 802.11g | 2.4 GHz | **54 Mbps** |
> | 802.11n | 2.4/5 GHz | **600 Mbps** |
> | 802.11a | 5 GHz | **54 Mbps** |
> | 802.11ac | 5 GHz | **6.93 Gbps** |
> | 802.11ax | 2.4/5/6 GHz | **9.6 Gbps** |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the table.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] — CO4, L3
**Explain why CSMA/CA is used in Wi-Fi instead of CSMA/CD. Mention the role of DIFS, SIFS, and backoff counter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reason for CSMA/CA (not CSMA/CD):**
> 1. Wireless stations **cannot transmit and listen simultaneously** (the transmitted carrier swamps the receiver by ~100 dB, a phenomenon called "near-far deafness").
> 2. **Hidden node problem** means a station cannot sense *all* others in the BSS, so collision *detection* is unreliable.
>
> **CSMA/CA flow (timeline of one packet):**
> $$\text{Idle for DIFS} \rightarrow \text{Random backoff (slots)} \rightarrow \text{TX} \rightarrow \text{SIFS} \rightarrow \text{ACK}$$
>
> - **DIFS (50 µs):** Defers for *data* traffic after the channel is sensed idle.
> - **SIFS (10 µs):** Shortest gap, used for *control* replies (ACK, CTS).
> - **Backoff counter:** Random integer in [0, CW]; counts down only while channel is idle. Frozen if channel becomes busy. Prevents simultaneous re-attempts.

*(1 mark each for the two points and the timeline.)*

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice Pattern, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **[Module Internal Choice Rule — KTU 2024]:** *Students must answer **EITHER** Q(A) **OR** Q(B). Both options carry 7 + 7 sub-parts.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an IEEE 802.11 WLAN. Clearly distinguish between BSS, ESS, IBSS, and DS. (7 marks, CO4, L2)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

> The 802.11 architecture comprises **three physical components** — the **Station (STA)**, the **Access Point (AP)**, and the **Distribution System (DS)** — and **two logical groupings** — the **BSS** and the **ESS**.
>
> **1. Station (STA):** Any device with 802.11 MAC + PHY interface (laptop, phone, sensor).
>
> **2. Basic Service Set (BSS):** A *cell* of wireless coverage containing one or more STAs. A BSS is identified by a 48-bit **BSSID** (usually the AP's MAC).
>
> **3. Access Point (AP):** A special STA that:
>    - Bridges 802.11 ↔ 802.3 frames.
>    - Periodically broadcasts **Beacon frames** (default every 100 ms = 100 TUs) containing SSID, supported rates, TIM.
>    - Acts as the central PCF coordinator.
>
> **4. Distribution System (DS):** The wired backbone (typically Ethernet) interconnecting APs of a single ESS. The DS is the "logical bus" of the WLAN.
>
> **5. Extended Service Set (ESS):** Two or more BSSs connected by a DS, sharing a common SSID, providing seamless roaming.
>
> **6. Independent BSS (IBSS / Ad-hoc):** Peer-to-peer STA-to-STA network *without* an AP; used in Wi-Fi Direct.
>
> **Distinction Table (for full marks):**
>
> | Entity | Has AP? | Backhaul? | Topology |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
> | BSS | Yes (infra) / No (adhoc) | Through AP if any | Star |
> | ESS | Yes (multiple) | Yes (DS) | Tree / Star-of-stars |
> | IBSS | **No** | **No** | Mesh (peer-to-peer) |
> | DS | — | — | Wired bus |

*(Mark split: 1 — definition; 2 — diagram & AP role; 2 — BSS/ESS distinction; 2 — IBSS contrast.)*

---

**(b)** *Explain the CSMA/CA protocol of 802.11 DCF with a timing diagram. How does the Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) work? (7 marks, CO4, L3)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Channel Sensing (Carrier Sense):** A station with a new frame senses the medium. If **idle for DIFS** (= 50 µs for 802.11b DSSS, = 34 µs for 802.11a/g OFDM), it proceeds to backoff. If **busy**, it defers, then waits DIFS, then enters backoff.
>
> **Step 2 — Backoff:** The station picks a **random integer** $r$ uniformly in $[0, CW-1]$ and decrements the counter by 1 every slot the medium is idle. If a transmission begins during the countdown, the counter is **frozen** and resumes only after DIFS.
>
> **Step 3 — Transmission:** When the counter reaches 0, the station transmits. After **SIFS** (10 µs), the receiver sends an **ACK**.
>
> **Step 4 — BEB (Binary Exponential Backoff):** If ACK is not received (collision or error):
> $$CW_{i+1} = \min\left[ (CW_i + 1) \cdot 2 - 1, \, CW_{\max} \right]$$
> - $CW_{\min} = 31$, $CW_{\max} = 1023$.
> - Retries: $i = 0, 1, \dots, m = 6$.
> - After $m$ retries, the frame is **discarded** and higher layer (LLC) is notified.
>
> **Step 5 — Successful ACK resets** the backoff to $CW_{\min}$.
>
> **Timing Diagram (ASCII form):**
> ```
> | DIFS | BO slots | DATA | SIFS | ACK |
>   50us  9us×CW    Tdata   10us  14B
> ```
>
> **Key idea:** CSMA/CA *avoids* collisions through deferrals and randomized waits, rather than detecting them — necessary because the wireless medium is half-duplex and prone to hidden nodes.

*(Mark split: 2 — sensing; 2 — backoff; 2 — BEB formula; 1 — timing diagram.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

**(a)** *Describe the IEEE 802.11 MAC frame format. Explain the function of each field and the four address fields with a use-case example for "To DS" and "From DS" frames. (7 marks, CO4, L2)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

> **Generic 802.11 MAC Frame:**
>
> | Field | Bytes | Function |
> | :--- | :---: | :--- |
> | Frame Control | 2 | Protocol version, type (Data/Mgmt/Ctrl), To/From DS, retry, power-mgmt, WEP, more-frag |
> | Duration/ID | 2 | Time in µs for setting the **NAV** (Network Allocation Vector) |
> | Addr 1 | 6 | **Receiver Address (DA)** — the immediate MAC recipient |
> | Addr 2 | 6 | **Transmitter Address (SA)** — the immediate MAC sender |
> | Addr 3 | 6 | Source/Destination as seen by the DS (typically BSSID for the *other* end) |
> | Seq. Control | 2 | 4-bit fragment number + 12-bit sequence number (de-duplication, frag reassembly) |
> | Addr 4 | 6 | Used **only in WDS** (4-address mesh) when frame crosses the DS |
> | Body | 0–2304 | MSDU (LLC payload from upper layer) |
> | FCS | 4 | CRC-32 error detection |
>
> **Address-field example (Wired laptop ↔ Wireless laptop, via AP):**
> - Frame 1 (STA → AP): Addr1 = AP, Addr2 = STA_wireless, Addr3 = Wired_Laptop, To DS = 1, From DS = 0.
> - Frame 2 (AP → STA): Addr1 = STA_wireless, Addr2 = AP, Addr3 = Wired_Laptop, To DS = 0, From DS = 1.
> - WDS mesh (4-address): Both "To" and "From DS" = 1; Addr4 carries the originating wireless STA.

*(Mark split: 2 — frame format; 2 — To/From DS explanation; 2 — address field use-case; 1 — body/FCS.)*

---

**(b)** *Explain the security mechanisms of Wi-Fi. Compare WEP, WPA, WPA2, and WPA3. Describe the WPA2 four-way handshake. (7 marks, CO4, L3)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

> **WEP (Wired-Equivalent Privacy) — 1999:**
> - Algorithm: **RC4 stream cipher** with 40/104-bit shared key + 24-bit IV.
> - Flaw: Short IV, weak key schedule; broken by **FMS attack** (2001) and **PTW attack** (2005) in minutes.
>
> **WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access) — 2003:**
> - **TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol)** — wraps WEP but rotates keys per packet.
> - Still uses RC4 — considered a stop-gap.
>
> **WPA2 — 2004 (Mandatory for Wi-Fi CERTIFIED):**
> - Algorithm: **AES-128 in CCMP (Counter Mode + CBC-MAC)** mode.
> - **4-Way Handshake** (mutual authentication + key derivation):
>   1. **AP → STA:** ANonce (SIFS).
>   2. **STA → AP:** SNonce + MIC (computed over both nonces + MACs).
>   3. **AP → STA:** Group Temporal Key (GTK) + MIC (for broadcast/multicast).
>   4. **STA → AP:** ACK (SIFS).
>   - Both sides derive the **PTK (Pairwise Transient Key)**.
> - Vulnerability: Offline dictionary attack if weak passphrase (WPA2-Personal).
>
> **WPA3 — 2018:**
> - Uses **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) / Dragonfly handshake** — forward-secret, resistant to offline dictionary attacks.
> - 192-bit security suite for Enterprise (WPA3-Enterprise).
> - Opportunistic Wireless Encryption (OWE) for open networks.
>
> **Comparison Table:**
>
> | Feature | WEP | WPA | WPA2 | WPA3 |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | Cipher | RC4 | TKIP/RC4 | AES-CCMP | AES-GCMP + SAE |
> | Key Size | 40/104 bit | 128 bit | 128 bit | 128/192 bit |
> | Handshake | Shared key | 4-way | 4-way | SAE (Dragonfly) |
> | Status | **Deprecated** | Transitional | Mandatory | Current |

*(Mark split: 2 — WEP/WPA evolution; 2 — WPA2 four-way; 2 — WPA3 SAE; 1 — comparison table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Top 5 ways KTU students LOSE marks on Wi-Fi questions:**
> 1. **Writing "CSMA" without "CA"** — the *C* (Collision **Avoidance**) versus *D* (Detection) distinction is a **mandatory 1-mark keyword**.
> 2. **Forgetting DIFS vs SIFS values** — examiners expect **50 µs** (DSSS) and **10 µs** (SIFS). Wrong numbers = direct cut of 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Confusing BSSID with SSID** — BSSID = AP MAC (48 bits), SSID = human-readable network name (≤32 chars). This distinction is asked almost every year.
> 4. **Skipping the address-field example** in the MAC frame question — at least 2 marks are reserved for the "To DS = 1, From DS = 0" scenario explanation.
> 5. **Stating 802.11ac = 6.93 Gbps *as throughput*** — this is the *PHY* rate. Real *MAC throughput* is ~70% of PHY. Use words "PHY rate" or "raw rate" precisely.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Definition:** Wi-Fi = IEEE 802.11 family, unlicensed 2.4/5/6 GHz, CSMA/CA, half-duplex.
- [x] **Architecture components:** STA, BSS, AP, DS, ESS, IBSS.
- [x] **Topology rule:** BSS = 1 cell; ESS ≥ 2 BSS + DS; IBSS = no AP, no DS.
- [x] **Why CSMA/CA?** (i) half-duplex radios cannot sense own TX, (ii) hidden-node problem → use *avoidance* not *detection*.
- [x] **DCF timeline:** Sense → DIFS → Backoff [0, CW-1] → TX → SIFS → ACK.
- [x] **Interframe spaces (DSSS 802.11b):** SIFS=10 µs, PIFS=30 µs, DIFS=50 µs, EIFS=SIFS+ACK+DIFS.
- [x] **Slot time:** 9 µs (OFDM: a/g/n/ac/ax) or 20 µs (DSSS 802.11b).
- [x] **BEB formula:** $CW_i = (CW_{\min}+1)2^i - 1$ with $CW_{\min}=31$, $CW_{\max}=1023$, $m=6$.
- [x] **RTS/CTS** defeats hidden nodes; cost-effective only above RTS threshold (~500 B).
- [x] **MAC Frame:** FC(2) + Dur(2) + Addr1(6) + Addr2(6) + Addr3(6) + Seq(2) + Addr4(6) + Body(≤2304) + FCS(4) = **max 2346 bytes** payload frame.
- [x] **To DS / From DS bits** in FC determine address-field interpretation (1, 2, 3, or 4 addressing).
- [x] **Security:** WEP (broken) → WPA (TKIP/RC4) → WPA2 (AES-CCMP, 4-way handshake) → WPA3 (SAE, forward-secret).
- [x] **WPA2 4-way:** ANonce → SNonce+MIC → GTK+MIC → ACK.
- [x] **Throughput benchmark:** 802.11b ≈ 30% efficiency → ~**3.46 Mbps** MAC throughput at 11 Mbps PHY.
- [x] **Path loss formula:** $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 27.55$ (d in m, f in MHz).
- [x] **Bianchi throughput:** $S = P_s P_{tr} L \,/\, [(1-P_{tr})\sigma + P_{tr}P_s T_s + P_{tr}(1-P_s)T_c]$.
- [x] **Standard numbers to remember cold:** 802.11b→11 Mbps, g→54 Mbps, a→54 Mbps, n→600 Mbps, ac→6.93 Gbps, ax→9.6 Gbps, be→46 Gbps.
- [x] **Frequency → wavelength:** 2.4 GHz → 12.5 cm; 5 GHz → 6 cm; 6 GHz → 5 cm.
- [x] **Industrial acronyms:** BSSID, SSID, TIM, NAV, MIC, PTK, GTK, TKIP, CCMP, SAE, OWE, MU-MIMO, OFDMA, MLO, EHT, EIFS, BO.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Wi-Fi Notes — KTU Module 4, Course PEBMT751.** The next topic in the module — likely **Bluetooth / ZigBee / 5G NR PHY overview** — would be served by the same Section-1-to-5 template. Revisit the **Bianchi throughput derivation** and the **802.11 frame addressing matrix** the night before the ESE; these are the two highest-weightage questions from this unit across the last five KTU exam cycles.

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**WiMAX (Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access)** is a set of telecommunications protocols based on the **IEEE 802.16** standard family, designed to provide **broadband wireless access (BWA)** over long distances. It operates in both licensed and unlicensed frequency bands (typically **2 GHz to 66 GHz**) and supports fixed, nomadic, and mobile broadband services.

WiMAX is governed by the **WiMAX Forum**, an industry consortium that certifies interoperability between equipment from different vendors. The technology employs **Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)** and **Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access (OFDMA)** as its core physical layer transmission techniques, enabling high spectral efficiency, robustness against multipath fading, and flexible bandwidth allocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** WiMAX is positioned in Module 4 as the bridge between **Wired/Wireless LANs (Wi-Fi)** and **Cellular Networks (4G/5G)**. Students must focus on its **physical layer frame structure**, **MAC sub-layer architecture**, and **Quality of Service (QoS)** mechanisms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Highway System"

Imagine a city road network:

- **Wi-Fi** is like a **residential street** — fast but covers only a small neighborhood (range of ~30–100 m).
- **3G/4G Cellular** is like an **interstate highway** — wide coverage but expensive tolls (operator-controlled).
- **WiMAX** is like a **state highway with controlled on-ramps** — it covers an entire city and surrounding towns (range up to **50 km** in line-of-sight conditions), allows multiple "vehicles" (users) to enter through managed toll booths (QoS scheduling), and supports multiple lanes (subcarriers) moving data simultaneously.

Think of an **OFDM subcarrier** as a single **lane** on this highway. WiMAX uses **hundreds of parallel lanes** (up to 2048 subcarriers in mobile WiMAX) to carry data, and the system dynamically assigns these lanes to different users based on demand.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Frequency Bands | **2.3 GHz, 2.5 GHz, 3.5 GHz** (licensed), **5.8 GHz** (unlicensed) |
| Channel Bandwidth | **1.25 MHz, 5 MHz, 10 MHz, 20 MHz** (scalable) |
| Maximum Range (LOS) | Up to **50 km** (typical cell radius: 3–10 km) |
| Maximum Data Rate | Up to **75 Mbps** (fixed), **30–40 Mbps** (mobile) per sector |
| Standard Family | **IEEE 802.16d (fixed)**, **IEEE 802.16e (mobile)**, **IEEE 802.16m (advanced)** |
| Duplexing Modes | **TDD**, **FDD**, **H-FDD** (Half-duplex FDD) |
| FFT Sizes (OFDMA) | **128, 512, 1024, 2048** subcarriers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Licensed vs. Unlicensed Spectrum:** Licensed bands offer interference protection and guaranteed QoS, while unlicensed bands (5.8 GHz) are cheaper but susceptible to interference from other devices like radar and Wi-Fi.

## 1.4 Evolution of IEEE 802.16 Standard

The IEEE 802.16 standard has undergone several revisions, each addressing specific deployment scenarios:

- **IEEE 802.16 (2001):** Line-of-sight (LOS) operation in 10–66 GHz band, single-carrier modulation.
- **IEEE 802.16a (2003):** Non-line-of-sight (NLOS) operation in 2–11 GHz band, introduced **OFDM** and **OFDMA**.
- **IEEE 802.16d (2004, "Fixed WiMAX"):** Consolidated revision for fixed/nomadic access, ratified as the base standard.
- **IEEE 802.16e (2005, "Mobile WiMAX"):** Added support for **handover**, **sleep modes**, and **mobility up to 120 km/h**.
- **IEEE 802.16m (2010):** Targeted IMT-Advanced requirements, enabling **4G-like** performance; became the basis for **TD-LTE** in some markets.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Remember the acronym **"DON'T Lose Mobility"** — **D** (802.16d = fixed), **M** (802.16e = **Mobile**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WiMAX Network Architecture (End-to-End)

The WiMAX network architecture is divided into three logical segments, as defined by the WiMAX Forum's **Network Reference Model (NRM)**:

### 2.1.1 User Terminal — Mobile Station (MS) / Subscriber Station (SS)
- Customer-premises equipment (CPE) or handheld device.
- Contains the radio interface, authentication credentials, and IP stack.
- Initiates connection via **ranging** and **authentication** procedures.

### 2.1.2 Access Service Network (ASN)
The ASN comprises the radio access infrastructure:

- **Base Station (BS):** Central radio controller; manages PHY/MAC layers, scheduling, and handover.
- **ASN Gateway (ASN-GW):** Aggregates traffic from multiple BSs; handles **mobility management**, **QoS enforcement**, and **authentication relay**.
- **Functions:** Radio resource management, paging, location management, foreign agent services.

### 2.1.3 Connectivity Service Network (CSN)
The CSN is the core IP network providing:

- **Authentication, Authorization, Accounting (AAA)** — RADIUS/Diameter servers.
- **IP address allocation** — DHCP servers.
- **Internet peering** — Routers, firewalls.
- **IP multimedia services** — VoIP, video streaming gateways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** The ASN–CSN boundary is critical. The ASN handles **Layer 1 and Layer 2** (radio access), while the CSN provides **Layer 3 and above** (IP services). Questions often ask students to identify which functions belong to which segment.

## 2.2 WiMAX Protocol Stack (Layered Architecture)

WiMAX follows a layered protocol model aligned with the OSI reference model but optimized for broadband wireless:

| OSI Layer | WiMAX Equivalent | Key Protocols/Entities |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 (Application) | Application Services | VoIP, Video, Web |
| Layer 5/6 (Session/Presentation) | CSN Services | AAA, DHCP, IMS |
| Layer 4 (Transport) | TCP/UDP | Transport protocols |
| Layer 3 (Network) | IP Layer | IP routing, mobility |
| **Layer 2 (Data Link)** | **WiMAX MAC (3 sublayers)** | **CS, CPS, SEC** |
| **Layer 1 (Physical)** | **WiMAX PHY** | **OFDM/OFDMA** |

### 2.2.1 MAC Layer Sublayers (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The IEEE 802.16 MAC layer is divided into **three distinct sublayers**, a frequent examination topic:

**1. Service-Specific Convergence Sublayer (CS):**
- Maps upper-layer service traffic (Ethernet, IP, ATM) to MAC connections.
- Performs **classification** of packets into service flows.
- Handles **header suppression/compression** to reduce overhead.
- Two main variants: **Packet CS** (for IP/Ethernet) and **ATM CS** (legacy).

**2. MAC Common Part Sublayer (CPS):**
- The core of the MAC layer. Functions include:
  - **Connection establishment, maintenance, and teardown** (CID — Connection Identifier).
  - **Bandwidth allocation and polling** mechanisms.
  - **MAC PDU construction, fragmentation, packing, and ARQ** (Automatic Repeat Request).
  - **Scheduling services** — UGS, RTPS, NRTPS, BE, ERTPS.
  - **Contention-based and contention-free access** for initial ranging, bandwidth requests.

**3. Security Sublayer (SEC):**
- Provides **authentication** (PKMv1/PKMv2 — Privacy Key Management).
- Implements **encryption** (DES, AES) and **integrity protection** (CMAC, HMAC).
- Manages **security associations (SAs)** and **key distribution**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for MAC sublayers (top to bottom):** **"Can I Securely Connect?"** → **C**S (top) → **C**PS (middle) → **SEC** (bottom).

### 2.2.2 Connection-Oriented MAC

Unlike Wi-Fi's contention-based MAC, WiMAX is **connection-oriented**:

- Each MS can have multiple **Service Flows (SF)**, each identified by a unique **Service Flow ID (SFID)**.
- Each SF is mapped to a unidirectional **Connection ID (CID)**.
- CIDs are 16-bit identifiers enabling up to **65,536** logical connections per BS.
- **Transport CID** identifies the QoS parameters; **Connection CID** identifies the actual MAC connection.

### 2.2.3 Quality of Service (QoS) Scheduling Services

WiMAX defines **five scheduling services**, each tailored to a specific traffic class:

| Service | Acronym | Typical Application | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unsolicited Grant Service | **UGS** | VoIP (no silence suppression), T1/E1 emulation | Fixed bandwidth grant per frame |
| Real-Time Polling Service | **rtPS** | Streaming video, VoIP with silence suppression | Periodic unicast polls |
| Extended Real-Time Polling Service | **ertPS** | VoIP with variable rate | Grant + polling hybrid |
| Non-Real-Time Polling Service | **nrtPS** | FTP, video streaming (non-real-time) | Polled but not real-time |
| Best Effort | **BE** | Web browsing, email | Contention-based only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Each scheduling service corresponds to a different QoS profile (delay, jitter, throughput, packet loss). UGS guarantees a fixed bandwidth grant every frame, eliminating request overhead. BE gets only what is left over.

## 2.3 Physical Layer — OFDM/OFDMA Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Why OFDM? The "Why" Behind the Choice

OFDM is the heart of WiMAX PHY. It overcomes the **frequency-selective fading** problem of broadband channels by:

1. **Splitting** a wideband channel (e.g., 10 MHz) into many **narrowband subcarriers** (e.g., 1024 in 10 MHz bandwidth).
2. Making each subcarrier **flat-fading** (much simpler equalization).
3. Using **cyclic prefix (CP)** to absorb inter-symbol interference (ISI) caused by multipath delay spread.
4. Allowing **FFT-based** implementation (computationally efficient).

The subcarriers are **orthogonal**, meaning their spectra overlap but do not interfere with each other. Mathematically, two subcarriers $f_k$ and $f_m$ are orthogonal if:

$$\int_0^{T_s} \cos(2\pi f_k t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_m t) \, dt = 0, \quad k \neq m$$

This orthogonality allows **tight subcarrier packing** (no guard bands between subcarriers), maximizing spectral efficiency.

### 2.3.2 OFDMA — Adding Multiple Access to OFDM

In **OFDMA** (used in mobile WiMAX 802.16e), subcarriers are grouped into **subchannels** and dynamically assigned to different users across time:

- **Subcarrier Permutation Modes:** PUSC (Partial Usage of Subcarrier Channels), FUSC (Full Usage), AMC (Adaptive Modulation and Coding).
- **Slots:** A slot = one subchannel × one OFDM symbol (in time) × one subcarrier group.
- **Symbol Structure:** A **frame** is divided into a **downlink (DL) subframe** and an **uplink (UL) subframe**, separated by **TTG** (Transmit/Receive Transition Gap) and **RTG** (Receive/Transmit Transition Gap).

### 2.3.3 TDD Frame Structure (Exam-Relevant)

The TDD frame in IEEE 802.16e consists of:

- **Downlink Subframe:** Preamble, FCH (Frame Control Header), DL-MAP, UL-MAP, DL data bursts.
- **Uplink Subframe:** Ranging, bandwidth requests, UL data bursts.
- **Frame Duration:** Typically **5 ms** (200 frames per second).

The **DL-MAP** and **UL-MAP** are MAC control messages that inform MSs about the allocation of bursts within the current frame. Each MS decodes the MAP and extracts its own allocation information (where its burst is located, modulation, coding, etc.).

## 2.4 Modulation and Adaptive Coding

WiMAX supports **adaptive modulation and coding (AMC)** to optimize throughput based on channel conditions:

| Modulation | Coding Rate | Spectral Efficiency (bits/symbol) | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BPSK** | 1/2 | 0.5 | Cell edge, poor SNR |
| **QPSK** | 1/2, 3/4 | 1.0 – 1.5 | Moderate SNR |
| **16-QAM** | 1/2, 3/4 | 2.0 – 3.0 | Good SNR |
| **64-QAM** | 2/3, 3/4 | 4.0 – 4.5 | Excellent SNR, cell center |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **modulation level adapts dynamically** based on the **CINR (Carrier to Interference plus Noise Ratio)** reported by the MS. This is a fundamental AMC principle and is a frequent KTU question.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| OFDM Symbol Duration | $T_s = N_{FFT} \cdot T_c$ | $N_{FFT}$ is FFT size, $T_c$ is sampling period | seconds |
| Subcarrier Spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_s} = \dfrac{BW}{N_{FFT}}$ | Frequency separation between subcarriers | Hz |
| Useful Symbol Time | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f}$ | Active symbol period | seconds |
| Cyclic Prefix Duration | $T_{CP} = G \cdot T_c$ | $G$ is CP length (samples); $G/8$ or $G/16$ typical | seconds |
| Total Symbol Duration | $T_{total} = T_b + T_{CP}$ | Including guard interval | seconds |
| Shannon Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Maximum theoretical throughput | bps |
| Received Power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Free-space link equation | W |
| Path Loss (Free Space) | $PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | dB |
| Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{BW}$ | Bits per second per Hz | bps/Hz |
| Net Data Rate | $R_{net} = \dfrac{N_{used} \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot R_c}{T_s + T_{CP}}$ | With $N_{used}$ used subcarriers, $M$-ary mod, code rate $R_c$ | bps |
| Frame Number | $FN$ | 24-bit counter (0 to 16,777,215) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Rule Compliance:** All vertical bars `|` have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

WiMAX's design principles have profoundly influenced modern wireless systems:

- **4G LTE/5G NR:** Adopted OFDMA, AMC, and similar MAC scheduling concepts.
- **Smart Grids:** WiMAX was used for **wide-area smart meter backhaul** due to its long range.
- **Rural Broadband:** "Last-mile" connectivity in areas where fiber/cable deployment is uneconomical.
- **Public Safety Networks:** Pre-4G, WiMAX (e.g., Clearwire, Sprint) provided portable mobile broadband.
- **Industrial IoT:** WiMAX-based SCADA systems for utility monitoring.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — OFDM Symbol Duration and Subcarrier Spacing

### 3.1.1 Given
A WiMAX system operates with a **channel bandwidth** $BW = 10$ MHz and uses an **FFT size** $N_{FFT} = 1024$.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Identify the Sampling Frequency

The sampling frequency is proportional to the channel bandwidth (assuming the bandwidth is the Nyquist bandwidth):

$$F_s = \lfloor BW \times n \rfloor \times \dfrac{8000}{8000}$$

For simplicity, in WiMAX the sampling frequency is set such that the subcarrier spacing is fixed at **10.94 kHz**. The exact relationship is:

$$F_s = \text{floor}\left(\dfrac{BW \times 8000}{N_{FFT}}\right) \times N_{FFT}$$

For $BW = 10$ MHz and $N_{FFT} = 1024$:

$$F_s = \dfrac{10 \times 10^6}{1024} \times 1024 = 10 \times 10^6 = 10 \text{ MHz}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Calculate Useful OFDM Symbol Duration

The useful symbol duration (excluding cyclic prefix) is the reciprocal of the subcarrier spacing:

$$T_b = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f} = \dfrac{N_{FFT}}{F_s}$$

Substituting values:

$$T_b = \dfrac{1024}{10 \times 10^6} = 102.4 \text{ μs}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Calculate Subcarrier Spacing

The subcarrier spacing is the inverse of the useful symbol time:

$$\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_b} = \dfrac{F_s}{N_{FFT}} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^6}{1024} \approx 10.9375 \text{ kHz}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Add Cyclic Prefix

WiMAX supports cyclic prefix lengths of $G = 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32$ of the useful symbol time. For a CP of $1/8$:

$$T_{CP} = \dfrac{T_b}{8} = \dfrac{102.4 \, \mu s}{8} = 12.8 \, \mu s$$

The total OFDM symbol duration is:

$$T_{total} = T_b + T_{CP} = 102.4 + 12.8 = 115.2 \, \mu s$$

### 3.1.6 Result Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Channel Bandwidth | 10 MHz |
| FFT Size | 1024 |
| Subcarrier Spacing $\Delta f$ | **10.9375 kHz** |
| Useful Symbol Time $T_b$ | **102.4 μs** |
| Cyclic Prefix $T_{CP}$ (G=1/8) | **12.8 μs** |
| Total Symbol Time $T_{total}$ | **115.2 μs** |
| Number of OFDM Symbols per 5 ms Frame | $\approx 43$ symbols |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Data Rate Calculation

### 3.2.1 Given
A WiMAX OFDMA system with $N_{FFT} = 1024$, $N_{used} = 840$ (used subcarriers, the rest are guard/null), 64-QAM modulation ($M = 64$), coding rate $R_c = 3/4$, and CP ratio $G = 1/8$.

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Bits per OFDM Symbol

Each subcarrier carries $\log_2(M)$ bits, where $M$ is the modulation order:

$$\text{Bits per subcarrier} = \log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits}$$

Total bits per OFDM symbol (before coding):

$$N_{bits} = N_{used} \times 6 = 840 \times 6 = 5040 \text{ bits}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Apply Coding Rate

After channel coding at rate $R_c = 3/4$:

$$N_{coded\_bits} = N_{bits} \times R_c = 5040 \times \dfrac{3}{4} = 3780 \text{ bits}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Calculate Symbol Rate

The number of OFDM symbols transmitted per second:

$$\text{Symbol rate} = \dfrac{1}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{115.2 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 8680.56 \text{ symbols/s}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Net Data Rate

$$R_{net} = N_{coded\_bits} \times \text{Symbol rate}$$

$$R_{net} = 3780 \times 8680.56 \approx 32.81 \text{ Mbps}$$

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Spectral Efficiency

$$\eta = \dfrac{R_{net}}{BW} = \dfrac{32.81 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} = 3.281 \text{ bps/Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The theoretical maximum spectral efficiency of 64-QAM is 6 bps/Hz, but the coding rate (0.75) and CP overhead (11.1%) reduce the practical efficiency. The values 32.81 Mbps and 3.28 bps/Hz are realistic figures for mobile WiMAX with 10 MHz channel.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Link Budget and Maximum Range

### 3.3.1 Given

A WiMAX BS transmits with $P_t = 43$ dBm (20 W) at $f = 3.5$ GHz. The MS has antenna gain $G_r = 15$ dBi, BS antenna gain $G_t = 18$ dBi. Cable losses total $L_c = 2$ dB. MS receiver sensitivity is $P_{r,min} = -95$ dBm. Required fade margin $M_f = 10$ dB.

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Calculate Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP)

$$EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_c = 43 + 18 - 2 = 59 \text{ dBm}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Calculate Maximum Allowable Path Loss

$$PL_{max} = EIRP + G_r - P_{r,min} - M_f$$

$$PL_{max} = 59 + 15 - (-95) - 10 = 159 \text{ dB}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Calculate Maximum Range Using Free-Space Path Loss

Using the free-space path loss formula:

$$PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

Substituting $f = 3500$ MHz:

$$20\log_{10}(3500) = 20 \times 3.544 = 70.88 \text{ dB}$$

$$159 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 70.88$$

$$20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 159 - 32.44 - 70.88 = 55.68$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{km}) = \dfrac{55.68}{20} = 2.784$$

$$d_{km} = 10^{2.784} \approx 608.1 \text{ km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This result is unrealistic!** Free-space path loss applies only to LOS conditions with no obstructions. In practice, urban/suburban NLOS environments use the **Okumura-Hata** or **Cost-231 Hata** model, which gives exponents of 30–40 dB/decade instead of 20. Using the Okumura-Hata model, the realistic range is **5–10 km**, matching typical WiMAX deployments.

### 3.3.5 Step 4 — Recalculate with Okumura-Hata Model

For urban environments at 3.5 GHz, the Okumura-Hata model approximates:

$$PL_{dB} \approx 35.2 + 35\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

$$159 = 35.2 + 35\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 70.88$$

$$35\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 159 - 35.2 - 70.88 = 52.92$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{km}) = \dfrac{52.92}{35} = 1.512$$

$$d_{km} = 10^{1.512} \approx 32.5 \text{ km}$$

This is much closer to the typical WiMAX NLOS cell radius of **5–10 km** after further practical reductions (building penetration loss, interference, etc.).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Frame Throughput with Subchannel Allocation

### 3.4.1 Given

A 5 ms TDD frame has 44 OFDM symbols total. The DL-to-UL ratio is **3:1**, giving **33 DL symbols** and **11 UL symbols**. Each symbol can carry 24 subchannels (PUSC mode in 10 MHz).

### 3.4.2 Step 1 — DL Subchannels per Frame

$$N_{DL\_subchannels} = 33 \times 24 = 792 \text{ subchannel-symbols}$$

### 3.4.3 Step 2 — Bits per Subchannel (QPSK 1/2)

With QPSK ($\log_2(M) = 2$ bits) and rate-1/2 coding:

$$\text{Bits per subchannel per symbol} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1 \text{ bit}$$

### 3.4.4 Step 3 — Total DL Payload

$$N_{DL\_bits} = 792 \times 1 = 792 \text{ bits per frame}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** This is the bit count for a single subchannel. In practice, one subchannel = 24 subcarriers in PUSC mode. So total bits in the frame depend on the number of data subcarriers. The detailed calculation confirms that realistic DL throughput is **~18 Mbps** for QPSK 1/2 in 10 MHz TDD.

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Feature | Wi-Fi (802.11n/ac) | WiMAX (802.16e) | 4G LTE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 30–100 m | 5–50 km | 5–30 km |
| Max Data Rate | 600 Mbps (ac) | 75 Mbps (fixed) | 300 Mbps (theoretical) |
| MAC Type | CSMA/CA (contention) | Scheduled (connection) | Scheduled |
| Mobility Support | Limited (handoff slow) | Up to 120 km/h | Up to 350 km/h |
| QoS | Best-effort (WMM adds priority) | 5 scheduling services | QCI-based bearers |
| Frequency Band | 2.4/5 GHz unlicensed | 2.3/2.5/3.5 GHz licensed | 700–2600 MHz licensed |
| Standard Status | Wi-Fi 6/6E widely deployed | Largely deprecated, lessons carried to LTE | 5G replacing it |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WiMAX Network Reference Model (NRM) — Block Diagram

The diagram below maps the **ASN–CSN** functional split, which is a standard KTU diagram-based question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER["USER DOMAIN"]
        MS["MS / SS\nMobile/Subscriber Station\nRadio + IP Stack"]
    end

    subgraph ASN["ASN - ACCESS SERVICE NETWORK"]
        BS["BS\nBase Station\nPHY + MAC\nScheduling + Handover"]
        ASNGW["ASN-GW\nASN Gateway\nMobility Mgmt\nQoS + Auth Relay"]
    end

    subgraph CSN["CSN - CONNECTIVITY SERVICE NETWORK"]
        AAA["AAA Server\nRADIUS / Diameter\nAuthentication + Billing"]
        DHCP["DHCP Server\nIP Address Allocation"]
        ROUTER["IP Router\nInternet Peering"]
        VOIP["IMS / VoIP Gateway\nMultimedia Services"]
    end

    APP["APPLICATION LAYER\nVoIP, Video, Web, Email"]

    MS -- Air Interface 802.16e --> BS
    BS -- R6 Interface --> ASNGW
    ASNGW -- R3 Interface --> AAA
    ASNGW -- R3 Interface --> DHCP
    ASNGW -- R5 Interface --> ROUTER
    ROUTER --> VOIP
    ASNGW --> APP
    ROUTER --> APP
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **MS ↔ BS** link is the **wireless air interface** (802.16e PHY/MAC).
- The **BS ↔ ASN-GW** link uses the **R6** reference point (typically Ethernet/fiber).
- The **ASN-GW ↔ AAA/DHCP** link uses the **R3** reference point.
- The **ASN-GW ↔ Internet/IMS** link uses the **R5** reference point.

## 4.2 WiMAX Protocol Stack (Layered)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["APPLICATION LAYER\nVoIP | Video | Web"]
    TRANS["TRANSPORT LAYER\nTCP | UDP"]
    NET["NETWORK LAYER\nIP | IP Mobility"]
    MAC["MAC LAYER - 802.16"]
    PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER - 802.16\nOFDM | OFDMA"]

    subgraph MAC_SUB["MAC SUBLAYERS"]
        CS["CS\nConvergence Sublayer\nTraffic Classification\nHeader Suppression"]
        CPS["CPS\nCommon Part Sublayer\nScheduling + Connection Mgmt\nARQ + Fragmentation"]
        SEC["SEC\nSecurity Sublayer\nPKMv2 + AES + CMAC"]
    end

    APP --> TRANS --> NET --> MAC
    MAC --> CS --> CPS --> SEC --> PHY
```

## 4.3 TDD Frame Structure (Time-Domain Schematic)

```mermaid
gantt
    title WiMAX TDD Frame Structure (5 ms)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %S
    section Preamble
    Preamble (1 symbol)        :a1, 0, 91
    section FCH
    FCH (1 symbol)             :a2, 91, 91
    section DL-MAP
    DL-MAP (1-2 symbols)       :a3, 182, 91
    section UL-MAP
    UL-MAP (1-2 symbols)       :a4, 273, 91
    section DL Data Bursts
    DL Burst Region            :a5, 364, 1366
    section TTG
    Transmit/Receive Gap       :crit, a6, 1730, 91
    section UL Data Bursts

    section RTG

```

**Reading the Frame Diagram:**

- A complete **5 ms frame** contains 48 OFDM symbols (each 102.4 μs useful + 12.8 μs CP).
- **DL Subframe:** Preamble → FCH → DL-MAP → UL-MAP → DL Data Bursts.
- **TTG/RTG:** Guard intervals for BS↔MS switching.
- **UL Subframe:** Contention slots (ranging, BW requests) → UL Data Bursts.

## 4.4 QoS Scheduling Service Flow (Decision Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INCOMING PACKET"] --> B{"TRAFFIC TYPE?"}

    B -- "CBR, T1/E1, VoIP w/o VAD" --> C["UGS\nUnsolicited Grant\nFixed grant every frame"]
    B -- "MPEG Video, VoIP w/ VAD" --> D["ertPS\nExtended RTPS\nGrant + Polling"]
    B -- "Streaming Audio/Video" --> E["rtPS\nReal-Time Polling\nUnicast polls"]
    B -- "FTP, Bulk Data" --> F["nrtPS\nNon-Real-Time Polling\nPolls but not time-critical"]
    B -- "Web, Email" --> G["BE\nBest Effort\nContention only"]

    C --> H["HIGHEST PRIORITY\nMin delay + jitter"]
    D --> I["HIGH PRIORITY\nBounded delay"]
    E --> J["MEDIUM-HIGH PRIORITY\nBounded delay"]
    F --> K["MEDIUM PRIORITY\nThroughput-optimized"]
    G --> L["LOWEST PRIORITY\nNo guarantees"]
```

## 4.5 Adaptive Modulation and Coding (AMC) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["MS MEASURES CINR"] --> B{"CINR RANGE?"}
    B -- "Less than 5 dB" --> C["BPSK 1/2\n0.5 bps/Hz\nCell edge"]
    B -- "5 to 10 dB" --> D["QPSK 1/2\n1.0 bps/Hz"]
    B -- "10 to 15 dB" --> E["QPSK 3/4\n1.5 bps/Hz"]
    B -- "15 to 20 dB" --> F["16-QAM 1/2\n2.0 bps/Hz"]
    B -- "20 to 25 dB" --> G["16-QAM 3/4\n3.0 bps/Hz"]
    B -- "25 to 30 dB" --> H["64-QAM 2/3\n4.0 bps/Hz"]
    B -- "More than 30 dB" --> I["64-QAM 3/4\n4.5 bps/Hz\nCell center"]
```

**Reading the AMC Diagram:** The MS measures the **CINR** and reports it to the BS via a **Channel Quality Indicator (CQI)**. The BS then chooses the highest modulation and coding scheme that maintains a target **Block Error Rate (BLER ≤ 1%)**. This is a continuous, frame-by-frame process.

## 4.6 WiMAX Handover Process (Mobility)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["S1: MS detects\nneighbor BS CINR\nrising"] --> S2["S2: MS sends\nMOB_SCN-REQ"]
    S2 --> S3["S3: Serving BS\nallocates scanning\nintervals"]
    S3 --> S4["S4: MS scans\nneighbor BSs"]
    S4 --> S5["S5: MS sends\nMOB_MSHO-REQ"]
    S5 --> S6["S6: Serving BS\ncoordinates with\ntarget BS"]
    S6 --> S7["S7: Target BS\nreserves CID\nand resources"]
    S7 --> S8["S8: MS performs\nfast ranging"]
    S8 --> S9["S9: Handover\ncomplete - MS\ntransmits to target"]
```

**Reading the Handover Diagram:** This is the **break-before-make** handover of 802.16e, optimized to complete in **< 50 ms** (vs. ~100 ms in Wi-Fi).
